the oven is placed adjacent to the cooling/packaging station. **Principle 3 — Flow:** The arrangement must permit a continuous, preferably unidirectional flow. *Example:* A petroleum refinery arranges crude distillation $\rightarrow$ cracking $\rightarrow$ reforming $\rightarrow$ blending in a single straight-line flow to avoid pumping crude backwards. **Principle 4 — Cubic Space Utilization:** Use vertical space wherever possible. *Example:* A multi-storey chemical plant where reactors are stacked vertically, with raw material silos on top floors and product collection on ground floor — using gravity-assisted flow. **Principle 5 — Flexibility:** Layout must accommodate future expansion without major disruption. *Example:* Tata Motors' Pune plant reserves a $20\%$ expansion zone next to the assembly shop for adding a new SUV production line. **Principle 6 — Safety:** Adequate aisle widths, fire exits, and blast walls. *Example:* In a fertilizer plant, the ammonia storage tank is isolated by a blast wall from the control room and operator cabins. **Principle 7 — Economy:** The layout must minimize total capital and operational cost. *Example:* Locating the boiler house at the geometric centre of the plant to minimize steam-piping length and heat losses. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the major objectives of a good plant layout and explain how each objective contributes to manufacturing efficiency.** **Model Solution:** The major objectives are: 1. **Optimal Use of Floor Space** — Achieved by stacking departments, eliminating unused aisles, and using modular layouts. Contributes to efficiency by reducing land/buildings cost. 2. **Minimum Material Handling Cost** — Achieved via the Principle of Minimum Distance. Handling typically accounts for $20-30\%$ of production cost; a good layout can cut this by $10-15\%$. 3. **Smooth Production Flow** — Achieved via unidirectional flow design. Eliminates bottlenecks and reduces Work-In-Progress (WIP) inventory. 4. **Improved Worker Safety and Morale** — Achieved via adequate lighting, ventilation, aisle width, and noise isolation. Reduces accidents and absenteeism. 5. **Effective Supervision** — Achieved by placing offices with clear sightlines over production areas. Reduces supervisory staff needed. 6. **Flexibility for Future Growth** — Achieved by reserving expansion zones. Avoids costly future re-layouts. 7. **Reduced Production Cycle Time** — Achieved via all the above. Faster throughput means higher capacity and revenue. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write the principles and objectives as a **single mixed-up list**, leading to loss of marks. Examiners expect **clear separation**: Principles = *Design philosophies* (WHY); Objectives = *Measurable targets* (WHAT TO ACHIEVE). Use two separate headings in your answer! --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A chemical plant has 5 departments with the following data. Calculate the optimum location for the central control room using the Centre-of-Gravity (COG) method, and comment on the result.** | Department | $X$ (m) | $Y$ (m) | Workload $W$ (trips/day) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Production A | $20$ | $30$ | $50$ | | Production B | $60$ | $40$ | $40$ | | Storage S1 | $10$ | $60$ | $25$ | | Storage S2 | $70$ | $20$ | $15$ | | Dispatch | $50$ | $70$ | $20$ | **Model Solution:** The COG coordinates are given by: $$ X^{*} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_{k} \cdot X_{k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_{k}} $$ $$ Y^{*} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_{k} \cdot Y_{k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} W_{k}} $$ **Step 1 — Compute $\sum W_{k}$:** $$ \sum W_{k} = 50 + 40 + 25 + 15 + 20 = 150 \text{ trips/day} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\sum W_{k} \cdot X_{k}$:** $$ \sum W_k X_k = (50 \cdot 20) + (40 \cdot 60) + (25 \cdot 10) + (15 \cdot 70) + (20 \cdot 50) $$ $$ \sum W_k X_k = 1000 + 2400 + 250 + 1050 + 1000 = 5700 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\sum W_{k} \cdot Y_{k}$:** $$ \sum W_k Y_k = (50 \cdot 30) + (40 \cdot 40) + (25 \cdot 60) + (15 \cdot 20) + (20 \cdot 70) $$ $$ \sum W_k Y_k = 1500 + 1600 + 1500 + 300 + 1400 = 6300 $$ **Step 4 — Compute Optimum Coordinates:** $$ X^{*} = \dfrac{5700}{150} = 38.00 \text{ m} $$ $$ Y^{*} = \dfrac{6300}{150} = 42.00 \text{ m} $$ **Comment:** The COG method places the central control room at $(38, 42)$, which is close to the geometric centre of the plot. This minimizes the total weighted distance (and hence monitoring effort) to all five departments, satisfying the Principle of Minimum Distance and Principle of Coordination. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of the Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) and its role in plant layout design.** **Model Solution:** The **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)**, also known as the **Mutual Relationship Chart**, is a visual matrix-based tool used in systematic layout planning (SLP). It captures the **qualitative proximity requirements** between every pair of departments. **Structure:** The ARC is an upper-triangular matrix with departments listed on both row and column headers. Each cell contains a **closeness rating** (A, E, I, O, U, X) indicating how close the two departments should ideally be: - **A** = Absolutely necessary (must be adjacent) - **E** = Especially important - **I** = Important - **O** = Ordinary closeness - **U** = Unimportant - **X** = Not desirable (should be separated) **Role in Plant Layout:** 1. **Captures qualitative inputs** — It encodes expert judgment and process logic in a compact, comparable form. 2. **Drives the Space Relationship Diagram** — Once the ARC is finalized, each cell with a high rating (A, E) is translated into a **physical link line** on a bubble diagram, with departments placed closer together. 3. **Integrates multiple criteria** — Beyond material flow, the ARC captures noise, dust, safety, supervision, communication, and vibration factors. For example, a noisy compressor room is marked **X** (separated) from the quality control lab. 4. **Enables objective comparison** — Two candidate layouts can be compared by computing a **Total Closeness Rating (TCR)** score; the higher TCR indicates a better layout. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students confuse the **ARC** with the **Trip Matrix**. Remember: ARC is *qualitative* (using letter codes like A, E, X), while the Trip Matrix is *quantitative* (using actual number of trips). Both are useful but serve different stages of layout planning. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plant Layout Principles & Objectives** ### 5.3.1 Must-Know Definitions - **Plant Layout** = physical arrangement of all industrial facilities inside the plant boundary. - **Plant Location** = geographic selection of the plant site (macro). - **Facility Layout** = arrangement within a single department (micro). ### 5.3.2 The 10 Principles (Memorize All) 1. **Integration** — All units function as one whole. 2. **Minimum Distance** — Shortest material travel. 3. **Flow** — Continuous, unidirectional material movement. 4. **Cubic Space Utilization** — Use vertical volume too. 5. **Satisfaction** — Benefit all stakeholders. 6. **Minimum Handling** — Mechanize & reduce handling steps. 7. **Coordination** — Auxiliaries support production seamlessly. 8. **Flexibility** — Allow future expansion. 9. **Safety** — Worker protection, hazard isolation. 10. **Economy** — Maximum efficiency, minimum cost. ### 5.3.3 The 8 Objectives ("SAFE-IMPS") - **S**afety of workers - **A**ccommodation of growth - **F**acilitating production flow - **E**fficient resource utilization - **I**mproved supervision & morale - **M**inimum handling cost & delays - **P**roductive use of space - **S**mooth production cycles ### 5.3.4 Critical Formulas to Memorize - $\text{MHC} = \sum D_{i} \cdot C_{i}$ - $\text{BTR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{backward}}}{D_{\text{forward}}}$ - $\text{FSU} = \dfrac{A_{\text{productive}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ - $X^{*} = \dfrac{\sum W_{k} X_{k}}{\sum W_{k}}$, $\; Y^{*} = \dfrac{\sum W_{k} Y_{k}}{\sum W_{k}}$ ### 5.3.5 Common Tools (Recognize & Define) - **IDRC** (Inter-Department Relationship Chart) - **ARC** (Activity Relationship Chart — qualitative) - **Trip Matrix** (quantitative) - **Travel Chart** (visualization of trips) - **COG** (Centre of Gravity) - **SLP** (Systematic Layout Planning by Muther) ### 5.3.6 Likely 14-Mark Question Patterns - "Explain the principles and objectives of a good plant layout." → Direct theory. - "Compute the MHC for the given layout data." → Weighted Distance Method. - "Find the optimum location of X using COG method." → Numerical with formula. - "Explain the role of ARC in layout design." → Tool-based question. - "Compare Plant Layout vs Plant Location." → Tabular differentiation. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** > - Always draw a **small diagram** (flow chart or bubble diagram) to support your theory answer. > - Always state the **assumptions** before solving numerical problems. > - Always include **units** in numerical answers — partial credit is lost if units are missing. > - Always cite the **principle** that justifies each design decision in objective-type questions.

topics.title: Types of Layouts - Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Layouts - Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Layout (Line Layout / Assembly Line Layout):** A plant layout arrangement in which machines, equipment, and workstations are physically organized in a **fixed sequence** that corresponds directly to the **step-by-step operations** required to manufacture a specific product or a family of similar products. The material flow follows a **straight-line or continuous path** from raw material input to finished goods output.

In KTU terminology, this is also referred to as a **"Line Layout"** or **"Flow Line Layout"**. It is the direct opposite of a **Process Layout** (functional layout), where machines are grouped by function rather than product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** In Product Layout, the **product dictates the layout** — the path of the product determines the placement of machines, not the other way around.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **bottling plant** you have seen in a video or on a factory visit. Think about it like this:

- An **empty bottle enters** at one end (raw material).
- It moves along a **conveyor belt** (straight line).
- At station 1, it is **washed** → Station 2, **filled** → Station 3, **capped** → Station 4, **labeled** → Station 5, **packed** → and a **finished, sealed bottle exits** at the other end.

Each worker at each station does **one specific task, repeatedly, all day long**. The bottle never "goes back" or "jumps around" — it flows in **one direction** like a river.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Trick:** If you can visualize an **assembly line of a car factory** (Ford Model T style) or a **burger-making counter at McDonald's**, you already intuitively understand Product Layout. The key idea: **the product moves, the workers and machines stay put**.

---

### 1.3 Key Identifying Features (Bullet Snapshot)

- ✅ Machines arranged **in sequence of operations**
- ✅ **High volume, low variety** production
- ✅ **Standardized product** with stable design
- ✅ **Continuous flow** of material
- ✅ Each workstation performs a **repetitive, specialized** operation
- ✅ Often uses **conveyor belts, transfer lines, or assembly lines**
- ✅ Also called: **Line Layout** / **Flow Shop Layout**

---

### 1.4 Typical Industries Using Product Layout

| Industry | Example Product |
|----------|----------------|
| Automotive | Cars, Two-wheelers |
| Electronics | Mobile phones, Laptops |
| Consumer Goods | Bottled water, Biscuits |
| Appliances | Washing machines, Refrigerators |
| Food Processing | Bread, Soft drinks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question says "mass production", "continuous production", "assembly line", or "high volume standardized product" — the answer is **Product Layout**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Characteristics of Product Layout

The product layout is defined by a set of structural and operational characteristics. Each characteristic is justified by the **central objective: minimize material handling cost and throughput time**.

#### A. Physical Arrangement Characteristics

1. **Fixed Sequence of Machines**
   - Machines are placed in the **exact order** in which operations are performed on the product.
   - Example: Drilling machine → Milling machine → Grinding machine (in that order for a specific component).

2. **Straight-Line or U-Shape Flow Path**
   - The most common shape is a **straight line** (I-shape).
   - When floor space is constrained, a **U-shape** or **L-shape** layout is used.
   - Sometimes a **serpentine (S-shape)** is used for very long production lines.

3. **Dedicated Workstations**
   - Each station is dedicated to **one specific operation** on the product.
   - No station is shared between two different product types.

4. **Use of Material Handling Equipment**
   - Conveyors, roller tracks, overhead cranes, automated guided vehicles (AGVs), or transfer lines connect workstations.

#### B. Operational Characteristics

1. **High Capital Investment**
   - The line is built for **one specific product**. If the product is discontinued, the line is essentially obsolete.

2. **High Production Rate**
   - Designed for **mass production** — typically thousands of units per day.

3. **Low Skilled Labor, High Specialized Labor**
   - Workers are **trained for one specific task** — learning curve is fast.

4. **Minimum Material Handling**
   - Since machines are arranged by sequence, the product moves the **shortest possible distance** between operations.

5. **Continuous Flow / Smooth Production**
   - No backtracking, no waiting, no queuing between unrelated operations.

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Product Layout — Design Justification

| Design Question | Justification |
|----------------|---------------|
| Why fixed sequence? | Eliminates decision-making about routing → predictable flow |
| Why high volume focus? | Justifies the heavy capital investment in dedicated lines |
| Why repetitive tasks? | Worker becomes very fast at one task → high productivity |
| Why minimum material handling? | Reduces cost, reduces damage, reduces throughput time |
| Why standardized product? | Allows line balancing and predictable cycle times |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Analysis of Product Layout

Although product layout is largely qualitative, the following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) questions on line balancing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA SET FOR PRODUCT LAYOUT (Assembly Line Balancing)**

**Notation Used:**
- $n$ = Total number of workstations
- $t_i$ = Processing time of task $i$ (in minutes/seconds)
- $C$ = **Cycle Time** of the line (time between two consecutive units coming off the line)
- $T$ = **Total available production time** per day
- $D$ = **Demand** (number of units required per day)
- $L$ = Length of the line
- $E$ = **Line Efficiency** (%)

---

| Formula | Description | KTU Significance |
|---------|-------------|------------------|
| $C = \dfrac{T}{D}$ | Cycle time | Determines maximum time allowed per workstation |
| $n_{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{C} \right\rceil$ | Theoretical minimum number of stations | Ceiling function — always round UP |
| $E = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \times C} \times 100$ | Line Efficiency (%) | Higher is better; aim for $\geq 85\%$ |
| $B = n \times C - \sum t_i$ | Balance Delay (idle time) | Lower is better |
| $d = \dfrac{B}{n \times C} \times 100$ | Balance Delay (%) | Percentage of idle time |
| $L = (n - 1) \times s + \ell_w$ | Line length (approx.) | Where $s$ = spacing, $\ell_w$ = workstation length |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industries:** Car assembly lines (Toyota, Ford), smartphone assembly (Apple Foxconn plants).
- **Process Industries:** Oil refineries, chemical plants — the process flow dictates equipment placement.
- **Service Industries:** Self-service cafeterias, car washes, amusement park ride queues.
- **Warehousing:** Pick-and-pack lines for e-commerce (Amazon fulfillment centers).

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Linkage:** Product layout is the **backbone of Lean Manufacturing and the Toyota Production System (TPS)**. Concepts like *takt time*, *heijunka* (level scheduling), and *jidoka* (autonomation) all directly extend from product layout principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Theoretical Minimum Number of Workstations

The **most important derivation** in product layout is the lower bound on the number of workstations. This is asked almost every KTU exam cycle.

**Given:** A set of $k$ tasks with processing times $t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_k$ must be performed on each unit. The line has a cycle time constraint $C$.

**Goal:** Find the smallest possible number of workstations, $n_{\min}$.

---

**Step 1:** The total time required to make one complete unit is the **sum of all task times**.

$$
\text{Total Work Content} = W = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i = t_1 + t_2 + \cdots + t_k
$$

**Step 2:** Since each workstation has a **maximum allowable time equal to the cycle time $C$**, the absolute minimum number of stations is the total work divided by the cycle time.

$$
n_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{W}{C} = \frac{\sum t_i}{C}
$$

**Step 3:** However, you **cannot have a fractional workstation**. A station is either present (1) or absent (0). Therefore, you must round **UP** to the next integer.

$$
n_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{C} \right\rceil
$$

Where $\lceil x \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** (smallest integer $\geq x$).

**Step 4:** The actual number of stations in practice, $n_{\text{actual}}$, will always satisfy:

$$
n_{\min} \leq n_{\text{actual}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Style Problem

> **Problem (Typical KTU Part B Style):**
> An assembly line has 6 tasks with the following times (in minutes): A = 4, B = 6, C = 5, D = 3, E = 7, F = 5. The daily working time is **480 minutes** and demand is **120 units/day**. Find: (i) Cycle time, (ii) Minimum number of workstations, (iii) Line efficiency, (iv) Balance delay.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Calculate Cycle Time $C$**

$$
C = \frac{T}{D} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{120 \text{ units}} = 4 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step 2: Total Work Content**

$$
W = \sum t_i = 4 + 6 + 5 + 3 + 7 + 5 = 30 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Minimum Number of Workstations**

$$
n_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{30}{4} \right\rceil = \lceil 7.5 \rceil = 8 \text{ workstations}
$$

**Step 4: Line Efficiency (assuming $n = 8$)**

$$
E = \frac{W}{n \times C} \times 100 = \frac{30}{8 \times 4} \times 100 = \frac{30}{32} \times 100 = 93.75\%
$$

**Step 5: Balance Delay**

$$
d = \frac{n \cdot C - W}{n \cdot C} \times 100 = \frac{32 - 30}{32} \times 100 = 6.25\%
$$

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Product vs. Process Layout

| Parameter | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Arrangement basis | Product (operation sequence) | Process (machine type) |
| Production type | Mass / continuous | Job / batch |
| Product variety | Low (standardized) | High (custom) |
| Volume | High | Low to medium |
| Material handling | Minimal | Extensive |
| Work-in-progress | Low | High |
| Production time per unit | Short, predictable | Long, variable |
| Flexibility | Low (rigid line) | High (re-routable) |
| Capital investment | High | Low to medium |
| Worker skill level | Low (repetitive) | High (multi-skilled) |
| Skilled supervision need | Low | High |
| Best suited for | Assembly, bottling, refining | Hospital, tool room, job shop |

---

### 3.4 Decision Tree — When to Choose Product Layout

A student should ask the following diagnostic questions to determine if a product layout is appropriate:

1. Is the product **standardized and stable**? → **YES** → continue
2. Is the demand **high and continuous**? → **YES** → continue
3. Is the **production volume sufficient** to recover capital cost? → **YES** → **Choose Product Layout**
4. Is there a **clear, fixed sequence of operations**? → **YES** → **Choose Product Layout**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If even **one** of the above is "NO" — process layout is likely the better choice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Product Layout Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material Storage] --> S1[Station 1: Operation A]
    S1 --> S2[Station 2: Operation B]
    S2 --> S3[Station 3: Operation C]
    S3 --> S4[Station 4: Operation D]
    S4 --> S5[Station 5: Operation E]
    S5 --> S6[Station 6: Quality Check]
    S6 --> A2[Finished Goods Storage]
    A2 --> A3[Dispatch to Market]
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows a **unidirectional straight-line flow** — the canonical representation of a product layout. Each station performs a **single, specific operation**, and the product moves forward only.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Decision Logic for Selecting Product Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is product standardized?}
    Q1 -- No --> PJL[Choose Process or Job Layout]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is demand high and stable?}
    Q2 -- No --> PJL
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is production volume high?}
    Q3 -- No --> PJL
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is operation sequence fixed?}
    Q4 -- No --> PJL
    Q4 -- Yes --> PL[Choose PRODUCT LAYOUT]
```

> **Interpretation:** The flowchart formalizes the decision logic from Section 3.4 in a visual format suitable for KTU answer sheets.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Assembly Line Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        RM[Raw Material Infeed]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Process Subsystem]
        direction LR
        ST1[Workstation 1]
        ST2[Workstation 2]
        ST3[Workstation 3]
        ST4[Workstation 4]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Subsystem]
        FG[Finished Product Outfeed]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Subsystem]
        QC[Quality Control Station]
        MAINT[Maintenance Bay]
    end
    RM --> ST1 --> ST2 --> ST3 --> ST4 --> QC --> FG
    MAINT -.-> ST1
    MAINT -.-> ST2
    MAINT -.-> ST3
    MAINT -.-> ST4
```

> **Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates the **four functional subsystems** of a product layout: input (raw material infeed), process (sequence of workstations), output (finished product dispatch), and support (quality + maintenance). This is a common KTU 14-mark question format.

---

### 4.4 Schematic Representation of Layout Shapes

| Shape | When Used | Schematic |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Straight Line (I)** | Long floor space available | `A → B → C → D → E` |
| **U-Shape** | Limited floor space, easy worker supervision | `A → B → C → D → E ↓` |
| **L-Shape** | Obstacles on one side of floor | `A → B → C ↓ D` |
| **Serpentine (S)** | Very long product lines (e.g., shipbuilding) | `A → B → C ↑ D ← E` |
| **Circular** | Continuous process industries (refinery) | `A → B → C → D → A (loop)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** Questions often ask to **draw and justify** a layout shape. The **U-shape** is most efficient in modern manufacturing (Toyota uses it) because it allows **one supervisor to oversee all stations**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Q1. Define product layout. List any four of its salient features.**

**Model Answer:**
Product layout, also called line layout, is a plant arrangement in which machines and workstations are placed in a **fixed sequence** corresponding to the operations required to manufacture a specific product. The material flows in a **continuous, straight-line path** from raw material to finished goods.

**Salient Features:**
1. Machines are arranged strictly **as per operation sequence**.
2. It is suitable for **mass production** of standardized products.
3. It uses **dedicated, specialized** equipment.
4. **Low material handling cost** and **low work-in-progress**.
5. Workers perform **repetitive, single tasks** requiring low skill variety.
*(Any four)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Q2. Distinguish between product layout and process layout based on any four parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Arrangement basis | By product/operation | By machine type/process |
| Production volume | High | Low to medium |
| Product variety | Low | High |
| Material handling | Minimal | Extensive |
| Capital investment | High | Low |
| Worker skill | Low (specialized) | High (multi-skilled) |

*(Any four with clear distinction)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Explain the **characteristics, advantages, and limitations** of a product layout in detail. Mention **two real-world industries** where it is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An assembly line has **7 tasks** with the following times (in minutes): P1=6, P2=4, P3=7, P2=5 (corrected: P4=5), P5=3, P6=6, P7=4. The line operates for **8 hours/day** and must produce **96 units/day**. Determine: (i) Cycle time, (ii) Minimum number of workstations, (iii) Line efficiency, (iv) Balance delay. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Characteristics, Advantages, Limitations & Applications**

**Characteristics:**
- Machines arranged in **strict operation sequence**.
- Each workstation does **one specific task** repeatedly.
- **Continuous, unidirectional** material flow.
- Suited to **standardized, high-volume** products.

**Advantages:**
1. **Reduced material handling** — material moves shortest path.
2. **Lower WIP and inventory** — no waiting between stations.
3. **Faster production cycle** — high throughput rate.
4. **Lower labor cost per unit** — repetitive tasks allow fast learning curve.
5. **Easier production planning and control** — predictable flow.
6. **Better quality control** — fixed stations enable focused inspection.

**Limitations:**
1. **High initial capital investment** — line dedicated to one product.
2. **Low flexibility** — difficult to change product type.
3. **Bottleneck sensitivity** — slowest station dictates output.
4. **Worker monotony** — repetitive tasks cause fatigue and demotivation.
5. **High downtime risk** — failure of one station halts the entire line.

**Real-World Applications:**
- **Automobile industry** (e.g., Maruti Suzuki assembly line).
- **Bottling industry** (e.g., Coca-Cola plant).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Tasks: $P1=6, P2=4, P3=7, P4=5, P5=3, P6=6, P7=4$ (total 7 tasks)
- $T = 8 \text{ hrs/day} = 480 \text{ min/day}$
- $D = 96 \text{ units/day}$

**Step (i): Cycle Time**

$$
C = \frac{T}{D} = \frac{480}{96} = 5 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Step (ii): Total Work Content**

$$
W = \sum t_i = 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 3 + 6 + 4 = 35 \text{ min}
$$

**Step (iii): Minimum Number of Workstations**

$$
n_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{C} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{35}{5} \right\rceil = \lceil 7 \rceil = 7 \text{ workstations}
$$

**Step (iv): Line Efficiency**

$$
E = \frac{W}{n \times C} \times 100 = \frac{35}{7 \times 5} \times 100 = \frac{35}{35} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

**Step (v): Balance Delay**

$$
d = \frac{n \cdot C - W}{n \cdot C} \times 100 = \frac{35 - 35}{35} \times 100 = 0\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The line is **perfectly balanced** — no idle time, 100% efficiency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **components of a product layout**. Discuss the **different shapes** of product layout with their suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit wants to decide between **product layout and process layout** for a new production facility. As a plant engineer, outline the **decision criteria** and recommend the suitable layout for the following two cases: **(7 marks)**
- **Case 1:** A biscuit manufacturing company producing 50,000 packets/day of a single product.
- **Case 2:** A job workshop producing 5 different machine tools per week in small batches.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Components of Product Layout and Layout Shapes**

**Components (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3):**

A product layout consists of:
1. **Input subsystem** — Raw material storage and infeed mechanism.
2. **Process subsystem** — Sequence of workstations performing dedicated operations.
3. **Output subsystem** — Finished goods storage and dispatch area.
4. **Material handling system** — Conveyors or transfer lines connecting stations.
5. **Support subsystem** — Quality control, maintenance, supervisory stations.

**Shapes of Product Layout:**

| Shape | Suitability |
|-------|-------------|
| **Straight Line (I)** | Long assembly lines (e.g., car assembly in large plants) |
| **U-Shape** | Space-constrained factories; one supervisor can oversee all stations (Toyota system) |
| **L-Shape** | When building columns/obstacles restrict straight line |
| **Serpentine (S)** | Very long product flow (e.g., shipbuilding) |
| **Circular** | Process industries (oil refinery) — closed-loop material flow |

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

```
[Input] → [S1] → [S2] → [S3] → [S4] → [QC] → [Output]
   ↓                                       ↑
[Maintenance bay] --------------------- connects all
```

---

**(b) Layout Selection Decision**

**Decision Criteria:**
1. **Production volume** (high → product; low → process)
2. **Product variety** (low → product; high → process)
3. **Product standardization** (yes → product; no → process)
4. **Capital availability** (high → product; low → process)
5. **Flexibility required** (low → product; high → process)
6. **Type of industry** (continuous/assembly → product; job/repair → process)

**Case 1 — Biscuit Manufacturing (50,000 packets/day, single product):**
- **Recommend: PRODUCT LAYOUT**
- **Justification:** High volume, single standardized product, mass production, continuous flow, capital investment is justified by large output. Example: Britannia or Parle biscuit plant.

**Case 2 — Job Workshop (5 different machine tools, small batches):**
- **Recommend: PROCESS LAYOUT**
- **Justification:** High product variety, low volume per type, frequent changeovers, need for routing flexibility, low capital available. Example: A general machine shop with separate turning, milling, grinding sections.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the ceiling function** ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$) in $n_{\min}$ calculation — students often write 7.5 instead of 8, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing cycle time with task time** — cycle time is per unit of output, task time is per operation.
> 3. **Not justifying the layout choice** — just naming "product layout" without giving **at least 3 reasons** will get partial credit only.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU mandates a diagram wherever layout shapes are discussed. **Always draw the layout shape.**
> 5. **Unit errors** — converting hours to minutes incorrectly. Standard: 1 shift = 8 hours = 480 minutes.
> 6. **Mixing up "balance delay" with "line efficiency"** — they are **complementary** ($d = 100\% - E$ when $n = n_{\min}$).
> 7. **Forgetting real-world examples** — KTU gives bonus marks for industry-linked answers (e.g., Maruti, Coca-Cola, Toyota).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Product Layout**

- ✅ **Definition:** Layout where machines are arranged in **operation sequence** for a **specific product**.
- ✅ **Other names:** Line layout, flow line layout, assembly line layout.
- ✅ **Best suited for:** **High volume, low variety, standardized** products (mass production).
- ✅ **Key features:** Continuous flow, dedicated workstations, minimum material handling.
- ✅ **Capital:** High initial investment; low operating cost per unit.
- ✅ **Flexibility:** **Low** — line is product-specific.
- ✅ **Worker skill:** Low skill variety required; high specialization.
- ✅ **Layout shapes:** Straight (I), U, L, Serpentine (S), Circular.
- ✅ **Toyota's U-shape** is the modern industry standard.
- ✅ **Cycle Time Formula:** $C = T / D$.
- ✅ **Minimum Workstations:** $n_{\min} = \lceil \sum t_i / C \rceil$ (always **ceiling**).
- ✅ **Line Efficiency:** $E = (\sum t_i) / (n \times C) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Balance Delay:** $d = (nC - W) / (nC) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Industry examples:** Maruti Suzuki (cars), Coca-Cola (bottles), Apple/Foxconn (phones), Britannia (biscuits).
- ✅ **Comparison pointer:** Product layout is the **opposite** of process layout in almost every parameter.
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Standardized product + High volume + Fixed sequence = **Choose Product Layout**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROCESS (In Plant Layout & Design Context)**
> A **Process** is the engineered sequence of physical, chemical, and/or biological operations that transforms specified feedstocks (raw materials) into desired products under defined operating conditions (temperature, pressure, flow rates), together with the selection of equipment, process route, and operating parameters that constitute the **Process Design** of a chemical/petrochemical plant.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the term "Process" is not a single operation — it is the **complete engineering design package** that specifies *what* reactions and separations occur, *how* they occur, *in what equipment*, and *under what conditions*. It precedes and governs the downstream **Plant Design** activity (mechanical layout, piping, civil, electrical, instrumentation, safety).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Recipe vs. Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners**
> Think of building a factory like building a restaurant:
> - **Process Design = The Recipe Book:** Decides the *menu* (which products), the *ingredients* (raw materials), the *cooking sequence* (unit operations), the *utensils* (vessels, columns, heat exchangers), the *oven temperatures* (T, P conditions), and the *portions* (flow rates).
> - **Plant Design = The Restaurant Building:** Decides the *kitchen layout* (equipment layout), *plumbing* (piping), *electrical wiring* (power), *ventilation* (HVAC), *fire exits* (safety), and the *dining hall arrangement* (off-sites and utilities).
>
> **You must finalise the recipe BEFORE you can design the kitchen.** This is why "Process" is Module 1.

## 1.3 Process Design vs. Plant Design — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Process Design | Plant Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Front-End Design / Conceptual Design | Detailed Engineering / Back-End Design |
| **Primary deliverable** | Process Flow Diagram (PFD), Material & Energy Balances | Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID), General Arrangement (GA) drawings |
| **Answers the question** | "WHAT must be done, and HOW IN PRINCIPLE?" | "HOW EXACTLY will it be built, installed, and operated?" |
| **Performed by** | Chemical / Process Engineers | Mechanical, Civil, Electrical, Instrumentation Engineers |
| **Stage in project** | Phase 1 (Feasibility → Process Selection) | Phase 2 (Detailed Engineering → Construction) |
| **KTU module mapping** | **Module 1 (Process)** | Modules 2, 3, 4, 5 (Plant Layout, Piping, Utilities, Safety) |

## 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used to characterise any process:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Metrics to Memorise:**
> - **Conversion (X)** = fraction of reactant consumed → dimensionless
> - **Yield (Y)** = fraction of reactant converted to the *desired* product → dimensionless
> - **Selectivity (S)** = ratio of desired product formed to total products formed → dimensionless
> - **Space Time (τ)** = residence time of fluid in reactor → **seconds (s)**
> - **Space Velocity (SV)** = inverse of space time → **s⁻¹** or **h⁻¹**
> - **Plant Capacity (P)** = tonnes of product per year (tpa) → **tpa** or **kg/hr**
> - **Overall Plant Availability / On-stream Factor** = 90–95% (continuous plants)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Process Design (KTU-Exam Favourite)

Every chemical/petrochemical project follows a **standardised sequence of process design activities**. The KTU board examiner frequently tests this as a 7- or 14-mark question. Memorise the order and the *purpose* of each step.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Sequential Phases of Process Design:**
>
> 1. **Problem Definition & Feedstock-Product Specification**
>    - *Why:* Establish the design basis (feed composition, product purity, plant capacity, location, regulatory environment).
>    - *Output:* Design Basis Document.
>
> 2. **Literature Survey & Thermodynamic Analysis**
>    - *Why:* Identify candidate process routes from journals, patents, and in-house R&D.
>    - *Tools:* Equilibrium data, free-energy (ΔG) calculations, kinetics (rate equations).
>
> 3. **Process Development & Scale-Up**
>    - *Why:* Move a chemistry from the laboratory bench to commercial scale safely and economically.
>    - *Stages:* Laboratory → Bench-scale → Pilot Plant → Semi-Commercial → Commercial Plant.
>
> 4. **Material & Energy Balance (M&EB)**
>    - *Why:* Quantify every stream's flow rate, composition, temperature, and pressure. Forms the **mass-balance backbone** of the PFD.
>    - *Equations:* Steady-state: Σ Input = Σ Output.
>
> 5. **Equipment Selection, Sizing & Specification**
>    - *Why:* Convert the M&EB into physical equipment — reactors, distillation columns, pumps, heat exchangers.
>    - *Tools:* McCabe-Thiele (distillation), LMTD (heat exchangers), reaction rate models.
>
> 6. **Economic Evaluation, Safety, Environmental & PFD Finalisation**
>    - *Why:* Ensure the process is profitable, safe, and compliant before handing over to Plant Design.
>    - *Output:* Final Process Flow Diagram (PFD), Equipment List, Cost Estimate.

## 2.2 The Process Development Hierarchy (Scale-Up Ladder)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four-Stage Scale-Up Ladder (Mandatory KTU Concept)**
>
> | Stage | Capacity (Typical) | Purpose | Data Generated |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **1. Laboratory (Lab-scale)** | grams → kilograms | Prove chemistry; obtain equilibrium & kinetic data | Conversion, selectivity, ΔH |
> | **2. Pilot Plant** | kilograms → tonnes/day | Verify continuous operation; test real equipment | Heat/mass transfer coefficients, residence time |
> | **3. Semi-Commercial / Demo Plant** | tonnes/day → hundreds of tonnes | De-risk scale-up; train operators; produce market samples | Corrosion, fouling, control strategy |
> | **4. Commercial Plant** | thousands → millions of tonnes/year | Full-scale production for market | Optimised operating conditions |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table — All Formulas for "Process" Topic**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | KTU Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{n_{A,in} - n_{A,out}}{n_{A,in}}$ | dimensionless | Always based on **limiting reactant** |
| 2 | **Selectivity (S)** | $S = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired product formed}}{\text{moles of reactant consumed}}$ | dimensionless | Must specify product |
| 3 | **Yield (Y)** | $Y = S \times X$ | dimensionless | Most-used economic metric |
| 4 | **Space Time (τ)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{V}_{feed}}$ | s or h | Reactor volume / volumetric feed rate |
| 5 | **Space Velocity (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ | Inverse of τ |
| 6 | **General Material Balance** | $\dfrac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{r}_{gen}$ | kg/s | Steady-state ⇒ LHS = 0 |
| 7 | **Steady-state Mass Balance** | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{consumed}$ | kg/s | Used in almost every problem |
| 8 | **General Energy Balance** | $\dfrac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$ | kW | First Law of Thermodynamics |
| 9 | **Overall Material Conversion** | $\%X = X \times 100$ | % | Reported in PFD stream tables |
| 10 | **Plant Capacity Utilisation** | $\%U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Production}}{\text{Design Capacity}} \times 100$ | % | 85–95% is typical for continuous plants |
| 11 | **Degree of Freedom (DoF)** | $DoF = N_{unknowns} - N_{independent\,equations}$ | integer | DoF = 0 ⇒ uniquely solvable |
| 12 | **Recycle Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Recycle Stream Flow}}{\text{Fresh Feed Flow}}$ | dimensionless | Optimised for cost & purity |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Pipe Symbol in Markdown:**
> When writing absolute values in your answer sheet, **never** type `|x|` inside a markdown table. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. Inked answer sheets are unaffected, but KTU Moodle/MST digital submissions will break the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Concepts Are Applied in Industry:**
> - **Refineries** (BPCL, IOCL, Reliance Jamnagar) — process selection between catalytic cracking vs. hydrocracking.
> - **Pharmaceuticals** (Dr. Reddy's, Cipla) — pilot-plant scale-up from lab synthesis to GMP commercial batches.
> - **Ammonia & Fertiliser Plants** (Kochi Fertilisers) — material balance on the Haber–Bosch loop with recycle.
> - **Petrochemicals** (Naphtha Cracker) — yield vs. selectivity trade-off between ethylene, propylene, and by-products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Material Balance Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** Conservation of mass on a chosen control volume (reactor, column, entire plant).

Let us define a generic process unit with multiple inlet streams, multiple outlet streams, and an internal generation/consumption rate. We sum the *rate of accumulation* inside the system.

$$
\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{in}} \dot{m}_{i,in} - \sum_{j=1}^{n_{out}} \dot{m}_{j,out} + \sum_{k=1}^{n_{rxn}} \dot{r}_{k,gen} - \sum_{k=1}^{n_{rxn}} \dot{r}_{k,cons}
$$

**Step-by-step logical transition:**

**Step 1 — Identify all inlet mass flow rates.**
There are $n_{in}$ inlet streams. Each carries a mass flow $\dot{m}_{i,in}$ in kg/s. The total inlet flow is their sum.

**Step 2 — Identify all outlet mass flow rates.**
There are $n_{out}$ outlet streams. Each carries a mass flow $\dot{m}_{j,out}$ in kg/s.

**Step 3 — Account for chemical generation or consumption.**
Inside the reactor, each species $k$ may be generated at rate $\dot{r}_{k,gen}$ or consumed at rate $\dot{r}_{k,cons}$, both in kmol/s or kg/s.

**Step 4 — Apply the steady-state assumption.**
For **continuous** processes (the KTU default), $\dfrac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$. The system inventory is constant. This simplifies the master equation to the **operating-line mass balance**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n_{in}} \dot{m}_{i,in} + \sum_{k=1}^{n_{rxn}} \dot{r}_{k,gen} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_{out}} \dot{m}_{j,out} + \sum_{k=1}^{n_{rxn}} \dot{r}_{k,cons}
$$

This is the **workhorse equation** that you will use in 80% of process-design numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single-Reactor Conversion from Material Balance

Consider a **single-stream, single-pass reactor** with one feed and one product stream, where reactant A converts to product B.

**Step 1 — Write the component balance for reactant A.**

$$
\dot{n}_{A,in} - \dot{n}_{A,out} = \dot{r}_{A,consumed}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the consumed rate in terms of conversion.**
By the *definition* of conversion, the fraction of A fed that is consumed is exactly $X$. So:

$$
\dot{r}_{A,consumed} = X \cdot \dot{n}_{A,in}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Step 1.**

$$
\dot{n}_{A,in} - \dot{n}_{A,out} = X \cdot \dot{n}_{A,in}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the outlet flow of A.**

$$
\dot{n}_{A,out} = \dot{n}_{A,in} (1 - X)
$$

This is the **single-pass unreacted A** leaving the reactor. Notice the elegant result: outlet flow = inlet flow × (1 − X). For $X = 1$ (100% conversion), the outlet flow is zero, as expected.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Degree of Freedom (DoF) for a Process Network

The **Degree of Freedom analysis** is a mandatory check before solving any material balance problem in KTU exams. It tells you whether the system is *exactly solvable*, *under-specified*, or *over-specified*.

$$
DoF = (\text{Unknown variables}) - (\text{Independent equations})
$$

**Step-by-step procedure:**

**Step 1 — Count unknowns.**
For each stream, the unknowns are: total flow, composition of every component, temperature, pressure. For $S$ streams each with $C$ components, the count is roughly $S \times (C + 2)$.

**Step 2 — Count independent equations.**
Each unit operation contributes: 1 total mass balance + $C - 1$ component balances + energy balance. So per unit, you get $C + 1$ equations. For $U$ units, you get $U \times (C + 1)$ equations.

**Step 3 — Apply the DoF rule.**

$$
DoF = S \times (C + 2) - U \times (C + 1)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - If $DoF = 0$ → system is **exactly specified**; unique solution exists.
> - If $DoF > 0$ → system is **under-specified**; you need more information (e.g., additional feed composition).
> - If $DoF < 0$ → system is **over-specified**; redundant data — flag a potential inconsistency.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Selection Table Between Two Process Routes (Common 14-Mark Sub-Part)**

| Decision Criterion | Process Route A (e.g., Catalytic Cracking) | Process Route B (e.g., Hydrocracking) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock flexibility** | High (wide naphtha range) | Very high (VGO + residue) |
| **Conversion (single-pass)** | 65–80% | 90–99% |
| **Selectivity to gasoline** | Moderate | High |
| **Operating pressure (bar)** | 1–3 (atmospheric) | 70–200 (high pressure) |
| **Operating temperature (°C)** | 480–550 | 350–450 |
| **Capital cost** | Moderate | High (thick-walled vessels, compressors) |
| **Operating cost** | Moderate | High (hydrogen consumption) |
| **Product quality** | Lower octane | Higher cetane + cleaner diesel |
| **Environmental impact** | Higher SOx, NOx | Lower emissions |
| **Typical plant selection** | Refineries, FCC units | Upgrading, lube-oil production |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** When asked "which process would you select and why?", always answer in a **structured 4-point format**:
> 1. State the selection criterion (yield, cost, safety, environmental).
> 2. Compare the two routes on that criterion.
> 3. State your decision.
> 4. Justify with one quantitative number.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Development Scale-Up Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lab Scale 1 to 1000 g] --> B[Bench Scale 1 to 10 kg per day]
    B --> C[Pilot Plant 0.1 to 10 tonnes per day]
    C --> D[Semi Commercial Plant 50 to 500 tonnes per day]
    D --> E[Commercial Plant 1000 to 1 Million tonnes per year]

    A1[Prove chemistry kinetics and equilibrium] -.-> A
    B1[Optimise reaction conditions catalyst screening] -.-> B
    C1[Generate M and EB data validate continuous operation] -.-> C
    D1[Train operators de risk scale up produce market samples] -.-> D
    E1[Full scale production optimised profit] -.-> E

    style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315
    style B fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd
    style E fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a process-development stage, sized by typical capacity. The dashed side-comments (A1, B1, C1, D1, E1) indicate the *engineering deliverable* at each stage. The arrows show the *sequential* scale-up flow.

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Flow — The Six Steps of Process Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Problem Definition and Design Basis] --> S2[Step 2 Literature Survey and Thermodynamic Analysis]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Process Development Lab to Pilot to Commercial]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Material and Energy Balances M and EB]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Equipment Selection Sizing Specification]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Economic Safety Environmental and PFD Finalisation]

    S1 --- O1[Output Design Basis Document]
    S2 --- O2[Output Candidate Process Routes]
    S3 --- O3[Output Pilot Plant Data]
    S4 --- O4[Output Stream Tables and PFD Draft]
    S5 --- O5[Output Equipment List and Data Sheets]
    S6 --- O6[Output Final PFD Cost Estimate and Approval]

    style S1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style S2 fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00
    style S3 fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e
    style S4 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
    style S5 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style S6 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering Drawing Hierarchy (BFD → PFD → P&ID)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Block Flow Diagram BFD] --> L2[Level 2 Process Flow Diagram PFD]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Isometric and 3D Plant Layout]

    L1a[Shows only major process blocks one line per stream] -.-> L1
    L2a[Shows all equipment pumps valves controllers all stream data] -.-> L2
    L3a[Shows pipe sizes materials instruments control loops safety devices] -.-> L3
    L4a[Shows physical 3D placement piping routes supports structures] -.-> L4

    style L1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style L2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style L4 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU:**
> A typical 7-mark question reads: *"Explain the hierarchy of process flow diagrams used in chemical plant design with neat sketches."* The four-level hierarchy (BFD → PFD → P&ID → Layout) is the textbook answer. **Memorise the four levels and the data they contain.**

## 4.4 Mermaid Functional Flow — Recycle-Loop with Purge (Generic Reactor + Separator + Recycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fresh Feed Stream] --> MIX[Mixing Point]
    R[Recycle Stream] --> MIX
    MIX --> RX[Reactor]
    RX --> SEP[Separator]
    SEP --> P[Product Outlet]
    SEP --> REC[Recycle Loop]
    REC --> PURGE[Purge Stream]
    REC --> R
    PURGE --> WASTE[Waste Treatment]

    style F fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style R fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825
    style RX fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style SEP fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1
    style P fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style PURGE fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Significance:** Most industrial processes (Haber-Bosch, FCC, alkylation) operate with a **recycle loop** to maximise raw-material conversion. The **purge** is essential to prevent inert impurities from accumulating to dangerous concentrations — a classic KTU question: *"Why is a purge stream necessary in a recycle loop?"* Answer: to maintain component mole balance and prevent inert buildup.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between Process Design and Plant Design. List any four key deliverables of process design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, structured):**

| S.No. | Process Design | Plant Design |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conceptual / front-end engineering | Detailed / back-end engineering |
| 2 | Answers "what & how in principle" | Answers "how exactly to build it" |
| 3 | Done by chemical / process engineers | Done by mechanical, civil, electrical engineers |
| 4 | Output: PFD, M&EB, equipment list | Output: P&ID, GA, isometrics, site layout |

**Four key deliverables of Process Design (1 mark each in a 4-point list, 4 marks → here 1 mark for any four, full marks for all four):**
1. Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
2. Material and Energy Balance sheets
3. Equipment list with specifications (data sheets)
4. Preliminary cost estimate and economic feasibility report

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Explain the four-stage scale-up ladder of process development with one sentence per stage.**

**Model Answer (1 mark per stage, 3 marks for any 3, full 3 marks for all 4):**
- **Lab Scale:** Prove chemistry; obtain kinetic and thermodynamic data at the bench (g to kg level).
- **Pilot Plant:** Verify continuous operation; measure heat/mass transfer coefficients and residence times (kg to tonnes/day).
- **Semi-Commercial Plant:** De-risk scale-up; train operators; produce market-development samples (tonnes to hundreds of tonnes/day).
- **Commercial Plant:** Full-scale production with optimised cost and operating conditions (thousands to millions of tonnes/year).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has internal choice (either OR). Each choice carries **14 marks**, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

### 5.2.1 Question B (Module 1 Choice A)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **hierarchy of process flow diagrams** (BFD → PFD → P&ID) used in chemical plant design. List the data/information contained in each level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A feed stream containing **80 mol% A** and **20 mol% inert I** enters a reactor at a total flow rate of **100 kmol/h**. The single-pass conversion of A is **60%**. The reactor effluent passes through a separator that removes unreacted A and inerts completely, sending only pure product B forward. **A portion of the unreacted stream is recycled** back to mix with the fresh feed at a recycle ratio (R/F) of **0.5**. A purge stream removes 10% of the recycle. Calculate:
> 1. The flow rate of A in the fresh feed.
> 2. The overall conversion of A (with recycle).
> 3. The flow rate of the purge stream. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B-A(a) — Hierarchy of Process Flow Diagrams (7 marks)**

The hierarchy of engineering flow diagrams represents increasing levels of detail as the project progresses from conceptual to detailed design.

| Level | Diagram | Information Contained | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** | Major process blocks (e.g., "Reactor", "Separator"); single line per stream; no stream data; overall material balance only. | 2 |
| **2** | **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** | All major equipment (vessels, columns, exchangers, pumps, compressors); all process streams with flow rate, composition, T, P; major control loops; utility streams; stream tables. | 2 |
| **3** | **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** | All piping (sizes, materials, schedules); all valves; every instrument and control loop; safety devices (PSV, RSD); equipment internals; insulation specs. | 2 |
| **4** | **Plot Plan / General Arrangement (GA)** | Physical 3D placement of equipment, buildings, pipe racks, roads, drainage. | 1 |

*(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for the visual hierarchy.)*

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The progression BFD → PFD → P&ID → Layout represents the engineering lifecycle from concept to construction, with each level adding detail for procurement, fabrication, and construction.

---

**Model Solution to B-A(b) — Recycle-Loop Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Fresh feed: $F = 100$ kmol/h, $z_A = 0.80$, $z_I = 0.20$
- Single-pass conversion in reactor: $X_{sp} = 0.60$
- Separator: unreacted A and inerts recycled, pure B leaves
- Recycle ratio: $R/F = 0.5 \Rightarrow R = 0.5 \times 100 = 50$ kmol/h
- Purge = 10% of recycle stream

**Step 1 — Fresh feed flow of A and I (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{n}_{A,F} = F \times z_A = 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{I,F} = F \times z_I = 100 \times 0.20 = 20 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Recycle composition analysis (1 mark):**
Since the separator removes ALL unreacted A and inerts into the recycle stream, the recycle composition is identical to the *combined feed entering the reactor*. For now, we treat the recycle as containing unreacted A and the inerts.

**Step 3 — Overall conversion with recycle (3 marks):**

Overall conversion is the **fraction of fresh A actually converted to product B** (not just consumed in a single pass).

Total A fed to reactor = Fresh A + Recycled A = $\dot{n}_{A,F} + \dot{n}_{A,R}$

Let $\dot{n}_{A,R}$ be the recycle flow of A. From the single-pass balance:
$$
\dot{n}_{A,R} = \dot{n}_{A,F} \times (1 - X_{sp}) = 80 \times 0.40 = 32 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

(assuming first-pass recycle estimate, then iterating for convergence).

Total A entering reactor = $80 + 32 = 112$ kmol/h.
A converted to B per pass = $112 \times 0.60 = 67.2$ kmol/h.
Since all converted A → B, and B is removed, the net conversion of *fresh* A is 100% (no A leaves in the product stream).

**Final answer (1 mark):** **Overall conversion of fresh A = 100%** (with sufficient recycle).

**Step 4 — Purge stream flow rate (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Purge} = 0.10 \times R = 0.10 \times 50 = 5 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

The purge carries 5 kmol/h of unreacted A + inerts, which prevents inert buildup in the loop.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (Module 1 Choice B — Alternative)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Define **Process** in the context of chemical plant design. Explain the **six steps of process design** with a neat labelled flowchart. Discuss briefly the importance of material and energy balance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR) is fed with **200 kmol/h of pure reactant A**. The conversion per pass is **70%**. The reactor volume is **2 m³**.
> 1. Calculate the **space time** and **space velocity**.
> 2. If the same reactor is now operated in **series** with an identical second CSTR (so the effluent of the first is the feed to the second), what is the **overall conversion** for the two-CSTR-in-series arrangement? Assume constant density.
> **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B-B(a) — Definition + Six Steps + M&EB Importance (7 marks)**

**Definition of Process (1 mark):**
A process is the engineered sequence of chemical, physical, and biological unit operations that transforms feedstocks into desired products, including the selection of process route, equipment, and operating conditions.

**Six Steps of Process Design (4 marks — 1 mark each for naming + brief description; refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):**

1. Problem definition and design basis.
2. Literature survey and thermodynamic analysis.
3. Process development (lab → pilot → commercial).
4. Material and energy balance.
5. Equipment selection and sizing.
6. Economic, safety, environmental evaluation and PFD finalisation.

**Importance of Material and Energy Balance (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner-Expected Bullet Points (write at least 4 for full marks):**
> - Quantifies all inlet and outlet streams.
> - Forms the basis for the **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**.
> - Provides input data for **equipment sizing** (column diameter, pump head, heat-exchanger area).
> - Establishes the **recycle, purge, and bypass** flow requirements.
> - Identifies the **bottleneck streams** for capacity expansion.
> - Required for **energy integration and utility sizing**.

---

**Model Solution to B-B(b) — Space Time & Conversion in CSTR Series (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Feed: $F = \dot{V} = 200$ kmol/h (pure A, constant density → volumetric flow = 200 m³/h? No, *assume given units; treat 200 as volumetric* for space-time)
- Single-pass conversion: $X_1 = 0.70$
- Reactor volume: $V = 2$ m³

**Step 1 — Space Time (2 marks):**

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{\dot{V}} = \frac{2 \text{ m}^3}{200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}} = 0.01 \text{ h} = 36 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Space Velocity (1 mark):**

$$
SV = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{0.01 \text{ h}} = 100 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion in CSTR Series (3 marks):**

For **two identical CSTRs in series**, the overall conversion $X_{overall}$ is found by applying the single-CSTR conversion formula twice.

Let $C_{A0}$ = inlet concentration. After the first CSTR:

$$
C_{A1} = C_{A0} (1 - X_1) = C_{A0} (1 - 0.70) = 0.30 \, C_{A0}
$$

After the second CSTR, with the same $X_1$ based on its own feed:

$$
C_{A2} = C_{A1} (1 - X_1) = 0.30 \, C_{A0} \times 0.30 = 0.09 \, C_{A0}
$$

Therefore:

$$
X_{overall} = 1 - \frac{C_{A2}}{C_{A0}} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91 = 91\%
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison (1 mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A single CSTR achieves 70% conversion, but two CSTRs in series achieve 91% conversion. This demonstrates that *reactor staging* significantly improves overall conversion without changing individual reactor size or operating conditions — a key design strategy in process engineering.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Lose Marks on "Process" Questions (Read Carefully)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Process Design" with "Plant Design"** — Examiners specifically test this distinction. If the question says "process," answer in terms of *PFD, M&EB, equipment list, P&ID is NOT part of process design*. P&ID is plant design.
>
> 2. **Skipping the "Design Basis"** — Before any calculation, students jump straight to formulas. The examiner awards **1–2 marks** for *clearly stating the given data and assumptions* (steady state, constant density, isothermal, etc.). **Always write a "Given & Assumption" box** at the start of your numerical answer.
>
> 3. **Mermaid / Diagram Without Labels** — A PFD with unlabelled streams (no flow rates, no compositions, no T & P) gets **zero marks** for the diagram component. KTU examiners check for **stream numbers, names, and key data points**.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing kmol/h with m³/s without conversion. **Standardise units at the start** of every numerical.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Purge Justification** — In recycle problems, many students compute the purge flow but never explain *why* it is necessary. **Always add a one-line justification** (e.g., "Purge prevents inert accumulation in the recycle loop").
>
> 6. **Sign Convention in Material Balance** — Generation is positive, consumption is negative. A wrong sign in $\dot{r}_{gen}$ is a 1-mark deduction, often unnoticed by students.
>
> 7. **Bloated "General" Definitions** — Don't write paragraphs. Use **bulleted structure**. The examiner's key is point-wise, not essay-style.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU Module 1 — "Process" : High-Density Revision Card**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Process = engineered sequence of operations + selection of route, equipment, and operating conditions that transform feedstocks into products.
>
> ✅ **Process Design vs. Plant Design** — Conceptual (PFD, M&EB) vs. Detailed (P&ID, GA, isometrics).
>
> ✅ **Six Steps of Process Design:** Problem Definition → Literature → Development → M&EB → Equipment → Economics + PFD.
>
> ✅ **Scale-Up Ladder:** Lab → Bench → Pilot → Semi-Commercial → Commercial.
>
> ✅ **Diagram Hierarchy:** BFD → PFD → P&ID → Plot Plan / GA.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas (memorise verbatim):**
> - $X = \dfrac{n_{A,in} - n_{A,out}}{n_{A,in}}$
> - $Y = S \times X$
> - $\tau = V / \dot{V}$
> - $SV = 1/\tau$
> - $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{consumed}$ (steady state)
> - $DoF = \text{Unknowns} - \text{Equations}$
>
> ✅ **Recycle Loop Components:** Fresh Feed, Mixing Point, Reactor, Separator, Product Outlet, Recycle Stream, Purge Stream.
>
> ✅ **Purge is Mandatory** to remove inerts / by-products that would otherwise accumulate.
>
> ✅ **CSTR Series Conversion:** $X_{overall} = 1 - (1 - X_1)^N$ for $N$ identical CSTRs.
>
> ✅ **Standard Plant Capacity Units:** tpa (tonnes per annum) for commercial scale; kg/day for pilot.
>
> ✅ **On-stream Factor (Plant Availability):** 90–95% for continuous plants; lower for batch.
>
> ✅ **Examiner's Pet Topic:** "Compare and contrast two process routes" — always structure as: criterion → compare → decide → justify with one number.
>
> ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Saying P&ID is part of process design. **P&ID is plant design.**
>
> ✅ **Key Kerala Industrial Examples:** BPCL Kochi Refinery, FACT Cochin, HOCL, KRL — all follow the Lab → Pilot → Commercial scale-up path.
>
> ✅ **Green-Process Considerations** (NEP 2020 alignment): Atom Economy, E-Factor, Carbon Footprint must appear in modern KTU answers for full marks on Section (a) "process selection" questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Position
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fixed Position

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fixed Position Layout** is a type of plant/facility layout in which the **product being produced is too large, too heavy, or too fragile to be moved** from its place of manufacture. Consequently, the product remains stationary at a single geographic location (the *fixed site*), and all required **men, machines, materials, tools, sub-assemblies, and supporting services are brought to that location** in a coordinated sequence to perform the necessary operations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is also referred to as the **"Site Layout"** or **"Project Layout."** It is one of the **four classical types of plant layout** recognized in Operations Management and Industrial Engineering, the others being *Process (Functional) Layout*, *Product (Line) Layout*, and *Combined (Hybrid) Layout*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPET634, Module 1):** The KTU 2024 Scheme places Fixed Position Layout as the introductory layout type, since it conceptually anchors the entire layout taxonomy — the *product* itself defines the static reference frame around which all flow planning is executed.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Under Construction" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (House-Building):** Imagine you are building a house. The house does not travel to the brick kiln, the cement factory, or the carpenter's workshop. Instead, every brick batch, every cement bag, every window frame, every electrician, plumber, and painter *travels to the construction site*. The product (the house) stays put; everything else comes to it. That is Fixed Position Layout.

The same logic applies to:
- **A ship in a shipyard** — the hull stays in the dry dock, welders, engines, cranes, and steel plates are brought to it.
- **An aircraft on an assembly jig** — the airframe is anchored; engines, avionics, fuselage panels, and technicians converge on it.
- **A locomotive being overhauled** — the locomotive is parked in the shed; parts and engineers arrive.

The **central reference coordinate** is the product itself — analogous to a stationary Sun around which all "planets" (resources) orbit in a structured manner.

---

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following quantitative descriptors are commonly associated with Fixed Position Layout design in KTU 2024 board examinations:

| Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Product-to-equipment mobility ratio** | $\mathbf{0}$ (product immobile) | Defines the layout as Fixed Position |
| **Worker-to-site travel ratio** | $\mathbf{High}$ | Workers move around the stationary product |
| **On-site material storage intensity** | $\mathbf{60\%-80\%}$ of project cost tied to materials on-site | High working capital lock-up |
| **Space utilization at fixed site** | $\mathbf{30\%-50\%}$ | Most space is circulation/buffer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** In Fixed Position Layout, **flow is reversed** — instead of material flowing to stationary machines (as in Product Layout), here *machines and labor flow to the stationary material*.

---

### 1.4 Scope and Boundary Conditions

A Fixed Position Layout is invoked under **all** of the following boundary conditions:

1. The finished product is **physically immobile** (e.g., a building foundation, a dam).
2. The product is **too large** to fit through shop doors or onto transport vehicles (e.g., aircraft carriers, rockets).
3. The product is **too heavy** for available in-plant handling equipment (e.g., power plant boilers, large turbines).
4. The product requires **on-site assembly, testing, and commissioning** (e.g., refinery columns, satellite assembly).
5. **Moving the partially completed product would cause unacceptable damage** (e.g., delicate scientific instruments, ship hulls).

If **none** of these conditions are met, a Process or Product Layout is preferred over Fixed Position.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Fixed Position Layout

The layout decomposes into **five concentric functional zones** around the stationary product. Each zone has a distinct logistical role.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concentric Zone Model (KTU Board Exam Favourite):**
> - **Zone 0 — Product Core:** The immobile product itself (e.g., ship hull, building frame).
> - **Zone 1 — Immediate Work Envelope:** Direct workforce + tools currently active on the product.
> - **Zone 2 — Sub-Assembly & Buffer Zone:** Sub-assemblies staged near the product for imminent installation.
> - **Zone 3 — Material Storage & Warehousing:** Bulk raw materials, components, consumables.
> - **Zone 4 — Service & Support Zone:** Cranes, mobile equipment, supervisory offices, first-aid, utilities (power, water, compressed air lines).

The **further** a zone is from Zone 0, the **lower** its activity intensity but the **larger** its spatial footprint.

---

### 2.2 Why and How — Operational Logic Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Identify the Anchor (Zone 0).**
Locate the product physically. This becomes the **fixed Cartesian origin** $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ of the entire layout. All downstream planning is referenced to this origin.

**Step 2: Schedule Operations in the Critical Path.**
Since the product cannot move, **all operations must be sequenced temporally** (one after another on the same site) rather than spatially (parallel machines). This makes the **Critical Path Method (CPM)** and **Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)** the natural planning tools.

**Step 3: Stage Resources in Concentric Readiness.**
Materials, tools, and labor are pre-positioned in Zones 3 → 2 → 1 → 0 in a **just-in-time cascading manner** to minimize double-handling.

**Step 4: Coordinate Multi-Trade Workflows.**
Because multiple trades (welding, electrical, plumbing, painting) converge on the same fixed product, **trade-scheduling matrices** are essential to prevent spatial collisions.

**Step 5: Monitor with S-Curves and Milestones.**
Project progress is charted using **S-curves** (cumulative progress vs. time) because the *product itself* is the only visible deliverable accumulating at the site.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Fixed Position Layout Quantitative Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam relevant.** Practice numeric substitutions on each.

$$
\text{Handling Cost (HC)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \cdot C_i \cdot W_i
$$

Where:
- $D_i$ = distance travelled by resource $i$ (metres)
- $C_i$ = cost per unit distance per unit weight for resource $i$ (₹/m·kg)
- $W_i$ = weight of resource $i$ (kg)
- $n$ = total number of resource movements

$$
\text{Space Utilization Efficiency (SUE)} = \frac{A_{\text{product}} + A_{\text{active work}}}{A_{\text{total site}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Worker Density} = \frac{N_{\text{workers on-site}}}{A_{\text{active zone}}}
\quad \left[\text{workers per m}^2\right]
$$

$$
\text{Project Compression Ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{crash}}}{T_{\text{normal}}} \quad \text{(for CPM analysis)}
$$

$$
\text{Buffer Stock Level} = \text{Lead Time Demand} + \text{Safety Stock}
$$

| Quantity | Formula / Notation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Handling Cost** | $\sum D_i C_i W_i$ | Minimize by sequencing trades logically |
| **Space Utilization** | $SUE = \frac{A_{\text{used}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ \times $100\%$ | KPI for site congestion |
| **Critical Path Duration** | $T_{\text{CP}} = \max \sum t_{\text{path}}$ | Project completion estimator |
| **Worker Congestion Index** | $WCI = \frac{N_{\text{trades active simultaneously}}}{A_{\text{zone 1}}}$ | Trade-scheduling optimizer |
| **Material Storage Ratio** | $MSR = \frac{A_{\text{storage}}}{A_{\text{total site}}}$ | Inventory-to-space efficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (never the literal ` \vert ` character) inside markdown table cells to avoid breaking table parsing.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fixed Position Layout is the **dominant layout type** in:
- **Heavy Engineering:** Shipyards, locomotive workshops, aircraft hangars.
- **Construction:** Skyscrapers, bridges, dams, metro tunnels.
- **Power & Energy:** Thermal power plant boiler erection, wind turbine installation, refinery column fabrication.
- **Defence & Aerospace:** Rocket assembly buildings (VAB), submarine construction halls, satellite integration facilities.
- **On-site Maintenance:** Large turbine overhauls, ship dry-docking, transformer repairs at substations.

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests students on whether they can **identify** the correct layout for a given industry scenario — Fixed Position is the answer when the product is immobile.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Logic

### 3.1 Comparative Layout Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The following 10-criterion analytical matrix is a high-yield KTU 2024 exam artifact.** Memorize the contrasting axes — they appear frequently in Part B questions.

$$
M_{\text{layout}} = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{Criterion} & \text{Fixed Position} & \text{Process} & \text{Product} \\
\hline
\text{Product mobility} & \text{None} & \text{Moves between centers} & \text{Flows on line} \\
\text{Worker mobility} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{Low} \\
\text{Investment in machines} & \text{Low} & \text{High} & \text{Very High} \\
\text{Flexibility of product mix} & \text{Very High} & \text{High} & \text{Low} \\
\text{Production volume} & \text{One-of-a-kind / Low} & \text{Medium} & \text{High} \\
\text{Throughput time} & \text{Very High} & \text{High} & \text{Low} \\
\text{Inventory in WIP} & \text{On-site staging} & \text{Inter-departmental} & \text{Minimal} \\
\text{Supervision} & \text{Project-based} & \text{Functional} & \text{Line} \\
\text{Planning tool} & \text{CPM / PERT} & \text{Load charts} & \text{Line balancing} \\
\text{Example} & \text{Ship, building} & \text{Hospital, job shop} & \text{Car assembly} \\
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Total On-Site Material Handling Cost

This is the **single most important quantitative derivation** in the Fixed Position unit. It is a routine KTU 14-mark question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Derivation of Total Handling Cost $\Sigma HC$:**

**Statement:** In a fixed-position project, $n$ distinct resources (sub-assemblies, tools, consumables) are transported from their respective staging locations to the product site. Compute the total handling cost.

**Step 1 — Identify the Cartesian distance of each resource.**
For each resource $i$, the straight-line on-site transport distance from its staging zone to the active work zone is:
$$
D_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_0)^2 + (y_i - y_0)^2}
$$
where $(x_0, y_0)$ is the location of the fixed product (Zone 0 origin).

**Step 2 — Express the unit cost of movement.**
The cost of moving one kilogram of resource $i$ over one metre of site terrain is given by the equipment-specific rate:
$$
C_i = k_i \cdot \eta_i^{-1}
$$
where $k_i$ is the equipment rental cost (₹/hour) and $\eta_i$ is the equipment throughput (kg·m/hour).

**Step 3 — Weight of resource moved.**
Each resource has a total mass $W_i$ (kg) to be delivered to the site.

**Step 4 — Form the per-resource handling cost.**
The handling cost attributable to resource $i$ alone is the product of distance, weight, and unit cost:
$$
HC_i = D_i \cdot W_i \cdot C_i
$$

**Step 5 — Sum across all resources.**
$$
HC_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} HC_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \cdot W_i \cdot C_i
$$

**Step 6 — Introduce location-aware optimization.**
To minimize $HC_{\text{total}}$, the gradient with respect to staging position $x_i$ must vanish:
$$
\frac{\partial HC_{\text{total}}}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x_i - x_0}{D_i} \cdot W_i \cdot C_i = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for optimum staging location.**
$$
x_i^{\text{opt}} = x_0 \quad (\text{collapse staging into Zone 0})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The calculus result implies that *in the ideal limit*, resources should be staged directly adjacent to the product. In practice, **Zone 0 space is finite**, so we instead minimize $HC_{\text{total}}$ subject to the constraint $A_{\text{Zone 0}} \geq \sum A_i^{\text{resource}}$, leading to a *gravity-model* placement of staging zones — heavier and more frequently used resources placed closer to Zone 0.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical:** A shipyard is assembling a hull. Four sub-assemblies must be moved from their staging zones to the hull:
> - Sub-assembly A: $D_A = 50$ m, $W_A = 2000$ kg, $C_A = 0.05$ ₹/m·kg
> - Sub-assembly B: $D_B = 30$ m, $W_B = 1500$ kg, $C_B = 0.04$ ₹/m·kg
> - Sub-assembly C: $D_C = 80$ m, $W_C = 1000$ kg, $C_C = 0.06$ ₹/m·kg
> - Sub-assembly D: $D_D = 20$ m, $W_D = 2500$ kg, $C_D = 0.03$ ₹/m·kg
>
> **Compute the total handling cost.**

**Step 1:** Compute $HC_i = D_i \cdot W_i \cdot C_i$ for each resource:
$$
HC_A = 50 \cdot 2000 \cdot 0.05 = 5000 \text{ ₹}
$$
$$
HC_B = 30 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.04 = 1800 \text{ ₹}
$$
$$
HC_C = 80 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.06 = 4800 \text{ ₹}
$$
$$
HC_D = 20 \cdot 2500 \cdot 0.03 = 1500 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum:
$$
HC_{\text{total}} = 5000 + 1800 + 4800 + 1500 = 13{,}100 \text{ ₹}
$$
