- Mark **buildings**, **roads**, **railway sidings**, **drainage**, **fire-water network**.

### Phase 4 — Equipment Location and Spacing

**Purpose:** Optimize the physical placement of each unit.

**Activities:**
- Position **heavy/vibrating equipment** on hard soil strata (e.g., compressors on pile foundations).
- Place **tall columns** away from buildings to avoid shadowing and allow overhead crane access.
- Group **heating/cooling utilities** near their consumers to minimize pipe runs.
- Maintain **safety distances** as per Table 2.2.
- Allow **maintenance access**: minimum **1.0 m** clearance on each side of any heat exchanger, **2.0 m** above for tube bundle withdrawal.

### Phase 5 — Utility and Service Integration

**Purpose:** Design the lifeline systems.

**Activities:**
- Plan **steam and condensate headers** (typically along a service corridor / pipe rack).
- Plan **cooling water** intake and return lines.
- Plan **electrical substation** location — preferably on the upwind side, away from fire-prone areas.
- Plan **instrument air** distribution.
- Plan **flare stack** location — remote, upwind, with **radius of 100–150 m** from operating units.
- Plan **drainage and effluent treatment plant (ETP)** at the lowest contour point.

### Phase 6 — Safety, Environmental, and Economic Review

**Purpose:** Validate the design.

**Activities:**
- Conduct **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** and **HIRA (Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment)**.
- Verify **emergency assembly points**, **fire escape routes**, **fire-fighting equipment placement**.
- Compute **payback period** and **Return on Investment (ROI)**.
- Check **Plot Ratio**, **FAR (Floor Area Ratio)**, and **Green Belt** compliance.
- Prepare **3D model** (in modern practice) to check for interferences.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **definitive reference** for all quantitative aspects of the Plant Layout Procedure. KTU examiners routinely test these formulas.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plot Ratio (Building Coverage) | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{A_{\text{building}}}{A_{\text{plot}}}$ | 0.30 – 0.50 (dimensionless) | Land utilization check |
| 2 | Circulation Factor | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{A_{\text{roads}} + A_{\text{open}}}{A_{\text{plot}}}$ | 0.15 – 0.25 | Movement area check |
| 3 | Green Belt Fraction | $\text{GBF} = \dfrac{A_{\text{green}}}{A_{\text{plot}}}$ | $\geq 0.20$ | Environmental compliance |
| 4 | Floor Area Ratio (FAR) | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\sum A_{\text{floor}}}{A_{\text{plot}}}$ | 0.8 – 1.5 for industrial | Building bulk check |
| 5 | Construction Cost Index (CCI) | $\text{CCI}_{n} = \text{CCI}_{0} \cdot \left(1 + r\right)^{n}$ | $r$ = inflation rate | Time-value of cost |
| 6 | Optimum Equipment Spacing | $S = f \cdot \sqrt[3]{V}$ | $f$ = 1 to 2, $V$ = equipment volume (m³) | 3D spatial rule |
| 7 | Pipe Rack Load | $W_{\text{rack}} = W_{\text{pipe}} + W_{\text{fluid}} + W_{\text{insul}} + W_{\text{snow}}$ | kN/m | Pipe rack design |
| 8 | Fire Water Demand | $Q_{\text{fire}} = K \cdot \sqrt{P}$ | $K$ = 5.4, $P$ = population in thousands | Fire network sizing |
| 9 | Travel Distance Index (TDI) | $\text{TDI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i} \cdot f_{i}$ | $d_i$ = distance, $f_i$ = frequency | Material flow cost |
| 10 | Material Handling Cost | $C_{\text{MH}} = C_{0} \cdot L \cdot W \cdot T$ | ₹/year | Operating cost estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** Throughout KTU 2024 Scheme answer scripts, use the term **"Plot Ratio"** (not "Building Coverage Ratio") for marking compliance with the local building bylaws in Kerala.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Plant Layout Procedure

| Industry | Application of Procedure | Specific Output |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum Refinery | Layout of atmospheric/vacuum distillation units | Safety distance from township |
| Pharmaceutical Plant | Clean room zoning (Grade A/B/C/D) | AHU placement, return air path |
| Cement Plant | Stockpile, crusher, kiln, cooler, packer arrangement | Material gravity flow |
| Food Processing | Hygiene zoning (high-risk / low-risk) | CIP system routing |
| Petrochemical Complex | Plot plan for ethylene cracker | Flare stack, ETP, tank farm |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Kerala's industrial geography**, the procedure is heavily influenced by the **monsoon climate** and **high water table**. Open-type layouts are common; building elevations are raised by **0.45 m to 0.6 m** above the highest flood level (CWC data).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Optimum Plot Ratio for Minimum Total Cost

The Plot Ratio is **not chosen arbitrarily**. It emerges from an economic optimization. This is a high-yield KTU analytical question. We derive the optimum below.

**Step 1: Define the cost components.**

Let:
- $A$ = total plot area (m²)
- $\text{PR}$ = Plot Ratio (dimensionless, $0 < \text{PR} < 1$)
- Then building footprint area $A_{b} = \text{PR} \cdot A$
- Open area $A_{o} = (1 - \text{PR}) \cdot A$

**Step 2: Express the cost of land and infrastructure.**

$$
C_{\text{land}} = p_{l} \cdot A
$$

where $p_{l}$ = unit land cost (₹/m²). Land cost grows linearly with total plot area.

$$
C_{\text{infra}} = p_{i} \cdot A
$$

where $p_{i}$ = unit infrastructure cost (roads, drainage, fencing, lighting) (₹/m²).

**Step 3: Express the cost of vertical construction.**

If the building has $n$ floors, then the floor area available is:

$$
A_{\text{floor}} = n \cdot A_{b} = n \cdot \text{PR} \cdot A
$$

If a fixed total floor area $A_{\text{required}}$ is needed to house the plant (process + admin + warehouse), then:

$$
n \cdot \text{PR} \cdot A = A_{\text{required}}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow A = \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}}
$$

**Step 4: Express the cost of vertical (multi-storey) construction.**

Let $c_{v}$ = incremental cost per m² of going vertical (extra structural, lifts, fire stairs, etc.) above the ground floor.

$$
C_{\text{vertical}} = c_{v} \cdot A_{\text{required}} \cdot (n - 1)
$$

**Step 5: Total cost function.**

$$
C_{\text{total}}(\text{PR}, n) = (p_{l} + p_{i}) \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}} + c_{v} \cdot A_{\text{required}} \cdot (n - 1)
$$

**Step 6: Minimize with respect to PR and n.**

For a fixed number of floors $n$, minimize with respect to PR:

$$
\dfrac{\partial C_{\text{total}}}{\partial \text{PR}} = -(p_{l} + p_{i}) \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}^{2}} = 0
$$

This implies $\text{PR} \to 1$, meaning in theory, **occupy the entire plot**. But this is constrained by safety, fire access, and environmental bylaws, which give the practical upper bound of **PR ≤ 0.50**.

The realistic optimization is constrained:

$$
\text{PR}_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.40 \pm 0.05
$$

This is why the **standard benchmark of 0.30 – 0.50** emerges in plant engineering practice.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem (KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style):**
> A chemical plant requires a total floor area of **12,000 m²** for process, utility, and administrative buildings. The land cost is **₹4,500/m²** and infrastructure cost is **₹1,200/m²**. The incremental cost of going vertical is **₹1,800/m²** per additional floor. Determine the optimum plot ratio for a **2-floor** building scheme, and the corresponding land area required. State the practical constraint.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.

$$
C_{\text{total}} = (p_{l} + p_{i}) \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}} + c_{v} \cdot A_{\text{required}} \cdot (n - 1)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
C_{\text{total}} = (4500 + 1200) \cdot \dfrac{12000}{2 \cdot \text{PR}} + 1800 \cdot 12000 \cdot (2 - 1)
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 5700 \cdot \dfrac{12000}{2 \cdot \text{PR}} + 1800 \cdot 12000
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{34{,}200{,}000}{\text{PR}} + 21{,}600{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the practical upper bound (from earlier derivation, $\text{PR}_{\text{max}} = 0.50$).

We compare $\text{PR} = 0.40$ vs $\text{PR} = 0.50$:

For $\text{PR} = 0.40$:

$$
A = \dfrac{12000}{2 \cdot 0.40} = 15{,}000 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{34{,}200{,}000}{0.40} + 21{,}600{,}000 = 85{,}500{,}000 + 21{,}600{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10.71 \text{ Crore}
$$

For $\text{PR} = 0.50$:

$$
A = \dfrac{12000}{2 \cdot 0.50} = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{34{,}200{,}000}{0.50} + 21{,}600{,}000 = 68{,}400{,}000 + 21{,}600{,}000 = \text{₹ } 9.00 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 4:** Decision.

$\text{PR} = 0.50$ is economically optimal under unconstrained conditions. However, applying the **safety and green belt constraint** of $\text{GBF} \geq 0.20$ and $\text{CF} \geq 0.15$, the **practical optimum is $\text{PR} = 0.40$**, giving a plot area of **15,000 m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the kind of "show full working" problem KTU examiners love.** Marks are awarded for: identifying the formula (2 marks), correct substitution (3 marks), tabulated comparison (3 marks), final justified answer with constraint (2 marks). Total 10 marks for the analytical part.

## 3.3 Equipment Spacing Derivation — The "Cube-Root Rule"

For two cylindrical vessels of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$, the minimum center-to-center spacing $S$ is empirically:

$$
S = 0.5(D_1 + D_2) + K_{\text{service}}
$$

where $K_{\text{service}}$ is the maintenance clearance:

- For pumps: $K_{\text{service}} = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- For heat exchangers: $K_{\text{service}} = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- For columns: $K_{\text{service}} = 2.0 \text{ m}$ (for davit access)

In 3D, the clearance between two adjacent vessels of heights $H_1$ and $H_2$ must allow safe operation. A volume-based rule (popular in European practice) is:

$$
V_{\text{clearance}} = f^{3} \cdot \sqrt[3]{V_{1} \cdot V_{2}}
$$

where $f$ is a factor (1.0 for non-hazardous, 1.5 for flammable, 2.0 for highly toxic), and $V_1, V_2$ are equipment volumes. This is the basis of modern **3D plant design software** (AVEVA E3D, Intergraph SmartPlant).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow Diagram — The Six-Phase Plant Layout Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Project Sanction]) --> phase1
    phase1["Phase 1: Data Collection and Analysis"]:::phase
    phase2["Phase 2: Process Flow Development BFD to PFD to P and ID"]:::phase
    phase3["Phase 3: Plot Plan Generation"]:::phase
    phase4["Phase 4: Equipment Location and Spacing"]:::phase
    phase5["Phase 5: Utility and Service Integration"]:::phase
    phase6["Phase 6: Safety, Environment and Economic Review"]:::phase
    dec1{Approved by Client?}:::decision
    dec2{Meets Safety Norms?}:::decision
    dec3{Economically Viable?}:::decision
    endA([Final Detailed Engineering Layout]):::final
    rejA([Revise and Reiterate]):::rework

    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> dec1
    dec1 -- No --> rejA
    rejA --> phase2
    dec1 -- Yes --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 --> dec2
    dec2 -- No --> rejA
    rejA --> phase4
    dec2 -- Yes --> phase6
    phase6 --> dec3
    dec3 -- No --> rejA
    rejA --> phase5
    dec3 -- Yes --> endA

    classDef phase fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#f4a261,stroke:#e76f51,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rework fill:#e63946,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each blue rectangle is a phase. The orange diamonds are review gates where the design is checked. The red "Revise and Reiterate" path reflects that plant layout is **iterative**, not linear. The green rounded box is the final deliverable.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Flow in the Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[Market Demand]:::input --> dataHub
    inp2[Process Design Data]:::input --> dataHub
    inp3[Site Topography]:::input --> dataHub
    inp4[Regulatory Norms]:::input --> dataHub
    dataHub[(Central Design Basis Document)]:::storage --> pfd[PFD Generation]:::process
    pfd --> spacing[Equipment Sizing and Spacing]:::process
    spacing --> plot[Plot Plan Draft]:::process
    plot --> utility[Utility Header Design]:::process
    utility --> safety[Safety and HAZOP Review]:::process
    safety --> econ[Economic Analysis]:::process
    econ --> out1[Approved Plot Plan]:::output
    econ --> out2[Cost Estimate]:::output
    econ --> out3[Construction Sequence]:::output

    classDef input fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef storage fill:#f1faee,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef process fill:#457b9d,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Layout Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Number of Products?}:::decision
    q2{Volume of Each Product?}:::decision
    q3{Equipment Size Relative to Plant?}:::decision
    l1[Product Line Layout]:::layout
    l2[Process Functional Layout]:::layout
    l3[Fixed Position Layout]:::layout
    l4[Combined Hybrid Layout]:::layout

    q1 -- Single Product --> l1
    q1 -- Many Products --> q2
    q2 -- High Volume Each --> l1
    q2 -- Low Volume Many --> l2
    q3 -- Very Large Items --> l3
    q3 -- Standard Sizes --> l4

    classDef decision fill:#ffb703,stroke:#fb8500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef layout fill:#8ecae6,stroke:#023047,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Safety Zoning Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    z1[Zone 1: High Hazard Area Reactors, Distillation Columns, Furnaces]:::zone
    z2[Zone 2: Medium Hazard Area Pumps, Compressors, Heat Exchangers]:::zone
    z3[Zone 3: Storage Area Tanks, Drums, Warehouses]:::zone
    z4[Zone 4: Service Area Boiler, Cooling Tower, Substation, Control Room]:::zone
    z5[Zone 5: Administrative Area Office, Canteen, Parking]:::zone
    buf1[(Min 30 m Buffer with Fire Wall)]:::buffer
    buf2[(Min 15 m Green Belt)]:::buffer
    buf3[(Min 20 m Open Space)]:::buffer
    buf4[(Min 50 m from Zone 1)]:::buffer

    z1 --- buf1 --- z2
    z2 --- buf2 --- z3
    z3 --- buf3 --- z4
    z4 --- buf4 --- z5

    classDef zone fill:#e63946,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef buffer fill:#f4a261,stroke:#e76f51,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> **Reading this architecture:** This is a **block-level zoning map** showing how a chemical plant is divided into five concentric risk zones. Zone 1 (highest hazard) is at the center, with progressively safer zones radiating outward. The buffer widths (15 m, 20 m, 30 m, 50 m) are the *minimum* safety distances as per OISD-118 and Kerala Factory Rules.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q1.** Define the term *Plant Layout Procedure*. List any **four** items of input data required for designing a plant layout.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Plant Layout Procedure is the systematic sequence of engineering activities—starting from data collection, through process flow development, plot planning, equipment placement, utility integration, and ending with safety and economic review—used to physically arrange all process and non-process units of an industrial plant for optimum operation.

**Four items of input data:**
1. **Process flow diagram (PFD)** with material and energy balance.
2. **Site topography and soil data** (bearing capacity, flood level, contour map).
3. **Product specifications** (purity, quantity in TPA, storage class).
4. **Regulatory constraints** (KSPCB norms, Factory Act, OISD, MSIHC Rules).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the list. Examiners award 0.5 per correct item.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **Q2.** What is the significance of the **Plot Ratio** in plant layout? State its recommended range for a chemical plant.

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:**
Plot Ratio (PR) = (Area occupied by buildings) / (Total plot area). It indicates how efficiently the land is utilized for built structures. A higher PR means denser construction but leaves less open space for safety, drainage, and green belt. A lower PR wastes land. Therefore, an optimum PR balances land cost, safety, and operability.

**Recommended range:** **0.30 to 0.50** for chemical process plants.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for the numerical range.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the **complete procedure of plant layout** with the help of a neat flowchart. Highlight the role of input data and process flow development. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** A chemical plant requires a **total floor area of 8,000 m²**. Land cost is ₹3,000/m² and infrastructure cost is ₹900/m². The incremental cost of vertical construction is ₹1,500/m² per additional floor. Determine the **optimum plot area and total cost** for a 2-floor scheme, assuming a practical plot ratio of **0.40**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The plant layout procedure is a **six-phase** activity:

1. **Data Collection** — Market study, process design data, site survey, statutory norms.
2. **Process Flow Development** — Block Flow Diagram → PFD → P&ID. Establishes the *sequence* of operations.
3. **Plot Plan Generation** — Decides overall arrangement (product/process/fixed/combined layout), orientation, zoning.
4. **Equipment Location and Spacing** — Apply spacing rules, group utilities, allow maintenance access.
5. **Utility and Service Integration** — Pipe racks, drainage, electrical, instrument air, flare.
6. **Safety, Environment, and Economic Review** — HAZOP, green belt check, ROI computation, 3D model review.

A neat flowchart (as drawn in **Section 4.1** of these notes) should accompany the answer, with the six phases as boxes and three decision gates (Client Approval, Safety Check, Economic Viability) as diamonds.

**Role of input data:** It forms the *design basis* — every layout decision is traceable to an input. Without proper data, the layout will be revisited repeatedly, causing cost and schedule overrun.

**Role of process flow:** It defines the *order* of equipment, enabling minimum backtracking and shortest pipe runs. Process flow is the *skeleton* of the plot plan.

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for 6 phases = 6 marks; 1 mark for the role summary. Total 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Use the formula derived in Section 3.1:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = (p_{l} + p_{i}) \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}} + c_{v} \cdot A_{\text{required}} \cdot (n - 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

- $A_{\text{required}} = 8000 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $p_{l} = 3000 \text{ ₹/m}^{2}$
- $p_{i} = 900 \text{ ₹/m}^{2}$
- $c_{v} = 1500 \text{ ₹/m}^{2}$
- $n = 2$ floors
- $\text{PR} = 0.40$

**Step 3 — Compute land cost + infrastructure term:**

$$
(p_{l} + p_{i}) \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}} = (3000 + 900) \cdot \dfrac{8000}{2 \cdot 0.40}
$$

$$
= 3900 \cdot \dfrac{8000}{0.80} = 3900 \cdot 10000 = \text{₹ } 3.90 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute vertical construction cost:**

$$
c_{v} \cdot A_{\text{required}} \cdot (n - 1) = 1500 \cdot 8000 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1500 \cdot 8000 = \text{₹ } 1.20 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 3.90 + 1.20 = \text{₹ } 5.10 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 6 — Optimum plot area:**

$$
A_{\text{plot}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}} = \dfrac{8000}{2 \cdot 0.40} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment:**

The plot area of **10,000 m²** (about 1 hectare) is feasible for a medium-scale chemical plant. The total cost of **₹5.10 Crore** is dominated by land+infrastructure (76.5%), highlighting that **land cost is the primary economic driver** in plant layout. A higher PR would reduce cost but violate green-belt norms.

*(Mark split: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution and computation, 1 mark area, 1 mark total cost, 2 marks for justification/comment. Total 7 marks.)*

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to A) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **Q.B (a)** Discuss in detail the **factors to be considered in plot plan development** for a chemical plant. List any **five safety zoning rules** that must be applied. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** Two cylindrical storage tanks have diameters **6 m and 4 m** and are located in a flammable area. Both have a height of **8 m**. Calculate the **minimum center-to-center spacing** between them using the cube-root rule with $f = 1.5$ for flammable service, and verify it against the empirical spacing rule with $K_{\text{service}} = 1.5$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Factors in plot plan development (any 6):**
1. **Process flow direction** — to minimize backtracking and pipe runs.
2. **Site topography** — gravity flow where possible; raise hazardous buildings above flood level.
3. **Prevailing wind direction** — place vent stacks, flare, and ETP downwind of inhabited zones.
4. **Future expansion** — reserve at least 20% plot area for phase-2.
5. **Utility corridors** — pipe racks, cable trays, drainage aligned.
6. **Access and circulation** — minimum 6 m wide road around all process blocks; turning radius ≥ 12 m.
7. **Safety distances** — per OISD and Kerala Factory Rules.
8. **Environmental compliance** — green belt ≥ 20%, ETP at lowest contour, rainwater harvesting.

**Five safety zoning rules:**
1. Hazardous reactors and columns (Zone 1) at least **30 m** from Zone 2.
2. Storage tanks (Zone 3) downwind of process units and **at least 15 m** from Zone 2 with a fire wall.
3. Service area (Zone 4) at least **20 m** from Zone 3.
4. Administrative zone (Zone 5) at least **50 m** from Zone 1.
5. Emergency assembly point located upwind of all hazardous zones, on the **highest accessible contour**.

*(Mark split: 4 marks for factors, 3 marks for zoning rules. Total 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute equipment volumes.**

$$
V_1 = \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_1}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot H = \pi \cdot (3)^{2} \cdot 8 = 72\pi \approx 226.19 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
V_2 = \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot H = \pi \cdot (2)^{2} \cdot 8 = 32\pi \approx 100.53 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cube-root rule.**

$$
V_{\text{clearance}} = f^{3} \cdot \sqrt[3]{V_1 \cdot V_2}
$$

$$
V_{\text{clearance}} = (1.5)^{3} \cdot \sqrt[3]{226.19 \cdot 100.53}
$$

$$
= 3.375 \cdot \sqrt[3]{22{,}738.2}
$$

$$
\sqrt[3]{22{,}738.2} \approx 28.36 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_{\text{clearance}} = 3.375 \cdot 28.36 \approx 95.7 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert clearance volume to a linear spacing.**

Assuming the clearance envelope is a cube:

$$
S_{\text{cube}} = \sqrt[3]{V_{\text{clearance}}} = \sqrt[3]{95.7} \approx 4.57 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Empirical spacing rule.**

$$
S_{\text{empirical}} = 0.5 \cdot (D_1 + D_2) + K_{\text{service}}
$$

$$
= 0.5 \cdot (6 + 4) + 1.5 = 5.0 + 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**

The empirical rule gives a more conservative value (**6.5 m > 4.57 m**). For flammable service, the empirical rule is preferred because it includes the **safety clearances for fire-fighting access and heat radiation**, which the simple cube-root rule does not fully account for.

**Adopted spacing:** $\boxed{S = 6.5 \text{ m}}$

*(Mark split: 1 mark volume computation, 2 marks cube-root rule, 2 marks empirical rule, 1 mark comparison, 1 mark final answer. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the data collection phase:** Many students start their answer with "we arrange the equipment as shown in the figure…" This is a **direct mark-loser**. Always begin with Phase 1 (data).
> 2. **No iteration loop:** A linear, non-iterative flow is considered a *partial* answer. The procedure must show **at least one feedback loop** to score full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Mark penalty of **0.5 per question** for missing units in numerical problems. Always write m², ₹/m², m³ explicitly.
> 4. **Conflating "plot plan" with "plant layout":** Plot plan is a *deliverable*; plant layout is a *procedure*. Examiners expect students to distinguish.
> 5. **No mention of statutory norms:** Failing to cite KSPCB / Factory Act / OISD standards costs at least **1 mark** per Part B question.
> 6. **Incorrect formula use in Part B numerical:** The expression $C_{\text{total}} = (p_l + p_i) \cdot \dfrac{A}{n \cdot \text{PR}} + c_v \cdot A \cdot (n-1)$ is the **only accepted form**. Variations not based on this cost-breakdown structure receive zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] Plant Layout Procedure = **6 phases**: Data → Process Flow → Plot Plan → Equipment Location → Utility → Safety/Economic Review.
- [x] The procedure is **iterative**, with at least 3 review gates: Client Approval, Safety Check, Economic Viability.
- [x] **Plot Ratio (PR)** standard range for chemical plants: **0.30 – 0.50**.
- [x] **Green Belt Fraction** minimum: **0.20** (KSPCB norm).
- [x] **Circulation Factor** typical: **0.15 – 0.25**.
- [x] **Safety distance** between high-hazard and medium-hazard zones: **≥ 30 m** with fire wall.
- [x] **Fire water demand** formula: $Q = K \cdot \sqrt{P}$, where $K = 5.4$, $P$ = population in thousands.
- [x] **Equipment spacing** (cylindrical): $S = 0.5(D_1 + D_2) + K_{\text{service}}$.
- [x] **Cube-root rule** for 3D clearance: $V_{\text{clearance}} = f^{3} \cdot \sqrt[3]{V_1 \cdot V_2}$, $f = 1.0$ (safe), $1.5$ (flammable), $2.0$ (toxic).
- [x] **Maintenance clearance** above shell-and-tube exchangers: **≥ 2.0 m** (tube bundle withdrawal).
- [x] **Flare stack** distance from operating units: **100 – 150 m** radius.
- [x] **Raised floor elevation** in Kerala: **+0.45 to +0.6 m** above HFL (Highest Flood Level).
- [x] **Optimum plot area formula**: $A_{\text{plot}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}}$.
- [x] **Total cost formula**: $C_{\text{total}} = (p_l + p_i) \cdot \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{n \cdot \text{PR}} + c_v \cdot A_{\text{required}} \cdot (n-1)$.
- [x] **Reserved plot area** for future expansion: **≥ 20%** of total plot.
- [x] **Kerala-specific constraint**: High rainfall (**3000 mm/yr**), high humidity, so open-type layouts preferred; covered storage only for hygroscopic materials.
- [x] **Statutory references** to cite in any KTU answer: **Factories Act 1948, KSPCB norms, OISD-118, MSIHC Rules 1989, MoEFCC EIA Notification 2006**.
- [x] **Layout type selection rule**: Single product + high volume → Product Layout; Many products + low volume → Process Layout; Very large item → Fixed Position; Default → Combined Layout.
- [x] **HAZOP and HIRA** are mandatory review steps in the final phase.
- [x] **3D modeling** (AVEVA E3D, SmartPlant) is the modern tool replacing 2D plot plans — mention this in Part A for "modern methods" questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Design of Factories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Layout Design of Factories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Factory Layout Design** is the systematic planning and physical arrangement of industrial facilities, machinery, equipment, workstations, storage areas, material handling paths, and service utilities within a plant boundary to achieve the most effective inter-relationship between men, materials, machines, and movement.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPET634), factory layout is defined as:
> A pre-determined spatial organization of all production and service facilities, designed to optimize the **four Ms (Men, Materials, Machines, Movement)** in a manufacturing or process industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *good* layout is one which provides the **maximum possible material handling efficiency**, **minimum manufacturing time**, **optimum utilization of floor space**, **better working conditions**, **maximum safety**, and **maximum flexibility** for future expansion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)
Imagine the **kitchen of a busy restaurant**.
- The **stove**, the **sink**, the **cutting counter**, the **refrigerator**, and the **serving window** must all be placed such that the chef (Man) takes the least number of steps to move raw materials (Material) using pans and ladles (Movement) between stations (Machine).
- If the sink is placed in the far corner away from the stove, the chef wastes energy carrying heavy pots — this is **inefficient layout**.
- If everything is placed logically in the **flow of cooking** (wash → cut → cook → serve), the workflow is seamless — this is an **efficient product-style layout**.

A factory is essentially a giant version of this kitchen. The **Layout Design** is the act of deciding **where each "ingredient station" goes** so that raw materials enter, get transformed, and finished products exit with the **least wasted motion, time, and cost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Ms of Layout** — universally quoted in KTU exams:
> 1. **Men** (workforce)
> 2. **Materials** (raw + WIP + finished)
> 3. **Machines** (equipment)
> 4. **Movement** (material handling)
> 
> A layout is judged by how economically it manages the interaction of these four.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics
- **Material Handling Cost (MHC)** — should be **≤ 30% to 50%** of total manufacturing cost (Schaffer's benchmark).
- **Distance Traveled (DT)** — measured in **metres per unit** of output.
- **Backtracking Percentage** — ideal target is **< 10%** of total travel.
- **Space Utilization Ratio** — **Usable Area / Total Plant Area** should be **≥ 0.7 to 0.8**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of a Good Factory Layout
1. **Minimize material handling cost** — less distance, fewer lifts, less backtracking.
2. **Optimize man-hour utilization** — smooth workflow without idle time.
3. **Maximize floor space utilization** — no wasted corridors, no unused pockets.
4. **Provide better working conditions** — adequate light, ventilation, noise control, safety.
5. **Ensure operational flexibility** — easy to expand, re-arrange, or scale up.
6. **Reduce work-in-process (WIP) inventory** — shorter cycle time.
7. **Facilitate production planning & control** — clear bottleneck identification.
8. **Reduce production lead time** — faster order-to-delivery cycle.

### 2.2 Types of Factory Layouts (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Layout Type | Also Called | Key Idea | Best Suited For | Limitations |
|---|------------|-------------|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Product Layout** | Line Layout | Machines arranged as per **sequence of operations** | Mass production, assembly lines | High initial cost, low flexibility |
| 2 | **Process Layout** | Functional Layout | Machines **grouped by function** (e.g., all lathes in one bay) | Job-shop, batch production | Long material travel, complex routing |
| 3 | **Fixed-Position Layout** | Project Layout | **Product stays put**, men/machines move to it | Shipbuilding, aircraft, construction | High coordination cost |
| 4 | **Cellular Layout (GT)** | Group Technology | Combines product + process; part-families grouped in cells | Mid-volume, mid-variety | Requires part-family analysis |
| 5 | **Combined / Hybrid Layout** | Mixed Layout | A blend of two or more layouts | Most real-world factories | Design complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick to Remember:** "**PPCM FC**" → **P**roduct, **P**roject (=Fixed), **C**ellular, **M**ixed(=Combined), **F**unctional (=Process).

### 2.3 Principles of Factory Layout Design
- **Principle of Integration** — integrate men, material, machine.
- **Principle of Minimum Distance** — shortest possible movement.
- **Principle of Workflow** — straight-line, unidirectional flow.
- **Principle of Space Utilization** — cubic (3-D) usage, not just floor (2-D).
- **Principle of Flexibility** — easily alterable for future needs.
- **Principle of Safety & Satisfaction** — compliance with Factories Act, 1948.
- **Principle of Minimum Investment** — least capital on fixed assets, buildings.

### 2.4 Factors Influencing Factory Layout
- Nature of the **product** (size, weight, design changes).
- **Volume of production** (mass vs. batch vs. job).
- **Manufacturing process** (continuous vs. intermittent).
- **Type of equipment** (general-purpose vs. special-purpose).
- **Plant location & site** (shape, topography, climate).
- **Building** (type — single-storey preferred for heavy industry).
- **Material handling system** (conveyor, crane, AGV, forklift).
- **Human factors** (lighting, ventilation, ergonomics).
- **Service facilities** (power, water, compressed air, steam).
- **Future expansion** plans.

### 2.5 Systematic Layout Planning (SLP) — Muther's Approach
Richard Muther's **SLP** is the **most frequently asked framework** in KTU exams. It has **4 phases × 3 stages = 12 steps**, simplified below:

**Phase I — Location (where to build?)**
1. Analyze P-Q-R-S-T data (Product, Quantity, Routing, Supporting services, Timing).
2. Select region and site.

**Phase II — General Layout (overall arrangement)**
3. Project **Flow Analysis** (material flow between activities).
4. Project **Activity Relationship Analysis** (Activity Relationship Chart, ARC).
5. Develop **Activity Relationship Diagram** (ARD) → spaghetti chart.
6. Compute **Space Requirements** (using templates/blocks).
7. Generate **Block Diagram / Layout Alternatives**.
8. Evaluate alternatives using **Activity Relationship vs. Space Adjacency matrix**.

**Phase III — Detailed Layout**
9. Plan individual departments, workstations, aisles.
10. Plan material handling equipment, storage, services.
11. Finalize the layout.

**Phase IV — Installation**
12. Execute and review.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units / Standard |
|---|-------------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | MHC = C × D × T × F | Material Handling Cost (per period) | Rs / month or Rs / year |
| 2 | Throughput Time = Sum of Operation Time + Wait Time + Move Time | Time from raw → finished | minutes / hours |
| 3 | Line Balance Efficiency $\eta$ = $\frac{\sum t_i}{n \times C_t}$ | How balanced the line is | **η ≤ 100%** (ideal = 100%) |
| 4 | Balance Delay $BD$ = $1 - \eta$ | Idle time fraction | Fraction (0 to 1) |
| 5 | Cycle Time $C_t$ = $\frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | Time between successive units | min / unit |
| 6 | Number of Stations $n$ = $\left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{C_t} \right\rceil$ | Round up to next integer | Integer |
| 7 | Flow Index = $\frac{\text{Number of Favorable Flows}}{\text{Total Number of Flows}}$ | Spaghetti chart metric | 0 to 1 (≥ 0.7 desired) |
| 8 | Space per Worker = $\frac{\text{Workshop Area}}{\text{Number of Workers}}$ | Standard = **3 to 5 m²** | m² / person |
| 9 | Floor Space Index = $\frac{\text{Built-up Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | Land-use efficiency | 0.3 to 1.5 |
| 10 | Closeness Rating (CR) | A, E, I, O, U, X | Mnemonic: "**A**ll **E**ngineers **I**n **O**ur **U**niversity **X**" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) Closeness Codes (Muther):**
> - **A** = Absolutely Necessary (red)
> - **E** = Especially Important (orange)
> - **I** = Important (yellow)
> - **O** = Ordinary / OK (green)
> - **U** = Unimportant (blue)
> - **X** = Not Desirable (grey)

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
Factory layout design is the **first and most capital-intensive** decision in any industrial project — once a plant is built, retrofitting layout costs **5 to 10 times** the original savings. It directly impacts:
- **Operating cost** (up to **30%** of conversion cost comes from material handling).
- **Product quality** (controlled environment, reduced damage).
- **Lead time** and **on-time delivery** KPIs.
- **Worker safety** and **ergonomics** (Factories Act compliance).
- **Scalability** for Industry 4.0 / smart manufacturing retrofits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Line Balancing for Product Layout
Consider a product that requires **6 sequential operations** with standard times $t_1$ through $t_6$. The required output is **60 units per 8-hour shift**.

**Given Data:**
- $t_1 = 4$ min, $t_2 = 6$ min, $t_3 = 3$ min, $t_4 = 5$ min, $t_5 = 2$ min, $t_6 = 4$ min
- Available time = $8 \times 60 = 480$ min
- Demand = 60 units / shift
- Precedence: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6$

**Step 1: Calculate Cycle Time**

$$
C_t = \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ units}} = 8 \text{ min / unit}
$$

**Step 2: Sum of Operation Times**

$$
\sum t_i = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6
$$

$$
\sum t_i = 4 + 6 + 3 + 5 + 2 + 4 = 24 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum Theoretical Number of Workstations**

$$
n_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{C_t} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{24}{8} \right\rceil = \lceil 3 \rceil = 3 \text{ stations}
$$

**Step 4: Assign Tasks Using Longest-Work-Element Heuristic (LWH)**
Starting from precedence + largest time first, one feasible assignment:

- **Station 1:** $t_1 + t_2 = 4 + 6 = 10$ min → exceeds 8 min ❌
- **Refine using LWH:** Station 1 → $t_2 (6) + t_1 (4) = 10$ ❌. Try $t_2 (6) + t_3 (3) = 9$ ❌. Try $t_2 (6) + t_4 (5)$ — no precedence. Try $t_2 (6) + t_5 (2) = 8$ ✓ → **Station 1 = {2, 5}** = 8 min.
- **Station 2:** remaining {1, 3, 4, 6}. Try $t_1 (4) + t_3 (3) + t_6 (4) = 11$ ❌. Try $t_4 (5) + t_3 (3) = 8$ ✓ → **Station 2 = {4, 3}** = 8 min.
- **Station 3:** remaining {1, 6}. $t_1 (4) + t_6 (4) = 8$ ✓ → **Station 3 = {1, 6}** = 8 min.

**Final Assignment Table:**

| Station | Tasks | Total Time (min) | Idle Time (min) |
|---------|-------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 2, 5 | 8 | $8 - 8 = 0$ |
| 2 | 4, 3 | 8 | $8 - 8 = 0$ |
| 3 | 1, 6 | 8 | $8 - 8 = 0$ |

**Step 5: Compute Line Balance Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{n \times C_t} = \frac{24}{3 \times 8} = \frac{24}{24} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

**Step 6: Compute Balance Delay**

$$
BD = 1 - \eta = 1 - 1.0 = 0 = 0\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An efficiency of **100%** means a **perfectly balanced** line — no idle time, ideal KTU textbook outcome.

### 3.2 Derivation — Material Handling Cost Minimization
The total annual material handling cost between two workstations $i$ and $j$ is:

$$
C_{ij} = M_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times C_u
$$

Where:
- $M_{ij}$ = quantity of material moved between $i$ and $j$ (kg or units / year)
- $d_{ij}$ = distance between $i$ and $j$ (m)
- $C_u$ = unit handling cost (Rs / m / kg)

**Total Plant Material Handling Cost (TMC):**

$$
\text{TMC} = C_u \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

For a **product layout**, the objective is to minimize TMC by sequencing machines in the **order of operations**, ensuring that the **heaviest-traffic** pairs are placed **closest together**.

**CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique) Adjacency Penalty:**

$$
\Delta\text{Cost} = \sum_{(i,j)} C_{ij} \cdot (d'_{ij} - d_{ij})
$$

Where $d_{ij}$ is original distance and $d'_{ij}$ is new distance after an exchange. **If $\Delta\text{Cost} < 0$, accept the exchange** (cost reduces).

### 3.3 Derivation — Space Requirement for a Workstation
Required floor area for a machine + operator + aisle + clearance:

$$
A = L \times B + (C_o + C_a)
$$

Where:
- $L$ = machine length (m)
- $B$ = machine breadth (m)
- $C_o$ = clearance for operator = **0.6 m to 1.0 m**
- $C_a$ = clearance for aisle / material movement = **0.9 m to 1.5 m** (one-way) or **1.8 m to 2.5 m** (two-way)

**Standard clearance per KTU / Factory Inspectorate norms:**

| Machine Type | Operator Clearance | Aisle Clearance |
|--------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Light bench work | 0.6 m | 0.9 m |
| Medium machinery | 0.9 m | 1.2 m |
| Heavy machinery (press, lathe) | 1.0 m | 1.5 m |
| Extra heavy (rolling mill) | 1.2 m | 2.5 m |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Layout Types

| Parameter | Product | Process | Fixed Position | Cellular | Combined |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------------|----------|----------|
| Capital investment | Very High | Low | Highest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Flexibility | Very Low | Very High | Low | Moderate | High |
| Material handling cost | Lowest | Highest | Moderate | Low | Low-Moderate |
| WIP inventory | Lowest | Highest | N/A | Low | Moderate |
| Skilled labor need | Low | High | Highest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Production rate | Highest | Lowest | Lowest | High | High |
| Best for volume | High | Low | One-off | Medium | Variable |
| Bottleneck risk | High | Moderate | Low | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Product Layout Flow (Line Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Material Store] --> s1[Station 1 Cutting]
    s1 --> s2[Station 2 Bending]
    s2 --> s3[Station 3 Welding]
    s3 --> s4[Station 4 Painting]
    s4 --> s5[Station 5 Assembly]
    s5 --> s6[Station 6 Testing]
    s6 --> fg[Finished Goods Store]
    s6 --> dispatch[Dispatch Bay]
```

> **Caption:** Unidirectional sequential flow — typical of automobile assembly lines.

### 4.2 Process Layout Flow (Functional / Job Shop)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw[Raw Store] --> lathe[Lathe Section]
    raw --> mill[Milling Section]
    raw --> drill[Drilling Section]
    raw --> grind[Grinding Section]
    raw --> heat[Heat Treatment]
    lathe --> qc[Quality Control]
    mill --> qc
    drill --> qc
    grind --> heat
    heat --> qc
    qc --> fg[Finished Goods]
    qc --> rework[Rework Bay]
    rework --> lathe
```

> **Caption:** Functional grouping of similar machines — typical of job-shop manufacturing.

### 4.3 Fixed-Position Layout (Project / Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    project[Product at Centre - eg Aircraft or Ship]
    project --> crew1[Fabrication Crew Moves In]
    project --> crew2[Assembly Crew Moves In]
    project --> crew3[Welding Crew Moves In]
    project --> crew4[Testing Crew Moves In]
    project --> crane[Mobile Cranes and Equipment]
    project --> store[Temporary Material Yard]
```

> **Caption:** Product stationary; men, machines, and materials travel to it.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SLP (Muther's Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1[Phase I Location Analysis] --> pqrs[P Q R S T Data Collection]
    pqrs --> site[Region and Site Selection]
    site --> phase2[Phase II General Layout]
    phase2 --> flow1[Flow Analysis of Materials]
    phase2 --> arc1[Activity Relationship Analysis ARC]
    flow1 --> ard[Activity Relationship Diagram ARD]
    arc1 --> ard
    ard --> space1[Space Requirements Calculation]
    space1 --> block[Block Diagram Layout Alternatives]
    block --> eval[Evaluation and Selection]
    eval --> phase3[Phase III Detailed Layout]
    phase3 --> detail[Department Workstation Aisle and Service Planning]
    detail --> phase4[Phase IV Installation]
    phase4 --> review[Implementation and Review]
```

> **Caption:** Sequential framework of Systematic Layout Planning (SLP) — most-asked KTU framework.

### 4.5 ARC Closeness-Rating Matrix Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    deptA[Department A] -->|A Absolutely Necessary| deptB[Department B]
    deptA -->|E Especially Important| deptC[Department C]
    deptA -->|U Unimportant| deptD[Department D]
    deptA -->|X Not Desirable| deptE[Department E]
    deptB -->|I Important| deptC
    deptB -->|O Ordinary| deptD
    deptC -->|O Ordinary| deptE
```

> **Caption:** Symbolic representation of Muther's closeness codes in an ARC.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define factory layout. List any four objectives of a good factory layout.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Factory layout is the systematic physical arrangement of industrial facilities, machines, workstations, storage, and service utilities within a plant to optimize the inter-relationship between men, materials, machines, and movement.

**Four Objectives (0.5 mark each, 2 marks total):**
1. Minimize material handling cost.
2. Maximize floor space utilization.
3. Reduce work-in-process (WIP) inventory.
4. Provide better working conditions and safety.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between product layout and process layout. Mention one industry example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Arrangement | Machines in **sequence of operations** | Machines **grouped by function** |
| Material flow | Straight-line, unidirectional | Complex, back-and-forth |
| Cost | High initial | Low initial |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Industry example (1 mark) | **Maruti Suzuki assembly line** (automobile mass production) | **Bharat Heavy Electricals Ltd — machining shop** (job-shop with mixed batch orders) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks each with internal choice. Each sub-part below carries **7 marks**.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

(a) Explain the **Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** procedure proposed by Richard Muther with a neat flow diagram. Discuss any **four phases** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** SLP is a structured, logical, and data-driven procedure for layout design developed by Richard Muther. It converts user requirements into a base layout and then refines it.

**Four Phases (1.5 marks each):**

**Phase I — Location:** Determines the **region and site** of the plant using **P-Q-R-S-T data** — Product (what to make), Quantity (how much), Routing (how it's made), Supporting services (utilities), Timing (when needed).

**Phase II — General Layout (most important — 2 marks):** Develops the overall block plan using:
- **Flow analysis** of materials (From-To chart).
- **Activity Relationship Analysis** (ARC with A/E/I/O/U/X codes).
- **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** — a circular bubble diagram of departments with closeness-indicating lines.
- **Space requirements** (template or block-size method).
- **Block diagram alternatives** generated and evaluated.
- **Evaluation matrix**: Plot closeness rating vs. space adjacency to find the most balanced layout.

**Phase III — Detailed Layout:** Plans each department — exact position of machines, workbenches, aisles, storage, lighting, ventilation, material handling equipment (cranes, conveyors, AGVs), and safety provisions.

**Phase IV — Installation:** Executes the plan — orders equipment, builds, installs, commissions. Includes a **post-installation review** to verify the design intent.

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):** (See SECTION 4, diagram 4.4)

---

(b) The following **six machines** must be sequenced on a product line. Precedence and times are given:

| Operation | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time (min) | 4 | 6 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 4 |
| Predecessor | — | 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4,5 |

Available time = **480 min/day**, demand = **60 units/day**. Determine:
(i) Cycle time and minimum number of workstations
(ii) Assign tasks to stations using the **Longest Work Element** heuristic
(iii) Line balance efficiency and balance delay **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Cycle Time (1 mark):**

$$
C_t = \frac{480}{60} = 8 \text{ min/unit}
$$

**Minimum stations (1 mark):**

$$
n_{\min} = \lceil (4+6+3+5+2+4)/8 \rceil = \lceil 24/8 \rceil = 3
$$

**(ii) Task Assignment using LWH (3 marks):**

- **Station 1:** Available: {2,3,5}. Largest = 6 (Op 2). Add: 2+3=9 ❌, 2+5=8 ✓ → **{2, 5}** = 8 min, idle 0.
- **Station 2:** Available: {3,4,6}. Largest = 5 (Op 4). Add: 4+6=9 ❌, 4+3=8 ✓ → **{4, 3}** = 8 min, idle 0.
- **Station 3:** Available: {1,6}. Largest = 4 (Op 1). Add: 1+6=8 ✓ → **{1, 6}** = 8 min, idle 0.

| Station | Tasks | Time (min) | Idle Time (min) |
|---------|-------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 2, 5 | 8 | 0 |
| 2 | 4, 3 | 8 | 0 |
| 3 | 1, 6 | 8 | 0 |

**(iii) Efficiency and Balance Delay (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{24}{3 \times 8} = \frac{24}{24} = 100\%
$$

$$
BD = 1 - 1.0 = 0\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A **perfectly balanced** product line with **zero idle time** and **zero balance delay**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

(a) Explain the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** and the **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** used in factory layout design. Illustrate with a sample chart of **6 departments** using closeness codes A, E, I, O, U, X. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ARC (Activity Relationship Chart) — 2 marks:**
- A tabular (matrix) form showing the **degree of closeness required** between every pair of departments.
- Muther's closeness codes: **A, E, I, O, U, X** with priority **A > E > I > O > U > X**.
- Codes are entered for each pair, with a reason code in the lower half (e.g., "1 = use same equipment", "2 = share personnel", "3 = noise", etc.).

**ARD (Activity Relationship Diagram) — 2 marks:**
- A **spaghetti/balloon diagram** where each department is a numbered node, and lines connecting nodes represent closeness.
- The thickness / number of lines indicates intensity: **A = 4 lines, E = 3 lines, I = 2 lines, O = 1 line, U = no line, X = dashed line with cross**.

**Sample ARC for 6 Departments (1–6) — 3 marks:**

| Dept | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 (Receiving)** | — | A/1 | U/— | X/6 | U/— | O/4 |
| **2 (Stores)** | A/1 | — | E/2 | I/3 | O/4 | U/— |
| **3 (Production)** | U/— | E/2 | — | A/1 | I/2 | O/4 |
| **4 (Assembly)** | X/6 | I/3 | A/1 | — | E/2 | U/— |
| **5 (Testing)** | U/— | O/4 | I/2 | E/2 | — | A/1 |
| **6 (Dispatch)** | O/4 | U/— | O/4 | U/— | A/1 | — |

**Reason codes:** 1 = Material flow, 2 = Common personnel, 3 = Shared equipment, 4 = Convenience, 6 = Noise/hazard.

**Interpretation (sample):** Dept 1 (Receiving) must be **absolutely adjacent** to Dept 2 (Stores) — code A, reason 1 (material flow). Dept 1 and Dept 4 (Assembly) are **not desirable** adjacent — code X, reason 6 (noise).

---

(b) Compare the **five major types of factory layouts** in a tabular format, covering at least **six parameters** (capital cost, flexibility, material handling cost, WIP, labor skill, volume suitability). Give **one industry example** for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Product | Process | Fixed-Position | Cellular | Combined |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------------|----------|----------|
| Capital cost | Very High | Low | Highest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Flexibility | Very Low | Very High | Low | Moderate | High |
| Material handling cost | Lowest | Highest | Moderate | Low | Low-Moderate |
| WIP inventory | Lowest | Highest | N/A | Low | Moderate |
| Labor skill required | Low | Very High | Highest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Suitable production volume | High | Low | One-off | Medium | Variable |

**Industry Examples (each 0.2 mark, 1 mark total):**
1. **Product** → Maruti Suzuki assembly line (cars).
2. **Process** → BHEL heavy machine shop (custom jobs).
3. **Fixed-Position** → Cochin Shipyard (shipbuilding).
4. **Cellular** → TVS Motor gear cell (grouped by part-family).
5. **Combined** → Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (machining + assembly + engine testing).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Most modern factories use a **combined (hybrid) layout** — a section of process layout for jobbing, a product layout for high-volume sub-assemblies, and fixed-position for very large items.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Process Layout" with "Product Layout"** — remember: *Process = functional grouping (machines by type), Product = sequential grouping (machines by operation order).*
> 2. **Forgetting reason codes in ARC** — KTU examiner awards marks only when both **closeness code (A/E/I/O/U/X)** AND **reason code (1–9)** are shown.
> 3. **In line balancing, not using $\lceil \rceil$ ceiling** — if 24/8 = 3 exactly, students sometimes write 3.0; **always show $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ explicitly** for 1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning "available time" units correctly** — convert hours to minutes (or vice versa) before computing $C_t$.
> 5. **Skipping the precedence constraints** while assigning tasks — task 2 cannot come before task 1 if it is a successor.
> 6. **Writing "min" instead of "min/unit"** for $C_t$ — always write the **per unit** qualifier.
> 7. **In SLP, mixing Phase I and Phase II** — Phase I is **location** (macro: where?), Phase II is **general layout** (meso: arrangement), Phase III is **detailed layout** (micro: machine position).
> 8. **No diagram for 14-mark question = -2 marks minimum** — always draw a labeled block / flowchart / ARC / ARD.

---

### 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** Layout = spatial arrangement optimizing **Men, Materials, Machines, Movement**.
- ✅ **5 Layout Types:** Product, Process, Fixed-Position, Cellular, Combined.
- ✅ **4 Ms of Layout:** Men, Material, Machine, Movement.
- ✅ **SLP (Muther)** has **4 phases**: Location → General Layout → Detailed Layout → Installation.
- ✅ **ARC Codes:** A, E, I, O, U, X (in decreasing order of closeness).
- ✅ **ARD** is the visual spaghetti/balloon representation of ARC.
- ✅ **Cycle Time** $C_t$ = Available Time / Demand (units: min/unit).
- ✅ **Number of stations** $n_{\min} = \lceil \sum t_i / C_t \rceil$.
- ✅ **Line balance efficiency** $\eta = \sum t_i / (n \times C_t)$, ideal = **100%**.
- ✅ **Balance Delay** $BD = 1 - \eta$.
- ✅ **Material Handling Cost** TMC = $C_u \sum M_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Space per worker standard** = **3 to 5 m²** (KTU & Factories Act norm).
- ✅ **Aisle clearance** for heavy machinery = **1.5 m (one-way) to 2.5 m (two-way)**.
- ✅ **MHC benchmark** = **30%–50%** of total manufacturing cost.
- ✅ **Backtracking target** = **< 10%** of total material travel.
- ✅ **CRAFT algorithm** — accept an exchange if $\Delta$C < 0.
- ✅ Always draw a **neat block / flow diagram** in Part B — it's **compulsory** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Office
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Office

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **OFFICE (Plant Layout Context):** An **office** is a centralized administrative and communication hub within an industrial plant that serves as the operational nerve center for coordinating production, personnel, records, and management decisions. In the discipline of Plant Layout and Design, the office is treated as a distinct service department whose location, size, layout, and inter-departmental relationships are engineered to maximize managerial efficiency, minimize information lag, and optimize the **flow of authority, communication, paperwork, and personnel** between the shop floor, the stores, the dispatch section, and the top management.

According to KTU-aligned **Plant Layout and Design** pedagogy, an office is not merely a room with desks — it is a **system of activities** consisting of planning, organizing, directing, controlling, and coordinating the resources of the enterprise, supported by a physical infrastructure.

### 1.2 Constituent Elements of an Industrial Office

A modern industrial office typically integrates the following functional sub-units:

- **Administrative Office** – Houses top-level executives (Managing Director, Plant Manager, Department Heads).
- **Production Planning & Control (PPC) Office** – Handles scheduling, dispatch, routing, and expediting.
- **Personnel / Human Resources Office** – Manages recruitment, training, payroll, welfare, and industrial relations.
- **Accounts & Finance Office** – Oversees costing, budgeting, billing, taxation, and audit.
- **Sales, Purchase & Marketing Office** – Handles customer orders, vendor coordination, and market analysis.
- **Quality Control / Inspection Office** – Manages testing, inspection reports, and statistical quality data.
- **Engineering / R&D Office** – Hosts design engineers, draftsmen, and CAD workstations.
- **Reception and Waiting Lounge** – The first point of contact for visitors and suppliers.
- **Records / Filing / Archives Room** – Storage for physical and digital documentation.
- **Conference / Board Room** – Used for meetings, presentations, and management reviews.
- **Canteen / Pantry / Washrooms** – Ancillary but mandatory facilities under the Factories Act.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 Think of a Plant Office as the "Bridge of a Ship"!**
>
> Imagine a giant cargo ship crossing the ocean. The **engine room** is the shop floor (raw production), the **cargo holds** are the warehouse and stores, and the **crew cabins** are the welfare area. But the **bridge** — that elevated glass cabin from where the captain steers, the officers give orders, the radio operator talks to the port, and the navigator plots the course — is the **OFFICE**. Without the bridge, the ship has no direction, no communication, and no control. Similarly, without a well-designed office, even the best-laid plant will drift operationally.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Quantitative Anchors

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Office Area per Executive:** **7.0 m² to 11.5 m² (75 – 125 sq ft)** per person.
> - **Cubicle/Partitioned Bay Size:** **2.4 m × 2.4 m (8 ft × 8 ft)** is the KTU-recommended modular standard.
> - **Corridor Width:** Minimum **1.8 m (6 ft)** for main aisles; **1.2 m (4 ft)** for secondary aisles.
> - **Ceiling Height:** Minimum **3.0 m (10 ft)** for proper ventilation and lighting.
> - **Daylight Factor:** Office spaces require a minimum daylight factor of **1.5% to 2.0%**.
> - **Illumination Level:** **300–500 lux** as per IS 3646 / ISO 8995.
> - **Acoustic Standard:** Background noise not to exceed **45 dB(A)** in administrative offices.
> - **Office-to-Production Area Ratio:** Generally **1:5 to 1:10**, depending on industry type.

### 1.5 Importance of Office in Plant Layout

| S. No. | Functional Importance | Layout Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centralized decision-making | Office must be near the main entrance |
| 2 | Communication link between departments | Position close to high-traffic zones |
| 3 | Record keeping & documentation | Requires quiet, secure, dust-free zone |
| 4 | Coordination of production | Must be near the shop floor |
| 5 | Personnel management | Located near welfare facilities |
| 6 | Public interface | Reception near the main gate |
| 7 | Security and confidentiality | Isolated from noisy, hazardous zones |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives and Functions of an Industrial Office

The office is the **central nervous system** of the plant. Its functions can be classified into **Primary (Operational)** and **Secondary (Supportive)** functions.

#### A. Primary Functions (Management Functions – POLC Framework)

1. **Planning** – Forecasting demand, scheduling production, budgeting.
2. **Organizing** – Allocating men, machines, materials, and methods (4M's).
3. **Leading / Directing** – Issuing work orders, instructions, and motivational directives.
4. **Controlling** – Comparing actual performance vs. standards and initiating corrective action.
5. **Coordinating** – Harmonizing the efforts of different departments (production, stores, dispatch, quality, marketing).

#### B. Secondary Functions

- Maintaining statutory records under the **Factories Act 1948**, **Payment of Wages Act**, **Minimum Wages Act**, and **ESI / PF Acts**.
- Handling correspondence, mail, telephone, and digital communication.
- Filing, indexing, retrieving, and archiving documents.
- Maintaining personnel records, attendance, leave, and payroll.
- Processing purchase requisitions, sales invoices, and gate passes.
- Acting as the information interface between the plant and the outside world (customers, vendors, government, banks).

### 2.2 Types of Offices in a Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Favourite Topic:** "Classify and explain the types of offices." This appears frequently as a **3-mark** or **7-mark** sub-question.

| Type | Description | Typical Occupants | Location Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Administrative / Executive Office** | Top management suite | MD, GM, Directors, Plant Head | Near main gate, on upper floor with a clear view |
| **2. Production / PPC Office** | Plans and controls shop floor | Production Manager, PPC Engineer, Schedulers | Adjacent to production hall |
| **3. Engineering / Technical Office** | Design, R&D, process engineering | Design Engineers, CAD operators | Near production, isolated from vibration/noise |
| **4. Personnel / HR Office** | Manpower, welfare, training | HR Manager, Welfare Officer | Near canteen, gate, and welfare block |
| **5. Accounts / Finance Office** | Money matters, costing, audit | Accountants, Cashier, Auditor | Secured location, near administrative office |
| **6. Sales / Purchase Office** | Customer/supplier interaction | Sales Manager, Purchase Officer, Marketing team | Near main entrance and parking |
| **7. Quality Control / Inspection Office** | Lab reports, inspection records | QC Engineer, Inspectors | Adjacent to QC lab and dispatch |
| **8. Stores / Inventory Office** | Stock records, GRN, issue notes | Storekeeper, Inventory analyst | Inside or adjoining the stores |
| **9. Security / Gate Office** | Entry pass, visitor log | Security Supervisor | At the main gate |
| **10. Reception / Lobby** | First point of contact | Receptionist, PA | At the main entrance |

### 2.3 Location Principles for the Office Block

The placement of the office is governed by a set of **well-established industrial engineering principles**. Mastering these is critical for KTU full-mark answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 10 Golden Rules of Office Location:**
>
> 1. **Proximity to Main Gate** – For public accessibility, visitor handling, and prestige.
> 2. **Closeness to Production** – To minimize the time lag between order and execution.
> 3. **Away from Hazardous Areas** – Not adjacent to furnaces, chemicals, or noisy machinery.
> 4. **Upwind of Pollutants** – Office should be on the windward side to avoid fumes, dust, and odor.
> 5. **Separate from Heavy Traffic** – To avoid noise, vibration, and dust.
> 6. **Centrality of Authority** – The senior-most executive should occupy a room that is central and overlooks the plant.
> 7. **View of the Shop Floor** – Often the MD/Plant Manager's cabin has a glass wall facing production.
> 8. **Easy Access to Communication** – Close to telephone exchange, IT server room, and internet hub.
> 9. **Adequate Parking** – Both two-wheeler and four-wheeler parking nearby.
> 10. **Compliance with Factories Act** – Minimum statutory distances from hazardous processes.

### 2.4 Office Layout Principles

The internal layout of the office itself follows **systematic layout planning (SLP)** and **ergonomic** guidelines.

- **Principle of Workflow:** Sequence of work should be linear; incoming mail → sorting → processing → filing.
- **Principle of Supervision:** Senior cabins should have line-of-sight over juniors.
- **Principle of Communication:** Frequently communicating departments should be co-located.
- **Principle of Privacy:** Confidential work (payroll, R&D, costing) should be in enclosed cabins.
- **Principle of Flexibility:** Use of **modular partitions** (2.4 m × 2.4 m grid) to allow easy reconfiguration.
- **Principle of Comfort:** Adequate lighting (300–500 lux), ventilation (4–6 ACH), and noise control (≤ 45 dB).
- **Principle of Safety:** Fire extinguishers, emergency exits, and earthquake-resistant furniture.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all **calculations and empirical formulas** that the KTU Board Examiner loves to test in Part B questions.

| S. No. | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Office Area | $A_{office} = N \times A_{per\,person}$ | m² | $N$ = number of office workers; $A_{per\,person}$ = 7–11.5 m² |
| 2 | Number of Cabin Modules | $n_{cabins} = \dfrac{A_{office}}{A_{module}}$ | modules | $A_{module}$ = 5.76 m² (2.4 × 2.4 m grid) |
| 3 | Corridor Area | $A_{corridor} = L_c \times W_c$ | m² | $L_c$ = length; $W_c$ ≥ 1.8 m (main) |
| 4 | Office-to-Production Ratio | $R = \dfrac{A_{office}}{A_{production}}$ | ratio | Typical: 1:5 to 1:10 |
| 5 | Illumination Required | $E = \dfrac{\Phi \times N \times \eta}{A}$ | lux | $\Phi$ = lumens/lamp; $N$ = no. of lamps; $\eta$ = utilization factor |
| 6 | Number of Lamps Required | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \eta \times M}$ | lamps | $M$ = maintenance factor (0.7–0.8) |
| 7 | Air Changes per Hour | $N_{ach} = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | ACH | Office: 4–6 ACH; $Q$ = fan capacity (m³/h); $V$ = room volume (m³) |
| 8 | Daylight Factor | $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$ | % | Minimum 1.5–2.0% for offices |
| 9 | Noise Attenuation (Wall) | $NR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{source}}{P_{receiver}}\right)$ | dB | Office requirement: ≤ 45 dB(A) |
| 10 | Population Density | $\rho = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | persons/m² | Standard: 1 person per 7–11.5 m² |
| 11 | Ergonomic Desk Height | $h_{desk} = 0.4 \times H_{person}$ | m | Approx. 0.72 m for average adult |
| 12 | Fire Extinguisher Spacing | One CO₂ extinguisher per 200 m² | m² | As per IS 2190 |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TYPING RULE FOR KTU ANSWERS:** Do **NOT** write vertical bars `|x|` inside a markdown table cell. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX. This applies to your final exam answer sheet too — avoid sloppy absolute-value notation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Plants** – Offices are located in **clean-zone buffers** with positive pressure, separated from manufacturing by airlocks.
- **Automotive Assembly Plants** – Open-plan offices with **floor-to-ceiling glass walls** overlook the assembly line (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors).
- **Petrochemical Refineries** – Offices are inside **blast-proof control buildings** located 200 m+ from process units.
- **Software / IT Parks** – Open-plan, hot-desking, agile pods, and **biophilic design** are the new norms.
- **Food Processing Plants** – Offices are in a **separate hygiene block** with foot-overshoes and pest-control barriers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Mantra:** "Show every line of calculation. Marks are awarded for steps, not just the final answer." The following two worked examples are the **most frequently asked** numerical problems on the Office topic.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Office Area Calculation

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2022 Pattern]:**
> A manufacturing plant employs **120 workers** on the shop floor and **24 office staff**. The standard office area per person is **9.0 m²**. The corridor accounts for **18%** of the gross office area. Determine:
> (a) The net office area required.
> (b) The gross office area including corridors.
> (c) The number of standard 2.4 m × 2.4 m modular cabin units required.
> (d) The total length of the main corridor if the corridor width is 1.8 m.

#### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**
- Number of office staff, $N = 24$
- Office area per person, $A_{per} = 9.0 \text{ m}^2$
- Corridor overhead, $C_{ovh} = 18\% = 0.18$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Net Office Area**

The net office area is the actual area occupied by desks, chairs, cabins, and furniture — excluding corridors.

$$
A_{net} = N \times A_{per}
$$

$$
A_{net} = 24 \times 9.0
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{net} = 216 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Gross Office Area (Including Corridors)**

The gross area includes the net usable area plus corridors, restrooms, and structural walls.

$$
A_{gross} = A_{net} \times (1 + C_{ovh})
$$

$$
A_{gross} = 216 \times (1 + 0.18)
$$

$$
A_{gross} = 216 \times 1.18
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{gross} = 254.88 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Number of Modular Cabin Units**

Each modular cabin has a footprint of $2.4 \text{ m} \times 2.4 \text{ m}$.

$$
A_{module} = 2.4 \times 2.4
$$

$$
A_{module} = 5.76 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
n_{cabins} = \frac{A_{net}}{A_{module}}
$$

$$
n_{cabins} = \frac{216}{5.76}
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{cabins} = 37.5 \approx 38 \text{ modules}}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Main Corridor Length**

The total corridor area is the difference between gross and net area.

$$
A_{corridor} = A_{gross} - A_{net}
$$

$$
A_{corridor} = 254.88 - 216 = 38.88 \text{ m}^2
$$

Given corridor width $W_c = 1.8 \text{ m}$,

$$
L_{corridor} = \frac{A_{corridor}}{W_c}
$$

$$
L_{corridor} = \frac{38.88}{1.8}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{corridor} = 21.6 \text{ m}}
$$

#### Final Answer Summary

| Parameter | Calculated Value |
|---|---|
| Net Office Area | 216 m² |
| Gross Office Area | 254.88 m² |
| Number of Cabin Modules | 38 |
| Main Corridor Length | 21.6 m |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Illumination Design for the Office

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]:**
> An office room measures **12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m** (height). The required illumination is **400 lux**. Each fluorescent lamp provides **3000 lumens** and the utilization factor is **0.5**. The maintenance factor is **0.75**. Calculate:
> (a) The number of lamps required.
> (b) The total lumens supplied.
> (c) The air changes per hour if the exhaust fan delivers **1200 m³/h**.

#### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**
- Room area, $A = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ m}^2$
- Required illumination, $E = 400 \text{ lux}$
- Lumens per lamp, $\Phi = 3000 \text{ lm}$
- Utilization factor, $\eta = 0.5$
- Maintenance factor, $M = 0.75$
- Fan capacity, $Q = 1200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Number of Lamps**

The standard illumination formula is:

$$
E = \frac{\Phi \times N \times \eta \times M}{A}
$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \eta \times M}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N = \frac{400 \times 96}{3000 \times 0.5 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
N = \frac{38400}{1125}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 34.13 \approx 35 \text{ lamps}}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Total Lumens Supplied**

$$
\Phi_{total} = N \times \Phi_{per\,lamp}
$$

$$
\Phi_{total} = 35 \times 3000
$$

$$
\boxed{\Phi_{total} = 105{,}000 \text{ lumens}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**

The room volume is:

$$
V = A \times h
$$

$$
V = 96 \times 3.5
$$

$$
V = 336 \text{ m}^3
$$

Air changes per hour:

$$
N_{ach} = \frac{Q}{V}
$$

$$
N_{ach} = \frac{1200}{336}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{ach} = 3.57 \approx 3.6 \text{ ACH}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An ACH of 3.6 is on the **lower side** for an office (recommended: 4–6 ACH). The designer may need to add another fan of 600 m³/h capacity to bring it up to the standard.

#### Final Answer Summary

| Parameter | Calculated Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Lamps | 35 | — |
| Total Lumens | 105,000 lm | — |
| Air Changes per Hour | 3.6 ACH | Recommended 4–6 ACH |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Office Location

This decision matrix is widely used in KTU case-study questions and industry practice.

| Selection Criterion | Weight (w) | Option A (Near Gate) | Option B (Near Shop Floor) | Option C (Central) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accessibility for visitors | 0.20 | 9 / 1.80 | 5 / 1.00 | 7 / 1.40 |
| Proximity to production | 0.25 | 4 / 1.00 | 10 / 2.50 | 7 / 1.75 |
| Communication ease | 0.20 | 6 / 1.20 | 8 / 1.60 | 9 / 1.80 |
| Distance from noise/hazard | 0.15 | 8 / 1.20 | 3 / 0.45 | 7 / 1.05 |
| Parking convenience | 0.10 | 10 / 1.00 | 5 / 0.50 | 6 / 0.60 |
| Security & prestige | 0.10 | 9 / 0.90 | 5 / 0.50 | 8 / 0.80 |
| **Weighted Score** | **1.00** | **7.10** | **6.55** | **7.40** |

$$
\text{Score}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \times r_{ij}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Option C (Central Location) scores the highest (**7.40**), making it the optimal choice. The same matrix is also used in **SLP (Systematic Layout Planning)** activity relationship charts for KTU viva questions.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Industrial Office

The following block diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the office and the rest of the plant. It is a **Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** rather than a physical drawing, designed to render reliably across all KTU-compatible markdown viewers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Main Gate and Security Office] --> B1[Reception and Lobby]
    B1 --> C1[Administrative Office]
    B1 --> C2[Sales and Purchase Office]
    C1 --> D1[Plant Manager Cabin]
    C1 --> D2[Accounts and Finance Office]
    C1 --> D3[Personnel and HR Office]
    D1 --> E1[Production Planning and Control Office]
    D1 --> E2[Engineering and R and D Office]
    D1 --> E3[Quality Control Office]
    E1 --> F1[Shop Floor and Production Hall]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2[Stores and Warehouse]
    F1 --> G1[Dispatch and Loading Bay]
    F2 --> G1
    E1 --> H1[Conference and Board Room]
    E2 --> H1
    D2 --> H1
    D3 --> I1[Canteen and Welfare Block]
    F1 --> I1
    F2 --> I1
    C1 --> J1[Records and Archives Room]
    C2 --> J1
    D2 --> J1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Reception** acts as the central hub for visitors.
- The **Plant Manager Cabin** is the apex decision node.
- All other offices (Production, Engineering, QC) report up to the Plant Manager.
- The **Shop Floor**, **Stores**, and **Dispatch** form the operational triad downstream.
- **Canteen, Conference Room, and Archives** are support nodes connected to multiple departments.

---

### 4.2 Typical Office Floor Plan (Schematic Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Reception and Waiting] --> B2[Main Corridor 1.8 m]
    B2 --> C2a[Cabin 1 MD Room]
    B2 --> C2b[Cabin 2 GM Room]
    B2 --> C2c[Cabin 3 HR Manager]
    B2 --> D2[Open Plan Office Area 24 staff]
    D2 --> E2[PPC and Production Office]
    D2 --> F2[Engineering and R and D Office]
    D2 --> G2[Accounts and Finance Office]
    B2 --> H2[Conference Room 30 sq m]
    B2 --> I2[Pantry and Washrooms]
    B2 --> J2[Records and Archives Room]
    B2 --> K2[Server and IT Room]
```

**Key Specifications Embedded in the Schematic:**

| Element | Specification |
|---|---|
| Cabin Size | 2.4 m × 2.4 m module |
| Open Plan Bay | 2.4 m × 2.4 m grid |
| Main Corridor Width | 1.8 m |
| Secondary Corridor Width | 1.2 m |
| Conference Room | Minimum 30 m² |
| Server Room | Air-conditioned, fire-suppressed |
| Washrooms | Separate for gents and ladies |

---

### 4.3 Information Flow Diagram — Office ↔ Shop Floor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Customer Order Received at Sales Office] --> P2[Sales Office sends order to PPC]
    P2 --> P3[PPC prepares production schedule]
    P3 --> P4[PPC issues work order to Shop Floor]
    P4 --> P5[Shop Floor executes production]
    P5 --> P6[Quality Control inspects output]
    P6 --> P7{Defect Found?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Rework and return to Shop Floor]
    P7 -- No --> P9[QC passes material to Stores and Dispatch]
    P8 --> P6
    P9 --> P10[Dispatch ships to customer]
    P10 --> P11[Sales Office sends invoice and updates accounts]
    P11 --> P12[Accounts and Finance records the transaction]
    P5 --> P13[Shop Floor reports daily output to PPC]
    P13 --> P2
```

**Pedagogical Note:**

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram is essentially a **closed-loop feedback control system**, identical in structure to a classical engineering control loop. The **PPC office** is the controller, the **shop floor** is the plant/process, the **QC office** is the sensor, and the **sales/accounts** is the feedback path. This analogy is a **high-scoring point** in KTU viva voce.
