## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank strictly follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. The internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** simulates the actual university question paper where Module 1 carries a compulsory 14-mark question with an internal option.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Define an "Office" in the context of Plant Layout and Design. List any four functions of an industrial office.**

**Model Answer:**

In the context of Plant Layout and Design, an **office** is the centralized administrative and communication hub of an industrial plant that coordinates the activities of all other departments. It serves as the operational nerve center where planning, organizing, directing, and controlling functions of management are executed.

**Four Functions of an Industrial Office:**

1. **Planning and Scheduling** – Forecasting demand, preparing production schedules, and allocating resources.
2. **Coordination** – Harmonizing the efforts of production, stores, dispatch, quality, and marketing departments.
3. **Record Keeping and Documentation** – Maintaining statutory registers, personnel files, and financial accounts.
4. **Communication Interface** – Acting as the link between the plant, customers, vendors, government, and the public.

*(Self-explanatory, diagrammatic answers earn 0.5 bonus marks.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

> **State and briefly explain any three factors that determine the location of the office within a plant layout.**

**Model Answer:**

The location of the office within a plant is determined by the following factors:

1. **Proximity to the Main Gate** – For visitor accessibility, prestige, and ease of public interface. Typically, the reception and administrative office are placed near the entrance.

2. **Proximity to the Production Area** – The PPC and production offices are placed adjacent to the shop floor to minimize communication lag and to allow quick response to production problems.

3. **Distance from Hazardous and Noisy Areas** – Offices must be located away from furnaces, chemical plants, heavy machinery, and dust-generating processes to ensure the health, safety, and comfort of office staff. The office should ideally be on the **windward side** of any pollutant source.

4. **Communication Infrastructure** – Proximity to telephone exchanges, IT server rooms, and the main electrical distribution panel.

5. **Parking and Approach Roads** – Adequate space for two-wheeler and four-wheeler parking must be planned near the office.

---

### PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 1 carries a **14-mark compulsory question** with internal choice. Answer **either (a) OR (b)**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **objectives and functions of an office** in a manufacturing plant. Discuss the role of the office as a **central nervous system** of the organization. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A new administrative office is to be designed for **40 staff members** in a chemical plant. The standard area per person is **10 m²**, and corridors and passages occupy **20%** of the gross area. The office is to be laid out on a **2.4 m × 2.4 m** modular grid. Calculate:
>    (i) The net office area
>    (ii) The gross office area
>    (iii) The number of modular cabin units required
>    (iv) The total length of the main corridor if its width is **2.0 m**
>
> Also suggest **two modern trends** in office design applicable to a chemical plant. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Objectives and Functions of an Office

The office is the **central nervous system** of any industrial enterprise. Just as the human nervous system coordinates the brain, limbs, and sensory organs, the office coordinates the activities of production, stores, dispatch, quality, personnel, finance, and marketing.

**Objectives of an Office:**

1. To provide a **centralized location** for managerial decision-making.
2. To ensure **smooth flow of information** between the plant and the outside world.
3. To **maintain statutory and operational records** as required by law.
4. To **coordinate, control, and direct** the activities of all departments.
5. To provide a **secure, comfortable, and ergonomic** working environment for office staff.

**Primary Functions (POLC Framework):**

- **Planning** – Demand forecasting, capacity planning, and budgeting.
- **Organizing** – Allocation of the 4M's (Men, Machines, Materials, Methods).
- **Leading** – Issuing work orders, instructions, and motivational communication.
- **Controlling** – Comparing actual performance with standards and initiating corrective actions.
- **Coordinating** – Synchronizing inter-departmental activities.

**Secondary Functions:**

- Mail handling, correspondence, telephone and digital communication.
- Filing, indexing, and retrieval of records.
- Maintaining attendance, leave, payroll, and statutory registers.
- Processing purchase orders, sales invoices, and gate passes.
- Hosting meetings, conferences, and management reviews.

**Diagram (Optional, 1 bonus mark):**

```
                ┌──────────────────────┐
                │  PLANT MANAGER (MD)  │  ← Apex decision
                └──────────┬───────────┘
       ┌────────────┬──────┴──────┬────────────┐
       │            │             │            │
   PRODUCTION   ENGINEERING      HR         ACCOUNTS
       │            │             │            │
       └────────────┴──────┬──────┴────────────┘
                          │
                    SHOP FLOOR (Body)
```

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Number of staff, $N = 40$
- Area per person, $A_{per} = 10 \text{ m}^2$
- Corridor overhead, $C_{ovh} = 20\% = 0.20$
- Module size, $2.4 \text{ m} \times 2.4 \text{ m}$
- Corridor width, $W_c = 2.0 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Net Office Area**

$$
A_{net} = N \times A_{per} = 40 \times 10 = 400 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Gross Office Area**

$$
A_{gross} = A_{net} \times (1 + C_{ovh}) = 400 \times 1.20 = 480 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Modular Cabin Units**

$$
A_{module} = 2.4 \times 2.4 = 5.76 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
n_{cabins} = \frac{A_{net}}{A_{module}} = \frac{400}{5.76} = 69.44 \approx 70 \text{ modules}
$$

**Step 4 — Length of Main Corridor**

$$
A_{corridor} = A_{gross} - A_{net} = 480 - 400 = 80 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
L_{corridor} = \frac{A_{corridor}}{W_c} = \frac{80}{2.0} = 40 \text{ m}
$$

**Two Modern Trends in Office Design for a Chemical Plant:**

1. **Paperless / Digital Office** – Use of ERP systems (SAP, Oracle), cloud-based documentation, and digital signatures to reduce paperwork and improve traceability.
2. **Biophilic and Ergonomic Design** – Incorporation of natural light, indoor plants, ergonomic furniture, and acoustic panels to enhance employee well-being and productivity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

> **(a)** Classify the **different types of offices** found in a large manufacturing plant. For each type, state its primary function and the recommended location within the plant layout. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An office room of size **15 m × 10 m × 4 m** (height) is to be illuminated to a level of **500 lux**. Each LED panel provides **4000 lumens**. The utilization factor is **0.6** and the maintenance factor is **0.8**. Determine:
>    (i) The number of LED panels required.
>    (ii) The total wattage consumed if each LED panel is rated **40 W**.
>    (iii) The air changes per hour if three exhaust fans of capacity **1500 m³/h** each are installed.
>
> Comment on whether the ventilation meets the **IS / KTU standard** of 4–6 ACH for offices. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Classification of Offices in a Manufacturing Plant

| S. No. | Type of Office | Primary Function | Recommended Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Administrative / Executive Office** | Strategic decision-making, policy formulation | Near the main gate, on the upper floor |
| 2 | **Production / PPC Office** | Production planning, scheduling, dispatching | Adjacent to the shop floor |
| 3 | **Engineering / R&D Office** | Product design, process improvement, CAD | Near production, isolated from vibration |
| 4 | **Personnel / HR Office** | Recruitment, training, payroll, welfare | Near the canteen and main gate |
| 5 | **Accounts / Finance Office** | Costing, budgeting, billing, taxation | Secure location near the administrative office |
| 6 | **Sales / Purchase Office** | Customer orders, vendor coordination, marketing | Near the main entrance and parking |
| 7 | **Quality Control Office** | Inspection reports, statistical quality data | Adjacent to the QC lab and dispatch bay |
| 8 | **Stores / Inventory Office** | Stock records, GRN, issue notes | Inside or adjoining the warehouse |
| 9 | **Security / Gate Office** | Entry pass, visitor log, vehicle control | At the main gate |
| 10 | **Reception / Lobby** | First point of contact for visitors | At the main entrance, near the waiting lounge |

**Diagrammatic Representation (Optional, 1 bonus mark):**

```
[Main Gate] → [Reception] → [Admin Office]   → [MD Cabin]
                  ↓                ↓               ↓
            [Sales Office]   [HR Office]    [Finance Office]
                                  ↓
                          [Canteen / Welfare]
                                  ↓
                          [Shop Floor]
                                  ↓
                      [QC] → [Stores] → [Dispatch]
```

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Room area, $A = 15 \times 10 = 150 \text{ m}^2$
- Required illumination, $E = 500 \text{ lux}$
- Lumens per LED panel, $\Phi = 4000 \text{ lm}$
- Utilization factor, $\eta = 0.6$
- Maintenance factor, $M = 0.8$
- LED wattage, $P_{LED} = 40 \text{ W}$
- Room height, $h = 4 \text{ m}$
- Number of fans, $n = 3$; each fan capacity, $Q_{fan} = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$

**Step 1 — Number of LED Panels Required**

$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \eta \times M}
$$

$$
N = \frac{500 \times 150}{4000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
N = \frac{75000}{1920}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 39.06 \approx 40 \text{ LED panels}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Wattage Consumed**

$$
P_{total} = N \times P_{LED}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 40 \times 40
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{total} = 1600 \text{ W} = 1.6 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 3 — Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**

Room volume:

$$
V = A \times h = 150 \times 4 = 600 \text{ m}^3
$$

Total fan capacity:

$$
Q_{total} = n \times Q_{fan} = 3 \times 1500 = 4500 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

$$
N_{ach} = \frac{Q_{total}}{V} = \frac{4500}{600}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{ach} = 7.5 \text{ ACH}}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with KTU / IS Standard**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ACH for offices = 4 to 6 ACH.**
> **Calculated ACH = 7.5 ACH.**
>
> The ventilation **exceeds** the upper limit of the standard. While this is not harmful (more fresh air is generally better), it indicates **over-design** and unnecessary energy consumption. **Recommendation:** Reduce to 2 fans (3000 m³/h), giving an ACH of 5.0, which is **exactly at the standard's midpoint** and saves 33% fan energy.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the "Office" Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Office" with "Welfare Block":** Many students describe canteens, rest rooms, and first-aid stations when asked about the office. **Canteen is a welfare facility, NOT an office function** (except HR Office, which manages it).
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "area = 240" without specifying m² or sq ft costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the Diagram:** KTU examiners award up to **1 bonus mark** for a neat labeled diagram in a 7-mark sub-question. Students who skip the diagram routinely lose this mark.
> 4. **Wrong ACH Interpretation:** Writing "more ACH is always better" — this is WRONG. Excessive ACH wastes energy and can cause drafts and noise.
> 5. **Modular Grid Arithmetic:** Using **2.4 m × 3.0 m** instead of **2.4 m × 2.4 m** is a frequent error. Always confirm the **standard modular grid is 2.4 m × 2.4 m** unless specified otherwise.
> 6. **Mixing Up Office Types:** Writing "Production Office handles recruitment" — a clear mark-loser. Know the function of each office type.
> 7. **Ignoring the Factories Act:** KTU questions often test statutory requirements. Always mention compliance with the **Factories Act 1948**, **IS 3646** (lighting), and **IS 3103** (ventilation).

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- 📌 **Definition:** Office = centralized administrative + communication hub of a plant.
- 📌 **Functions:** POLC framework (Planning, Organizing, Leading, Controlling, Coordinating) + secondary support functions.
- 📌 **10 Types of Offices:** Administrative, Production, Engineering, HR, Accounts, Sales, QC, Stores, Security, Reception.
- 📌 **Location Rule #1:** Near main gate (for public interface).
- 📌 **Location Rule #2:** Near shop floor (for production coordination).
- 📌 **Location Rule #3:** Away from hazard/noise, on the windward side.
- 📌 **Standard Area per Person:** **7.0 – 11.5 m²**.
- 📌 **Modular Grid:** **2.4 m × 2.4 m**.
- 📌 **Corridor Width:** Main **1.8 m**, Secondary **1.2 m**.
- 📌 **Ceiling Height:** Minimum **3.0 m**.
- 📌 **Illumination:** **300–500 lux** as per IS 3646.
- 📌 **ACH Standard:** **4–6 ACH** for offices.
- 📌 **Daylight Factor:** Minimum **1.5–2.0%**.
- 📌 **Noise Limit:** Maximum **45 dB(A)**.
- 📌 **Key Formulas:**
    - $A_{net} = N \times A_{per}$
    - $A_{gross} = A_{net} \times (1 + C_{ovh})$
    - $N_{lamps} = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \eta \times M}$
    - $N_{ach} = \dfrac{Q}{V}$
- 📌 **Compliance:** Factories Act 1948, IS 3646, IS 3103, IS 2190 (fire extinguishers).
- 📌 **Modern Trends:** Paperless ERP-based office, biophilic design, hot-desking, agile pods, IoT-enabled smart offices.
- 📌 **Office-to-Production Ratio:** 1:5 to 1:10 depending on industry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Area (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A *Storage Area* is a dedicated, geographically segregated zone within the boundary limits of a process plant where **raw materials, in-process intermediates, finished products, by-products, catalysts, chemicals, and waste materials** are systematically stockpiled under controlled environmental and safety conditions until they are required for downstream consumption, dispatch, or disposal.

In a petroleum/chemical plant, the storage area is typically called a **Tank Farm** when it stores liquids (crude, naphtha, diesel, LPG, etc.) and a **Warehouse** / **Godown** when it stores solids (bagged chemicals, catalysts, finished lubricants in drums).

The storage area is considered one of the **most safety-critical** utilities in plant layout because hydrocarbon inventories represent the largest single concentrated fire/explosion hazard inventory on site.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **human digestive system**. The stomach is essentially a *storage area* where food (raw material) is held temporarily in a controlled manner before being released in measured quantities into the intestines (process units) for conversion into energy (finished product).

- The **stomach** = Storage Tank
- The **food** = Crude oil / feedstock
- The **controlled release** = Pumping station
- The **intestines** = Process unit
- The **size of the stomach** = Storage capacity (designed for 2–3 meals = 15–30 days inventory)

Just as a stomach that is *too small* causes indigestion (process upset, supply starvation), a *too-large* storage area wastes capital, while a *mismanaged* one causes "acid reflux" (vapour emissions, fire hazard).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Constants for KTU Board Examinations (memorize these):**
> - **OISD-118 minimum inter-tank spacing (floating roof) = 0.5 × (D₁ + D₂) or 10 m**, whichever is greater.
> - **OISD-118 minimum inter-tank spacing (fixed roof) = 0.5 × (D₁ + D₂) or 6 m**, whichever is greater.
> - **Dike/bund wall capacity = 110 %** of the largest tank volume within the dyke.
> - **Tank filling efficiency = 85 % to 95 %** (typically **0.9** for design).
> - **Standard storage period = 15 to 30 days** for crude; **5 to 10 days** for finished products.
> - **Fire water demand** for tank farm = **2 hours continuous** supply at design rate.
> - **Dead stock allowance = 10 % to 15 %** of working capacity (for heel / sediment).

---

## 1.4 Role of Storage Area in Plant Layout

| Function | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Buffer between supply and process** | Decouples transport interruptions from plant uptime |
| **Inventory holding** | Allows batch vs. continuous flow mismatch absorption |
| **Quality settling** | Enables water/sediment separation in crude tanks |
| **Blending** | Multiple tanks allow in-line or recirculation blending |
| **Emergency reserve** | Holds strategic reserve for shutdown / power failure scenarios |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Storage Area

### (A) By Physical State of Material

- **Solid storage** — Open yards, covered godowns, silos (for catalysts, coke, sulphur).
- **Liquid storage** — Atmospheric tanks (cone roof, dome roof, floating roof), pressure tanks (spheres, bullets, Horton spheres).
- **Gaseous storage** — High-pressure cylinders, mounded bullets, cryogenic tanks (LNG, LOX, LIN).

### (B) By Function in Plant

- **Raw material storage** — Crude oil, natural gas, feedstock chemicals.
- **Intermediate / in-process storage** — Between two process units (e.g., naphtha between atmospheric and vacuum distillation).
- **Product storage** — Finished fuels, petrochemicals ready for dispatch.
- **Utility storage** — DM water, condensate, lube oil, fuel oil for boilers.
- **Waste / slop storage** — Spent caustic, oily water, slop oil.

### (C) By Hazard Classification (OISD / NFPA zoning)

- **Class A (Flammable liquids, FP < 23 °C)** — Motor spirit, naphtha.
- **Class B (Flammable liquids, FP 23–65 °C)** — Kerosene, diesel.
- **Class C (Flammable liquids, FP 65–93 °C)** — Heavy furnace oil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazardous classification directly drives:** dike sizing, inter-tank spacing, foam pourer design, fire-fighting system capacity, electrical area classification (Zone 0/1/2), and remoteness from control room.

---

## 2.2 Types of Storage Tanks (Detailed)

| Tank Type | Service | Design Pressure | Why Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cone Roof Tank** | Diesel, FO, non-volatile products | Atmospheric | Cheapest for low-vapour-pressure liquids |
| **Dome / Umbrella Roof** | Same as above, larger diameters | Atmospheric | Allows larger diameters than cone roof |
| **External Floating Roof (EFR)** | Crude, gasoline, naphtha | Atmospheric | Eliminates vapour space → reduces evaporative loss (EVL) |
| **Internal Floating Roof (IFR)** | Same, where weather protection needed | Atmospheric | Roof inside a fixed roof shell |
| **Spherical (Horton Sphere)** | LPG, propylene, butane | ~ 5–15 bar (g) | Best volume/pressure efficiency |
| **Bullet / Horizontal Cylinder** | LPG, propane (small volume) | ~ 7–18 bar (g) | Used for smaller inventory |
| **Mounded Bullet** | LPG, ethylene | High | Underground / under-mound for safety |
| **Cryogenic Tank** | LNG, LOX, LIN | Near-atmospheric | Double-wall, perlite vacuum insulation |

---

## 2.3 Design Considerations for Storage Area

1. **Capacity sizing** — Based on turn-around time of supply ships/tankers and dispatch schedule.
2. **Tank diameter and height** — Optimized for minimum cost: optimum H/D ≈ **1.0** for fixed roof, **0.5** for floating roof.
3. **Number of tanks** — Minimum **2 tanks per grade** (so plant never stops when one is in maintenance).
4. **Layout orientation** — Tanks arranged in rows with clear access roads and turn-around for fire tenders.
5. **Dyke walls** — Earthen or RCC, volume = 110 % of largest tank.
6. **Road access** — All-weather road around each dyke (minimum **6 m** wide).
7. **Drainage** — Sloped towards oil-water separator; no storm water enters dyke uncontrolled.
8. **Vapour recovery** — Required for products with Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) > 0.5 bar.
9. **Fire fighting** — Foam pourers, water spray, hydrants at every 30 m.
10. **Spacing from process unit** — As per OISD-118 (Annexure based on tank class).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — questions frequently appear in KTU Part A and Part B.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total storage volume required | $V_{total} = \dfrac{Q \times D_{s}}{\rho}$ | m³ |
| 2 | Working capacity of one tank | $V_{w} = V_{tank} \times \eta_{fill}$ | $\eta_{fill} = 0.9$ |
| 3 | Number of tanks required | $N = \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{w}}$ | Round up to integer |
| 4 | Optimum H/D (fixed roof) | $H/D \approx 1.0$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Optimum H/D (floating roof) | $H/D \approx 0.5$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Tank diameter (cylindrical) | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} H$ | m |
| 7 | Inter-tank spacing (floating roof) | $S_{FR} = \max\!\left(0.5\,(D_1 + D_2),\;10\,m\right)$ | m |
| 8 | Inter-tank spacing (fixed roof) | $S_{FX} = \max\!\left(0.5\,(D_1 + D_2),\;6\,m\right)$ | m |
| 9 | Dyke volume | $V_{dyke} \geq 1.10 \times V_{largest\,tank}$ | m³ |
| 10 | Dyke area (rectangular) | $A_{dyke} = L \times B$ | m² |
| 11 | Effective dyke height | $H_{dyke} = V_{dyke} / A_{dyke}$ | $\leq 1.8\,m$ (OISD) |
| 12 | Tank-to-property-line spacing | $\geq 1.5 \times (D_1 + D_2)$ | m |
| 13 | Tank farm floor area (approx) | $A_{farm} \approx 1.4 \times \sum A_{tank\,footprint}$ | m² |
| 14 | Annual evaporative loss (EFR tank) | $E_{EFR} \approx K_1 \times (P)^{\,0.5}$ | m³/year (API MPMS Ch.19) |
| 15 | Fire water demand | $Q_{fire} = q \times A_{farm}$ | $\geq 190\,L/min$ per tank for 2 hr |

Where:
- $Q$ = mass flow rate of liquid (kg/day or T/day)
- $D_s$ = storage period in days
- $\rho$ = density of stored liquid (kg/m³)
- $D_1,\;D_2$ = diameters of two adjacent tanks (m)
- $\eta_{fill}$ = volumetric fill efficiency (dimensionless)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** Crude storage sized for **15-day** supply to survive a missed VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) arrival.
- **Petrochemical complexes:** Product tanks sized for **7-day** shipping window.
- **LNG terminals:** Cryogenic storage sized for **carrier turnaround** (typically **2 to 3 days**).
- **Offshore platforms:** Storage eliminated → "Storage on demand" philosophy via shuttle tankers, hence no fixed storage area exists.

Storage area is the **single largest land consumer** in a refinery (often **30–40 %** of total plot area). Hence its design directly dictates total plant capital cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Number of Tanks and Total Storage Area

**Given Data (typical KTU problem):**
A refinery consumes **$Q = 4000$ tonnes/day** of crude. Storage period $D_s = 20$ days. Density of crude $\rho = 900\;kg/m^{3}$. Standard tank diameter chosen = **30 m**, tank height = **12 m**. Determine (a) number of tanks required, (b) dyke dimensions, (c) total tank farm area.

---

### Step 1 — Total storage volume required

$$
V_{total} \;=\; \frac{Q \times D_s}{\rho}
$$

> Conversion logic: mass divided by density gives volume; multiply by number of storage days gives total inventory volume.

$$
V_{total} \;=\; \frac{(4000 \times 1000\;\text{kg/day}) \times 20\;\text{days}}{900\;\text{kg/m}^{3}}
$$

$$
V_{total} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000{,}000}{900}
$$

$$
V_{total} \;=\; 88{,}888.89\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Capacity of one tank

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} H
$$

> Conversion logic: cylindrical tank volume = base area × height.

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (30)^2 (12)
$$

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; 0.7854 \times 900 \times 12
$$

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; 8482.30\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Working capacity per tank (with fill efficiency)

$$
V_w \;=\; V_{tank} \times \eta_{fill}
$$

> Conversion logic: tank cannot be filled to 100 % — leave ullage for thermal expansion and safety. KTU accepted value = **0.90**.

$$
V_w \;=\; 8482.30 \times 0.90 \;=\; 7634.07\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Number of tanks required

$$
N \;=\; \frac{V_{total}}{V_w}
$$

> Conversion logic: divide total volume demand by working capacity per tank; round **up** to next integer (you cannot have 0.7 of a tank).

$$
N \;=\; \frac{88{,}888.89}{7634.07} \;=\; 11.64
$$

$$
\boxed{N \;=\; 12 \;\text{tanks (rounded up)}}
$$

**Reserve tank recommendation:** Add **1 standby tank** ⇒ Total **13 tanks**.

---

### Step 5 — Dyke volume calculation

$$
V_{dyke} \;\geq\; 1.10 \times V_{largest\,tank}
$$

$$
V_{dyke} \;\geq\; 1.10 \times 8482.30 \;=\; 9330.53\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Dyke layout

Place 4 tanks per dyke (industry standard). 3 dykes × 4 tanks = 12 working tanks.

For each dyke (4 tanks of $V_{tank} = 8482.30$ m³ each):

$$
V_{dyke\,one} \;\geq\; 1.10 \times 8482.30 \;=\; 9330.53\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

Assume dyke of length $L = 70$ m, width $B = 30$ m:

$$
A_{dyke} \;=\; 70 \times 30 \;=\; 2100\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
H_{dyke} \;=\; \frac{V_{dyke}}{A_{dyke}} \;=\; \frac{9330.53}{2100} \;=\; 4.44\;\text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** OISD-118 caps dyke effective height at **1.8 m**. Therefore the dyke must be **enlarged in footprint**, not height. Recompute:

$$
L \times B \;\geq\; \frac{9330.53}{1.8} \;=\; 5183.6\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

Adopt $L = 90$ m, $B = 60$ m ⇒ $A_{dyke} = 5400\;\text{m}^{2}$.

$$
\boxed{H_{dyke\,effective} \;=\; \frac{9330.53}{5400} \;=\; 1.728\;\text{m} \;\leq\; 1.8\;\text{m}\;\; \checkmark}
$$

---

### Step 7 — Inter-tank spacing check (floating roof assumed)

$$
S_{FR} \;=\; \max\!\left[0.5\,(D_1 + D_2),\;10\;\text{m}\right]
$$

For identical tanks of D = 30 m:

$$
0.5 \times (30 + 30) \;=\; 30\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{FR} \;=\; 30\;\text{m}\;\;\text{(centre-to-centre shell spacing)}}
$$

> KTU note: this is **shell-to-shell** spacing between tank walls. The **centre-to-centre** spacing is $30 + 30 = 60$ m. Always clarify in your answer.

---

### Step 8 — Total tank farm footprint

$$
A_{farm} \;\approx\; 1.4 \times N \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}
$$

> Conversion logic: the 1.4 multiplier accounts for spacing between tanks, access roads, dyke walls, and pump islands.

$$
A_{farm} \;=\; 1.4 \times 12 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (30)^2
$$

$$
A_{farm} \;=\; 1.4 \times 12 \times 706.86
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{farm} \;\approx\; 11{,}875\;\text{m}^{2} \;\approx\; 1.19\;\text{hectares}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Storage Options

| Criterion | Fixed Roof Tank | Floating Roof Tank | Spherical Tank | Mounded Bullet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure rating** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | 5–15 bar | 7–18 bar |
| **Service** | Diesel, FO, lube | Crude, gasoline, naphtha | LPG, propylene | LPG, ethylene |
| **Evaporative loss** | High | Very low (~ 90 % reduction) | Low | Very low |
| **Capital cost (per m³)** | Lowest | Medium | High | Highest |
| **Plot area / m³ stored** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | Low |
| **Safety risk** | High (vapour space) | Medium | Medium | Lowest (buried) |
| **Maintenance access** | Easy | Roof inspection difficult | Difficult | Most difficult |

---

## 3.3 KTU Logical Reasoning — Why Two Tanks Per Grade Minimum

- If only **1 tank** exists for crude, then when it is taken out for inspection/cleaning, the entire plant shuts down.
- **2 tanks** allows one in service while the other is down.
- **Multiple tanks** (3+) allows blending of two crude grades to meet specification (e.g., API gravity).
- Rule of thumb: **N_min = 2**; recommended **N = (Throughput days / Turnaround days) + 1**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Storage Area

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[STORAGE AREA IN PLANT LAYOUT] --> rootB[BY PHYSICAL STATE]
    rootA --> rootC[BY FUNCTION]
    rootA --> rootD[BY HAZARD CLASS]

    rootB --> rootB1[SOLID STORAGE]
    rootB --> rootB2[LIQUID STORAGE]
    rootB --> rootB3[GASEOUS STORAGE]

    rootB1 --> rootB1a[Open Yard]
    rootB1 --> rootB1b[Covered Godown]
    rootB1 --> rootB1c[Silo]

    rootB2 --> rootB2a[Fixed Roof Tank]
    rootB2 --> rootB2b[Floating Roof Tank]
    rootB2 --> rootB2c[Pressure Spherical Tank]
    rootB2 --> rootB2d[Bullet Tank]

    rootB3 --> rootB3a[High Pressure Cylinder]
    rootB3 --> rootB3b[Cryogenic Tank]

    rootC --> rootC1[Raw Material Storage]
    rootC --> rootC2[Intermediate Storage]
    rootC --> rootC3[Product Storage]
    rootC --> rootC4[Utility Storage]
    rootC --> rootC5[Waste Storage]

    rootD --> rootD1[Class A FP below 23 C]
    rootD --> rootD2[Class B FP 23 to 65 C]
    rootD --> rootD3[Class C FP 65 to 93 C]
```

## 4.2 Tank Farm Block-Level Layout Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    jettyA[Marine Jetty or Pipeline Receipt] --> manifoldA[Receipt Manifold]
    manifoldA --> tankA1[Crude Tank A1]
    manifoldA --> tankA2[Crude Tank A2]
    manifoldA --> tankA3[Crude Tank A3]
    manifoldA --> tankA4[Crude Tank A4]

    tankA1 --> dykeA[Dyke A 5400 sqm]
    tankA2 --> dykeA
    tankA3 --> dykeA
    tankA4 --> dykeA

    dykeA --> pumpA1[Crude Charge Pump]
    pumpA1 --> processA[CDU Process Unit]

    processA --> tankB1[Naphtha Tank B1]
    processA --> tankB2[Kerosene Tank B2]
    processA --> tankB3[Diesel Tank B3]

    tankB1 --> dykeB[Dyke B]
    tankB2 --> dykeB
    tankB3 --> dykeB

    dykeB --> dispatchA[Tanker Loading Gantry]

    processA --> sphereA[LPG Horton Sphere]
    sphereA --> dispatchB[Bullet Truck Loading]

    fwSys[Fire Water System] --> dykeA
    fwSys --> dykeB
    fwSys --> sphereA

    owsA[Oil Water Separator] --> dykeA
    owsA --> dykeB
```

## 4.3 Storage Area Material Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    inNode1[Inbound Feedstock] --> nodeFilter1[Receipt Strainer]
    nodeFilter1 --> nodeMeter1[Flow Meter]
    nodeMeter1 --> nodeTank[Storage Tank]
    nodeTank --> nodePump[Transfer Pump]
    nodePump --> nodeProc[Process Unit]
    nodeProc --> nodeProdTank[Product Storage Tank]
    nodeProdTank --> nodeLoad[Loading Gantry]
    nodeLoad --> outNode1[Outbound Dispatch]
    nodeTank --> nodeVap[Vapour Recovery Unit]
    nodeVap --> nodeTank
    nodeTank --> nodeFW[Fire Water Network]
    nodeTank --> nodeDrain[Dyke Drain to OWS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block represents a physical functional unit. Arrows show the direction of material/utility flow. The Storage Tank is the central hub, with three connections — **inbound** (feedstock), **outbound** (to process) and **safety/environmental** (vapour recovery, fire water, drainage).

## 4.4 Single Tank Cross-Section Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    tankShell[Tank Shell] --> roofSys[Roof System]
    tankShell --> bottomPlate[Tank Bottom Plate]
    bottomPlate --> foundationR[Ring Wall Foundation]
    tankShell --> nozzleIn[Inlet Nozzle]
    tankShell --> nozzleOut[Outlet Nozzle]
    tankShell --> nozzleVent[Vent Nozzle]
    tankShell --> nozzleMix[Mixer Nozzle]
    roofSys --> nozzleRoof[Roof Drain]
    tankShell --> ladderSys[External Ladder with Cage]
    tankShell --> fwSpray[Fire Water Spray Ring]
    tankShell --> foamPour[Foam Pourer]
    bottomPlate --> drainNoz[Drain Nozzle to OWS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define "Storage Area" as applied to a process plant. List any four types of storage tanks used in petroleum refineries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — board-validated keyword density):**
A storage area is a dedicated zone within the plant boundary used for the **systematic stockpiling** of raw materials, intermediates, finished products, by-products and waste materials under controlled safety and environmental conditions.

Four types of storage tanks used in petroleum refineries:

1. **Fixed roof (cone/dome roof) tank** — for non-volatile products like diesel and fuel oil.
2. **External floating roof tank** — for crude oil and gasoline to minimize evaporative loss.
3. **Spherical (Horton sphere)** — for LPG and light hydrocarbons under pressure.
4. **Bullet / horizontal cylindrical tank** — for smaller LPG inventories.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State OISD-118 minimum inter-tank spacing for (a) floating roof tanks and (b) fixed roof tanks. Why is this spacing essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(a) Floating roof tanks:** Spacing = $\max[0.5\,(D_1 + D_2),\;10\;m]$
- **(b) Fixed roof tanks:** Spacing = $\max[0.5\,(D_1 + D_2),\;6\;m]$

**Why spacing is essential:**
The spacing allows **fire-fighting access** by mobile tenders, prevents **radiation heat transfer** between tanks during a pool fire, and provides **safe evacuation route** for personnel during emergencies. Without adequate spacing, a single tank fire can escalate into a cascading tank farm catastrophe (e.g., Jaipur 2009, Buncefield 2005).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

A refinery processes **5000 tonnes/day** of crude oil. Crude is received by pipeline and stored for **25 days**. Density of crude = **880 kg/m³**. Design a suitable storage facility using **fixed roof tanks of diameter 32 m and height 14 m**.

#### (a) Calculate the number of tanks required and recommend standby. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total volume required:**

$$
V_{total} = \frac{Q \times D_s}{\rho} = \frac{5000 \times 1000 \times 25}{880} = 142{,}045.45\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume per tank:**

$$
V_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} H = \frac{\pi}{4} (32)^2 (14) = 11{,}255.36\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Working capacity per tank (η = 0.90):**

$$
V_w = 11{,}255.36 \times 0.90 = 10{,}129.82\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of tanks:**

$$
N = \frac{142{,}045.45}{10{,}129.82} = 14.02 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 15\;\text{tanks (round up)}
$$

**Step 5 — Standby recommendation:**

Add **2 standby tanks** (one for cleaning, one for blending alternate crude grade).

$$
\boxed{\text{Total tanks} = 15 + 2 = 17}
$$

---

#### (b) Design the dyke system and compute total tank farm footprint. (7 marks)

**Step 6 — Dyke grouping:** 4 tanks per dyke ⇒ ⌈15/4⌉ = **4 dykes** (3 dykes × 4 + 1 dyke × 3 = 15 tanks).

**Step 7 — Dyke volume per dyke (for 4-tank dyke):**

$$
V_{dyke} \geq 1.10 \times V_{tank} = 1.10 \times 11{,}255.36 = 12{,}380.90\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 8 — Dyke footprint (H_dyke ≤ 1.8 m):**

$$
A_{dyke} \geq \frac{12{,}380.90}{1.8} = 6878.28\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

Adopt $L = 100$ m, $B = 70$ m ⇒ $A_{dyke} = 7000\;\text{m}^{2}$.

$$
H_{effective} = \frac{12{,}380.90}{7000} = 1.769\;\text{m}\;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 9 — Inter-tank spacing (fixed roof):**

$$
S_{FX} = \max[0.5\,(32+32),\;6] = \max[32,\;6] = 32\;\text{m (shell-to-shell)}
$$

**Step 10 — Tank farm total footprint:**

$$
A_{farm} = 1.4 \times 15 \times \frac{\pi}{4} (32)^2 = 1.4 \times 15 \times 804.25 = 16{,}889.25\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{farm} \approx 1.69\;\text{hectares}}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the classification of storage tanks based on **roof type** and **pressure rating**. Discuss the criteria for selecting a suitable tank for storing **(i) crude oil** and **(ii) LPG**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A petrochemical plant stores **30,000 m³** of naphtha. Using floating roof tanks of diameter **40 m** and height **16 m**, determine the number of tanks required. Also calculate the **fire water demand** if design rate is **12 L/min per m² of tank footprint** for **2 hours**. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-B (a):

**Classification:**

| By Roof | By Pressure |
|---|---|
| Cone roof (small dia, < 20 m) | Atmospheric (P < 0.5 bar g) |
| Dome / umbrella roof | Low pressure (0.5–2 bar g) |
| External floating roof (EFR) | Medium pressure (2–10 bar g) |
| Internal floating roof (IFR) | High pressure (> 10 bar g) |

**(i) Crude oil selection:** Crude has **high vapour pressure** and large inventory volume ⇒ **External Floating Roof (EFR) tank** is selected because:
- Eliminates vapour space ⇒ ~ 90 % reduction in evaporative loss.
- Suitable for very large diameters (40–100 m).
- Atmospheric design ⇒ cheapest per m³ stored.

**(ii) LPG selection:** LPG has very **high vapour pressure at ambient** (≈ 6 bar g for propane) ⇒ **Horton Spherical tank** is selected because:
- Sphere has minimum surface area per unit volume ⇒ minimum wall material per m³ stored.
- Even stress distribution under pressure.
- Optimized H/D ≈ 1.0 ⇒ uniform pressure.

---

#### Solution to Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Volume per tank:**

$$
V_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4}(40)^2 (16) = 20{,}106.19\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Working capacity (η = 0.90):**

$$
V_w = 20{,}106.19 \times 0.90 = 18{,}095.57\;\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of tanks:**

$$
N = \frac{30{,}000}{18{,}095.57} = 1.66 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 2\;\text{tanks}
$$

**Step 4 — Tank footprint per tank:**

$$
A_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4} (40)^2 = 1256.64\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Fire water demand per tank:**

$$
q_{tank} = 12 \times 1256.64 = 15{,}079.64\;\text{L/min}
$$

**Step 6 — Total demand for 2 tanks, 2 hours:**

$$
Q_{total} = 2 \times 15{,}079.64 \times 60 \times 2
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 2 \times 15{,}079.64 \times 120 = 3{,}619{,}114\;\text{L} \approx 3619\;\text{kL}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 3.62 \times 10^{6}\;\text{L of fire water for 2 hr operation}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing shell-to-shell vs. centre-to-centre spacing** — always clarify which one you compute. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting dyke height cap (1.8 m)** — examiners will deduct marks if $H_{dyke}$ exceeds 1.8 m and you haven't recomputed footprint. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Not rounding up tank count** — fractional tanks are not buildable; always **N = ⌈x⌉ + standby**. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up classes (Class A/B/C)** — must quote flash point range correctly. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping η = 0.9 fill efficiency** — direct use of geometric volume is a frequent error. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting the 1.4 footprint multiplier** for roads and access in tank farm area calculation. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Storage Area" (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
>
> - ✅ Storage area = largest land consumer in a refinery (~ 30–40 % of plot area).
> - ✅ Types by state — Solid / Liquid / Gas; by function — Raw / Intermediate / Product / Utility / Waste.
> - ✅ Tank selection rule — **FP < 23 °C** → floating roof; **FP 23–65 °C** → fixed roof; **pressurized LPG** → spherical.
> - ✅ Minimum **2 tanks per grade**; recommended standby = 1 cleaning + 1 blending.
> - ✅ Standard fill efficiency $\eta_{fill} = 0.9$ (memorize this number).
> - ✅ Optimum H/D: fixed roof ≈ 1.0; floating roof ≈ 0.5; spherical ≈ 1.0.
> - ✅ OISD-118 spacing: floating roof = $\max[0.5(D_1+D_2),\;10\;m]$; fixed roof = $\max[0.5(D_1+D_2),\;6\;m]$.
> - ✅ Dyke volume = **110 %** of largest tank within the dyke.
> - ✅ Dyke effective height ≤ **1.8 m** (extend footprint, never height).
> - ✅ Fire water demand = design rate × tank footprint, for **2 hours continuous**.
> - ✅ Tank farm footprint ≈ **1.4 × Σ (tank base areas)** to include roads, dykes and pump islands.
> - ✅ Vapour recovery unit (VRU) mandatory for products with RVP > 0.5 bar.
> - ✅ Drainage from dyke must slope toward **oil-water separator (OWS)** — never directly to storm drain.
> - ✅ Storage period: 15–30 days for crude; 5–10 days for finished products (used as default if KTU problem doesn't specify).
> - ✅ Key standards to mention in answers: **OISD-118**, **NFPA-30**, **API-650**, **API-620**, **IS-803**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consideration of Working People
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Consideration of Working People

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Consideration of Working People** in Plant Layout and Design refers to the systematic integration of human factors, ergonomics, environmental controls, welfare amenities, and safety provisions into the physical and operational design of an industrial facility. It is the engineering discipline that ensures the **plant serves the worker** — not the other way around — by aligning machinery, workflow, building dimensions, and ambient conditions with human physiological, psychological, and social needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The consideration of working people encompasses all design decisions that affect human comfort, efficiency, health, safety, and morale within a manufacturing or process plant. It is a multi-disciplinary function combining **Ergonomics**, **Industrial Hygiene**, **Human Factors Engineering**, and **Welfare Engineering**.

### 1.2 Core Sub-Domains (Per KTU PEPET634 Module 1)

| Sub-Domain | Focus Area | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Ergonomics** | Human-machine interface | Workstation, tool, control design |
| **Anthropometry** | Body dimensions | Aisle width, bench height |
| **Illumination Engineering** | Visual environment | Lux levels, glare control |
| **Ventilation & HVAC** | Air quality | ACH, fresh air supply |
| **Noise & Vibration Control** | Acoustic environment | dB limits, isolation |
| **Welfare Facilities** | Human needs | Canteen, rest rooms, lockers |
| **Safety Engineering** | Hazard mitigation | Exits, PPE, signage |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cockpit Analogy" — Making It Simple**
> Imagine designing an airplane cockpit. You wouldn't place the throttle at floor level, the altimeter behind the pilot, or the oxygen mask in the co-pilot's seat — even if those positions were "efficient" for the aircraft structure. The cockpit is built **around the pilot's body, vision, and reach**.
> A **plant is a giant cockpit** for thousands of workers. The machines, conveyors, aisles, control panels, lighting, ventilation ducts, and welfare blocks must all be sized, positioned, and conditioned so that **human beings** — with their fixed body dimensions, sensory limits, fatigue cycles, and social needs — can operate them safely and productively for 8 hours a day, 5–6 days a week, for **30+ years**.

### 1.4 Why It Matters — The Human Cost of Bad Design

A plant that ignores working people suffers from:

- ⬇️ **Lower productivity** (estimated 10–25% loss in poorly designed plants)
- ⬆️ **Higher absenteeism and turnover**
- ⬆️ **More accidents and occupational diseases**
- ⬆️ **Higher insurance and compensation costs**
- ⬇️ **Lower product quality** (fatigue-induced errors)
- ⬇️ **Poor morale and industrial relations**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Under the **Factories Act 1948 (India)** and **ILO Conventions**, consideration of working people is not optional — it is a **legal obligation**. The KTU examiner expects you to cite at least one statutory requirement in long-answer questions.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard reference values are used throughout this topic:

- **Standard human eye height (standing):** **1.65 m** (95th percentile Indian male ≈ 1.72 m)
- **Standard reach envelope:** **600 mm** (functional) to **900 mm** (maximum)
- **Standard aisle width (one-way):** **1.0 m minimum**
- **Standard aisle width (two-way):** **1.8 m minimum**
- **Standard illumination (general assembly):** **300 lux**
- **Standard illumination (fine bench work):** **500–750 lux**
- **Standard noise limit (8-hour exposure):** **85 dB(A)** (OSHA / Indian Factories Rules)
- **Standard fresh air supply:** **30 m³/hr/person** (minimum, per IS 3103 / ASHRAE 62.1)
- **Standard thermal comfort range:** **20–27 °C** with **30–70% RH**
- **Standard Anthropometric Reference:** **95th–5th percentile** design rule

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ergonomic Triad — The Foundation Concept

Ergonomics in plant layout operates on three interlocking principles:

1. **Fit the Job to the Worker** (not the worker to the job)
2. **Accommodate Human Variability** (5th–95th percentile design)
3. **Optimize Human-System Performance** (safety × productivity × comfort)

---

## 2.2 Seven Pillars of Working-People Consideration

### 🔹 Pillar 1 — Anthropometry & Workstation Geometry

**Concept:** The science of body dimensions. Plant dimensions (bench height, reach, clearance) must be derived from a population percentile — typically the **5th percentile female to 95th percentile male** range.

**Why it matters:** A bench that fits the "average" worker fits **no one** (averages don't exist in reality — you are either small or large).

**Design rule:**
$$\text{Adjustable Range} = P_{95} - P_{5} \quad \text{(accommodates 90\% of population)}$$

If adjustability is not feasible, design for the **dominant user group** (e.g., 90th percentile male for heavy industry).

---

### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Illumination (Lighting)

**Concept:** Adequate, glare-free, uniform lighting is essential for visual tasks, safety, and reduced eye fatigue.

**Key metric:**
$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad \text{(illuminance in lux)}$$

where:
- $E$ = illuminance (lux, $\text{lx}$)
- $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lumens, $\text{lm}$)
- $A$ = illuminated area ($\text{m}^2$)

**KTU Standard Illumination Table (must memorize):**

| Task / Area | Recommended Illumination (lux) |
|---|---|
| Warehouses, corridors | 100 – 150 |
| Rough bench & machine work | 200 – 300 |
| General assembly | 300 – 500 |
| Fine bench work, inspection | 500 – 1000 |
|精密 (precision) instrument assembly | 1000 – 2000 |
| Electronics, watchmaking | 2000 – 5000 |

---

### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Ventilation & Air Quality

**Concept:** Supply of fresh air and removal of contaminants, heat, and humidity.

**Two governing metrics:**

**Metric 1: Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**
$$\text{ACH} = \frac{\text{Volumetric flow rate}}{\text{Room volume}} = \frac{Q \times 60}{V}$$

where:
- $Q$ = flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $V$ = room volume in $\text{m}^3$

**Metric 2: Per-person fresh air supply** — **30 m³/hr/person** minimum for general industry.

**Recommended ACH values:**

| Area Type | ACH Required |
|---|---|
| General factory (no contaminants) | 4 – 6 |
| Welding shop | 10 – 15 |
| Paint shop | 15 – 25 |
| Battery charging | 20 – 30 |
| Foundry | 25 – 40 |

---

### 🔹 Pillar 4 — Temperature & Humidity

**Concept:** Human thermal comfort zone.

**Effective Temperature (ET) / Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT)** index is used for hot industries.

For most factories:
$$\text{Optimal} = 20\text{ °C} \text{ to } 27\text{ °C} \text{ at } 30\% \text{ to } 70\% \text{ RH}$$

Beyond this:
- Productivity drops **2% per °C** above 27 °C in sedentary work
- Heat stress (WBGT > 30 °C) requires mandatory rest cycles

---

### 🔹 Pillar 5 — Noise & Vibration

**Concept:** Sustained exposure to high noise causes permanent hearing loss (NIHL).

**Sound Pressure Level formula:**
$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where:
- $L_p$ = sound pressure level (dB)
- $P$ = measured RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- $P_0$ = reference pressure $= 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$

**Combined noise from multiple sources:**
$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_i/10}\right)$$

**Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL):** **85 dB(A) for 8 hours**, with **+5 dB halving exposure time** (exchange rate).

---

### 🔹 Pillar 6 — Welfare Facilities (Statutory under Factories Act 1948)

**Concept:** Facilities that maintain worker health, hygiene, and morale.

**Mandatory provisions:**

| Facility | Statutory Standard |
|---|---|
| Drinking water | 1 tap per 100 workers, within 50 m |
| Washing facilities | 1 per 30 workers (1 per 25 for women) |
| Toilets (male) | 1 per 25 workers |
| Toilets (female) | 1 per 15 workers |
| Canteen | Required if > 250 workers |
| First-aid box | 1 per 150 workers |
| Crèche | Required if > 30 women workers |
| Rest rooms | Required for > 150 workers |
| Lunch room | Required for > 50 workers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Factories Act mandates these. Examiners love asking for the **minimum statutory numbers**. Memorize the table above.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 7 — Safety Engineering

**Concept:** Engineering controls that prevent accidents.

**Key design rules:**

- **Emergency exit width:** minimum **1.5 m**, one per 50 m travel distance
- **Exit capacity:** **60 persons per metre of exit width**
- **Fire extinguisher spacing:** **15 m maximum travel distance** to nearest extinguisher
- **PPE storage:** within **10 m** of every work zone
- **Machine guarding:** all rotating shafts, belts, gears — mandatory

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance | $E = \Phi / A$ | lx | $1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$ |
| 2 | Lamp count | $N = (E \times A) / (\Phi \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF})$ | — | UF = Utilization Factor (0.4–0.7), MF = Maintenance Factor (0.7–0.8) |
| 3 | ACH | $\text{ACH} = (Q \times 60) / V$ | hr$^{-1}$ | Q in m³/s, V in m³ |
| 4 | Fresh air/person | $V_a \geq 30$ | m³/hr/person | Per IS 3103 |
| 5 | Sound pressure level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ | dB | $P_0 = 20 \mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | Combined SPL | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\sum 10^{L_i/10}$ | dB | Use when adding noise sources |
| 7 | Anthropometric adjustment | Range $= P_{95} - P_{5}$ | m or mm | For 90% population fit |
| 8 | Exit capacity | $C = 60 \times W$ | persons | W = exit width in metres |
| 9 | Heat index (approx) | $HI = T + 0.5 \times RH$ | °C | Quick comfort check |
| 10 | Noise dose | $D = 100 \times \sum (C_i / T_i)$ | % | OSHA criterion |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Consideration |
|---|---|
| **Electronics / PCB assembly** | High illumination (1000–2000 lux), ESD flooring, microscopes |
| **Foundries / Steel** | Heat stress management, ventilation (ACH 25–40), radiant heat shields |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Cleanrooms (HEPA, ACH 600+), positive pressure, gowning rooms |
| **Textile** | Humidity control (50–80% RH) for yarn strength, dust control |
| **Automotive assembly** | Ergonomic jigs, torque-tool support, conveyor height matching |
| **Chemical plants** | Toxic gas monitoring, emergency showers, SCBA storage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Number of Lamps Required for a Work Area

**Problem Statement:** A workshop hall of dimensions **20 m × 10 m × 4 m (height)** requires an illumination level of **300 lux**. LED panels of **5000 lumens** each are used. The utilization factor (UF) is **0.55** and maintenance factor (MF) is **0.75**. Determine the number of lamps required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the required inputs.**
- Floor area: $A = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$
- Required illuminance: $E = 300 \text{ lx}$
- Luminous flux per lamp: $\Phi_L = 5000 \text{ lm}$
- Utilization factor: $\text{UF} = 0.55$
- Maintenance factor: $\text{MF} = 0.75$

**Step 2 — Write the governing equation.**

The total luminous flux that reaches the working plane equals the product of lamp output, number of lamps, and the two efficiency factors:

$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = N \times \Phi_L \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}$$

The illuminance is the total useful flux divided by the area:

$$E = \frac{\Phi_{\text{total}}}{A} = \frac{N \times \Phi_L \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for N.**

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_L \times \text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$N = \frac{300 \times 200}{5000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$5000 \times 0.55 = 2750$$

$$2750 \times 0.75 = 2062.5$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$300 \times 200 = 60{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Compute N.**

$$N = \frac{60{,}000}{2062.5} = 29.09$$

**Step 8 — Round up to the next whole lamp (you cannot install 0.09 of a lamp).**

$$\boxed{N = 30 \text{ lamps}}$$

**Step 9 — Check actual achieved illuminance.**

$$E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{30 \times 5000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75}{200} = 309.4 \text{ lx}$$

This is **3.1% above design** — well within tolerance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Air Changes per Hour and Fan Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A welding shop has dimensions **15 m × 8 m × 5 m**. The design ACH is **15**. Determine:
1. The required volumetric flow rate in m³/hr
2. The required flow rate in m³/s
3. The number of exhaust fans (each rated **3.0 m³/s**) needed

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute room volume.**

$$V = L \times W \times H = 15 \times 8 \times 5 = 600 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ACH equation.**

$$Q \times 60 = \text{ACH} \times V$$

where $Q$ is in m³/s. Solve for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$Q = \frac{15 \times 600}{60} = \frac{9000}{60} = 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Wait — units check. ACH is in hr⁻¹, V in m³, so $Q$ would be in m³/hr directly. Let me redo carefully.

**Correct unit analysis:**

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q \text{ (m}^3/\text{hr)}}{V \text{ (m}^3)}$$

So:

$$Q = \text{ACH} \times V = 15 \times 600 = 9000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m³/s.**

$$Q = \frac{9000}{3600} = 2.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Number of fans required.**

Each fan delivers 3.0 m³/s, so:

$$N_{\text{fans}} = \frac{Q_{\text{required}}}{Q_{\text{fan}}} = \frac{2.5}{3.0} = 0.833$$

**Step 6 — Round up.**

$$\boxed{N_{\text{fans}} = 1 \text{ exhaust fan (3 m}^3/\text{s)}}$$

However, with redundancy, **2 fans** are recommended (one standby).

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Combined Noise from Two Machines

**Problem Statement:** Machine A produces **88 dB(A)** and Machine B produces **85 dB(A)** at a worker's position. Compute the combined sound pressure level.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert each dB level to linear intensity ratio.**

For Machine A:
$$10^{L_A/10} = 10^{88/10} = 10^{8.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{8}$$

For Machine B:
$$10^{L_B/10} = 10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the linear ratios.**

$$S = 6.31 \times 10^{8} + 3.16 \times 10^{8} = 9.47 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to dB.**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(9.47 \times 10^{8})$$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times [\log_{10}(9.47) + 8]$$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times [0.976 + 8] = 10 \times 8.976$$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 89.76 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation.**

$$\boxed{L_{\text{total}} \approx 89.8 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

This is **above the 85 dB(A) PEL** — workers here require **hearing protection** (earplugs reduce by 20–30 dB; earmuffs by 25–35 dB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Two machines of 88 and 85 dB do **not** add to 173 dB. They add to only **~90 dB** — because decibels are **logarithmic**. This is a frequent KTU question trap.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Adjustable Workstation Range from Anthropometry

**Problem Statement:** A workstation bench height must accommodate **5th percentile Indian female (P₅ = 1.40 m)** and **95th percentile Indian male (P₉₅ = 1.80 m)** elbow heights. The elbow height is approximately **0.60 × standing height**.

Determine the adjustment range.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute 5th percentile elbow height.**

$$E_{5} = 0.60 \times 1.40 = 0.840 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute 95th percentile elbow height.**

$$E_{95} = 0.60 \times 1.80 = 1.080 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Adjustment range.**

$$\Delta E = E_{95} - E_{5} = 1.080 - 0.840 = 0.240 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Bench adjustment range} = 240 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Design recommendation.**

Provide a hydraulic or screw-driven bench adjustable from **840 mm to 1080 mm**, accommodating 90% of the workforce.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for KTU Problems

| Given Scenario | Apply Formula | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|
| Workshop area + lux level + lamp lumens | $N = EA / (\Phi \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF})$ | Number of lamps |
| Room volume + ACH | $Q = \text{ACH} \times V$ | m³/hr of fresh air |
| Fan rating | $N_f = Q_{\text{req}} / Q_{\text{fan}}$ | Number of fans |
| Sound level of multiple machines | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log \sum 10^{L_i/10}$ | Combined SPL in dB |
| 5th & 95th percentile heights | Range $= P_{95} - P_{5}$ | Adjustment range in mm |
| Workers & exit width | Persons $= 60 \times W$ | Exit capacity |
| Workers in canteen area | $A = 1 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$ (sitting) | Canteen floor area |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Concept Map — The 7 Pillars

This Mermaid diagram maps the complete consideration framework for working people in plant layout.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CONSIDERATION OF WORKING PEOPLE] --> B[ERGENOMICS AND ANTHROPOMETRY]
    A --> C[ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROLS]
    A --> D[WELFARE FACILITIES]
    A --> E[SAFETY ENGINEERING]

    B --> B3[Adjustability Range]

    C --> C4[Noise and Vibration]

    D --> D2[Washrooms and Toilets]
```
