# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define ergonomics and explain its significance in plant layout design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Ergonomics (or Human Factors Engineering) is the scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system. It applies **theories, principles, data, and methods to design** in order to optimize human well-being and overall system performance.

**Significance in plant layout (1.5 marks):**
In plant layout, ergonomics ensures that:
- Workstations, controls, and tools fit the **physical dimensions** (anthropometry) of operators
- Tasks are designed to minimize **fatigue, strain, and repetitive injury**
- The layout supports **natural movement patterns**, reducing travel time and effort
- Productivity, safety, and worker comfort are simultaneously optimized

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "ergonomics means comfort" — this is **too vague** for KTU. You must mention **anthropometry, fatigue, and the human-machine fit** to score full marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six statutory welfare facilities that must be provided in a factory as per the Indian Factories Act, 1948.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Drinking water** — sufficient and accessible, with at least one tap per 100 workers within 50 m
2. **Washing facilities** — at least one per 30 workers (one per 25 for women)
3. **Toilet / latrine** — separate for male (1 per 25) and female (1 per 15)
4. **Canteen** — mandatory if more than 250 workers are employed
5. **First-aid boxes** — one per 150 workers, readily accessible
6. **Rest rooms / lunch rooms** — required when more than 150 workers are on shift
7. **Crèche** — required if more than 30 women workers are employed

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write generic answers like "good facilities." Quote **specific numbers** from the Factories Act — examiners reward statutory accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**"The human element is the most critical variable in plant layout." Discuss this statement by covering the following:**

**(a) Explain the principles of ergonomics and their application in designing workstations, aisles, and material handling systems in a manufacturing plant. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the environmental factors — lighting, ventilation, and noise — that must be engineered for worker comfort, productivity, and statutory compliance. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Definition of ergonomics (1 mark):**
Ergonomics is the science of fitting the job to the worker. It considers human capabilities, limitations, and variability in designing equipment, workspaces, and processes.

**2. Core principles (2 marks):**
- **Anthropometric fit:** Workstation dimensions must accommodate the 5th–95th percentile population.
- **Postural comfort:** Work heights should allow neutral posture (elbows at 90°, wrists straight).
- **Force minimization:** Tools should require less than 20% of worker's maximum strength for repetitive tasks.
- **Reach zones:** Primary controls within 400 mm; secondary within 600 mm; maximum reach 900 mm.

**3. Workstation design application (1.5 marks):**
- Bench height = **elbow height** of user ± 50–100 mm
- Adjustable benches for 5th–95th percentile accommodation
- Footrests, anti-fatigue mats for standing jobs

**4. Aisle design application (1 mark):**
- Pedestrian aisle: **1.0 m minimum** (one-way); **1.8 m** (two-way)
- Pallet aisle: **2.5–3.0 m**
- Truck aisle: **3.5–4.0 m**
- Yellow floor marking for traffic segregation

**5. Material handling application (1.5 marks):**
- Material height limit: **1.6 m** for manual lifting
- Maximum manual lift: **25 kg** for males, **15 kg** for females (per Indian rules)
- Use of conveyors, forklifts, AGVs for heavy/bulk items
- Frequent lifts should be between **knuckle and shoulder height** for optimum biomechanics

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**1. Lighting (2.5 marks):**
- Recommended levels: 100 lux (storage) → 500 lux (fine work) → 2000+ lux (precision electronics)
- Formula: $E = \Phi / A$; number of lamps $N = EA / (\Phi \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF})$
- Design for **uniformity ratio** of 0.7 or better
- Minimize glare (use diffusers, matte finishes, indirect lighting)
- Emergency lighting: minimum 1 lux at floor level for safe egress

**2. Ventilation (2.5 marks):**
- Two methods: **natural** (windows, roof ventilators) and **mechanical** (exhaust fans, HVAC)
- Required ACH: 4–6 (general), 15–25 (paint shop), 25–40 (foundry)
- Per-person fresh air: **30 m³/hr** minimum
- Formula: $Q = \text{ACH} \times V$; fans $= Q_{\text{req}} / Q_{\text{rated}}$
- Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at source of dust/fumes is most effective

**3. Noise (2 marks):**
- Permissible limit: **85 dB(A) for 8 hours**
- Combined SPL formula: $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10} \sum 10^{L_i/10}$
- Controls: source isolation, vibration dampers, acoustic enclosures, PPE (earmuffs/plugs)
- Hearing conservation programme mandatory if noise exceeds 85 dB(A)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part B:** Students often describe *what* ergonomics is without showing *how* it applies to specific dimensions, formulas, or numbers. **Every claim must be backed by a number or formula** to score 6+ marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Describe in detail the statutory welfare facilities required in a factory. Give at least one numerical standard for each. (7 marks)**

**(b) Two machines operating simultaneously produce sound pressure levels of 92 dB(A) and 89 dB(A) at a worker's station. Calculate the combined sound level. Discuss the engineering and administrative controls that must be implemented to protect the worker. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Statutory Welfare Facilities under the Factories Act, 1948:**

| Facility | Standard / Number | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Drinking water** | 1 tap per 100 workers, within 50 m; cool and clean | 1 |
| **Washing facilities** | 1 per 30 workers; 1 per 25 women workers | 1 |
| **Toilets (male)** | 1 per 25 workers; separate for each sex | 0.5 |
| **Toilets (female)** | 1 per 15 women workers | 0.5 |
| **Canteen** | Required if > 250 workers; 1 m²/person seating | 1 |
| **Rest rooms** | Required if > 150 workers | 0.5 |
| **Lunch room** | Required if > 50 workers | 0.5 |
| **First-aid boxes** | 1 per 150 workers | 1 |
| **Crèche** | Required if > 30 women workers | 0.5 |
| **PPE storage** | Within 10 m of work area | 0.5 |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert each dB value to linear intensity (2 marks):**

$$10^{92/10} = 10^{9.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{9}$$

$$10^{89/10} = 10^{8.9} = 7.943 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the linear values (1 mark):**

$$S = 1.585 \times 10^{9} + 7.943 \times 10^{8} = 2.379 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to dB (1 mark):**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.379 \times 10^{9})$$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times [\log_{10}(2.379) + 9]$$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times [0.376 + 9] = 10 \times 9.376 = 93.76 \text{ dB(A)}$$

$$\boxed{L_{\text{total}} \approx 93.8 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering controls (2 marks):**
- **Source control:** Replace noisy machines, use quieter alternatives, isolate machines in acoustic enclosures
- **Path control:** Sound-absorbing barriers between machines and operators; vibration dampers on machine mounts
- **Receiver control:** Provide earmuffs (NRR 25+ dB) or earplugs (NRR 20+ dB) to workers

**Step 5 — Administrative controls (1 mark):**
- Limit exposure time (rotate workers; max 2 hours at 95 dB)
- Mandatory audiometric testing every 6 months
- Training and signage in noise zones
- Hearing conservation programme per OSHA / Indian Factory Rules

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Noise Calculation:** A very common error is to simply **add the dB values** (92 + 89 = 181). Decibels are **logarithmic** — they cannot be added directly. Always convert to linear, sum, and convert back.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- Ergonomics = fit the job to the worker (not vice versa)
- 5th–95th percentile design rule for 90% population fit
- Standard standing eye height: **1.65 m**; reach envelope: **600 mm functional**

### ✅ Illumination Standards
- General: 300 lux | Fine work: 500–1000 lux | Precision: 1000–5000 lux
- Lamp count formula: $N = EA / (\Phi \cdot \text{UF} \cdot \text{MF})$
- Always round **UP** the number of lamps

### ✅ Ventilation Numbers
- ACH formula: $Q = \text{ACH} \times V$ (m³/hr)
- Per-person fresh air: **30 m³/hr/person**
- Foundries need 25–40 ACH; paint shops 15–25 ACH

### ✅ Noise
- Formula: $L_p = 20 \log(P/P_0)$, $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log \sum 10^{L_i/10}$
- PEL: **85 dB(A) for 8 hours**, +5 dB halves exposure time
- Decibels **cannot be added directly**

### ✅ Statutory Numbers (Factories Act 1948) — High-Yield
- Drinking water tap: 1 per 100 workers
- Wash basin: 1 per 30 (men) / 25 (women)
- Toilet: 1 per 25 (men) / 15 (women)
- Canteen: if > 250 workers
- Crèche: if > 30 women workers
- First-aid box: 1 per 150 workers
- Exit capacity: **60 persons per metre width**

### ✅ Safety
- Emergency exit: min **1.5 m wide**, 50 m max travel
- Fire extinguisher: within **15 m** travel distance
- PPE storage: within **10 m** of work zone

### ✅ Anthropometry Quick Reference
- Manual lift limit: 25 kg (male), 15 kg (female)
- Material lift range: knuckle to shoulder height
- Aisle width: 1.0 m (one-way), 1.8 m (two-way pedestrian)

### ✅ Examiner's Mantra
- **Always quote a number, formula, or statutory clause.**
- Vague qualitative answers score 2–3 out of 7.
- Quantitative, formula-backed answers score 6–7 out of 7.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Facilities and General Equipment for Amenities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage Facilities and General Equipment for Amenities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Storage Facilities — Formal Definition

In the context of **Plant Layout and Design (PEPET634)**, *storage facilities* refer to the engineered enclosures, vessels, structures, and civil works used within a process plant boundary to safely hold, preserve, and dispense **raw materials, in-process intermediates, finished products, by-products, and utilities** over a planned residence time without compromising their physical, chemical, or regulatory integrity.

According to standard process plant engineering practice adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, storage facilities are classified by:

1. **State of matter** — Solids, Liquids, and Gases.
2. **Function** — Receipt storage, in-process (surge) storage, and despatch storage.
3. **Hazard class** — Flammable, toxic, corrosive, cryogenic, and inert materials.

### 1.2 General Equipment for Amenities — Formal Definition

*General equipment for amenities* encompasses the **auxiliary, safety, welfare, and utility systems** that are not directly part of the process train but are indispensable for the plant to function safely, legally, and efficiently. These include fire-fighting networks, safety showers, ventilation, lighting, first-aid stations, control rooms, laboratories, canteens, and sanitary blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module-1 weightage specifically tests a student's ability to *select appropriate storage hardware* for a given duty and *size* it using volumetric, gravimetric, and design-code calculations. Memorizing at least 4–6 tank/hopper volume formulas is mandatory.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plant as a Kitchen" Model

Think of a process plant as a **large commercial kitchen**:

- **Pantry & Cold Storage** (warehouses, cold rooms) hold the *raw materials* (flour, vegetables, milk) — these are the **storage facilities**.
- **Pressure Cookers, Mixing Bowls, and Refrigerators** (tanks, hoppers, silos) are the *processing vessels* and the *intermediate storage*.
- **Gas Cylinders, Fire Extinguishers, Exhaust Hoods, Emergency Lighting, and First-Aid Boxes** scattered around the kitchen are the **general equipment for amenities** — they don't cook the food, but the kitchen cannot legally or safely operate without them.

In a chemical plant, a *failing gas cylinder* storage or an *absent safety shower* will shut down operations just as surely as a failed reactor.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and standard storage metrics are used throughout this module:

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of water | $\rho_{w}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard design wind speed (IS 875 / Kerala) | $V_{b}$ | **39 m/s (Zone III–IV)** |
| Recommended residence time — raw materials | $t_r$ | **7–30 days** |
| Recommended residence time — finished products | $t_r$ | **15–45 days** |
| Bulk solid angle of repose | $\phi$ | **30° – 45°** (typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use the **higher** of (a) the jurisdictionally mandated design pressure, or (b) the maximum possible working pressure when sizing storage vessels. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question trap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Storage Facilities

Storage facilities are systematically classified as follows:

**A. Storage of Solids**
- **Bagged/Drummed** — palletised warehouses with forklift access.
- **Bulk Solids in Silos** — vertical cylindrical steel/concrete structures for granular materials (cement, grain, plastic pellets).
- **Bunkers & Bins** — smaller rectangular/cylindrical hoppers for short-term in-process surge.
- **Open Yards** — for weather-resistant bulk solids (coal, ore, limestone).

**B. Storage of Liquids**
- **Fixed-Roof Atmospheric Tanks** — cone roof, dome roof; for non-volatile liquids (diesel, lube oil).
- **Floating-Roof Tanks** — for volatile, flammable liquids (crude, naphtha, gasoline). Loss of vapour is minimised.
- **Horizontal Cylindrical Tanks** — bullet tanks for LPG, propane at moderate pressure.
- **Spherical Pressure Vessels (Horton Spheres)** — for LPG, butane, ammonia under pressure.
- **Cryogenic Tanks** — double-walled vacuum-insulated for LNG, LOX, LIN, LAr.
- **Underground Storage Tanks (UST)** — for petrol stations and fire-water reserves.

**C. Storage of Gases**
- **Tube Trailers** — bundles of seamless high-pressure cylinders.
- **Cylinder Banks** — manifolded cylinders for oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen.
- **Spheres / Bullets** — for low-to-medium pressure bulk gas (Cl₂, H₂, NH₃).

### 2.2 Selection Logic — Why a Particular Storage Type?

A storage vessel is selected by traversing this decision tree:

1. **State of matter?** (Solid / Liquid / Gas).
2. **Vapour pressure at storage T?** Low → atmospheric; High → pressure; Very high → cryogenic.
3. **Flash point?** < 23 °C → floating roof or pressurised; 23–65 °C → fixed roof with flame arrestors; > 65 °C → open vent.
4. **Quantity?** Lab-scale → cylinders; Plant-scale → bullets/spheres; Refinery-scale → bulk tank farms.
5. **Hazard?** Toxic → dyked area with neutralisation; Corrosive → lined/coated; Flammable → flame-proof zone classification (IS 5571 / IEC 60079).
6. **Hygroscopic / moisture sensitive?** → Sealed silos with N₂ blanketing.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of vertical cylindrical tank | $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} H$ | m³ | D = diameter, H = shell height |
| 2 | Volume of horizontal cylindrical tank | $V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} L + \dfrac{\pi D^3}{12}$ | m³ | Last term = 2 hemispherical end caps |
| 3 | Volume of spherical tank | $V = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6}$ | m³ | Full sphere; partial = sector formula |
| 4 | Volume of conical hopper | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$ | m³ | r = base radius, h = cone height |
| 5 | Volume of rectangular bin | $V = L \times B \times H$ | m³ | Add pyramid bottom if conical outlet |
| 6 | Mass stored | $m = \rho \, V$ | kg | Use actual density, not SG × 1000 |
| 7 | Required storage volume | $V = \dfrac{m_{\text{daily}} \times t_r}{\rho \, \eta}$ | m³ | $\eta$ = utilisation factor (0.85–0.90) |
| 8 | Design pressure (vessel) | $P_d = P_w + \dfrac{S \cdot t}{D_i + S \cdot t} \cdot \sigma$ | kPa | Per ASME Sec VIII; $S$ = stress, $t$ = thickness |
| 9 | Hydrostatic test pressure | $P_h = 1.3 \, P_d$ | kPa | Mandatory for pressure vessels |
| 10 | Wind pressure on tank shell | $p_w = 0.6 \, V_z^2$ | N/m² | IS 875 Part 3; $V_z$ = design wind speed at height z |
| 11 | Wind moment on tank | $M_w = p_w \cdot D \cdot H^2 \, / \, 2$ | N·m | Overturning check |
| 12 | Hopper half-angle design | $\alpha < \phi - 5^\circ$ | degree | To ensure gravity discharge without arching |
| 13 | Gas storage (ideal gas) | $PV = nRT$ | — | Modified van der Waals at high P |
| 14 | Settlement / fill rate check | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$ | m³/h | Surge drum design |
| 15 | Fixed-roof tank vapour loss | $L_v = 0.001 \, V_{tank} \cdot p \, M \, K_E \, K_C$ | kg/yr | API MPMS Ch. 19 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (Board-exam favourite):** "Storage capacity should be sized for **at least 15 days of peak production**, plus a 10 % operational margin."

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These formulas are used in:
- **Refineries & Petrochemicals** — sizing crude, naphtha, and LPG spheres.
- **Fertiliser Plants** — sizing ammonia, urea, and CO₂ storage.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Bulk Drugs** — design of API warehouses (humidity-controlled).
- **Food & Dairy** — silo design with CIP (clean-in-place) compatibility.
- **Renewable Energy** — cryogenic storage of LH₂ and LCO₂ for carbon-capture hubs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Volume of a Vertical Cylindrical Storage Tank

A vertical cylindrical tank is generated by revolving a rectangle of width $D$ and height $H$ about its vertical centre-line. By Pappus's second theorem, or by direct integration:

**Step 1 —** Set up the area of the base:
$$
A = \pi r^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2 —** Multiply the base area by the height to obtain the volume of the cylinder:
$$
V = A \times H = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \, H
$$

**Step 3 —** Account for roof and bottom irregularities using a calibration factor $k_c \approx 0.97$ for cone-roof tanks:
$$
V_{\text{effective}} = k_c \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} H
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above factor $k_c$ is essential in **calibration charts** issued by IS 2007 / API 2550. Examiners often award bonus credit for applying it.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Volume of a Horizontal Cylinder (Bullet Tank)

A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel used for LPG, propane, and butane consists of a cylindrical shell plus two hemispherical heads.

**Step 1 —** Cylindrical shell volume:
$$
V_{shell} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} L
$$

**Step 2 —** Two hemispherical heads combine to form one complete sphere:
$$
V_{heads} = 2 \times \frac{2}{3} \pi \left( \frac{D}{2} \right)^3 = \frac{\pi D^3}{12}
$$

**Step 3 —** Total volume:
$$
V_{total} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} L + \frac{\pi D^3}{12}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Q. Type — KTU 14-mark main):**
> *A horizontal bullet tank is to store 20,000 kg of LPG at 8 °C. The liquid density is 540 kg/m³. The tank has a 2:1 L:D ratio. Find D and L.*

**Solution Path:**

Step 1 — Required volume:
$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{20{,}000}{540} = 37.04 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 2 — Apply L = 2D and $V_{total}$ formula:
$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} (2D) + \frac{\pi D^3}{12} = \frac{\pi D^3}{2} + \frac{\pi D^3}{12}
$$

Step 3 — Combine fractions:
$$
V = \pi D^3 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{12} \right) = \pi D^3 \left( \frac{6 + 1}{12} \right) = \frac{7 \pi D^3}{12}
$$

Step 4 — Substitute V = 37.04:
$$
37.04 = \frac{7 \pi D^3}{12} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D^3 = \frac{37.04 \times 12}{7 \pi} = \frac{444.48}{21.99} = 20.21 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 5 — Solve for D:
$$
D = \sqrt[3]{20.21} = 2.72 \text{ m}
$$

Step 6 — Compute L:
$$
L = 2D = 5.44 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation III — Volume of a Conical Hopper for Solid Discharge

A conical hopper is a frustum-like funnel with apex half-angle $\alpha$. For a true cone of base radius $r$ and vertical height $h$:

$$
V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h
$$

For a **truncated cone** (frustum) of top radius $R$, bottom radius $r$, and height $h$:
$$
V_{frustum} = \frac{\pi h}{3} (R^2 + Rr + r^2)
$$

**Step-by-step proof of frustum formula:**

Step 1 — Consider a cone truncated at a height $y$ from apex. Its radius scales linearly:
$$
r(y) = y \tan \alpha
$$

Step 2 — Differential volume of a thin disc at height $y$:
$$
dV = \pi \, r(y)^2 \, dy = \pi y^2 \tan^2 \alpha \, dy
$$

Step 3 — Integrate between $y_1$ (bottom radius) and $y_2$ (top radius):
$$
V = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \pi \tan^2 \alpha \, y^2 \, dy = \frac{\pi \tan^2 \alpha}{3} (y_2^3 - y_1^3)
$$

Step 4 — Replace $y = r / \tan \alpha$:
$$
V = \frac{\pi}{3} (R^2 r + R r^2 - r^3 - R^2 r + R r^2) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = \frac{\pi h}{3} (R^2 + Rr + r^2)
$$

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Required Storage Volume from Production Schedule

For a plant producing $Q$ tonnes/day of finished product, the storage volume required is:

**Step 1 —** Total mass to be held for residence time $t_r$ days:
$$
m_{total} = Q \times t_r \;\; (\text{tonnes})
$$

**Step 2 —** Convert mass to volume using density $\rho$ (kg/m³) and dividing by 1000:
$$
V_{req} = \frac{Q \times t_r \times 1000}{\rho} \;\; (\text{m}^3)
$$

Step 3 — Apply utilisation factor $\eta$ (typically 0.85–0.90) and freeboard $f$ (typically 1.10):
$$
V_{design} = \frac{V_{req}}{\eta} \times f
$$

### 3.5 Derivation V — Design Pressure for Pressure Storage (Simplified ASME)

For a thin-walled cylindrical shell under internal pressure:

**Step 1 —** Hoop stress equation:
$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 t}
$$

**Step 2 —** Allowable stress $S$ is fixed by the material (e.g., SA-516 Gr 70 → $S = 138$ MPa at 200 °C).

Step 3 —** Set $\sigma_h \le S$ and solve for required thickness $t$:
$$
t = \frac{P \, D_i}{2 \, S \, E - 1.2 \, P}
$$

Step 4 — Add corrosion allowance $c$ (typically 1.5–3 mm):
$$
t_{actual} = t + c
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Storage vs. Hazard

| Material Class | Example | Preferred Storage | Key Design Code | Amenity Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flammable liquid (FP < 23 °C) | Petrol, Hexane | Floating-roof tank | API 650, IS 803 | Dyke wall, foam pourer, vapour recovery |
| Toxic gas | Chlorine, Ammonia | Pressurised sphere, N₂ pad | ASME Sec VIII | Gas mask lockers, scrubber, alarm |
| Cryogenic | LNG, LOX | Double-wall vacuum tank | ASME Sec VIII Div 1 | Trench vent, water deluge |
| Hygroscopic solid | Caustic flakes, Citric acid | Sealed silo, desiccant | IS 9178 | Dry-air purge, RH < 30 % |
| Bulk powder (fine) | Cement, Fly ash | Pneumatic silo, bag filter | IS 4990 | Dust collector, earthing |
| Acid | H₂SO₄, HCl | Lined FRP/SS tank | IS 944 | Bunded dyke, neutralisation pit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Storage Hierarchy

This diagram maps the decision and operational flow of selecting and operating a storage system, from plant intake to despatch.

    D1 --> D2{Flash Point Check}

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> *Differentiate between a fixed-roof tank and a floating-roof tank. State any two applications of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fixed-Roof Tank | Floating-Roof Tank |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour space | Constant above liquid | Liquid touches roof; vapour space minimal |
| Evaporation loss | Higher (working + standing) | Very low (≈ 90 % reduction) |
| Construction | Cone/dome roof on shell | Roof rests on liquid, rises/falls |
| Suitable for | Diesel, lube oil, crude (low vapour) | Gasoline, naphtha, jet fuel (volatile) |
| Cost | Lower initial | Higher (seal maintenance) |

**Applications — Fixed-Roof:** (i) Storage of furnace oil, (ii) lube oil blending tanks.
**Applications — Floating-Roof:** (i) Crude oil tank farms, (ii) motor gasoline terminals.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> *What is the function of a Horton sphere? Why is it preferred over a horizontal cylinder for storing large quantities of gas?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Horton sphere* is a **spherical pressure vessel used for bulk storage of liquefied gases** (LPG, ammonia, vinyl chloride) at pressures typically between 0.3 and 1.6 MPa.

**Reasons for preference over horizontal cylinders:**
1. **Lower surface area per unit volume** → minimum heat in-leak, minimum boil-off gas.
2. **Even stress distribution** → wall thickness can be lower → material economy.
3. **Smaller footprint** for the same capacity, critical in congested tank farms.
4. **Aesthetic and aerodynamic** advantage in coastal cyclone-prone regions (Kerala).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-1 Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper / KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
> **(a)** A process plant consumes **15 tonnes/day of methanol (density 791 kg/m³)**. The receiving storage should hold a **30-day reserve** with a freeboard of **10 %** and a utilisation factor of **0.90**. The tank is to be a vertical cylindrical fixed-roof tank of diameter **6 m**. Calculate the **shell height required**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **design considerations for storage of flammable liquids** in a tank farm. Include at least **six** design points. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Mass to be stored (1 mark):**
$$
m = 15 \times 1000 \times 30 = 450{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual liquid volume (1 mark):**
$$
V_{liquid} = \frac{450{,}000}{791} = 568.96 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply freeboard and utilisation factor (1 mark):**
$$
V_{design} = \frac{568.96 \times 1.10}{0.90} = 695.40 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Equate to cylindrical volume and solve for H (2 marks):**
$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} H \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 695.40 = \frac{\pi \times 6^2}{4} \times H
$$

**Step 5 — Solve (2 marks):**
$$
H = \frac{695.40 \times 4}{\pi \times 36} = \frac{2781.6}{113.10} = 24.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:** Shell height $H \approx 24.6$ m.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

A tank farm for flammable liquids must be engineered with the following considerations:

1. **Dyke / Bund Wall** (1.5 marks) — capacity ≥ 110 % of the largest tank, made of earth or RCC, with impervious lining.
2. **Inter-tank Spacing** (1.0 mark) — minimum shell-to-shell distance ≥ 1.5 × diameter of the larger tank (per IS 803).
3. **Fire Protection** (1.5 marks) — foam pourers, water spray (deluge), hydrant network, fire-water reservoir sized for **4–6 h firefighting duration**.
4. **Vapour Recovery & Flame Arrestors** (1.0 mark) — pressure/vacuum relief valves, detonation arrestors on vents.
5. **Earthing & Lightning Protection** (0.5 mark) — multiple earth pits, IS 3043 compliant.
6. **Hazardous Area Classification** (1.0 mark) — Zone 0/1/2 mapping per IS 5571; flame-proof fittings.
7. **Emergency Shut-down (ESD)** (0.5 mark) — remotely operated isolation valves on each tank.

---

> **Q.B. [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **classification of storage facilities** for raw materials and finished products. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A chemical plant produces **10 m³/day of liquid resin (SG = 1.18)**. The product is stored in a **horizontal cylindrical tank** with **L = 4 m** and **D = 2.5 m**, including **two hemispherical ends**. Calculate the **number of days the storage will last** if the tank is filled to **80 % capacity**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

Storage facilities are classified as follows (textual schematic with key points):

```
STORAGE FACILITIES
├── (A) By State of Matter
│     ├── SOLID → Warehouse, Silo, Bunker, Open yard
│     ├── LIQUID → Tank (fixed/floating roof), Bullet, Sphere
│     └── GAS → Cylinder bank, Tube trailer, Horton sphere
│
├── (B) By Function
│     ├── Receipt Storage (intake bay, weighbridge)
│     ├── In-Process Storage (surge drums, day tanks)
│     └── Despatch Storage (finished goods warehouse)
│
└── (C) By Hazard
      ├── Flammable → Floating roof, foam, dyke
      ├── Toxic → Sealed + scrubber, gas mask
      └── Corrosive → Lined, coated, bunded
```

(Examiner reward: 1 mark for a clean schematic, 4 marks for enumerating 3 classes with ≥ 2 examples each, 2 marks for briefly stating the rationale of each class.)

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Total tank volume (1 mark):**
$$
V_{tank} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} L + \frac{\pi D^3}{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**
$$
V_{tank} = \frac{\pi (2.5)^2}{4} (4) + \frac{\pi (2.5)^3}{12}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (2 marks):**
$$
V_{tank} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 4}{4} + \frac{\pi \times 15.625}{12} = 19.63 + 4.09 = 23.72 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Usable volume (1 mark):**
$$
V_{usable} = 0.80 \times 23.72 = 18.98 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Days of storage (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Days} = \frac{18.98}{10} = 1.898 \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.9 \text{ days}}
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **1.9 days** of storage. (Interpretation: storage is undersized — should be revised.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the freeboard factor** — students often forget the 1.10 multiplier and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing mass flow with volumetric flow** — never multiply "tonnes/day" by density again.
> 3. **Ignoring the utilisation factor $\eta$** — IS codes treat η = 0.85–0.90 as non-negotiable.
> 4. **Using the wrong formula for spherical heads** — students frequently use $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ (single sphere) when the problem has *two* heads; the correct combined volume is also $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ only when D is the head diameter — the formula $\pi D^3 / 12$ already accounts for **two** heads.
> 5. **Skipping units** — every numerical answer in a 7-mark derivation should carry units. KTU examiners *will* deduct ½ mark for "m³" missing.
> 6. **No schematic in design questions** — a 7-mark "describe" question without a labelled diagram is capped at 5/7 in valuation key.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **State of matter is the first filter** — Solid → warehouse/silo; Liquid → tank; Gas → cylinder/sphere.
- [x] **Flash point dictates the tank type** — Floating roof for < 23 °C, fixed roof for 23–65 °C, open vent for > 65 °C.
- [x] **Volume formulas to memorise (Board-favourite):**
  - Cylinder: $V = \pi D^2 H / 4$
  - Sphere: $V = \pi D^3 / 6$
  - Cone: $V = \pi r^2 h / 3$
  - Horizontal bullet: $V = \pi D^2 L/4 + \pi D^3/12$
  - Frustum: $V = \pi h(R^2 + Rr + r^2)/3$
- [x] **Residence time defaults:** raw materials 7–30 days, finished products 15–45 days.
- [x] **Freeboard + Utilisation** factors are non-optional in KTU valuation.
- [x] **Flammable storage** mandates dyke wall, foam, flame arrestor, earthing, and zone classification.
- [x] **Toxic storage** mandates scrubber, gas mask, sealed venting, and emergency water deluge.
- [x] **Cryogenic storage** mandates double-wall vacuum, perlite/alumina insulation, pressure-relief.
- [x] **Hazardous area zoning** follows IS 5571 / IEC 60079 — Zone 0, 1, 2 for gases; Zone 20, 21, 22 for dusts.
- [x] **Amenities are not optional** — fire water (4–6 h), safety shower (within 10 s of hazard), eyewash, first aid, control room with HVAC, and admin block are statutory under the Factories Act 1948 (India) and must appear in every plant layout.
- [x] **Always present a labelled schematic** in any 7-mark "design/describe" answer — the cost is small, the reward is large.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TOPIC: PROCEDURE FOR PLANT LAYOUT DESIGN

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> The **Procedure for Plant Layout Design** is a systematic, scientific, and logical sequence of engineering steps used to determine the most efficient physical arrangement of men, machines, materials, and supporting services within a manufacturing facility. It transforms a production plan into an operational factory floor blueprint.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the procedure is modelled after **Muther's Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** — the globally accepted framework adopted in PEPET634 for laying out any type of plant (manufacturing, service, or process).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"
Imagine you want to construct a house. You do **not** simply start placing bricks randomly. Instead:
1. First, you decide **what rooms** are needed (kitchen, bedroom, hall).
2. Then you decide **how big** each room should be.
3. Then you decide **which rooms should be close** to each other (kitchen next to dining).
4. You draw a **bubble diagram**.
5. You **convert** the bubbles into actual rooms with walls.
6. Finally, you **evaluate** multiple house plans and pick the best.

Plant Layout Procedure follows the **exact same logic** — only the "rooms" are **departments/workstations**, and the "house" is the **factory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**
> Always remember the four-letter KTU summary: **P-Q-R-S-T** (Phase 1: Analysis) followed by **A-S-E** (Phase 2: Synthesis) and **V-E** (Phase 3: Evaluation). Examiners love asking students to "list the steps in order."

---

## 2. OBJECTIVES OF ADOPTING A STANDARD PROCEDURE

A formal procedure ensures:
- **Minimum material handling cost**
- **Optimum utilization of space**
- **Smooth flow of work** (no backtracking or criss-crossing)
- **Reduced production lead time**
- **Better working conditions and safety**
- **Flexibility for future expansion**

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks a 3-mark question: *"Why is a systematic procedure necessary for plant layout?"* — Memorize the 6 points above verbatim.

---

## 3. PHASE-WISE CLASSIFICATION OF THE PROCEDURE

The complete procedure is broadly divided into **THREE phases** and **FOUR major activities**, as codified by Muther and adopted in KTU PEPET634:

| Phase | Activity | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase I — Analysis (Search Phase)** | Product, Quantity, Routing, Supporting services, Time | **P-Q-R-S-T** |
| **Phase II — Synthesis (Search Phase)** | Activity Relationship, Space requirements & availability | **A-S** |
| **Phase III — Evaluation** | Modify considerations, Evaluate, Select | **M-E-S** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 4. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE KTU HIGH-YIELD PROCEDURE

The procedure is broken down into **numbered steps**. Each step has a precise engineering purpose and produces a specific deliverable. Mastering this sequence guarantees marks in both descriptive and numerical questions.

## STEP 1: PRODUCT ANALYSIS (P) — *What is being made?*

**Purpose:** Determine the **raw materials**, **components**, **sub-assemblies**, and **finished products** involved.
**Engineering Output:** A complete **BOM (Bill of Materials)** and product specification chart.
**Why?** The nature of the product (size, weight, hazardous nature, perishability) directly dictates the type of equipment and storage facilities required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** A heavy product demands a layout close to **shipping docks**. A fragile/perishable product demands **air-conditioned, low-traffic** zones.

## STEP 2: QUANTITY ANALYSIS (Q) — *How much is being made?*

**Purpose:** Determine the **volume of production** per unit time (daily/monthly/annual).
**Engineering Output:** Production quantity in pieces, kg, or litres.
**Why?** Quantity determines whether to use:
- **Line layout** (mass production, high volume)
- **Process layout** (low volume, high variety)
- **Cellular layout** (medium volume, family of parts)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Volume vs Layout Type):**
> - Q > 10,000 units/month → **Product/Line Layout**
> - Q < 1,000 units/month → **Process Layout**
> - 1,000 < Q < 10,000 → **Cellular/Group Layout**

## STEP 3: ROUTING ANALYSIS (R) — *How is it made?*

**Purpose:** Establish the **sequence of operations** and the **path** that material follows from raw stage to finished stage.
**Engineering Output:** **Operation Process Chart**, **Flow Process Chart**, **Route Sheet**.
**Why?** Identifies bottlenecks, backtracking paths, and chances for combining operations.

## STEP 4: SUPPORTING ACTIVITIES ANALYSIS (S) — *What auxiliary services are needed?*

**Purpose:** Identify non-production activities: tool room, maintenance, quality lab, store, canteen, restroom, dispatch, receipt, scrap yard.
**Engineering Output:** A list of supporting departments with their area requirements.
**Why?** These departments are often forgotten but consume **15–25%** of total plant area.

## STEP 5: TIME ANALYSIS (T) — *How long does each step take?*

**Purpose:** Determine the **time required** for each operation and the **cycle time** of the line.
**Engineering Output:** Cycle time chart, line balancing data.

---

## PHASE II — SYNTHESIS STEPS

## STEP 6: ACTIVITY RELATIONSHIP ANALYSIS (A)

**Purpose:** Determine **how closely related** each pair of departments should be based on the nature of interaction.
**Engineering Output:** **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** — a triangular matrix.

### KTU Closeness Rating Codes (Muther's Standard)

| Rating | Symbol | Closeness Rating Letter | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | `██████` double red line | Absolutely Necessary | Activities **must** be adjacent |
| **E** | `████` triple line | Especially Important | Very strong proximity required |
| **I** | `███` double line | Important | Strong proximity required |
| **O** | `██` single line | Ordinary Closeness | OK to be close |
| **U** | `-` dashed | Unimportant | No special need |
| **X** | `~~~~` zigzag line | Not Desirable | Should be **far apart** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** The closeness ratings are decided based on **6 factors**:
> 1. Flow of materials
> 2. Use of common equipment
> 3. Use of same workforce/supervision
> 4. Sharing of utilities (steam, water, gas)
> 5. Movement of personnel
> 6. Need for communication/contact

## STEP 7: SPACE REQUIREMENTS (S)

**Purpose:** Calculate the **floor area** needed for each department.
**Engineering Output:** **Space Relationship Diagram (Space From-To Chart)**.

## STEP 8: SPACE AVAILABILITY (S)

**Purpose:** Compare the **required space** (Step 7) with the **available space** (plot dimensions, building).
**Engineering Output:** Adjusted area chart; possibility of expansion.

## STEP 9: DETERMINE CLOSENESS RATINGS USING SLP PATTERNS

Based on Steps 6, 7, and 8, derive **final closeness ratings** that combine:
- Activity Relationship (qualitative) **AND**
- Space Requirements (quantitative)

---

## PHASE III — EVALUATION STEPS

## STEP 10: MODIFY CONSIDERATIONS (M)

Apply **practical constraints**:
- Building shape (L, T, U, I)
- Pillars, walls, fixed structures
- Aisle widths (main aisle = **3 m**, secondary = **2 m**, service = **1.5 m**)
- Safety regulations, fire exits

## STEP 11: EVALUATE ALTERNATIVE LAYOUTS (E)

Generate **multiple feasible layouts** and evaluate them using:
- **Material Handling Cost** (primary KTU criterion)
- **Travelled Distance**
- **Flexibility index**
- **Aisle congestion**
- **Worker comfort**

## STEP 12: SELECT THE BEST LAYOUT (S)

Choose the layout with the **lowest total handling cost** and **highest overall efficiency**.

---

## 5. KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **exam-grade gold**. Memorize every cell.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} D_{ij} \times F_{ij}$ | Total material handling cost / distance | m·units |
| 2 | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij} \times F_{ij} \times D_{ij}$ | Total cost where $C_{ij}$ = cost per unit move | ₹/month |
| 3 | $N_{layouts} = \dfrac{(N-1)!}{2}$ | Number of possible layouts for $N$ departments | dimensionless |
| 4 | $A_{dept} = \dfrac{L \times W \times n}{u}$ | Area of a department | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 5 | $A_{total} = \dfrac{\sum A_{dept}}{f}$ | Total plant area (with $f$ = layout efficiency factor, typically **0.7 to 0.85**) | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | $f_{layout} = \dfrac{\text{Productive Area}}{\text{Total Area}}$ | Layout efficiency factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{\text{Available Time per day}}{\text{Demand per day}}$ | Cycle time for line balancing | minutes/unit |
| 8 | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum D_{ij}}{N_{pairs}}$ | Average inter-department distance | meters |
| 9 | $B_{L} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{T_{cycle}}$ | Line balancing efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $N_{theoretical,min} = \lceil T_{cycle} / t_{longest} \rceil$ | Minimum number of workstations | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** In the above table, `\vert` and `\mid` symbols were intentionally avoided. Vertical bars (`\vert x \vert`) appear only inside LaTeX `$...$` blocks to keep the table rendering safe.

---

## 6. REAL-WORLD ENGINEERING UTILITY

The SLP procedure is applied in:
- **Tata Motors** — for new car assembly plants (Pune, Sanand)
- **ISRO** — for satellite integration halls
- **Apollo Tyres** — for batch production of rubber goods
- **KSIDC industrial parks** — for plotting layouts in Kerala
- **Hospital layouts** — adapted as Muther's later work on service layouts
- **Warehouse design** — Amazon fulfillment centres use modified SLP

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 7. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL EXAMPLES

This section provides **fully worked-out numerical problems** that have appeared (or are highly likely to appear) in KTU Module 2 examinations.

---

## EXAMPLE 1 — TOTAL MATERIAL HANDLING COST (TYPICAL 14-MARK QUESTION)

**Problem Statement:**
A plant has **4 departments (A, B, C, D)**. The inter-departmental material flow (loads per month) and distances (meters) between them are given in the table. Calculate the **total monthly material handling cost** if the cost rate is **₹2 per meter per load**.

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | – | 50 | 30 | 20 |
| **B** | 50 | – | 40 | 10 |
| **C** | 30 | 40 | – | 60 |
| **D** | 20 | 10 | 60 | – |

### Given Distances (in meters) for current layout:

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | – | 20 | 30 | 50 |
| **B** | 20 | – | 25 | 40 |
| **C** | 30 | 25 | – | 15 |
| **D** | 50 | 40 | 15 | – |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the formula from the cheat sheet:
$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C_{rate}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute each cell $F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$ (only for $i \neq j$ and using one direction, since flow is symmetric):

- $F_{AB} \cdot D_{AB} = 50 \times 20 = 1000$
- $F_{AC} \cdot D_{AC} = 30 \times 30 = 900$
- $F_{AD} \cdot D_{AD} = 20 \times 50 = 1000$
- $F_{BC} \cdot D_{BC} = 40 \times 25 = 1000$
- $F_{BD} \cdot D_{BD} = 10 \times 40 = 400$
- $F_{CD} \cdot D_{CD} = 60 \times 15 = 900$

**Step 3:** Sum all the products:
$$
\sum F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} = 1000 + 900 + 1000 + 1000 + 400 + 900 = 5200 \;\; \text{load·m/month}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by cost rate $C_{rate} = ₹2$ per meter per load:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= 5200 \times 2 \\
&= \mathbf{₹10{,}400 \; per \; month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always remember to use the **absolute distance** $D_{ij}$ in the formula, and multiply flow $F_{ij}$ by $D_{ij}$, then by the unit cost. Many students forget one of the three terms.

---

## EXAMPLE 2 — NUMBER OF POSSIBLE LAYOUTS

**Problem:** How many possible layouts exist for a plant with **N = 6 departments**?

### Derivation
For $N$ departments placed on a fixed site (not in a line), the number of relative arrangements equals:
$$
N_{layouts} = \dfrac{(N-1)!}{2}
$$

This is because:
- There are $N!$ ways to permute $N$ departments on $N$ positions.
- But **mirror images** of any layout are equivalent (you can flip them).
- So we divide by $2$.
- We also subtract 1 (since $(N-1)!$ counts arrangements relative to one fixed reference department).

### Numerical Substitution
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{layouts} &= \dfrac{(6-1)!}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{5!}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{120}{2} \\
&= \mathbf{60 \; possible \; layouts}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Some textbooks give $N_{layouts} = N!$ without the division by 2. KTU uses the divided version. For $N = 6$, always write **60**, not 720.

---

## EXAMPLE 3 — LINE BALANCING (CYCLE TIME & WORKSTATIONS)

**Problem:** The daily demand for a product is **240 units**. The plant operates **8 hours/day**. The longest operation time is **6 minutes**. Find:
1. Cycle time
2. Theoretical minimum number of workstations

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute available time per day in minutes:
$$
T_{available} = 8 \;\text{hrs} \times 60 = 480 \;\text{minutes/day}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cycle time formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= \dfrac{T_{available}}{D} \\
&= \dfrac{480}{240} \\
&= \mathbf{2 \; \text{minutes/unit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute theoretical minimum number of workstations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{t_{longest}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{2}{1} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — re-reading the problem: the **longest single operation** takes **6 minutes**, but **cycle time is 2 minutes**. This is a contradiction — the longest operation cannot exceed cycle time in a properly balanced line.

**Correction interpretation:** The **sum of all operation times** $t_{longest}$ referring to the **total work content** is the issue. Let's reframe:

**Total work content** (sum of all operation times) = **6 minutes** (given as the entire task time).
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 2 \;\text{min} \\
t_{total} &= 6 \;\text{min} \\
N_{min} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{total}}{T_{cycle}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{2} \right\rceil = \mathbf{3 \; workstations}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Clarification:** $t_{longest}$ in the formula refers to the **longest individual operation time** that must fit inside the cycle time. If cycle time < longest operation, the line is infeasible. Here we used total work content for clarity.

---

## EXAMPLE 4 — AREA CALCULATION USING LAYOUT EFFICIENCY FACTOR

**Problem:** The total productive area required for 5 departments is **850 m²**. If the layout efficiency factor is **0.85**, find the total plant area required.

### Solution

Using:
$$
A_{total} = \dfrac{\sum A_{dept}}{f_{layout}}
$$

Substituting values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= \dfrac{850}{0.85} \\
&= \mathbf{1000 \; m^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** The remaining **150 m²** is allocated to aisles, walls, columns, and service corridors — the "**non-productive area**".

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 9. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the steps involved in the systematic procedure for plant layout design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The systematic procedure, based on Muther's Systematic Layout Planning (SLP), consists of the following steps:

1. **Product analysis** — determining raw materials, components, finished goods.
2. **Quantity analysis** — estimating production volume.
3. **Routing analysis** — preparing operation and flow process charts.
4. **Supporting activities analysis** — identifying tool room, stores, QC, etc.
5. **Time analysis** — computing cycle time and work content.
6. **Activity relationship analysis** — preparing ARC with closeness ratings (A, E, I, O, U, X).
7. **Space requirements** — calculating area per department.
8. **Space availability** — comparing with the plot size.
9. **Determining closeness ratings** combining relationships and space.
10. **Activity relationship diagram** (bubble diagram) → **Space relationship diagram** (block layout).
11. **Modifying considerations** — building shape, aisles, safety.
12. **Evaluating alternatives** based on handling cost, distance, and safety.
13. **Selecting the best layout** and implementing it.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the closeness ratings used in Activity Relationship Chart (ARC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The closeness ratings in Muther's ARC are **six standard codes** that indicate the desirability of proximity between two departments:

| Rating | Meaning | Typical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Absolutely necessary | Heavy material flow between them |
| **E** | Especially important | Major flow of men or material |
| **I** | Important | Moderate interaction |
| **O** | Ordinary closeness | OK to be near |
| **U** | Unimportant | No special preference |
| **X** | Not desirable | Should be kept apart (e.g., noisy vs. precision dept.) |

The ratings are decided based on **6 factors**: material flow, common equipment, common workforce, common utilities, personnel movement, and the need for communication.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Phase I (Analysis)** steps of the plant layout procedure with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plant has **5 departments (A, B, C, D, E)**. The number of possible layouts is **12**. If two new departments F and G are added, calculate the new number of possible layouts. Justify whether Muther's SLP procedure can be applied to such a complex case.

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Phase I Analysis Steps [7 marks]

The **first phase** of plant layout procedure is the **Analysis Phase**, which collects all data needed for layout decisions. It consists of **five steps** (mnemonic: **P-Q-R-S-T**).

**1. Product Analysis (P) [1.5 marks]**
- Identify raw materials, bought-out components, sub-assemblies, and finished products.
- Determine physical characteristics: **weight, size, shape, hazardous nature, perishability**.
- Example: In a bakery plant, "bread" is the finished product; "flour, yeast, sugar" are raw materials.
- Output: **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and product specification chart.

**2. Quantity Analysis (Q) [1 mark]**
- Determine the **volume of production** in pieces/kg/litres per shift/day/month.
- Quantity dictates the **type of layout**:
  - High volume, low variety → **Product layout**.
  - Low volume, high variety → **Process layout**.
- Example: Maruti Suzuki produces ~1.5 lakh cars/month → **product layout**.

**3. Routing Analysis (R) [1.5 marks]**
- Determine the **sequence of operations** and the **path of material** through the plant.
- Tools used: **Operation Process Chart, Flow Process Chart, Route Sheet**.
- Identifies **backtracking** and **cross-flows** that need to be eliminated.

**4. Supporting Activities Analysis (S) [1.5 marks]**
- Identify non-production departments: tool room, maintenance, QC lab, stores, scrap yard, canteen, restrooms, parking, dispatch, receipt bay.
- These typically consume **15–25%** of the total plant area.
- Example: A QC lab must be **near** the production line (rating "I" or "E") but **away** from the noisy forging section (rating "X").

**5. Time Analysis (T) [1.5 marks]**
- Determine the **time required** for each operation.
- Compute **cycle time** $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$ and **work content**.
- Used for **line balancing** to assign tasks to workstations efficiently.

---

#### Part (b) — Number of Layouts Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1: Verify the formula used in the original problem.**
For $N$ departments, the number of possible layouts is:
$$
N_{layouts} = \dfrac{(N-1)!}{2}
$$

**Step 2: For original 5 departments, verify.**
$$
N_{layouts} = \dfrac{(5-1)!}{2} = \dfrac{4!}{2} = \dfrac{24}{2} = 12 \;\; \checkmark
$$

This matches the given value, confirming the formula.

**Step 3: Add 2 new departments F and G → Total $N = 7$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{layouts,new} &= \dfrac{(7-1)!}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{6!}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{720}{2} \\
&= \mathbf{360 \; possible \; layouts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Justify the applicability of Muther's SLP.**
Even with 360 possible layouts, SLP remains applicable because:
1. **Pairwise comparison** — SLP only analyses pairs (N(N-1)/2 pairs), so for 7 departments it analyses only **21 pairs**, not 360 layouts.
2. **Heuristic-based** — SLP uses closeness ratings to filter out poor layouts early.
3. **Computer-aided** — Tools like **CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique)** and **CORELAP (Computerized Relationship Layout Planning)** handle even 50+ departments.

Thus, the **new number of layouts = 360**, and **SLP is fully applicable** even for this complex case.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Muther's Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** with a neat flowchart. Discuss the importance of **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** and **Space Relationship Diagram (SRD)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The flow matrix between 4 departments (A, B, C, D) and the distance matrix (in m) for a proposed layout are given below. Compute the **total monthly handling cost** if the rate is **₹1.50 per meter per load**. Suggest ONE improvement to reduce cost.

| Flow F | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | – | 40 | 25 | 15 |
| **B** | 40 | – | 30 | 20 |
| **C** | 25 | 30 | – | 50 |
| **D** | 15 | 20 | 50 | – |

| Distance D | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | – | 15 | 25 | 40 |
| **B** | 15 | – | 20 | 35 |
| **C** | 25 | 20 | – | 10 |
| **D** | 40 | 35 | 10 | – |

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Muther's SLP Flowchart & Diagrams [7 marks]

**Muther's SLP** is a **four-phase, 14-step systematic procedure** developed by Richard Muther in 1961. It is the foundation of modern plant layout design. The phases are:

1. **Phase I — Analysis (P-Q-R-S-T):** Determine product, quantity, routing, supporting services, and time.
2. **Phase II — Synthesis (Activity Relationship & Space):** Combine relationships with space needs to create the ARD and SRD.
3. **Phase III — Evaluation:** Modify the SRD based on practical constraints, evaluate alternatives, and select the best.

**Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** [3 marks]
- A **bubble diagram** where each department is a circle/bubble.
- Departments with **A** (Absolutely necessary) closeness are drawn **touching**.
- Departments with **E** (Especially important) are drawn **very close**.
- Departments with **X** (Not desirable) are drawn **far apart**.
- **Purpose:** Provides a **visual qualitative** relationship before quantifying space.

**Space Relationship Diagram (SRD)** [3 marks]
- Converts the ARD into a **block diagram** where bubble sizes are proportional to **actual area** requirements.
- Blocks are positioned to honour the closeness ratings from ARD.
- This is the **first numerical layout** of the plant.
- **Purpose:** Provides a **quantitative, area-scaled** layout ready for evaluation and modification.

---

#### Part (b) — Handling Cost Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1: State the formula.**
$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i \neq j} F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C_{rate}
$$

**Step 2: Compute each cell.**

| Pair | $F_{ij}$ | $D_{ij}$ (m) | $F \times D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A-B | 40 | 15 | 600 |
| A-C | 25 | 25 | 625 |
| A-D | 15 | 40 | 600 |
| B-C | 30 | 20 | 600 |
| B-D | 20 | 35 | 700 |
| C-D | 50 | 10 | 500 |

**Step 3: Sum all products.**
$$
\sum F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} = 600 + 625 + 600 + 600 + 700 + 500 = 3625 \;\; \text{load·m/month}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by the cost rate ₹1.50 per meter per load.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= 3625 \times 1.50 \\
&= \mathbf{₹5{,}437.50 \; per \; month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Suggest an improvement.**
The highest $F \times D$ value is **700** for pair B-D. **Departments B and D should be brought closer together** to reduce handling cost. Specifically:
- Move D adjacent to B (if currently far apart).
- This will reduce $D_{BD}$ from 35 m to ~10 m, saving:
$$
\text{Saving} = (35 - 10) \times 20 \times 1.50 = ₹750 \; \text{per month}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in Plant Layout Procedure questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the formula statement** — Always write $C_{total} = \sum F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C_{rate}$ **before** substituting values. 1 mark lost otherwise.
> 2. **Counting flows twice** — The matrix is **symmetric**. Use **only one triangle** (e.g., upper triangle) and **multiply by 2 OR** count each pair only once. Showing $F_{AB}$ AND $F_{BA}$ separately is double-counting.
> 3. **Confusing P-Q-R-S-T with ARC steps** — P-Q-R-S-T are Phase 1. ARC, ARD, SRD are Phase 2. Students mix them up and lose sequence marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — Always write "₹/month", "load·m", "m²", etc.
> 5. **Missing the closeness rating "X"** — Many students only mention A, E, I, O, U but forget that **"X" (Not Desirable)** is critical for noisy, hazardous, or sterile departments.

---

## 10. TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam)**

- ✅ The procedure has **3 Phases** — **Analysis, Synthesis, Evaluation**.
- ✅ **Phase 1 mnemonic:** **P-Q-R-S-T** (Product, Quantity, Routing, Supporting, Time).
- ✅ **Phase 2 deliverables:** ARC (closeness ratings) → ARD (bubble diagram) → SRD (block diagram).
- ✅ **Phase 3 steps:** **Modify → Evaluate → Select**.
- ✅ **6 Closeness Ratings:** **A, E, I, O, U, X** — never forget **X** (undesirable).
- ✅ **Number of layouts formula:** $N_{layouts} = \dfrac{(N-1)!}{2}$.
- ✅ **Cycle time formula:** $T_{cycle} = T_{available} / D$.
- ✅ **Area formula:** $A_{total} = \sum A_{dept} / f_{layout}$ with $f = 0.7$ to $0.85$.
- ✅ **Handling cost formula:** $C_{total} = \sum F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C_{rate}$.
- ✅ **Layout efficiency factor** accounts for **aisles, walls, columns** (non-productive area).
- ✅ **Muther's SLP** is the KTU-recommended framework.
- ✅ **High volume + low variety** → **Product Layout**.
- ✅ **Low volume + high variety** → **Process Layout**.
- ✅ Always state the **formula first**, then substitute, then compute, then **write units**.
- ✅ Symmetric matrices → count each pair **only once**.
- ✅ Aim for the **layout with minimum total handling cost** in evaluation.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: From-To Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** From-To Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **From-To Chart** is a fundamental quantitative analytical tool in **Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** used to record and display the **volume, frequency, or intensity of movement (trips/loads)** of materials, personnel, or information between every pair of departments (or work areas, machines, or activities) in an industrial facility. It is presented as a **triangular or square matrix** where the rows represent the origin ("From") and the columns represent the destination ("To").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The From-To Chart is the *quantitative backbone* of SLP developed by **Richard Muther**. It is the direct input for constructing the **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** and the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)**.

Formally, the From-To Chart is constructed using the equation:
$$F_{ij} = \text{Total number of trips or load units from Department } i \text{ to Department } j$$

Where $F_{ij}$ represents the flow magnitude between departments $i$ and $j$. For an undirected material flow, $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$, and the matrix is **symmetric about the leading diagonal**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **principal of a large school** with 6 classrooms. You want to know which classrooms should be placed next to each other to **minimize students walking long distances** between them during the day (e.g., for lab practicals, library visits, sports, etc.). 

You ask every class teacher to maintain a **diary of every trip** students make to other classrooms in a week. At the end of the week, you sit down and create a big **table**:
- For each pair of classrooms (e.g., Class 1 and Class 2), you add up the number of trips.
- Class 1 ↔ Class 2 might have **120 trips** (lots of movement!)
- Class 4 ↔ Class 6 might have only **5 trips** (barely interact).

Now you say: *"Place Class 1 and Class 2 right next to each other, but Class 4 and Class 6 can be at opposite ends of the corridor."* 

**This table is exactly the From-To Chart.** The classrooms are the **departments**, and the trips are the **material flow**.

## 1.3 Classification of From-To Charts

| **Type** | **Quantity Measured** | **Primary Use** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material Flow Chart** | Weight, volume, or load count of material | Process-oriented plants | Quantifies physical tonnage |
| **Trip Frequency Chart** | Number of trips per unit time | Service facilities, warehouses | Quantifies movement intensity |
| **Personnel Movement Chart** | Number of people moving | Offices, hospitals, airports | Quantifies human flow |
| **Information Flow Chart** | Number of communications | Administrative layouts | Quantifies data flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The term *From-To Chart* in KTU Module 2 most commonly refers to the **Trip-based Chart**, where the cell entry is the number of trips or loads per day/shift between department pairs. The qualitative counterpart is the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)**.

## 1.4 Standard Unit Conventions

- **Trip-based units:** *trips/day*, *trips/shift*, *loads/day*
- **Weight-based units:** *kg/day*, *tonnes/month*
- **Relative scale (if absolute values unavailable):** *None (0)*, *Low (1)*, *Medium (2)*, *High (3)*, *Very High (4)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory of From-To Analysis

The From-To Chart rests on three foundational engineering principles:

### **Principle 1: The Closeness–Flow Proportionality**
> *Departments with high inter-flow should be located close together to **minimize the total material handling cost**.*

Mathematically, if $d_{ij}$ is the distance between departments $i$ and $j$, and $C$ is the cost per unit distance per unit flow, then the total material handling cost is:
$$\text{Total Cost} = C \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$$

To minimize this, departments with **large $F_{ij}$** must have **small $d_{ij}$**.

### **Principle 2: The Total Flow Indicator (TFI)**
For each department $i$, compute the sum of all flows *originating from* or *terminating at* $i$:
$$TFI_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{ij} \quad \text{(for the } i\text{-th row)}$$

Since the matrix is symmetric, this is identical to the column sum. **High TFI = highly connected department = should be central in the layout.**

### **Principle 3: Closeness Priority (Sum of Closings)**
For every pair $(i, j)$, the closeness priority $CP_{ij}$ is defined as:
$$CP_{ij} = TFI_i + TFI_j$$

**Higher $CP_{ij}$ ⟹ Stronger reason to place $i$ and $j$ adjacently.**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| $TFI_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{ij}$ | $F_{ij}$ = flow from $i$ to $j$, $TFI_i$ = Total Flow Indicator of dept $i$ | Ranks departments by connectedness |
| $CP_{ij} = TFI_i + TFI_j$ | $CP_{ij}$ = Closeness Priority of pair $(i,j)$ | Ranks pairs for adjacency |
| $TCR_k = \sum w_c \cdot n_c$ | $TCR_k$ = Total Closeness Rating of pair $k$, $w_c$ = weight of code $c$, $n_c$ = count | Used in ARC analysis |
| $I_{ij} = \dfrac{F_{ij}}{\max(F)}$ | Normalized flow intensity between $0$ and $1$ | Enables comparison across plants |
| $\text{Avg Flow} = \dfrac{2 \sum F_{ij}}{n(n-1)}$ | $n$ = number of departments | Sanity check for flow magnitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Closeness Rating Codes (used in ARC):**
> - **A** (Absolutely necessary) — weight $\approx 10^4$
> - **E** (Especially important) — weight $\approx 10^3$
> - **I** (Important) — weight $\approx 10^2$
> - **O** (Ordinary) — weight $\approx 10$
> - **U** (Unimportant) — weight $\approx 1$
> - **X** (Not desirable) — weight $\approx -10^4$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Construction Logic (Engineering Procedure)

1. **Identify all departments** — list every functional area, machine group, or workstation to be located.
2. **Select the unit of flow** — choose trips, loads, kg, etc., consistent with the analysis purpose.
3. **Collect flow data** — from process charts, routing sheets, interviews, or historical records.
4. **Build the matrix** — create an $n \times n$ grid where $n$ = number of departments.
5. **Populate the cells** — enter $F_{ij}$ for each pair; if symmetric, fill only the upper triangle and mirror.
6. **Compute TFI** — sum each row to get the total flow indicator of every department.
7. **Compute CP** — for every non-zero pair, add the TFI of both departments.
8. **Rank pairs** — sort pairs in descending order of $CP_{ij}$.
9. **Convert to ARC** — replace numerical flows with closeness codes (A/E/I/O/U/X) using breakpoints.
10. **Develop the layout** — place highest-CP pairs adjacent, iterate until all pairs are satisfied or compensated.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry Sector** | **Application of From-To Chart** |
|---|---|
| Automobile Assembly | Minimizing trolley/AGV travel between stamping, painting, and final assembly |
| Hospital Layout | Reducing patient/staff travel between OT, ICU, radiology, wards |
| Warehouse Design | Locating fast-moving SKUs near the dispatch dock |
| Office Design | Placing HR, Finance, and CEO office close due to frequent document flow |
| Airport Terminals | Positioning check-in, security, and gates for passenger flow optimization |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

### **Problem Statement**

A medium-scale manufacturing unit has **6 departments** numbered 1 through 6. The number of loads moved between the departments per day is given in the following table:

| **Pair (i, j)** | **Loads/day** | | **Pair (i, j)** | **Loads/day** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1, 2) | 100 | | (2, 6) | 0 |
| (1, 3) | 0 | | (3, 4) | 0 |
| (1, 4) | 200 | | (3, 5) | 100 |
| (1, 5) | 0 | | (3, 6) | 70 |
| (1, 6) | 50 | | (4, 5) | 90 |
| (2, 3) | 150 | | (4, 6) | 0 |
| (2, 4) | 80 | | (5, 6) | 40 |
| (2, 5) | 60 | | | |

**Tasks:**
1. Construct the From-To Chart (symmetric matrix).
2. Compute the Total Flow Indicator (TFI) for each department.
3. Compute the Closeness Priority (CP) for every pair.
4. Rank the department pairs.
5. Suggest an improved layout.

---

### **Step 1: Construct the Symmetric From-To Matrix**

Build an upper-triangular matrix with departments 1–6 on both axes. Mirror the given values across the leading diagonal. Diagonal cells (self-flow) are zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F = \begin{bmatrix}
F_{ij}
\end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 100 & 0 & 200 & 0 & 50 \\
100 & 0 & 150 & 80 & 60 & 0 \\
0 & 150 & 0 & 0 & 100 & 70 \\
200 & 80 & 0 & 0 & 90 & 0 \\
0 & 60 & 100 & 90 & 0 & 40 \\
50 & 0 & 70 & 0 & 40 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification of symmetry:** $F_{14} = F_{41} = 200$, $F_{23} = F_{32} = 150$, etc. ✓

---

### **Step 2: Compute Total Flow Indicator (TFI)**

Apply $TFI_i = \sum_{j=1}^{6} F_{ij}$ for each row (or column, equivalently):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TFI_1 &= 0 + 100 + 0 + 200 + 0 + 50 = 350 \\
TFI_2 &= 100 + 0 + 150 + 80 + 60 + 0 = 390 \\
TFI_3 &= 0 + 150 + 0 + 0 + 100 + 70 = 320 \\
TFI_4 &= 200 + 80 + 0 + 0 + 90 + 0 = 370 \\
TFI_5 &= 0 + 60 + 100 + 90 + 0 + 40 = 290 \\
TFI_6 &= 50 + 0 + 70 + 0 + 40 + 0 = 160
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Cross-check (sum of all TFI must equal twice the total of upper triangle):**
> $\sum TFI_i = 350+390+320+370+290+160 = 1880$
> $2 \times \sum_{\text{upper}} F_{ij} = 2 \times (100+0+200+0+50+150+80+60+0+0+100+70+90+0+40) = 2 \times 940 = 1880$ ✓

---

### **Step 3: Compute Closeness Priority (CP) for Every Pair**

Using $CP_{ij} = TFI_i + TFI_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CP_{1,2} &= 350 + 390 = 740 \\
CP_{1,3} &= 350 + 320 = 670 \\
CP_{1,4} &= 350 + 370 = 720 \\
CP_{1,5} &= 350 + 290 = 640 \\
CP_{1,6} &= 350 + 160 = 510 \\
CP_{2,3} &= 390 + 320 = 710 \\
CP_{2,4} &= 390 + 370 = 760 \\
CP_{2,5} &= 390 + 290 = 680 \\
CP_{2,6} &= 390 + 160 = 550 \\
CP_{3,4} &= 320 + 370 = 690 \\
CP_{3,5} &= 320 + 290 = 610 \\
CP_{3,6} &= 320 + 160 = 480 \\
CP_{4,5} &= 370 + 290 = 660 \\
CP_{4,6} &= 370 + 160 = 530 \\
CP_{5,6} &= 290 + 160 = 450
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Step 4: Rank the Pairs in Descending Order of CP**

| **Rank** | **Pair** | **CP Value** | **Flow Magnitude $F_{ij}$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (2, 4) | **760** | 80 |
| 2 | (1, 2) | **740** | 100 |
| 3 | (1, 4) | **720** | 200 |
| 4 | (2, 3) | **710** | 150 |
| 5 | (3, 4) | **690** | 0 |
| 6 | (2, 5) | **680** | 60 |
| 7 | (1, 3) | **670** | 0 |
| 8 | (4, 5) | **660** | 90 |
| 9 | (1, 5) | **640** | 0 |
| 10 | (3, 5) | **610** | 100 |
| 11 | (2, 6) | **550** | 0 |
| 12 | (4, 6) | **530** | 0 |
| 13 | (1, 6) | **510** | 50 |
| 14 | (3, 6) | **480** | 70 |
| 15 | (5, 6) | **450** | 40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting Observation:** Pair (3, 4) has $F_{34} = 0$ but a high $CP_{34} = 690$ because both are highly connected hubs. This pair is a "**bridge**" in graph terms — placing 3 and 4 adjacent reduces *overall* travel, even though they don't directly exchange material.

---

### **Step 5: Develop the Improved Layout**

Apply the **greedy adjacency heuristic**: place the highest-CP pair at the center, then attach the next-highest pair, breaking ties using $F_{ij}$ directly.

**Layout block diagram (text representation):**

```
            +-----------+
            |     3     |
            |  (TFI 320)|
            +-----+-----+
                  |
+--------+   +----+----+   +--------+
|   1    |---+    2    +---|   4    |
|(TFI350)|   | (TFI390)|   |(TFI370)|
+--------+   +----+----+   +---+----+
                  |             |
              +---+---+     +---+----+
              |   5   |     |   6    |
              |(TFI290)|   |(TFI160)|
              +-------+     +--------+
```

**Reasoning:**
1. **Department 2** (TFI = 390) is the most connected hub → place **centrally**.
2. **Department 4** (TFI = 370) is the second hub → place adjacent to 2 (matches highest CP = 760).
3. **Department 1** (TFI = 350) → adjacent to both 2 and 4 (matches CP = 740, 720).
4. **Department 3** (TFI = 320) → adjacent to 2 (matches CP = 710).
5. **Department 5** (TFI = 290) → adjacent to 2 and 4 (CP = 680, 660).
6. **Department 6** (TFI = 160, lowest) → at the periphery, adjacent to 1 (CP = 510).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This layout **minimizes the weighted inter-departmental distance** $\sum F_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$, which is the engineering objective of SLP.

---

### **Step 6: Total Material Handling Cost Reduction (Quantitative Validation)**

Assuming a unit cost of ₹1 per load per metre of travel, and a representative 2D layout where inter-departmental distances are approximated as:

| **Pair** | **$F_{ij}$** | **Distance (m) – Old layout** | **Distance (m) – New layout** | **Old Cost** | **New Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1, 2) | 100 | 20 | 5 | 2000 | 500 |
| (1, 4) | 200 | 30 | 8 | 6000 | 1600 |
| (2, 3) | 150 | 25 | 5 | 3750 | 750 |
| (2, 4) | 80 | 15 | 3 | 1200 | 240 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| **Total** | **940** | — | — | **₹28,500** | **₹7,800** |

> **Savings ≈ 72.6 %** — a dramatic reduction achieved *purely through layout*, with no equipment or process change. This is the **core economic justification** of From-To Chart analysis.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flowchart: From-To Chart Construction (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify all departments and activities] --> B[Step 2: Select unit of flow - trips or loads]
    B --> C[Step 3: Collect data from process charts and routing sheets]
    C --> D[Step 4: Build n x n symmetric matrix]
    D --> E[Step 5: Populate cells with flow values F sub ij]
    E --> F[Step 6: Compute Total Flow Indicator TFI sub i]
    F --> G[Step 7: Compute Closeness Priority CP sub ij]
    G --> H[Step 8: Rank all pairs in descending CP order]
    H --> I[Step 9: Convert numerical flows to closeness codes A E I O U X]
    I --> J[Step 10: Construct Activity Relationship Chart ARC]
    J --> K[Step 11: Develop Activity Relationship Diagram ARD]
    K --> L[Step 12: Generate Space Relationship Diagram with dimensional constraints]
    L --> M[Step 13: Produce final Block Layout and Master Plan]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SLP Methodology Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Location Analysis] --> P2[Phase 2: Overall Layouts]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Detailed Layouts]
    
    P2 --> FT[From-To Chart Construction]
    FT --> ARC[Activity Relationship Chart]
    FT --> ARD[Activity Relationship Diagram]
    ARC --> SRD[Space Relationship Diagram]
    ARD --> SRD
    SRD --> BL[Block Layout Plan]
    BL --> MP[Master Plan]
    
    P3 --> ML[Machine Layout]
    P3 --> CL[Cellular Layout]
    P3 --> PL[Process Layout]
    P3 --> FL[Fixed Position Layout]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Linkage Diagram: From-To Chart → ARC → Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FTC[From-To Chart\nQuantitative flow data] --> CP[Closeness Priority\nCP sub ij = TFI sub i + TFI sub j]
    CP --> RC[Ranked Pairs]
    RC --> TH{Apply Thresholds}
    TH -->|Top 20 percent| A_CODE[Code A - Absolutely necessary]
    TH -->|20 to 40 percent| E_CODE[Code E - Especially important]
    TH -->|40 to 60 percent| I_CODE[Code I - Important]
    TH -->|60 to 80 percent| O_CODE[Code O - Ordinary]
    TH -->|80 to 95 percent| U_CODE[Code U - Unimportant]
    TH -->|Bottom 5 percent| X_CODE[Code X - Not desirable]
    A_CODE --> ARC[Activity Relationship Chart]
    E_CODE --> ARC
    I_CODE --> ARC
    O_CODE --> ARC
    U_CODE --> ARC
    X_CODE --> ARC
    ARC --> ARD[Activity Relationship Diagram]
    ARD --> LAYOUT[Final Plant Layout]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Block Layout of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlantLayout[Improved Plant Layout - 6 Departments]
        D3[Department 3\nTFI = 320] 
        D2[Department 2\nTFI = 390\nCENTRAL HUB]
        D4[Department 4\nTFI = 370]
        D1[Department 1\nTFI = 350]
        D5[Department 5\nTFI = 290]
        D6[Department 6\nTFI = 160\nPERIPHERAL]
        
        D3 --- D2
        D2 --- D4
        D2 --- D1
        D2 --- D5
        D1 --- D4
        D4 --- D5
        D1 --- D6
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The `---` solid line indicates **direct adjacency** in the proposed layout. Notice that D6 (lowest TFI) is pushed to the periphery, while D2 (highest TFI) anchors the centre — this is the geometric essence of the From-To Chart principle.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Past Year Pattern Reference

The questions below are framed in the **exact KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) style** with appropriate mark distribution and choice-based internal selection.

---

### 📝 **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define a From-To Chart. State its significance in systematic layout planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **From-To Chart** is a tabular matrix representation that records the **volume, frequency, or intensity of material/personnel/information flow** between every pair of departments in a facility. It has departments listed along both the rows ("From") and columns ("To"), with cell entries $F_{ij}$ denoting the flow magnitude from department $i$ to department $j$.

**Significance in SLP (any 2 of the following for full marks):**
- (1 mark) It provides the **quantitative basis** for determining the closeness between departments, replacing intuition with numerical evidence.
- (1 mark) It is the **primary input** for constructing the Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) and the Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD).
- (1 mark) It enables **minimization of material handling cost** by guiding the placement of high-flow pairs adjacent to each other.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Distinguish between a From-To Chart and an Activity Relationship Chart (ARC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **From-To Chart** | **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of data** | Quantitative (numerical flows) | Qualitative (closeness codes: A, E, I, O, U, X) |
| **Input source** | Process charts, routing sheets, load data | From-To chart + non-flow relationships (noise, vibration, safety) |
| **Purpose** | Ranks pairs by Closeness Priority (CP) | Ranks pairs by qualitative importance and reason for closeness |
| **Output** | Numerical ranking + suggested pairs | Symbolic diagram for designer reference |

(1 mark each for any 3 distinguishing points)

---

### 📝 **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

---

## **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Model QP]**

A manufacturing plant has **5 departments** numbered 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. The number of loads (in tonnes/day) moved between the departments is given below:

| **From → To** | **Loads/day** | | **From → To** | **Loads/day** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | 80 | | 2 → 5 | 30 |
| 1 → 3 | 60 | | 3 → 4 | 100 |
| 1 → 4 | 0 | | 3 → 5 | 50 |
| 1 → 5 | 20 | | 4 → 5 | 70 |
| 2 → 3 | 0 | | | |
| 2 → 4 | 90 | | | |

**(a)** Construct the From-To Chart and compute the **Total Flow Indicator (TFI)** for each department. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Closeness Priority (CP)** for all pairs, rank them, and suggest an **improved layout** with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Construction and TFI**

**Step 1: Symmetric From-To Matrix**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 80 & 60 & 0 & 20 \\
80 & 0 & 0 & 90 & 30 \\
60 & 0 & 0 & 100 & 50 \\
0 & 90 & 100 & 0 & 70 \\
