# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Computer Tools

Computer applications in plant layout can be classified along a hierarchy of integration. KTU examiners often ask students to *list and explain* these categories — this is a high-yield topic.

### Tier 1 — Visualization & Drafting Tools

These are the foundational drafting platforms.

- **2D CAD**: AutoCAD, DraftSight, ZWCAD — used for general layout drawings, site plans, elevations.
- **3D CAD**: SolidWorks, CATIA, Autodesk Inventor — used for detailed equipment modeling.
- **Plant-Specific 3D**: **AVEVA E3D**, **Intergraph Smart 3D**, **Bentley OpenPlant** — purpose-built for piping, structural, and equipment modeling in a plant environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU answers, distinguish between *generic CAD* (AutoCAD) and *plant-specific 3D modeling* (AVEVA E3D). Generic CAD is 2D dominant; plant-specific tools are 3D dominant with built-in pipe routing intelligence.

### Tier 2 — Process Simulation Tools

These generate the input data for layout design — flow rates, pressures, temperatures, equipment sizes.

- **Aspen Plus / Aspen HYSYS** — Steady-state and dynamic process simulation.
- **ChemCAD** — Process simulation suite.
- **PRO/II (AVEVA)** — Refining and petrochemical simulation.
- **DWSIM (Open Source)** — Educational simulation.

**Workflow Linkage:**

$$
\text{Process Simulation} \xrightarrow{\text{Yields}} \text{Mass/Energy Balance} \xrightarrow{\text{Yields}} \text{Equipment Sizing} \xrightarrow{\text{Yields}} \text{Layout Inputs}
$$

### Tier 3 — Quantitative Layout Optimization Algorithms

These are the **mathematical engines** that compute the "best" arrangement of equipment.

- **CRAFT** (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique) — *Cost-based*
- **CORELAP** (Computerized Relationship Layout Planning) — *Relationship-based*
- **ALDEP** (Automated Layout Design Program) — *Relationship-based, multiple layouts*
- **MULTIPLE** (Multi-floor layout) — *Multi-story extension of CORELAP*

### Tier 4 — Discrete Event Simulation (DES)

Models dynamic behavior — material flow, congestion, queuing, downtime.

- **FlexSim**, **Arena**, **AnyLogic**, **Plant Simulation (Siemens)**.

### Tier 5 — Industry 4.0 / Integration Platforms

- **BIM** (Revit, Navisworks)
- **Digital Twin Platforms** (Siemens Xcelerator, Dassault 3DEXPERIENCE, GE Predix)
- **Plant Information Modeling (PIM)** — AVEVA PI System.

## 2.2 The Core Mathematical Foundation — Quantitative Layout Algorithms

### 2.2.1 CRAFT Algorithm — Cost-Based Optimization

CRAFT minimizes the **total material handling cost** between departments, subject to area constraints.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Begin with an initial layout (either manually entered or derived from CORELAP).
2. Calculate the centroids of each department:

$$
x_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} \cdot x_j}{a_i}, \quad y_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} \cdot y_j}{a_i}
$$

where $a_{ij}$ is the unit load between departments $i$ and $j$, and $a_i$ is the total load of department $i$.

3. Compute the rectilinear (Manhattan) distance between every pair of departments:

$$
d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert
$$

4. Calculate total material handling cost:

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}
$$

5. Perform **pairwise swapping** (or rotation) of two departments and recalculate cost. Keep the swap if cost decreases.
6. Iterate until no improvement is possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRAFT Limitation (Board-Favorite Question):** CRAFT works only on **rectangular departments** and assumes uniform flow between centroids. It cannot handle non-rectangular or multi-floor layouts directly.

### 2.2.2 CORELAP Algorithm — Relationship-Based Optimization

CORELAP uses **Muther's SLP closeness ratings** to maximize the total closeness score.

**Closeness Rating Scale (A–E / 1–5):**

| Rating | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Absolutely necessary | Must be adjacent |
| **E** | Especially important | Very close |
| **I** | Important | Close |
| **O** | Ordinary closeness | Moderate proximity acceptable |
| **U** | Unimportant | No preference |
| **X** | Undesirable | Should be far apart |

**Algorithm Logic:**

1. Construct the **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)**.
2. Compute the **Total Closeness Rating (TCR)** for each department:

$$
TCR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} R_{ij}
$$

where $R_{ij}$ is the numerical weight assigned to the closeness rating between $i$ and $j$.

3. Place the department with the highest TCR first at the center of the plot.
4. Iteratively place the next-highest TCR department adjacent to a placed department whose relationship matches the closeness rating.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Manhattan (Rectilinear) Distance | $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | CRAFT cost calculation |
| Euclidean Distance | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Straight-line crane access |
| Total Material Handling Cost | $C = \sum f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ | CRAFT objective function |
| Department Centroid (x) | $x_i = \sum (a_{ij} \cdot x_j) / a_i$ | CRAFT centroid computation |
| Total Closeness Rating | $TCR_i = \sum R_{ij}$ | CORELAP placement priority |
| Number of Pair Swaps in CRAFT | $N_{swaps} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Iteration complexity |
| Plot Area Utilization Factor | $\eta = A_{equipment} / A_{total\,plot}$ | Space efficiency metric |
| Payback Period (CAD Investment) | $PB = I / S$ | Justification of software cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Rule Applied:** All absolute value bars $\vert x \vert$ are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Layouts** — Companies like Reliance and Indian Oil use AVEVA E3D for full 3D plant modeling with integrated piping stress analysis.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants** — BIM + Digital Twin ensures FDA-compliant cleanroom design and validation.
- **Piping Cost Optimization** — CRAFT-based tools have demonstrated **20–25% piping cost savings** in published case studies.
- **Safety Visualization** — 3D models allow safety engineers to simulate fire, gas dispersion, and evacuation routes.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — CRAFT Cost Calculation (Single Swap Iteration)

**Problem Context:** A small plant has **3 departments** arranged on a grid. Compute the initial total material handling cost and evaluate one swap.

**Given Data:**

| Department | Area (m²) | Center (x, y) | Load to B | Load to C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 100 | (2, 1) | 50 trips/day | 30 trips/day |
| **B** | 80 | (6, 2) | — | 20 trips/day |
| **C** | 60 | (4, 5) | — | — |

Cost per trip per unit distance: $c = ₹5/\text{m}$.

### Step 1 — Compute Initial Rectilinear Distances

For pair **A–B**:

$$
d_{AB} = \vert x_A - x_B \vert + \vert y_A - y_B \vert = \vert 2 - 6 \vert + \vert 1 - 2 \vert = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

For pair **A–C**:

$$
d_{AC} = \vert 2 - 4 \vert + \vert 1 - 5 \vert = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ m}
$$

For pair **B–C**:

$$
d_{BC} = \vert 6 - 4 \vert + \vert 2 - 5 \vert = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Initial Total Cost

$$
C_{initial} = f_{AB} \cdot d_{AB} \cdot c + f_{AC} \cdot d_{AC} \cdot c + f_{BC} \cdot d_{BC} \cdot c
$$

$$
C_{initial} = (50)(5)(5) + (30)(6)(5) + (20)(5)(5)
$$

$$
C_{initial} = 1250 + 900 + 500 = 2650 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Perform a Swap (A ↔ B)

New centers:
- **A** at (6, 2)
- **B** at (2, 1)

Recompute distances:

$$
d_{AB}^{new} = \vert 6 - 2 \vert + \vert 2 - 1 \vert = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{AC}^{new} = \vert 6 - 4 \vert + \vert 2 - 5 \vert = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{BC}^{new} = \vert 2 - 4 \vert + \vert 1 - 5 \vert = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute New Total Cost

$$
C_{new} = (50)(5)(5) + (30)(5)(5) + (20)(6)(5)
$$

$$
C_{new} = 1250 + 750 + 600 = 2600 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Improvement

$$
\Delta C = C_{new} - C_{initial} = 2600 - 2650 = -50 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

Since $\Delta C < 0$, the swap **reduces cost** and is **accepted**.

**Cost Reduction Percentage:**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_{initial}} \times 100 = \frac{50}{2650} \times 100 \approx 1.89\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** In KTU solutions, students must (i) state the algorithm's objective, (ii) show explicit distance calculations using $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation, and (iii) clearly state the *acceptance criterion* (cost must decrease).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — CORELAP Placement Priority

**Problem Context:** Four departments with the following closeness ratings. Determine the order of placement.

| Pair | Rating | Numerical Weight $R_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| A–B | **A** (Absolutely necessary) | 16 |
| A–C | **E** (Especially important) | 8 |
| A–D | **O** (Ordinary) | 2 |
| B–C | **I** (Important) | 4 |
| B–D | **U** (Unimportant) | 1 |
| C–D | **X** (Undesirable) | -16 |

### Step 1 — Compute TCR for Each Department

$$
TCR_A = R_{AB} + R_{AC} + R_{AD} = 16 + 8 + 2 = 26
$$

$$
TCR_B = R_{BA} + R_{BC} + R_{BD} = 16 + 4 + 1 = 21
$$

$$
TCR_C = R_{CA} + R_{CB} + R_{CD} = 8 + 4 - 16 = -4
$$

$$
TCR_D = R_{DA} + R_{DB} + R_{DC} = 2 + 1 - 16 = -13
$$

### Step 2 — Rank Departments

$$
TCR_A (26) > TCR_B (21) > TCR_C (-4) > TCR_D (-13)
$$

### Step 3 — Placement Order

1. **A** placed first (highest TCR = 26) at center.
2. **B** placed adjacent to A (A–B = "A", highest residual rating).
3. **C** placed adjacent to A or B (whichever satisfies A–C = "E" or B–C = "I").
4. **D** placed **far** from C (C–D = "X" undesirable).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Tool?

| Engineering Need | Recommended Tool Category | Example Software |
|---|---|---|
| Initial draft site plan | 2D CAD | AutoCAD |
| Detailed 3D equipment modeling | 3D CAD | SolidWorks |
| Process data & equipment sizing | CAPE simulation | Aspen HYSYS |
| Quantitative layout optimization (cost) | CRAFT family | CRAFT / custom Python-MIP |
| Qualitative layout (closeness relationships) | SLP + CORELAP | CORELAP |
| Material flow & congestion study | Discrete Event Simulation | FlexSim, Arena |
| Clash detection during detailed design | Plant 3D + review | AVEVA E3D, Navisworks |
| Full lifecycle facility integration | BIM / Digital Twin | Revit, Siemens Xcelerator |
| Multi-floor / vertical plant | MULTIPLE algorithm | MULTIPLE |
| Safety & evacuation visualization | 3D + simulation | Pathfinder, FDS |

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Integration of Computer Tools in Plant Layout

This diagram shows the **functional architecture** linking process data, simulation, optimization, drafting, and lifecycle platforms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PFD[Process Flow Diagram PFD] --> SIM[Process Simulation Aspen HYSYS]
    SIM --> MB[Mass and Energy Balance Data]
    MB --> ES[Equipment Sizing Vessels Pumps Compressors]
    ES --> LO[Layout Optimization Engine CRAFT CORELAP ALDEP]
    LO --> OL[Optimized 2D Layout]
    OL --> CAD[3D Plant Modeling AVEVA E3D Smart3D]
    CAD --> CLASH[Clash Detection Navisworks]
    CLASH --> PID[P and ID Integration]
    PID --> BIM[BIM Platform Revit]
    BIM --> DT[Digital Twin Siemens Xcelerator]
    DT --> OP[Plant Operations and Maintenance]
    OP --> LOOP[Feedback Loop for Continuous Improvement]
    LOOP --> LO

    classDef data fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef process fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef output fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef integration fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843

    class PFD,SIM,MB,ES data
    class LO,OL,CAD process
    class CLASH,PID,BIM,DT,OP output
    class LOOP integration
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrow from `OP` (Plant Operations) back to `LO` (Layout Optimization) represents the **feedback loop** — actual operating data (flow bottlenecks, congestion) is fed back into the layout engine for re-optimization. This is the essence of a **Digital Twin**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — CRAFT Algorithm Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start CRAFT]) --> INI[Read Initial Layout Coordinates and Loads]
    INI --> CALC1[Calculate Centroids of Each Department]
    CALC1 --> DC1[Compute Rectilinear Distances dij]
    DC1 --> CC1[Calculate Total Material Handling Cost Cinitial]
    CC1 --> SWAP[Generate Next Pairwise Swap A swap B]
    SWAP --> CALC2[Recompute Centroids]
    CALC2 --> DC2[Recompute Distances dijnew]
    DC2 --> CC2[Calculate New Cost Cnew]
    CC2 --> DEC{Cnew less than Cinitial}
    DEC -->|Yes| ACC[Accept Swap and Update Layout]
    DEC -->|No| REJ[Reject Swap and Revert]
    ACC --> CHK{More Swaps to Try}
    REJ --> CHK
    CHK -->|Yes| SWAP
    CHK -->|No| END([Optimal Layout Found])
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Computer-Aided Plant Design (CAPD). List any four advantages of using CAPD over manual layout design.**

**Model Answer:**

Computer-Aided Plant Design (CAPD) is the application of computer-based tools and software for the planning, drafting, modeling, and optimization of industrial plant layouts by integrating process, mechanical, piping, electrical, and instrumentation data.

**Four advantages of CAPD:**

1. **Faster Iteration** — Multiple layout alternatives can be generated and evaluated in hours instead of weeks.
2. **Clash Detection** — 3D modeling identifies physical interferences between pipes, structures, and equipment before construction, preventing costly rework.
3. **Cost Optimization** — Algorithms like CRAFT systematically minimize material handling and piping costs.
4. **Improved Visualization** — 3D walkthroughs and Digital Twins enable better stakeholder communication and operator training.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of Digital Twin in the context of plant layout and operation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Digital Twin** is a real-time, virtual replica of a physical plant that integrates geometric (3D model), physical (process simulation), and operational (live sensor/SCADA) data to mirror the behavior of the actual facility.

In the context of plant layout, a Digital Twin serves three critical roles:

- **Design Validation** — Simulates layout performance (material flow, congestion, safety) before construction.
- **Operations Monitoring** — Live sensor feeds update the virtual model, enabling anomaly detection.
- **Predictive Maintenance** — AI modules predict equipment failure, allowing proactive intervention.

It bridges the historical gap between *engineering design* and *plant operations* through continuous data synchronization.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice — Full 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical structure of computer applications used in plant layout design**. Discuss the role of process simulation, optimization algorithms, 3D modeling, and BIM/Digital Twin platforms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plant has **four departments** A, B, C, D with the following data. Use the **CRAFT algorithm** to compute the initial total material handling cost and evaluate the swap of departments **A and D**.

**Initial Centers (x, y in meters):** A(2,1), B(5,3), C(7,6), D(1,4)

**Inter-departmental loads (trips/day):** $f_{AB}=40$, $f_{AC}=25$, $f_{AD}=60$, $f_{BC}=30$, $f_{BD}=20$, $f_{CD}=35$

Cost per trip per meter = **₹4**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Initial Rectilinear Distances:**

$$
d_{AB} = \vert 2 - 5 \vert + \vert 1 - 3 \vert = 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{AC} = \vert 2 - 7 \vert + \vert 1 - 6 \vert = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{AD} = \vert 2 - 1 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{BC} = \vert 5 - 7 \vert + \vert 3 - 6 \vert = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{BD} = \vert 5 - 1 \vert + \vert 3 - 4 \vert = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{CD} = \vert 7 - 1 \vert + \vert 6 - 4 \vert = 6 + 2 = 8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial Total Cost:**

$$
C_{init} = (40)(5)(4) + (25)(10)(4) + (60)(4)(4) + (30)(5)(4) + (20)(5)(4) + (35)(8)(4)
$$

$$
C_{init} = 800 + 1000 + 960 + 600 + 400 + 1120 = 4880 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Swap A ↔ D:**

New centers: A(1,4), D(2,1).

$$
d_{AB}^{new} = \vert 1 - 5 \vert + \vert 4 - 3 \vert = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{AC}^{new} = \vert 1 - 7 \vert + \vert 4 - 6 \vert = 6 + 2 = 8 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{AD}^{new} = \vert 1 - 2 \vert + \vert 4 - 1 \vert = 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{BC}^{new} = 5 \text{ m} \quad \text{(unchanged, no B/C swap)}
$$

$$
d_{BD}^{new} = \vert 5 - 2 \vert + \vert 3 - 1 \vert = 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{CD}^{new} = \vert 7 - 2 \vert + \vert 6 - 1 \vert = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — New Total Cost:**

$$
C_{new} = (40)(5)(4) + (25)(8)(4) + (60)(4)(4) + (30)(5)(4) + (20)(5)(4) + (35)(10)(4)
$$

$$
C_{new} = 800 + 800 + 960 + 600 + 400 + 1400 = 4960 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
\Delta C = C_{new} - C_{init} = 4960 - 4880 = +80 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

Since $\Delta C > 0$, the swap **increases cost** → **REJECT the swap**.

**Conclusion:** Original layout is preferred for this pairwise swap.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **CORELAP algorithm** with its closeness rating scale. How does it differ from CRAFT in approach and objective? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the following departments and closeness ratings, determine the **placement order** using the CORELAP algorithm and justify your ranking. **(7 Marks)**

| Department Pair | Closeness Rating | Numerical Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | A | 16 |
| 1–3 | E | 8 |
| 1–4 | O | 2 |
| 2–3 | I | 4 |
| 2–4 | U | 1 |
| 3–4 | X | –16 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute TCR for each department:**

$$
TCR_1 = R_{12} + R_{13} + R_{14} = 16 + 8 + 2 = 26
$$

$$
TCR_2 = R_{21} + R_{23} + R_{24} = 16 + 4 + 1 = 21
$$

$$
TCR_3 = R_{31} + R_{32} + R_{34} = 8 + 4 - 16 = -4
$$

$$
TCR_4 = R_{41} + R_{42} + R_{43} = 2 + 1 - 16 = -13
$$

**Step 2 — Rank:**

$$
TCR_1 (26) > TCR_2 (21) > TCR_3 (-4) > TCR_4 (-13)
$$

**Step 3 — Placement Order:**

1. **Department 1** placed first (TCR = 26, highest).
2. **Department 2** placed second — adjacent to Dept. 1 (their rating is "A").
3. **Department 3** placed third — close to Dept. 1 (rating "E") and Dept. 2 (rating "I").
4. **Department 4** placed last — **far from Dept. 3** because their rating is "X" (undesirable).

**Justification:** Department 1 has the strongest cumulative relationships and is therefore the anchor of the layout. Department 4 has strong negative interactions (X with Dept. 3) and should be isolated to maintain safety and operational efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Layout Algorithm Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to write the algorithm's objective explicitly.** Always begin with: *"CRAFT minimizes total material handling cost..."* or *"CORELAP maximizes total closeness rating..."*
> 2. **Using Euclidean distance instead of rectilinear (Manhattan) distance** in CRAFT problems. KTU standard is **rectilinear** for plant layouts because material moves along orthogonal corridors, not diagonally.
> 3. **Not stating the swap acceptance criterion.** Examiner expects: *"Swap is accepted if $C_{new} < C_{init}$."*
> 4. **Confusing CRAFT with CORELAP.** CRAFT = *cost-based, quantitative*. CORELAP = *relationship-based, qualitative (SLP-driven)*.
> 5. **Omitting units in cost calculations.** Always write *₹/day* or *₹/year* explicitly.
> 6. **Spelling acronyms incorrectly.** CRAFT ≠ "Craft," CORELAP ≠ "Corelap." Write in uppercase with full form on first mention.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computer Applications in Layout Designs**

### Core Concepts

- **CAPD** integrates CAD, CAPE, optimization, simulation, and lifecycle management.
- **CAD** handles geometry; **CAPE** handles process data; **CRAFT/CORELAP** handle optimization; **BIM/Digital Twin** handle lifecycle integration.

### Algorithms to Master

- **CRAFT** — Minimizes $C = \sum f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$; uses rectilinear distance; pairwise swap iteration.
- **CORELAP** — Maximizes total closeness rating using TCR; uses Muther's SLP rating scale (A, E, I, O, U, X).
- **ALDEP** — Generates multiple feasible layouts, ranked by closeness.

### Software Categories (Memorize 1–2 examples per category)

- 2D CAD → AutoCAD
- 3D Plant → AVEVA E3D, Intergraph Smart 3D
- Process Sim → Aspen HYSYS, ChemCAD
- DES → FlexSim, Arena
- BIM → Revit, Navisworks
- Digital Twin → Siemens Xcelerator, Dassault 3DEXPERIENCE

### Distinctions Examiners Love to Test

- **CRAFT vs. CORELAP** — Cost vs. Relationship; Quantitative vs. Qualitative.
- **CAD vs. BIM** — Geometry-only vs. Information-rich 3D model.
- **Simulation vs. Digital Twin** — Offline static model vs. real-time connected replica.
- **Generic CAD vs. Plant 3D** — AutoCAD is not optimized for pipe routing.

### Numerical Problem Pattern

1. Compute all rectilinear distances (initial layout).
2. Calculate initial total cost.
3. Perform specified swap(s).
4. Recompute cost.
5. State decision (accept/reject) with justification.

### Common Distance & Cost Formulas

$$
d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert \quad \text{(Rectilinear)}
$$

$$
d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2} \quad \text{(Euclidean)}
$$

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}
$$

$$
TCR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} R_{ij}
$$

### Final Strategic Note

KTU Module 2 (Computer Applications) questions typically combine **a descriptive part** (classification, advantages, algorithms) worth **7 marks** and **a numerical part** (CRAFT cost calculation or CORELAP ranking) worth **7 marks**. Practice both. Always define acronyms, show all distance steps, and explicitly state acceptance criteria.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Aspects of Design: Environmental Factors like Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmental Aspects of Design: Environmental Factors like Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Environmental Aspects in Plant Design — The Foundation

In the context of **Plant Layout and Design**, *Environmental Aspects* refer to the physical, ambient, and atmospheric conditions prevailing inside and around industrial premises that directly influence worker comfort, safety, productivity, product quality, and operational efficiency. These include **lighting, ventilation, temperature, humidity, noise, vibration, air quality, and ergonomics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Environmental aspects of design deal with the integration of human physiological, psychological, and safety requirements into the engineering layout of a plant so as to create a working environment that is healthy, safe, productive, and compliant with statutory standards (Factories Act 1948, IS 3646, and ILO conventions).*

## 1.2 The "Lighting" Environmental Factor

**Lighting** is the deliberate provision of visible light (illumination) to enable accurate and comfortable visual performance of industrial tasks. In the KTU framework, lighting is one of the most heavily weighted environmental factors because the human eye depends on adequate, uniform, and glare-free illumination for roughly **70–80 %** of all information perception in a manufacturing environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Metric (must memorize):**
> - **Illuminance (E)** — measured in **Lux (lx)** or **Foot-candles (fc)**
> - **Luminous Flux (F or Φ)** — measured in **Lumens (lm)**
> - **Luminous Intensity (I)** — measured in **Candela (cd)**
> - **Luminance (L)** — measured in **Candela per square metre (cd/m²)**
> - **Conversion:** $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Lighting" Analogy

Think of your own home. You do **not** install the same bulb in every room:
- The **kitchen** has bright, shadow-free white light → so you can safely chop vegetables.
- The **bedroom** has warm, dim, low-glare light → so you can relax and sleep.
- The **study table** has a focused, high-lux lamp → so you can read fine print.
- The **garage workshop** has uniform, shadow-free, high-intensity lighting → so you can weld or drill safely.

A factory follows exactly the same principle, but **scaled up, codified, and enforced by law**. The "type of activity" in each zone of the plant decides the **lux level**, the **lamp type**, the **luminaire arrangement**, and the **glare control** needed. The plant layout engineer must design the *light environment* just as carefully as the *equipment arrangement*.

## 1.4 Why Lighting is a Critical Design Parameter

| Functional Need | Consequence of Inadequate Lighting |
|---|---|
| Visual accuracy in inspection | Defective products escape quality control |
| Operator safety | Accidents, eye-strain, occupational disease |
| Productivity | Fatigue, slow throughput, absenteeism |
| Statutory compliance | Prosecution under the Factories Act & IS 3646 |
| Energy economics | Wasted electricity, high operating cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Hook (KTU Favourite):** Section 14 of the **Indian Factories Act, 1948** mandates that effective provision shall be made in every factory for securing and maintaining sufficient and suitable lighting, whether natural or artificial, in all parts of the factory where persons are working or passing through.

## 1.5 Classification of Lighting in Plants

**A. Natural Lighting (Daylight)**
- Provided by **skylights, windows, monitors (saw-tooth roofs), and light shelves**.
- Cheapest, healthiest, and most variable source.
- The **Daylight Factor (DF)** quantifies its effectiveness.

**B. Artificial Lighting**
- **General (uniform) lighting** — uniform illumination over the whole area; used in storages, lobbies.
- **Localized (or Localized-general) lighting** — a combination of general lighting + task-specific luminaires; used in machine shops.
- **Local (spot) lighting** — a single low-wattage lamp concentrated on a small task; used for inspection, gauge reading.

**C. Supplementary / Emergency Lighting**
- Battery-backed lamps for safe evacuation during power failure; mandatory for hazardous areas per **IS 3646 (Part 2)**.

## 1.6 Common Lamp Types in Industry (Concept Map)

- **Incandescent** — poor efficiency (10–15 lm/W), warm colour, short life.
- **Fluorescent (CFL/T5/T8)** — high efficiency (50–80 lm/W), long life, requires ballast.
- **Mercury Vapour (MV)** — bluish-white, 40–60 lm/W, used historically in factories.
- **Sodium Vapour (HPSV / LPSV)** — yellow-orange, very efficient (100–150 lm/W), used in yards, foundries.
- **Metal Halide (MH)** — excellent colour rendering, 80–100 lm/W, used for precision inspection.
- **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** — modern standard, 100–150 lm/W, long life (50,000 h), tunable spectrum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Good Industrial Lighting Design

A lighting design is judged against **five engineering criteria** (Katz & Brezina / R.G. Hopwood standards adopted by IS 3646):

1. **Adequate Illuminance Level** — the average lux on the working plane must equal the value prescribed in **IS 3646 (Part 1)** for that class of work.
2. **Uniformity of Illumination** — the ratio $\frac{E_{min}}{E_{avg}} \geq 0.8$ for general industrial lighting.
3. **Freedom from Glare** — controlled by luminaire shielding, mounting height, and finish of room surfaces.
4. **Suitable Colour Rendering & Colour Temperature** — a Colour Rendering Index (CRI) $\geq 80$ for inspection tasks.
5. **Freedom from Stroboscopic Effect (Flicker)** — important in fluorescent and HID lamps with rotating machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Question Trigger:** *"Explain the Lumen method of lighting design."* — the complete derivation and application of the **Lumen Formula** is the single most tested topic on Lighting.

## 2.2 The Lumen Method — Underlying Logic

The **Lumen Method** (also called the *Light Flux Method* or *Watts-per-square-metre method in reverse*) estimates the number of luminaires required to achieve a target illuminance in a room, accounting for the fact that **not all emitted lumens reach the work-plane** (some are lost in the luminaire itself, in the room surfaces, and over time).

The engineering logic follows this flow:

- **Step 1 — Fix the Required Illuminance (E):** Look up **IS 3646 Table 1** for the activity.
- **Step 2 — Compute the Work-plane Area (A):** $A = L \times B$ in m².
- **Step 3 — Compute Total Lumens Required at Work-plane:** $\Phi_{req} = E \times A$ (in lumen).
- **Step 4 — Account for Utilization Factor (U or UF):** It is the ratio of lumens reaching the work-plane to the lumens emitted by the lamps. It depends on **Room Index (K)**, **luminaire type**, and **room reflectances (ceiling, walls, floor)**.
- **Step 5 — Account for Maintenance Factor (M or MF):** It accounts for lamp ageing, dust, and luminaire depreciation. $M = \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{depreciation fraction}}$.
- **Step 6 — Compute the Number of Lamps (N):** $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}$, where $\Phi_{lamp}$ is the rated lumen output of ONE lamp.

## 2.3 The Room Index (K) — A Critical Auxiliary

The Utilization Factor tables are indexed by the **Room Index (K)**:

$$K = \frac{L \times B}{H_m \times (L + B)}$$

where $L$ = length, $B$ = width, and $H_m$ = **mounting height** of the luminaire **above the work-plane** (not the floor). This is the *why* of the formula — a tall, narrow room absorbs more flux than a square, low-ceilinged room of equal area.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verified, KTU-compliant, and frequently tested.**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Unit | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illuminance | **lux** | $1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}$ | Number of lamps | dimensionless | $N$ rounded **up** to integer |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{L \times B}{H_m \times (L+B)}$ | Room Index | dimensionless | $0.6 \leq K \leq 5$ |
| 4 | $DF = \dfrac{E_{inside}}{E_{outside}} \times 100$ | Daylight Factor | **percent (%)** | Good daylight: DF $\geq 5\%$ |
| 5 | $SHR = \dfrac{S}{H_m}$ | Spacing-to-Height Ratio | dimensionless | $0.5 \leq SHR \leq 1.5$ |
| 6 | $M = 1 - d$ (approx) | Maintenance Factor | dimensionless | $0.6 \leq M \leq 0.9$ |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$ | Luminous Intensity | **candela (cd)** | $\omega$ = solid angle in **steradian** |
| 8 | $L = \dfrac{I}{A_{proj}}$ | Luminance | **cd/m²** | $A_{proj}$ = projected area |
| 9 | $\eta_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi_{lamp}}{P_{lamp}}$ | Luminous efficacy | **lm/W** | Incand.≈12; LED≈120 |
| 10 | $U = 1 - (\text{absorption in room surfaces})$ | Utilization Factor | dimensionless | $0.3 \leq U \leq 0.7$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **PIPE / TABLE RULE:** Inside the markdown table above, the vertical bar $\vert$ has been intentionally replaced by `\\vert` in surrounding text to keep the table parser safe. In your answer book, write `$\\vert$` for modulus or "such that".

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Energy Audit & Green Building (GRIHA, LEED):** Lighting consumes **~25–30 %** of industrial electricity. The Lumen method lets you size luminaires *just enough*, avoiding the 3-typical errors of over-design.
- **Hazardous Area Lighting (IS 5571 / IS 9620):** Explosion-proof luminaires, intrinsically safe circuits — sizing is still done by the Lumen method, but with strict $U=0.4$ caps and $M=0.6$ pessimistic values.
- **Process Industry Sub-Fields:** Inspection of pharma tablets (CRI $\geq 90$, 1000+ lx), paint mixing (explosion-proof), paper mills (waterproof IP-65 luminaires), food processing (hygienic, easily cleanable, no glass).
- **Real-world utility:** Lumen-method outputs feed directly into **single-line electrical diagrams**, **cable sizing**, **lighting DB schedules**, and **BOQ (Bill of Quantities)** — i.e. the Lumen method is the *bridge* between civil layout and electrical design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lumen Formula

The Lumen Formula is not empirical — it is derived from first principles of energy balance. We start from the definition of illuminance and introduce correction factors one by one.

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal case (no losses).**
If every lumen emitted by the lamp reaches the work-plane uniformly, the illuminance $E_{ideal}$ is given by the standard definition:

$$E_{ideal} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Replace $\Phi_{total}$ with $N \times \Phi_{lamp}$.**
The total emitted flux equals the number of lamps times the rated flux of one lamp:

$$E_{ideal} = \frac{N \times \Phi_{lamp}}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Utilization Factor $U$.**
In reality, only a fraction $U$ of the emitted flux reaches the work-plane (the rest is absorbed by ceiling, walls, floor, or trapped inside the luminaire):

$$E_{work\text{-}plane} = \frac{N \times \Phi_{lamp} \times U}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Maintenance Factor $M$.**
Over months of operation, lamp output decays and luminaires gather dust. We multiply by a de-rating factor $M < 1$ so that the design illuminance is maintained *throughout* the lamp life, not just on day one:

$$E_{maintained} = \frac{N \times \Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for the number of lamps $N$.**

$$N = \frac{E_{maintained} \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}$$

This is the **Lumen Formula** in its final, board-exam-ready form. Every term has a physical meaning and can be independently measured or looked up in a table.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — KTU 2024 Style (Basic)

**Problem (typical KTU part-b 7-mark sub-question):**
A machine shop measures **40 m × 15 m**. The required maintained illuminance is **300 lux**. You may use **400 W high-pressure mercury vapour lamps** each giving **20,000 lumens**, mounted at a height of **6 m above the work-plane**. Given $U = 0.5$ and $M = 0.8$, find the number of lamps required and suggest a suitable layout.

**Solution — Step-by-step (no skipping, no abbreviation):**

**Step (a): Identify the area.**
$$A = L \times B = 40 \times 15 = 600 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (b): Total lumens required on the work-plane (ideal).**
$$\Phi_{req} = E \times A = 300 \times 600 = 180{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step (c): Apply U and M to get the lumens that must be *emitted*.**
$$\Phi_{emit} = \frac{180{,}000}{U \times M} = \frac{180{,}000}{0.5 \times 0.8} = \frac{180{,}000}{0.40} = 450{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step (d): Divide by the rated output of one lamp.**
$$N = \frac{450{,}000}{20{,}000} = 22.5$$

**Step (e): Round up to the next integer.**
$$\boxed{N = 23 \text{ lamps (minimum)}}$$

**Step (f): Suggested layout (5 rows × 5 columns = 25 lamps — gives better uniformity and a buffer).**
- Spacing along length: $40/5 = 8 \text{ m}$
- Spacing along width: $15/5 = 3 \text{ m}$
- Verify $SHR$: assuming $H_m = 6 \text{ m}$, $SHR = 8/6 \approx 1.33$ → acceptable for a wide-beam luminaire (typical MV luminaire $SHR = 1.0$–$1.5$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key teaching point:** In Step (f) we overshoot the minimum (25 > 23) for *uniformity* — exam answers that stop at 22.5 lamps are correct *arithmetically* but lose a half-mark for not addressing layout uniformity.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Daylight Factor (KTU 3-mark Favourite)

**Problem:** A window of effective area **4 m²** admits **30,000 lumens** into a room of floor area **30 m²**. The simultaneous outside illuminance on a horizontal plane is **10,000 lux**. Find the **Daylight Factor (DF)**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total daylight flux reaching the floor (assuming uniform distribution).**
$$\Phi_{inside} = E_{inside} \times A_{floor} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_{inside} = \frac{\Phi_{inside}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{30{,}000}{30} = 1{,}000 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 2: Apply the DF definition.**
$$DF = \frac{E_{inside}}{E_{outside}} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}000}{10{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{DF = 10\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** DF = 10% is considered **excellent** for industrial interiors (IS 3646 recommends a minimum DF of 2–5% for general factory areas). The room is well daylit.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Inverse Problem (Given N, Find E)

**Problem:** A **warehouse 25 m × 20 m** is fitted with **20 fluorescent luminaires**, each housing **2 tubes of 40 W** rated at **3,000 lumens** per tube. Given $U = 0.6$, $M = 0.75$, calculate the **maintained illuminance**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total lumens emitted.**
Total tubes = $20 \times 2 = 40$. Total emitted flux = $40 \times 3{,}000 = 120{,}000$ lm.

**Step 2: Useful flux reaching the work-plane (account for U and M).**
$$\Phi_{useful} = 120{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 0.75 = 54{,}000 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3: Illuminance.**
$$A = 25 \times 20 = 500 \text{ m}^2$$

$$E = \frac{\Phi_{useful}}{A} = \frac{54{,}000}{500} = 108 \text{ lux}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 108 \text{ lux}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering check:** IS 3646 recommends **100–150 lux** for general warehouse storage — the design is **just adequate** but on the lower end, suggesting either a few more luminaires or higher-wattage tubes should be specified.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — What Changes When Each Parameter Changes?

| Scenario | $E$ target | $A$ | $\Phi_{lamp}$ | $U$ | $M$ | Effect on $N$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Doubling the room area | same | $\times 2$ | same | same | same | $N \times 2$ |
| Switching from MV to LED ($\Phi$ doubles) | same | same | $\times 2$ | same | same | $N \times 0.5$ |
| Dirtier environment (paint shop) | same | same | same | same | $0.6$ | $N \times 1.33$ |
| Precision electronics inspection | $\times 5$ | same | same | same | same | $N \times 5$ |
| Wider room (U increases) | same | same | same | $0.6 \to 0.7$ | same | $N \times 0.83$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam interpretation line (write this verbatim if asked):**
> *"The number of luminaires varies **directly** with target illuminance $E$ and area $A$, and **inversely** with lamp flux $\Phi_{lamp}$, utilization factor $U$, and maintenance factor $M$."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Industrial Lighting Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify work activity in each zone] --> B[Step 2: Look up required illuminance E from IS 3646]
    B --> C[Step 3: Determine room dimensions L and B]
    C --> D[Step 4: Compute area A equal to L times B]
    D --> E[Step 5: Compute Room Index K from L, B, Hm]
    E --> F[Step 6: Select luminaire type and read U from manufacturer table]
    F --> G[Step 7: Decide Maintenance Factor M based on environment dirtiness]
    G --> H[Step 8: Select lamp wattage and rated lumen output Phi lamp]
    H --> I[Step 9: Apply Lumen Formula N equal to E times A divided by Phi lamp times U times M]
    I --> J[Step 10: Round N up to integer and check Spacing Height Ratio SHR]
    J --> K{Is SHR within luminaire range?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Step 11: Finalise layout rows and columns on layout drawing]
    K -->|No| M[Step 12: Adjust mounting height Hm or change luminaire]
    M --> I
    L --> N[Step 13: Compute Daylight Factor DF for energy audit]
    N --> O[Step 14: Issue BoQ and single line diagram to electrical designer]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Plant Lighting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[LT Panel] --> P2[Distribution Board DB1]
    P2 --> P3[General Lighting Circuit 415V AC]
    P2 --> P4[Task Lighting Circuit]
    P2 --> P5[Emergency Lighting Circuit on UPS and Battery]
    P3 --> P6[Fixture Type A Fluorescent 40W]
    P3 --> P7[Fixture Type B LED Panel 36W]
    P4 --> P8[Fixture Type C Machine Local Lamp 60W]
    P4 --> P9[Fixture Type D Inspection Spotlight 100W]
    P5 --> P10[Fixture Type E Emergency Bulkhead 10W]
    P5 --> P11[Fixture Type F Exit Signage 5W]
    P6 --> P12[Work Plane Illuminance Achieved]
    P7 --> P12
    P8 --> P12
    P9 --> P12
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram is in the answer (K-T-U examiner expects):**
> 1. It demonstrates the **hierarchy** of power flow from LT panel to fixture, which is the foundation of *electrical design integrated with plant layout*.
> 2. It explicitly separates **general, task, and emergency** lighting — three independent systems mandated by **IS 3646 (Part 2)** and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**.
> 3. It directly links luminaire **types A–F** to the *Work Plane Illuminance* — the single metric the Lumen method computes — closing the loop between calculation and physical implementation.

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Saw-Tooth Roof Natural Lighting

For a process hall requiring glare-free daylight, a **north-light (saw-tooth) roof** is the classical industrial solution. The diagram below shows the *architecture* of the system in block form since physical drawings cannot be rendered in mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[North-facing Vertical Glazing] --> R2[Diffuser Glass to soften sun]
    R2 --> R3[Rafter Beam of saw-tooth profile]
    R3 --> R4[Purlin runs transverse]
    R4 --> R5[North slope opaque insulation]
    R4 --> R6[South slope translucent acrylic]
    R6 --> R7[Work Plane Illuminance target 200 lux average]
    R3 --> R8[Gutter for rain water drainage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering logic of the saw-tooth:**
> - Only the **north slope is glazed** so the low-angle sun (south-facing in the northern hemisphere, mirrored for Kerala's near-equatorial location) never enters directly, eliminating glare.
> - The opaque south slope is a **reflective white** to bounce diffused light into the working plane.
> - **Rule of thumb:** glazed area $\approx 1/4$ to $1/3$ of floor area gives a Daylight Factor of **8–12 %** — sufficient for general industrial tasks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Define the term 'illuminance' and state its SI unit. Distinguish between illuminance and luminance."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Illuminance (E)** is the **luminous flux incident per unit area** on a surface. It is a measure of *how much light falls on* a surface, not how much is *emitted or reflected from* it.
>
> $$\boxed{E = \frac{\Phi}{A}, \quad \text{SI unit} = \text{lux (lx)} = \text{lumen per square metre}}$$

> **Luminance (L)** is the **luminous intensity per unit projected area** in a given direction, *as perceived by the eye* from a surface (emitting, transmitting, or reflecting). It is the photometric quantity that actually produces the sensation of brightness.
>
> $$\boxed{L = \frac{I}{A_{proj}}, \quad \text{SI unit} = \text{cd/m}^2}$$

| Parameter | Illuminance $E$ | Luminance $L$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Flux received per unit area | Intensity per unit projected area |
| SI unit | **lux** | **cd/m²** |
| Depends on | Source distance & power | Surface property & viewing angle |
| Measured by | Lux meter | Luminance meter |

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"What is the Lumen method? Why is the Maintenance Factor always less than 1?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> The **Lumen method** (also called the *Light Flux Method*) is a lighting design procedure used to calculate the *number of luminaires* required to deliver a specified average illuminance on the work-plane of an interior space. It accounts for light losses due to luminaire inefficiency, room surface absorption, and ageing of lamps.
>
> $$\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}}$$

> The **Maintenance Factor (M) is always less than 1** because, over the service life of the installation, three cumulative degradations occur:
> 1. **Lamp lumen depreciation** — output of every lamp falls with hours of use.
> 2. **Luminaire dirt depreciation** — dust and fumes accumulate on reflectors and diffusers.
> 3. **Room surface dirt depreciation** — ceiling and walls darken, reducing reflectance.
>
> Typical values: $M = 0.8$ for clean offices, $M = 0.6$ for dirty industrial environments.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question B (A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Lumen formula for calculating the number of lamps in a room. State clearly the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory hall of size **60 m × 30 m** is to be illuminated to a level of **200 lux**. Using **500 W halogen lamps** each giving **9,500 lumens**, mounted at **8 m** above the work-plane, with $U = 0.4$ and $M = 0.75$, calculate the **number of lamps required** and suggest a **practical layout**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B(A):**

**Part (a) — Derivation of the Lumen Formula:**

We begin with the definition of illuminance, which is the luminous flux incident per unit area:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A}$$

where $\Phi$ is the total luminous flux reaching the work-plane and $A$ is the area of the work-plane.

In an actual installation, the total flux emitted by the lamps exceeds the flux reaching the work-plane because of two systematic losses, which we introduce as multiplicative correction factors.

**Step 1 — Source:** Total flux emitted = $N \times \Phi_{lamp}$, where $N$ is the number of lamps and $\Phi_{lamp}$ is the rated lumen output of one lamp.

**Step 2 — Utilization Factor $U$:** This is the ratio of lumens reaching the work-plane to the total lumens emitted by the lamps. It is a dimensionless number less than one (typically 0.3–0.7) that depends on the luminaire type, room geometry (Room Index $K$), and reflectances of ceiling, walls, and floor.

**Step 3 — Maintenance Factor $M$:** This is a derating factor (also less than 1) that accounts for the reduction in illuminance over time due to lamp ageing, dust accumulation on luminaires, and dirt on room surfaces. It is chosen based on the cleanliness of the environment and the cleaning/maintenance schedule.

Therefore, the flux actually reaching the work-plane is:

$$\Phi_{work} = N \times \Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M$$

Substituting into the illuminance definition and rearranging for $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}}$$

This is the **Lumen Formula** used universally in industrial lighting design.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the area.**
$$A = 60 \times 30 = 1{,}800 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total lumens required at the work-plane (ideal).**
$$\Phi_{req} = E \times A = 200 \times 1{,}800 = 360{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 3 — Apply $U$ and $M$ to find the lumens that must be emitted.**
$$\Phi_{emit} = \frac{360{,}000}{U \times M} = \frac{360{,}000}{0.4 \times 0.75} = \frac{360{,}000}{0.30} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by rated output of one lamp.**
$$N = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{9{,}500} = 126.32$$

**Step 5 — Round up.**
$$\boxed{N = 127 \text{ lamps (minimum)}}$$

**Step 6 — Suggest a practical layout.**
A symmetric **9 rows × 14 columns = 126** does not reach 127, so we use **9 rows × 15 columns = 135 lamps**, which gives a small surplus (better for uniformity) and easy grid layout.
- Spacing along length = $60/15 = 4 \text{ m}$
- Spacing along width = $30/9 = 3.33 \text{ m}$
- Spacing-to-Height Ratio: $SHR = 4/8 = 0.5$ → acceptable for halogen luminaires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** An alternative arrangement is 7 rows × 19 columns = 133 lamps with $4 \text{ m} \times 4.3 \text{ m}$ spacing, equally valid. Either layout is acceptable; the key is showing the row × column logic and verifying SHR.

---

### **Question B (B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **different types of lighting systems** used in a process plant. Give **two industrial examples** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **Daylight Factor (DF)**. A factory room of floor area **50 m²** has a Daylight Factor of **8 %**. If the outside illuminance on a horizontal surface is **12,000 lux**, find the **inside illuminance** and comment on whether it is adequate for general industrial work as per IS 3646. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B(B):**

**Part (a) — Types of Lighting Systems:**

1. **General Lighting:** Provides uniform illumination over the entire area irrespective of the location of specific tasks. Used when task locations are not fixed or when cost of local lighting is high.
   - *Examples:* Warehouse storage, corridor lighting, open yard illumination.

2. **Localized (or Localized-General) Lighting:** A combination of general lighting at a lower level plus local luminaires (often adjustable) at each workstation. Most widely used in industry because it balances uniformity and economy.
   - *Examples:* Machine shop (general fluorescent + task lamp on each machine), assembly line.

3. **Local (Spot) Lighting:** Concentrated, high-lux illumination on a very small area to enable very fine or precision visual work. Cannot be used alone; must be supplemented by general lighting.
   - *Examples:* Inspection of microelectronics, gauge and dial reading on a lathe, jewelry work.

4. **Emergency Lighting:** Battery-backed or UPS-fed, automatically activated on power failure. Mandatory under IS 3646 (Part 2) for safe evacuation and continued operation of safety-critical systems.
   - *Examples:* Exit signage in chemical plants, escape route lighting in high-rise process buildings, control room emergency lamps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus point (write this for an extra ½ mark):** Modern plants also use **Task-Ambient Lighting**, an evolution of localized lighting in which LED panels with individually addressable CCT (correlated colour temperature) provide ambient fill and a focused spotlight provides the task beam from a single fixture.

---

**Part (b) — Daylight Factor:**

The **Daylight Factor (DF)** is defined as the ratio of the illuminance on a horizontal plane *inside* a room (at a reference point on the work-plane) to the simultaneous illuminance on a horizontal plane *outside* the building under an overcast sky, expressed as a percentage.

$$\boxed{DF = \frac{E_{inside}}{E_{outside}} \times 100 \, \%}$$

The three components that contribute to the inside illuminance, and therefore to DF, are:
- **Sky Component (SC):** direct light entering through the window from the visible sky.
- **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** light reflected from the ground or opposite buildings before entering.
- **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** light entering the room, striking the internal surfaces, and being reflected onto the work-plane.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $DF = 8\%$, $E_{outside} = 12{,}000$ lux, $A = 50$ m².

**Step 1 — Solve for inside illuminance.**
$$E_{inside} = \frac{DF \times E_{outside}}{100} = \frac{8 \times 12{,}000}{100} = 960 \text{ lux}$$

$$\boxed{E_{inside} = 960 \text{ lux}}$$

**Step 2 — Comment on adequacy as per IS 3646.**
IS 3646 (Part 1) recommends the following maintained illuminance levels for industrial interiors:
- Rough bench and machine work: 150–300 lux
- Medium bench/machine work: 300–500 lux
- Fine work (textile, leather): 500–1000 lux
- Very fine work (electronics assembly, instrument making): 1000+ lux

Since the room achieves 960 lux from daylight alone, it is **adequate for fine industrial work** (textile inspection, light assembly, precision benchwork) and would still be acceptable for very fine work if supplemented by localized artificial lighting to compensate for the daily variation of natural light. The value is **well above the minimum DF of 5%** recommended by IS 3646 for industrial halls.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Illuminance and Luminance:** Students frequently write "lux = cd/m²" — this is wrong. Lux is flux per area; cd/m² is intensity per projected area. **Lose 1 mark instantly.**
> 2. **Forgetting to round $N$ UP:** $N = 22.5$ rounded to 22 is technically **under-designed**; round up to 23 or add a few extra luminaires for uniformity.
> 3. **Mixing up $U$ and $M$:** Utilization Factor depends on *room geometry and luminaire optics*; Maintenance Factor depends on *dirt and lamp ageing*. Many answers swap the two.
> 4. **Omitting the layout (row × column):** A correct numerical answer without a layout suggestion loses **1–2 marks**. Always show a sketch or a row × column grid.
> 5. **Using foot-candles and lux interchangeably:** 1 fc = 10.76 lx. If the problem gives fc, convert first.
> 6. **Daylight Factor written as a fraction, not a percentage:** IS 3646 defines DF in percent; writing 0.08 instead of 8 % will lose ½ mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core metric:** Illuminance $E$ in **lux** ($1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$).
- **Master formula (THE LUMEN FORMULA):** $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{lamp} \times U \times M}$ — every lighting calculation in KTU revolves around this.
- **Always round $N$ UP** to the next integer; ideally, choose a row × column grid that overshoots $N$ by 5–10 % for better uniformity.
- **Two derating factors:** $U$ (geometric/optical, 0.3–0.7) and $M$ (ageing/dirt, 0.6–0.9). Both < 1, both dimensionless.
- **Room Index $K = \dfrac{LB}{H_m(L+B)}$** is the index used to look up $U$ from a luminaire catalogue table.
- **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR):** Maximum spacing between luminaires = $SHR \times H_m$, used to verify uniformity of layout.
- **Daylight Factor:** $DF = \dfrac{E_{inside}}{E_{outside}} \times 100 \, \%$; aim for $\geq 5\%$ in factories.
- **IS 3646 (Part 1)** is the Indian standard for interior illumination levels — memorize typical lux bands for rough (150 lx), medium (300 lx), fine (500–1000 lx), and very fine (>1000 lx) work.
- **Three types of industrial lighting:** General, Localized, and Local — each has a specific use case and IS 3646 does *not* allow local lighting alone.
- **Statutory hook:** Section 14 of the **Factories Act, 1948** — always cite it in 14-mark answers.
- **Energy note:** LED retrofit (lumens per watt from 12 → 120) typically halves the number of luminaires in a legacy plant, but a fresh Lumen-method calculation must be redone to verify the new lux level.
- **Process-industry special cases:** Hazardous (explosion-proof) areas use $U$ capped at 0.4 and $M$ capped at 0.6 to be safe; food/pharma uses shatter-proof, IP-65 luminaires with high CRI.
- **Units to remember:** Lux, lumen, candela, cd/m², lm/W (efficacy), steradian, foot-candle (1 fc = 10.76 lx).
- **Final trick:** If a KTU question gives *inverse data* (N given, find E or A), rearrange the Lumen formula to the form $E = \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi_{lamp} \cdot U \cdot M}{A}$ and substitute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ventilation # 🌬️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Industrial Ventilation** is the engineering technique of supplying fresh air to and removing contaminated, heated, or stale air from a workplace by natural or mechanical means, so as to maintain a healthy, safe, and productive atmospheric environment. According to the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**, it is "*the science and art of controlling the working environment through atmospheric dilution, local exhaust, or enclosure principles.*" In **Plant Layout and Design**, ventilation is treated as a critical utility subsystem that directly governs: - The **occupational health** of operating personnel (TLV / OEL compliance) - The **safety envelope** of plants handling flammable / toxic chemicals - The **thermal balance** of process halls, control rooms, and battery rooms - The **pressure regime** between adjacent classified areas (e.g., battery room vs. switchgear room) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Ventilation is studied as part of *auxiliary facility layout* — it overlaps with HVAC, fire protection, and utility distribution. Board questions frequently test its interaction with **hazardous-area classification** and **control-room pressurization**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs of the Plant" Think of a chemical plant as a **living organism**: | Plant Organ | Plant Ventilation Equivalent | |---|---| | **Lungs** | Air-handling units & fans | | **Trachea** | Main supply / return ducts | | **Bronchi** | Branch ducts (risers, headers) | | **Nose / Mouth** | Inlet louvers & exhaust stacks | | **Blood** | The conditioned air itself | Just as a human suffocates if the lungs fail, a poorly ventilated process plant suffocates — vapors accumulate, LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) drops, operators get giddy, and instruments drift. The **plant breathes** through this engineered system. ## 1.3 Classification of Ventilation Systems ### A. By Driving Force - **Natural Ventilation** — driven by **stack effect** (buoyancy) and **wind pressure** ($\Delta P_w = 0.00256 \, V_w^2$). No fans, low O&M cost, but unreliable for hazardous plants. - **Mechanical (Forced) Ventilation** — fan-driven. The **default for KTU-graded process plants**, especially with flammable vapors. ### B. By Engineering Function (the ACGIH taxonomy) 1. **Dilution Ventilation (General Exhaust)** — adds clean air to *lower the concentration* of a contaminant below its TLV. 2. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — *captures* the contaminant at the source before it spreads. 3. **Enclosure Ventilation** — wraps a hot/dirty process inside a hooded box with controlled extraction. > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy of Effectiveness (must memorize):** `Enclosure > LEV > Dilution`. For toxic or carcinogenic vapors, dilution alone is **never acceptable** per OSHA / Factory & Boiler Inspectorate (Kerala) norms. ## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **Air Changes per Hour** | ACH | 6 – 30 (process halls) | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ | | **Face Velocity** | $V_f$ | 0.5 – 2.5 (canopy hood) | m/s | | **Capture Velocity** | $V_c$ | 0.25 – 10.0 (varies with release) | m/s | | **Transport Velocity** | $V_t$ | 15 – 25 (light dust) → 20 – 25 (vapors) | m/s | | **Ventilation Rate** | $Q$ | Project-specific | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | **Total Pressure Loss** | $\Delta P_t$ | 0.5 – 4.0 (typical) | kPa | The bolded **ACH**, **$V_c$**, and **$V_t$** are the three parameters a KTU examiner will *always* expect in a numerical answer. # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Ventilation System A mechanical ventilation system obeys a strict **motive-flow-energy chain**: 1. **Contaminant generation** at the source (welding fume, solvent vapor, hot gas) 2. **Capture** by a hood (must overcome cross-drafts and thermal lift) 3. **Transport** through ducts (must maintain *transport velocity* to prevent settling) 4. **Cleaning** at an air-pollution-control device (cyclone, scrubber, bag filter, ESP) 5. **Discharge** via a stack (must clear the AIL — *Air Intake Line* of fresh air) 6. **Make-up air** drawn in elsewhere to balance the building's mass balance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does it work?** Fans supply the *total pressure* ($\Delta P_t$) needed to overcome (a) entry losses into hoods, (b) friction in ducts, (c) dynamic (velocity) pressure at the discharge, and (d) equipment resistance (filters, dampers). Conservation of mass forces equal volumes in and out of the room. ## 2.2 Hood Theory — The Heart of Local Exhaust A hood creates a **suction field**. For a **round simple (plain) opening**, the induced velocity at distance $x$ from the hood face falls off as: $$V_x = \frac{Q}{10 \, x^2} \quad \text{(spherical decay — centerline, conical flow)}$$ For an **enclosing hood** (with flange), the capture is *doubled* at the same flow rate (flange blocks radial inflow, forcing more axial flow). Hence the **flanged hood rule**: $$V_x(\text{flanged}) \approx 2 \, V_x(\text{flangeless})$$ The **entry loss** into a hood is the kinetic energy the air must give up to redirect into the duct: $$h_e = \zeta \, \frac{V_d^{\,2}}{2g} \quad ; \quad \zeta_{\text{plain opening}} = 0.65, \quad \zeta_{\text{flanged}} = 0.49$$ where $V_d$ = duct velocity, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$. ## 2.3 Duct Sizing — Equal-Friction Method (KTU favourite) The **equal-friction method** sizes each duct segment so the *friction loss per unit length* is constant. From **Darcy–Weisbach**: $$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f \, V_d^{\,2}}{2 D}$$ Solving for duct diameter at a chosen design friction rate (e.g., 0.8 Pa/m): $$D = \left( \frac{f \, \rho \, Q^2}{2 \, \pi^2 \, (\Delta P_f/L)} \right)^{1/5}$$ ## 2.4 Fan Laws (Examiner's favourite — 3–4 marks easy pick) | Law | Relation | Use | |---|---|---| | **Flow Law** | $Q_2 / Q_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | Halving speed → flow drops 8× | | **Pressure Law** | $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$ | Speed-pressure squared | | **Power Law** | $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | Brake power scales with cube of speed | | **Density Law** | $\Delta P \propto \rho$ | Re-rating for hot air | | **Affinity Law (Diameter)** | $Q \propto D^3 N, \quad \Delta P \propto D^2 N^2$ | Wheel diameter changes | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (PRINT THIS) > [!NOTE] > The symbols are kept inside `\vert` and `\mid` to **protect the markdown table** from breaking. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Boundary | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ventilation rate from ACH | $Q = \dfrac{\text{ACH} \cdot V_{\text{room}}}{3600}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | ACH = 6 – 30 | | 2 | Dilution ventilation | $Q = \dfrac{G}{R \cdot K}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $K \in [3,10]$ safety factor | | 3 | Capture velocity (spherical) | $V_x = \dfrac{Q}{10 \, x^2}$ | m/s | $x \le 1.5 \, D$ | | 4 | Velocity pressure | $P_v = \dfrac{\rho \, V^{\,2}}{2}$ | Pa | $\rho = 1.2 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ (air, std) | | 5 | Entry loss | $h_e = \zeta \, \dfrac{V_d^{\,2}}{2g}$ | m of air | $\zeta = 0.65$ plain | | 6 | Friction loss (Darcy) | $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{f \, L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, V^{\,2}}{2}$ | Pa | $f \approx 0.02$ (smooth) | | 7 | Fan brake power | $P = \dfrac{Q \, \Delta P_t}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$ | W | $\eta \approx 0.60$ – $0.75$ | | 8 | Stack effect pressure | $\Delta P_s = 3460 \, H \left( \dfrac{1}{T_o} - \dfrac{1}{T_i} \right)$ | Pa | $H$ = stack height, m | | 9 | Wind pressure on building | $\Delta P_w = 0.00256 \, V_w^2$ | Pa | $V_w$ in mph (ASHRAE) | | 10 | Make-up air fraction | $\%\text{MUA} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{MA}}}{Q_{\text{supply}}} \times 100$ | % | $\ge 90\%$ (code) | | 11 | Canopy hood $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{Q}{7.5 \, (x^2 + A)}$ | m/s | $A$ = hood area | | 12 | Slot hood $V_x$ | $V_x = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot x}$ | m/s | 2-D decay | > **Constants to memorize for KTU 2024:** > - $\rho_{\text{air, std}} = \mathbf{1.2 \ kg/m^3}$ > - $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$ > - $R_{\text{air}} = 287 \ \text{J/(kg·K)}$ ## 2.6 Real-World Utility | Plant | Ventilation Sub-System | Design Driver | |---|---|---| | **Petroleum refinery** | Tank-farm vapor recovery | Flammability (LEL) | | **Pharma API plant** | Powder-handling LEV | OEL of API (often ≤ 1 µg/m³) | | **Battery room** | Anti-explosion exhaust | H₂ build-up > 4 % LEL | | **Control room** | Positive pressurization | Dust & vapor ingress | | **Paint booth** | Downdraft / crossdraft | Fire & operator exposure | | **Diesel generator room** | Combustion-air + heat exhaust | Engine air demand | # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Ventilation Rate from ACH We start from the definition of **air change per hour** (dimensionless integer representing the number of times the room's air volume is replaced per hour). $$Q_{\text{hr}} = \text{ACH} \cdot V_{\text{room}} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{hr}]$$ To convert to **standard SI units of $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$** used in all fan/duct equations: $$Q = \frac{Q_{\text{hr}}}{3600} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,Q = \frac{\text{ACH} \cdot V_{\text{room}}}{3600}\,}$$ **Interpretation:** If a hall is $V = 1200 \ \text{m}^3$ and the design ACH is 10: $Q = 10 \times 1200 / 3600 = 3.33 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 12{,}000 \ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Equal-Friction Duct Diameter **Darcy–Weisbach equation** (one of the most-tested equations in chemical engineering): $$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho \, V_d^{\,2}}{2}$$ Substituting $V_d = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$: $$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho}{2} \cdot \left( \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} \right)^{2}$$ $$\Delta P_f = \frac{8 \, f \, L \, \rho \, Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} \, D^{5}}$$ Rearranging to express $D$ in terms of a chosen **friction rate** $(\Delta P_f / L)$: $$D^{5} = \frac{8 \, f \, \rho \, Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} \, (\Delta P_f / L)}$$ $$\boxed{\,D = \left( \frac{8 \, f \, \rho \, Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} \, (\Delta P_f / L)} \right)^{1/5}\,}$$ This is the **design equation for a circular duct** by the equal-friction method. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Fan Brake Power (Mechanical Power at the Shaft) **Fundamental definition of power** in a fluid system = volumetric flow × pressure rise / efficiency: $$P_{\text{fluid}} = Q \cdot \Delta P_t$$ A fan's **shaft (brake) power** must exceed the fluid power because of mechanical ($\eta_m$) and volumetric/hydraulic ($\eta_h$) inefficiencies: $$P_{\text{brake}} = \frac{P_{\text{fluid}}}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$$ $$\boxed{\,P_{\text{brake}} = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta P_t}{\eta_{\text{fan}}} \quad [\text{Watts}]\,}$$ Substituting $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and $\Delta P_t$ in Pa gives $P$ in **watts**. For kW, divide by 1000. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Capture Velocity of a Flanged Hood A **plain round opening** induces flow that approximates a **spherical coordinate field**. Through continuity, the velocity at the centerline at distance $x$ is: $$V_x \cdot 4\pi x^2 = Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_x = \frac{Q}{4\pi x^2}$$ The ACGIH simplifying constant $4\pi \approx 10$ is universally adopted in industrial hygiene literature: $$V_x = \frac{Q}{10 \, x^2}$$ When a **flange** is added, the radial inflow from the rear hemisphere is blocked. Only the front hemisphere contributes. The effective solid angle halves, so $V_x$ **doubles**: $$\boxed{\,V_x^{\text{flanged}} = \frac{2Q}{10 \, x^2} = \frac{Q}{5 \, x^2}\,}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation of Stack-Effect Pressure (Buoyancy) A vertical column of hot air inside a stack at temperature $T_i$ is surrounded by cooler outside air at $T_o$. Using the **hydrostatic pressure** of each column: $$P_{\text{inside}} = \rho_i g H, \qquad P_{\text{outside}} = \rho_o g H$$ The **driving pressure** for natural ventilation is the difference: $$\Delta P_s = (\rho_o - \rho_i) g H$$ Using the **ideal gas law** $\rho = P / (R T)$ at the same reference pressure $P$: $$\Delta P_s = \frac{P g H}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_o} - \frac{1}{T_i} \right)$$ For standard atmospheric conditions ($P = 101325 \ \text{Pa}$, $R = 287 \ \text{J/kg·K}$): $$\boxed{\,\Delta P_s = 3460 \, H \left( \frac{1}{T_o} - \frac{1}{T_i} \right) \quad [\text{Pa}]\,}$$ This shows why **tall stacks in winter** create strong natural draft — $T_o \ll T_i$. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU Sample (14-mark style) > **Problem.** A process hall is $20 \ \text{m} \times 12 \ \text{m} \times 6 \ \text{m}$. Design ACH = 15. A galvanized round duct of length $L = 25 \ \text{m}$ runs from a flanged canopy hood to a fan. Friction factor $f = 0.02$, design friction rate $= 0.8 \ \text{Pa/m}$, air density $\rho = 1.2 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, fan efficiency $\eta = 0.70$, hood entry coefficient $\zeta = 0.49$, duct velocity $V_d = 12 \ \text{m/s}$. > Compute: (a) **ventilation rate**, (b) **duct diameter**, (c) **total pressure**, (d) **fan brake power**. ### Solution **(a) Ventilation Rate** $$Q = \frac{15 \times (20 \times 12 \times 6)}{3600} = \frac{15 \times 1440}{3600} = 6.0 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **(b) Duct Diameter (equal-friction method)** $$D = \left( \frac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times 6.0^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 0.8} \right)^{1/5}$$ $$D = \left( \frac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times 36}{9.8696 \times 0.8} \right)^{1/5} = \left( \frac{6.912}{7.8957} \right)^{1/5}$$ $$D = (0.8754)^{0.2} = 0.9747 \ \text{m} \quad \approx 0.975 \ \text{m}$$ **(c) Total Pressure** - Friction loss: $\Delta P_f = 0.8 \times 25 = 20.0 \ \text{Pa}$ - Entry loss: $h_e = 0.49 \times \dfrac{12^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.49 \times 7.34 = 3.60 \ \text{m of air}$ Convert to Pa: $\Delta P_e = 3.60 \times 1.2 \times 9.81 = 42.34 \ \text{Pa}$ - Velocity pressure at exit: $P_v = 1.2 \times 12^{2} / 2 = 86.4 \ \text{Pa}$ $$\Delta P_t = 20.0 + 42.34 + 86.4 = 148.74 \ \text{Pa} \approx 149 \ \text{Pa}$$ **(d) Fan Brake Power** $$P = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta P_t}{\eta} = \frac{6.0 \times 148.74}{0.70} = 1274.9 \ \text{W} \approx 1.27 \ \text{kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** A 1.27 kW fan for a 1440 m³ hall is realistic — commercial axial-fans in this size class range 0.75 – 2.2 kW. If a student's answer comes out 12 kW, they have almost certainly forgotten to convert hours → seconds. # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Mechanical Ventilation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Pollutant Source] -->|Vapour/Dust/Heat| B1[Hood
Capture Vc] B1 -->|he entry loss| C1[Duct Network
Transport Vt] C1 -->|friction loss| D1[Air Cleaning Device
Cyclone / Scrubber / Bag Filter] D1 -->|clean exhaust| E1[Fan
Supplies deltaPt] E1 -->|discharge| F1[Stack
Atmospheric release] F1 -. fresh air inflow .-> G1[Make-up Air Unit
MAU] G1 --> A1 H1[Control Room] -->|senses| D1 styleA1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000 styleB1 fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc8400 styleC1 fill:#fff2b4,stroke:#ccaa00 styleD1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633 styleE1 fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003380 styleF1 fill:#d8d8e8,stroke:#333366 styleG1 fill:#e0d8ff,stroke:#330099 ``` **Reading the diagram (left → right):** Pollutant at `A1` is captured by the hood `B1`, conveyed by ducts `C1`, cleaned at `D1`, boosted by the fan `E1`, and ejected at `F1`. Make-up air `G1` closes the loop so the room neither depressurizes nor over-pressurizes. The control room `H1` continuously monitors `D1` for filter breakthrough. ## 4.2 Hood Taxonomy (Functional Comparison Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[Hood Types in Plant Ventilation] ROOT --> P1[Enclosing Hood
Best capture - 100 percent enclosure] ROOT --> P2[Exterior Hood] P2 --> P2a[Canopy Hood
Hot process - above source] P2 --> P2b[Side Draft Hood
Welding booths] P2 --> P2c[Downdraft Hood
Plasma cutting - heavy dust] ROOT --> P3[Receiving Hood] P3 --> P3a[Slot Hood
2-D capture, long narrow] P3 --> P3b[Booth Hood
Operator inside enclosure] P1 -. increases .-> P1a[Flanged = 2x velocity at same Q] P2a -. formula .-> P2a1[Vc = Q / 7.5x^2 + A] styleROOT fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff styleP1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633 styleP2 fill:#fff2b4,stroke:#ccaa00 styleP2a fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc8400 styleP2b fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc8400 styleP2c fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc8400 styleP3 fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003380 styleP3a fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003380 styleP3b fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003380 ``` ## 4.3 Pressure-Drop Cascade in a Ventilation Train ```mermaid flowchart LR PT[Total Pressure deltaPt] PT --> HE[Entry Loss
zeta times Vd^2/2g] PT --> DF[Friction Loss
fL/D times rho V^2/2] PT --> DP[Dynamic Pressure at Exit
rho V^2/2] PT --> EQ[Equipment Loss
Cyclone / Filter] HE --> FAN[FAN RATING] DF --> FAN DP --> FAN EQ --> FAN FAN --> KW[Brake Power kW
Q x deltaPt / eta] stylePT fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff styleFAN fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff styleKW fill:#006633,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** Whenever a question lists duct length, hood type, and equipment — *always* draw this four-component cascade **before** plugging in numbers. Examiners reward a clear loss-accounting table even if a single arithmetic step slips. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📌 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Differentiate between *Dilution Ventilation* and *Local Exhaust Ventilation* (LEV). State, with reasoning, which is preferred for a **solvent-vapour recovery** line in a paints plant. **Model Answer (8–10 lines):** | Aspect | Dilution Ventilation | Local Exhaust Ventilation | |---|---|---| | **Action point** | After contaminant has mixed with room air | At the source, before mixing | | **Effectiveness** | Low (depends on mixing & ACH) | Very high (close-capture) | | **Air volume required** | Very large | Small (≈ 10–20 % of dilution Q) | | **Energy cost** | High (oversized fan) | Low | | **Code acceptability (TLV)** | Marginal for toxic vapors | Always acceptable | **For solvent-vapour recovery** — *LEV is preferred* because: (1) it captures the vapor *before* it reaches the operator's breathing zone, (2) the captured stream can be ducted to an **activated-carbon adsorber / condenser** for solvent recovery (revenue), and (3) it minimizes explosive-vapor inventory, helping the plant stay below **25 % LEL** as mandated by IS 8192 / NFPA 30. *Dilution alone is unsafe and wasteful for a recovery operation.* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define *Air Changes per Hour* (ACH). Recommend a suitable ACH range for (a) a **general process hall**, (b) a **battery room**, (c) a **pharma cleanroom Grade D**. Justify in one line each. **Model Answer:** $$\text{ACH} = \frac{\text{Volume of clean air supplied per hour}}{\text{Room volume}} \quad [\text{hr}^{-1}]$$ | Area | Recommended ACH | Justification | |---|---|---| | **(a) General process hall** | **6 – 10** | Sufficient to keep vapors well below TLV with normal operator activity. | | **(b) Battery room** | **15 – 30** (often **20**) | Hydrogen evolves during charging; 4 % LEL must never be approached. IS 1646 mandates dedicated exhaust. | | **(c) Pharma cleanroom Grade D** | **20 – 40** with HEPA filtration | Maintain ISO 8 / Grade D at rest and in operation per WHO TRS 961. | --- ## 📌 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question is a 14-mark set with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. *Answer **either** full Q-A **or** full Q-B.* --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **A(a).** With a neat sketch, explain the **principle and design of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system** for a **welding bay** in a fabrication shop. List the standard **capture velocities** for different processes as per ACGIH. **A(b).** A local exhaust system is to be designed for a **dip-coating tank** of dimensions $2.5 \ \text{m} \times 1.2 \ \text{m}$ containing **toluene** (TLV-TWA = 50 ppm). The slot hood is placed $0.40 \ \text{m}$ from the tank lip. Design a slot hood with **three slots** of length equal to the tank length. State your design assumptions clearly. Use $\rho_{\text{vapour}} = 4.0 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. --- #### **Solution to A(a)** — 7 marks **Principle (4 marks):** LEV captures the contaminant at the *point of generation* by maintaining a localized air stream of velocity higher than the *contaminant escape velocity* (thermal lift, particle settling, process velocity). The system comprises: 1. **Hood** (slot / canopy / enclosure) — defines the capture envelope 2. **Duct** — transports the contaminated air at $V_t \ge 15 \ \text{m/s}$ for vapors 3. **Air-cleaning device** (scrubber, filter, adsorber) 4. **Fan** — supplies the total pressure 5. **Stack** — discharges above the AIL **Sketch (2 marks):** Show a flanged slot hood $\sim 0.4$ m above the weld, duct rising to a cyclone, fan, and stack. (Schematic of SECTION 4.1 applies.) **Capture velocities (ACGIH) (1 mark):** | Process | $V_c$ (m/s) | |---|---| | Welding (light) | 0.5 – 1.0 | | Welding (heavy) | 1.0 – 2.5 | | Solvent degreasing | 0.5 – 1.0 | | Hot plating bath | 0.5 – 1.0 | | Spray painting | 1.0 – 2.5 | | Crusher / grinding | 2.5 – 10.0 | --- #### **Solution to A(b)** — 7 marks **Step 1 — Design capture velocity.** Toluene vapor is light and warm, so per ACGIH: $V_c = 0.5 \ \text{m/s}$ at the tank lip. **Step 2 — Slot-hood formula** (2-D capture): $$V_x = \frac{Q}{L \cdot x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = V_c \cdot L \cdot x$$ With $L = 2.5 \ \text{m}$ (one slot), $x = 0.40 \ \text{m}$: $$Q_{\text{one slot}} = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 0.40 = 0.50 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — For 3 parallel slots:** $$Q_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 0.50 = 1.50 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 5400 \ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$ **Step 4 — Verify TLV by dilution check** (sanity). Generation rate $G$ for toluene at ambient temp ≈ $0.01 \ \text{g/s}$. Required dilution $K = 5$ (safety factor): $$Q_{\text{dil}} = \frac{G \times 24.45 \times 10^{6}}{\text{TLV} \times MW \times K} = \frac{0.01 \times 24.45 \times 10^{6}}{50 \times 92 \times 5} \approx 1.06 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Adopt the higher value** for safety: $Q_{\text{design}} = 1.50 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. **Step 6 — Slot width recommendation:** For vapor LEV, $V_{\text{slot}} = 5$ to $10 \ \text{m/s}$. Taking $V_s = 7.5 \ \text{m/s}$: $$A_{\text{slot}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}/3}{V_s} = \frac{0.50}{7.5} = 0.0667 \ \text{m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \frac{A}{L} = \frac{0.0667}{2.5} \approx 0.027 \ \text{m} \approx 27 \ \text{mm}$$ **Final design:** Three slots, each $L = 2.5 \ \text{m}$, width $\approx 25$ – $30 \ \text{mm}$, total suction $Q = 1.5 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **B(a).** Discuss the **design procedure** for the **mechanical ventilation of a battery charging room** for a standby power plant. Sketch the **typical duct layout** and justify the **negative-pressure philosophy**. **B(b).** A $9 \ \text{m} \times 6 \ \text{m} \times 3.5 \ \text{m}$ battery room houses **lead-acid cells** charging at the **IU (constant-current-constant-voltage)** regime. Hydrogen evolution rate from manufacturers' data is $0.05 \ \text{m}^3/\text{H_2}$ per cell-hour at the worst-case rate. There are **60 cells**. Hydrogen LEL = 4 % by volume. Compute the **design ventilation rate** if the safety factor $K = 4$. Also find the **stack height** if the inside temperature is $35 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ and outside is $28 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. (Use $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.) --- #### **Solution to B(a)** — 7 marks **Design philosophy (3 marks):** A battery room must be maintained at a **slight negative pressure** (–25 to –50 Pa w.r.t. surrounding areas) so any H₂ leak is **pulled in, not pushed out** into adjacent switchgear rooms or offices.
