| 9 | $D = \!\left[\!\dfrac{8 f L \rho \dot{V}^2}{\pi^2 \Delta P}\!\right]^{\!1/5}$ | Pipe diameter for given $\Delta P$ | m |
| 10 | $Q_{actual} = Q_{free} \cdot \dfrac{P_{free}}{P_{working}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{working}}{T_{free}}$ | Free-air vs. working-condition conversion | m³/min |
| 11 | $V_{rec} = \dfrac{Q \cdot t \cdot P_a}{P_2 - P_1}$ | Receiver tank volume | m³ |
| 12 | $Q_{design} = Q_{avg} \cdot (1 + L_f) \cdot S_f$ | Design CFM (avg × leakage × simultaneity) | m³/min |
| 13 | $N_{re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flow regime | – |
| 14 | $v = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Velocity in pipe | m/s |

Where $L_f$ = leakage factor (typically **0.10 – 0.20**), $S_f$ = simultaneity factor (typically **1.1 – 1.3**), and $f$ = Darcy friction factor (≈ 0.02 for turbulent flow in commercial steel pipe).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Trap to Avoid:** 1 CFM = **0.0283 m³/min** = **0.000472 m³/s**. Many students lose marks in KTU numericals by mixing CFM and m³/s.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Plant Design

Compressed air powers:
* **Pneumatic tools** (drills, grinders, impact wrenches) in assembly shops
* **Actuators and cylinders** in automated lines
* **Instrument air** for control valves (must be *oil-free and dry* — IS/ISO 8573-1 Class 2 minimum)
* **Sandblasting, paint spraying, chip blowing**
* **Process air** for oxidation, fluidisation, aeration in chemical plants
* **Pneumatic conveying** of solids (cement, grain, plastic pellets)

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work of Polytropic Compression

We begin with the **First Law for a closed system (control mass)**. For a slow, quasi-static process on a perfect gas:

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

The **polytropic process equation** relates $P$ and $V$:

$$P V^n = P_1 V_1^n = \text{constant}$$

Solve for $P$:

$$P = P_1 V_1^n \cdot V^{-n}$$

Substitute into the work integral from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \\[4pt]
  &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^n \cdot V^{-n} \, dV \\[4pt]
  &= P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV \\[4pt]
  &= P_1 V_1^n \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $V_1^{n-1}$ to obtain the canonical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \left[ \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1} - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the identity $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$, i.e., $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention used in KTU textbooks:** Work done **on the gas** (compressor) is **positive**. Some texts use the negative sign convention. Always state the convention at the start of the answer.

**Limiting Cases:**

| Condition | Index $n$ | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Isothermal (ideal cooling) | $n = 1$ | Use limit: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ |
| Adiabatic (no heat transfer) | $n = \gamma = 1.4$ | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(P_2/P_1\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ |
| Polytropic (realistic) | $1 < n < 1.4$ | $W_{poly}$ as derived above |

**Isothermal limit derivation via L'Hôpital:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to 1} \dfrac{n}{n-1}\left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
&= \lim_{n \to 1} \dfrac{r^{(n-1)/n} - 1}{(n-1)/n} \quad \text{(multiply/divide by } n) \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{n \to 1} \dfrac{d}{dn}\!\left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \Big/ \dfrac{d}{dn}\!\left[\dfrac{n-1}{n}\right] \\[4pt]
&= \ln r
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Temperature After Polytropic Compression

From the ideal-gas relation at states 1 and 2:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{P_1 V_1}$$

Using $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} &= \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} &= \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{-1/n} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1 - 1/n} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Receiver Sizing Formula

A receiver is filled by compressor delivery $Q$ and emptied by user demand. Let the user demand be zero momentarily (worst case — all delivered air goes into storage). Apply the **conservation of mass** to the control volume (receiver):

$$m_{in} - m_{out} = \dfrac{dm_{cv}}{dt}$$

Assuming the gas in the receiver obeys the ideal-gas law with $T$ ≈ constant (isothermal filling):

$$P \cdot V_{rec} = m \cdot R \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \dfrac{P \, V_{rec}}{R T}$$

Differentiate with respect to $P$ at constant $V_{rec}$, $T$:

$$\dfrac{dm}{dP} = \dfrac{V_{rec}}{R T}$$

Convert the inlet mass flow rate (at atmospheric density $\rho_a$) into a pressure rise rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_a \, Q &= \dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{rec}}{R T} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

The compressor intake is at $P_a$, so $\rho_a = \dfrac{P_a}{R T}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_a}{R T} Q &= \dfrac{V_{rec}}{R T} \dfrac{dP}{dt} \\[4pt]
P_a \, Q &= V_{rec} \dfrac{dP}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrate from $P_1$ to $P_2$ over time $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{t} P_a Q \, dt &= \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V_{rec} \, dP \\[4pt]
P_a Q t &= V_{rec} (P_2 - P_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the receiver volume:

$$
\boxed{V_{rec} = \dfrac{P_a \, Q \, t}{P_2 - P_1}}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem [Adapted from KTU Pattern]:**
A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses **1.5 m³/min** of free air from **1.013 bar, 30 °C** to **8 bar gauge**. The compression is polytropic with $n = 1.32$, and the intercooler cools the air back to **30 °C**. The mechanical efficiency is **85 %**. Find:
(a) Work of compression per minute
(b) Power required to drive the compressor
(c) Discharge temperature from the second stage

**Given:**
$P_1 = 1.013$ bar (abs), $P_2 = 8 + 1.013 = 9.013$ bar (abs), $T_1 = 303$ K, $V_1 = 1.5$ m³/min, $n = 1.32$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$.

**For a two-stage compressor with intercooling, optimum pressure ratio per stage:**

$$r_{stage} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9.013}{1.013}} = \sqrt{8.898} = 2.983$$

**Total work (2 stages):**

$$
W_{total} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ r_{stage}^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{0.32}{1.32} = 0.2424$$

$$
r_{stage}^{0.2424} = 2.983^{0.2424}
$$

Take logarithm: $\ln(2.983) = 1.0932$. Multiply: $0.2424 \times 1.0932 = 0.2650$. Exponentiate: $e^{0.2650} = 1.3035$.

Bracketed term: $1.3035 - 1 = 0.3035$.

Lead coefficient:

$$\dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.32}{0.32} = 4.125$$

Work per stage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{stage} &= 4.125 \times (1.013 \times 10^5) \times 1.5 \times 0.3035 \quad \text{[J, since 1 min used]} \\[4pt]
&= 4.125 \times 101300 \times 1.5 \times 0.3035 \\[4pt]
&= 190,279.6 \text{ J} \approx 190.28 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total work for 2 stages (the 2× is exactly correct because of optimum intercooling — $V$ at start of stage 2 equals $V_1$):

$$W_{total} = 2 \times 190.28 = 380.56 \text{ kJ/min}$$

**Part (b) — Brake Power:**

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{W_{total}/60}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{380.56/60}{0.85} = \dfrac{6.343}{0.85} = 7.46 \text{ kW}
$$

**Part (c) — Discharge Temperature:**

Since intercooling returns air to $T_1 = 303$ K at the start of stage 2:

$$
T_{discharge} = T_1 \left( r_{stage} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 303 \times 1.3035 = 395.0 \text{ K} = 121.9 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **discharge temperature is identical for both stages** when intercooling is optimum. This is the KTU proof-of-concept question every year. Memorise this.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Compressor Selection

| Demand Profile | Recommended Compressor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Intermittent, < 100 CFM | Reciprocating | Lower capital cost, tolerates on/off cycling |
| Continuous 8-hr shift, 100–500 CFM | Rotary screw (oil-flooded) | High uptime, lower maintenance |
| Oil-free requirement (food/pharma) | Scroll or oil-free screw | Eliminates oil contamination |
| Very high CFM (> 1000) | Centrifugal | Better part-load efficiency |
| Variable demand | VSD (Variable Speed Drive) screw | 30 % energy saving |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Compressed Air Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ambient Air] --> A2[Intake Filter]
    A2 --> A3[Compressor Stage 1]
    A3 --> A4[Intercooler]
    A4 --> A5[Compressor Stage 2]
    A5 --> A6[Aftercooler]
    A6 --> A7[Moisture Separator]
    A7 --> A8[Receiver Tank]
    A8 --> A9[Refrigerant Dryer]
    A9 --> A10[Distribution Main Loop]
    A10 --> B1[Branch 1 FRL Unit]
    A10 --> B2[Branch 2 FRL Unit]
    A10 --> B3[Branch 3 FRL Unit]
    B1 --> C1[Pneumatic Tool 1]
    B2 --> C2[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    B3 --> C3[Control Valve Actuator]
    A8 -.Condensate Drain.-> A11[Condensate Trap]
    A9 -.Moisture.-> A11
```

**Reading the diagram:**
* Air flows left-to-right from ambient to point-of-use.
* The receiver tank (A8) is the **central node** — all branches originate here.
* The condensate trap (A11) collects moisture from A8 and A9 via drain valves.
* Each branch (B1/B2/B3) hosts an FRL unit before delivery to end users (C1/C2/C3).

### 4.2 Distribution Layout Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LoopLayout["LOOP LAYOUT PREFERRED"]
        R1[Receiver] --> M1[Main Pipe]
        M1 --> P1[Point A]
        P1 --> P2[Point B]
        P2 --> P3[Point C]
        P3 --> P4[Point D]
        P4 --> M2[Return Main]
        M2 --> R1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TreeLayout["TREE LAYOUT LEGACY"]
        R2[Receiver] --> MP[Main Pipe]
        MP --> BR1[Branch 1]
        MP --> BR2[Branch 2]
        MP --> BR3[Branch 3]
        BR1 --> E1[End 1]
        BR2 --> E2[End 2]
        BR3 --> E3[End 3]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Pressure and Air Quality at Each Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[State 1 Atmospheric 1.013 bar 30 C Humid] -->|Compress| S2[State 2 Intercooled 3 bar 30 C Cooled]
    S2 -->|Compress| S3[State 3 Aftercooled 9 bar 35 C Wet]
    S3 -->|Drain| S4[State 4 Dried 9 bar 30 C Dry]
    S4 -->|Store| S5[State 5 Receiver 8 bar 30 C Dry]
    S5 -->|Distribute Drop 0.3 bar| S6[State 6 At Tool 7.7 bar Filtered Lubricated]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **progressive pressure drop** along the chain. The KTU designer's job is to ensure the final state (S6) is at or above the minimum working pressure of every tool. If S6 falls below 6 bar at the farthest tool, the plant fails its design specification.

### 4.4 FRL Unit — Detailed Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inlet Air] --> F1[Filter Element 5 micron]
    F1 --> F2[Auto Drain Trap]
    F2 --> R1[Pressure Regulator]
    R1 --> G1[Pressure Gauge]
    R1 --> L1[Lubricator Bowl]
    L1 --> OUT[Outlet to Tool]
```

Each block has a specific engineering role:
* **Filter (F1)** — removes particulates and bulk water.
* **Auto drain (F2)** — ejects collected condensate.
* **Regulator (R1)** — drops and stabilises pressure to tool rating.
* **Gauge (G1)** — visual feedback to operator.
* **Lubricator (L1)** — injects oil mist for cylinder life.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Define compressed air. List any four industrial applications of compressed air in a process plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Compressed air** is atmospheric air pressurised above ambient (typically to 6–10 bar) and used as a utility for power transmission and process work. *(1 mark)*

Four industrial applications: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*
1. Powering pneumatic tools (drills, grinders) in assembly shops.
2. Driving pneumatic cylinders and actuators in automation.
3. Supplying instrument air to process control valves.
4. Sandblasting, paint spraying, and chip blowing in finishing operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Similar Pattern]**
*State the function of an air receiver in a compressed air system. Mention any two functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An air receiver is a **storage vessel** installed between the compressor discharge and the distribution network. *(1 mark)*

Functions: *(2 marks — 1 mark each)*
1. **Storage reserve** — supplies air during peak demand or compressor shutdown.
2. **Pulse damping** — smooths out pulsations from reciprocating compressors.
3. **Condensate collection** — allows moisture to settle and be drained.
4. **Pressure stabilisation** — buffers minor pressure fluctuations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **work of polytropic compression** of a perfect gas. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A single-stage reciprocating compressor compresses **2.0 m³/min** of free air from **1.013 bar, 27 °C** to **7 bar gauge**, following the law $PV^{1.3} = C$. Calculate: *(7 marks)*
* (i) The work of compression per minute.
* (ii) The power required to drive the compressor if mechanical efficiency is **80 %**.
* (iii) The temperature of air at the end of compression.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Start from the first law for a closed system and the polytropic relation $PV^n = \text{constant}$. Follow the derivation in Section 3.1 above. **Key checkpoints for the examiner:**
* Correct setup of integral $W = \int P \, dV$ — *1 mark*
* Substitution of $P = P_1 V_1^n V^{-n}$ — *1 mark*
* Integration steps — *2 marks*
* Use of $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ — *1 mark*
* Final boxed expression $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ — *2 marks*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $V_1 = 2.0$ m³/min, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar (abs), $P_2 = 7 + 1.013 = 8.013$ bar (abs), $T_1 = 300$ K, $n = 1.3$, $\eta_m = 0.80$.

**Step (i) — Work per minute:**

Pressure ratio:

$$r = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{8.013}{1.013} = 7.910$$

Exponent:

$$\dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{0.3}{1.3} = 0.2308$$

Compute $r^{0.2308}$:

$\ln(7.910) = 2.0682$, then $0.2308 \times 2.0682 = 0.4773$, and $e^{0.4773} = 1.6117$.

Bracketed term: $1.6117 - 1 = 0.6117$.

Lead coefficient: $\dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} = 4.3333$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 4.3333 \times (1.013 \times 10^5) \times 2.0 \times 0.6117 \quad \text{[J]} \\[4pt]
  &= 4.3333 \times 101300 \times 2.0 \times 0.6117 \\[4pt]
  &= 537,153 \text{ J} \approx 537.15 \text{ kJ/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Brake Power:**

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{W/60}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{537.15/60}{0.80} = \dfrac{8.953}{0.80} = 11.19 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Discharge Temperature:**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times 1.6117 = 483.5 \text{ K} = 210.3 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **forget to convert gauge to absolute pressure** for $P_2$. Using $P_2 = 7$ bar (gauge) instead of **8.013 bar (abs)** causes an answer error of nearly **15 %** — full mark loss. Also, mixing **W (per minute)** with **Power (per second)** is the most common dimensional error. Always write down the unit conversion explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a complete compressed air plant** from intake to point-of-use. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A factory has the following pneumatic tools operating simultaneously: **10 impact wrenches (each 4 CFM), 6 grinders (each 6 CFM), 4 paint sprayers (each 3 CFM)**. The receiver operates between **6 bar and 8 bar gauge**. Atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**. The compressor takes **3 minutes** to fill the empty receiver. Calculate: *(7 marks)*
* (i) The free-air delivery (FAD) of the compressor in m³/min.
* (ii) The size of the receiver tank in m³.
* (iii) The minimum pipe diameter if the total length is **120 m**, allowable pressure drop is **0.3 bar**, and friction factor $f = 0.02$, using $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$ (assume $\rho = 7.5$ kg/m³ at working condition).

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Draw the block diagram (use the Mermaid chart in Section 4.1 as a reference). Sequence to be labelled: *Intake Filter → Compressor → Intercooler → Aftercooler → Moisture Separator → Receiver → Dryer → Distribution Main → FRL → Tool.* *(3 marks for diagram, 1 mark for correct flow arrows, 3 marks for labelling the function of each block.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step (i) — FAD Calculation:**

Apply the design equation: $Q_{design} = Q_{avg} \times (1 + L_f) \times S_f$ with $L_f = 0.15$, $S_f = 1.0$ (all tools run simultaneously per problem statement).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{avg} &= (10 \times 4) + (6 \times 6) + (4 \times 3) \\
        &= 40 + 36 + 12 = 88 \text{ CFM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert CFM → m³/min using **1 CFM = 0.02832 m³/min**:

$$Q_{avg} = 88 \times 0.02832 = 2.492 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$
Q_{design} = 2.492 \times 1.15 \times 1.0 = 2.866 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step (ii) — Receiver Sizing:**

Given $P_1 = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013$ bar (abs), $P_2 = 8 + 1.013 = 9.013$ bar (abs), $P_a = 1.013$ bar, $Q = 2.866$ m³/min, $t = 3$ min.

$$
V_{rec} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot Q \cdot t}{P_2 - P_1} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 2.866 \times 3}{9.013 - 7.013} = \dfrac{8.710}{2.000} = 4.355 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step (iii) — Pipe Diameter:**

Convert $\Delta P = 0.3$ bar = **30,000 Pa**, $L = 120$ m, $\rho = 7.5$ kg/m³, $Q = 2.866/60 = 0.04777$ m³/s, $f = 0.02$.

Solve the formula for $D$:

$$
D = \left[\dfrac{8 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 \Delta P}\right]^{1/5}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
8 \times 0.02 \times 120 \times 7.5 \times (0.04777)^2 = 1920 \times 0.002282 = 4.381
$$

Compute denominator:

$$\pi^2 \times 30000 = 296,131$$

Ratio: $\dfrac{4.381}{296,131} = 1.479 \times 10^{-5}$.

Fifth root:

$$
D = (1.479 \times 10^{-5})^{0.2} = 10^{-1} \times (1.479)^{0.2} = 0.1 \times 1.0814 = 0.1081 \text{ m} \approx 108 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** Three traps in this question:
> 1. **CFM → m³/min conversion** — forgetting 0.02832 factor loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Gauge vs. absolute** — the receiver formula REQUIRES absolute pressures in numerator AND denominator. Mixing gauge and absolute gives physically meaningless (negative) volumes.
> 3. **Friction factor equation form** — the formula given in the question uses $Q$ in m³/s, not m³/min. Forgetting the /60 conversion will lead to $D \approx 0.07$ m, an undersized pipe, and full mark loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Compressed Air**

- **Definition**: Compressed air = atmospheric air pressurised to **6 – 10 bar (gauge)** for use as a plant utility.
- **Standard Reference**: $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar, $T_{ref} = 288$ K, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K).
- **Conversion Mastery**: 1 CFM = 0.0283 m³/min; gauge to absolute: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$.
- **Polytropic Work Formula** (KTU's most-asked equation):
  $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$
- **Isothermal Limit**: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ — minimum possible work.
- **Adiabatic Limit**: $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ — maximum work.
- **Discharge Temperature**: $T_2 = T_1 \left(P_2/P_1\right)^{(n-1)/n}$.
- **Two-Stage Optimum Pressure Ratio Per Stage**: $r_{stage} = \sqrt{P_2/P_1}$ — yields minimum total work.
- **Receiver Volume**: $V_{rec} = \dfrac{P_a Q t}{P_2 - P_1}$ (use absolute pressures).
- **Design CFM**: Multiply average CFM by leakage factor (1.10 – 1.20) and simultaneity factor (1.10 – 1.30).
- **Maximum Allowable Pressure Drop** in distribution = 10 % of receiver pressure.
- **FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator** — installed at every branch.
- **Layout Choice**: Loop (preferred) vs. Tree (legacy) — loop gives uniform pressure.
- **Instrument Air Quality**: ISO 8573-1 Class 2 (oil-free, dry, filtered).
- **Common Traps**: forgetting gauge-to-absolute, mixing time units, omitting leakage factor.
- **Compressor Selection Rule of Thumb**: Reciprocating ≤ 100 CFM; Rotary Screw 100–500 CFM; Centrifugal > 1000 CFM; VSD for variable load.
- **Energy Tip**: A 1 bar drop in receiver pressure increases energy cost by **~ 7 %** — always size for minimum pressure drop.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Handling System and Equipment: Material Handling in Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Handling System and Equipment: Material Handling in Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Handling (MH)**: Material handling is the art and science of moving, packaging, storing, and controlling the flow of materials (raw materials, work-in-process, finished goods, and refuse) in a manufacturing or process plant with minimum cost, maximum safety, and optimum utilization of space, time, and equipment.

As per KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPET634 — Module 3), material handling is treated as an **integrated subsystem of plant design** that physically connects the stationary operations (machines, workstations, storage points) of a plant. It is the **"bridge between operations"** and is considered one of the most important criteria used for evaluating the quality of a plant layout.

The **American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)** defines material handling as:
> *"The moving of material from one place to another, the loading and unloading of material, the positioning of material for processing, and the controlling of these operations."*

The **American Material Handling Society (AMHS)** characterizes MH by the **"5 R's"**:

| R | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Right Quantity** | Of the right material |
| **Right Quality** | In the right condition |
| **Right Place** | At the right location |
| **Right Position** | In the right orientation |
| **Right Time** | At the right moment |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Human Circulatory System"**
>
> Think of a manufacturing plant as a **living human body**:
> - The **machines and workstations** are the **organs** (heart, lungs, kidneys) where actual "work" (blood purification, oxygenation) takes place.
> - The **material handling equipment** is the **circulatory system** (arteries, veins, capillaries, the heart pump) — it does no "useful transformation" by itself, but without it, every organ starves and dies.
>
> Just as a cardiologist studies blood flow to keep the body healthy, a process engineer designs the material flow to keep the plant productive. If the blood (material) does not reach the organ (machine) at the right time and in the right quantity, the body (plant) collapses. This is why **"the best material handling system is often the one you don't notice — it just works."**

## 1.3 Why Material Handling is the "Hidden Plant"

In an average process plant:
- **20% to 50%** of the total operating cost is attributable to material handling.
- **15% to 70%** of the total production time is spent in moving and queuing of materials.
- **Up to 25%** of the workforce may be directly involved in handling activities.
- Poor MH accounts for the **majority of industrial accidents** (back injuries, crush injuries, forklift incidents).

Therefore, a *well-designed material handling system* directly translates into:
1. Lower operating cost
2. Higher throughput
3. Improved safety record
4. Reduced inventory
5. Better ergonomic conditions for workers

## 1.4 Scope of Material Handling in KTU 2024 Syllabus

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 framework, the topic "Material Handling in Plants" encompasses:
- **Principles of material handling** (the 10 classical principles).
- **Classification of MH equipment** (industrial trucks, conveyors, cranes, hoists, robots, AGVs).
- **Selection criteria** for MH equipment.
- **Unit load concept** and its role in MH.
- **Integration of MH with plant layout** (the layout decision is essentially an MH decision).
- **Cost analysis** of MH systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10 Principles of Material Handling (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These 10 principles are an **end-semester favourite** in KTU PEPET634. Memorize the principle and be able to give one example each.

**Principle 1 — Planning Principle**: Plan all material handling activities to obtain the lowest overall cost, considering the seven "M"s (Men, Machines, Material, Methods, Measurement, Management, Maintenance).

**Principle 2 — Systems Principle**: Integrate handling activities into a coordinated system that includes inputs, outputs, and the operational sequence to optimize total cost.

**Principle 3 — Work Flow Principle**: Operate a flow-oriented layout pattern (e.g., straight line, U-shaped) to maximize the effectiveness of the handling system.

**Principle 4 — Ergonomic Principle**: Recognize the human element in material handling by designing the work to conform to human capabilities and limitations.

**Principle 5 — Unit Load Principle**: Handle materials in **unit loads** (largest quantity that can be handled as a single entity) to reduce handling cost per unit mass.

**Principle 6 — Space Utilization Principle**: Make effective use of cubic space — design handling systems that use both floor area *and* vertical space.

**Principle 7 — Standardization Principle**: Standardize methods, equipment, and controls to provide flexibility while reducing cost.

**Principle 8 — Environmental Principle**: Use energy-efficient equipment and methods that minimize environmental impact (noise, dust, emissions).

**Principle 9 — Mechanization Principle**: Mechanize the handling process where it is economically justified, considering labour cost, safety, and volume.

**Principle 10 — Flexibility Principle**: Provide flexibility in methods and equipment so that changing requirements can be met with minimum disruption.

## 2.2 Classification of Material Handling Equipment

Material handling equipment is broadly classified into **five functional categories**:

### A. Industrial Trucks (Non-Powered & Powered)
- **Non-powered**: Hand truck, pallet jack, wagon, cart.
- **Powered**: Forklift truck (electric/diesel/LPG), reach truck, order picker, side-loader, counterbalanced truck.

### B. Conveyors
- **Powered/Continuous**: Belt conveyor, chain conveyor, screw conveyor, bucket elevator, pneumatic conveyor, vibrating conveyor.
- **Non-powered/Gravity**: Roller conveyor, chute, spiral chute, wheel conveyor.

### C. Cranes & Hoists
- **Jib crane**, **bridge crane (EOT)**, **gantry crane**, **monorail hoist**, **overhead crane**, **stackercrane**.

### D. Robotic & Automated Systems
- **Articulated robot arm**, **SCARA robot**, **gantry robot**, **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)**, **Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)**, **conveyor-based AS/RS**.

### E. Auxiliary & Storage Equipment
- **Pallets** (wooden, plastic, metal), **pallet racks**, **drums**, **containers**, **bins**, **hoppers**, **silos**, **tote pans**.

## 2.3 Selection Criteria of MH Equipment

A KTU 2024 question often asks: *"List and explain the factors governing the selection of material handling equipment."*

The selection of equipment depends on:

1. **Properties of material** — size, shape, weight, fragility, hazardous nature, temperature, moisture.
2. **Volume of flow** — tonnage per hour/day, number of unit loads per shift.
3. **Distance of movement** — short (< 30 m), medium (30–300 m), long (> 300 m).
4. **Path of movement** — horizontal, vertical, inclined; fixed or variable.
5. **Layout pattern** — process, product, fixed-position, cellular.
6. **Cost** — capital cost, operating cost, cost per unit handled.
7. **Flexibility & Versatility** — required vs. available.
8. **Safety & Ergonomics** — OSHA/Factory Act compliance.
9. **Space constraints** — floor space vs. vertical space.
10. **Maintenance & Reliability** — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), availability of spares.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 equations for MH cost analysis.** All units are explicitly stated. Note: vertical bars in formulas are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{cycle} = T_{load} + T_{travel} + T_{unload} + T_{idle}$ | Total cycle time of a single handling operation | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{T_{available}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Number of trips per shift per equipment | trips/shift |
| 3 | $Q = N \times U$ | Total throughput per shift, where $U$ = unit load mass | tonnes/shift |
| 4 | $C_{per\,unit} = \dfrac{C_{annual}}{Q_{annual}}$ | Material handling cost per unit mass | ₹/tonne |
| 5 | $C_{annual} = C_{capital} \times CRF + C_{operating}$ | Annual cost of equipment (Capital Recovery Factor $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$) | ₹/year |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | Equipment utilization / efficiency | % |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{W \times v}{\eta_{m}}$ | Power required to drive a conveyor (where $W$ = mass flow, $v$ = velocity, $\eta_{m}$ = mechanical efficiency) | kW |
| 8 | $L_{req} = L_{horiz} \times f_{lift}$ | Effective length of a belt conveyor (with lift factor $f_{lift}$) | m |
| 9 | $n_{pallets} = \dfrac{D_{demand}}{D_{pallet}}$ | Number of pallets required, where $D_{pallet}$ = pallet capacity | pallets |
| 10 | $C_{saved} = n \times \Delta t \times W \times c_{labour}$ | Annual labour cost saved by mechanization, where $n$ = number of workers displaced, $\Delta t$ = time saved per worker, $c_{labour}$ = labour cost per hour | ₹/year |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The material handling system is the **physical manifestation of the plant layout**. In industries such as:

- **Petrochemical & Refining** — long-distance pipelines, belt conveyors for coal, bucket elevators for catalyst, EOT cranes for heavy reactor vessels.
- **Automotive Assembly** — overhead conveyors, AGVs, robotic pick-and-place.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — cleanroom-compatible AGVs, vacuum conveyors for powders.
- **Warehousing & E-commerce** — AS/RS, unit-load AS/RS, sortation conveyors.
- **Cement & Steel** — heavy-duty belt conveyors, screw conveyors, stacker-cum-reclaimers.

> **Key Insight:** A poorly designed MH system can wipe out 30–40% of the productivity gains from an otherwise perfect layout. This is why the **layout decision = MH decision**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unit Load Concept & Cost Reduction

**Problem Context:** A plant handles **40 000 tonnes/year** of finished goods. Each unit load on a pallet weighs **0.5 tonnes**. Currently, individual handling (one box at a time) is practised. Each manual handling event costs **₹15** and 8 handling events are needed per tonne. If a unit-load system (one pallet = 0.5 t per handling event) is introduced, the cost per handling event becomes **₹40** but only **2.2** events are needed per tonne (1 trip per 0.5 t = 2 trips per tonne, plus 0.2 trips for partial pallet).

Determine the **savings per year** and the **percentage reduction in handling cost**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the total number of handling events per year.**

$$ \text{Annual mass} = 40\,000 \text{ tonnes/year} $$

For the **existing system (manual, one-box-at-a-time):**

$$ n_{old} = 40\,000 \text{ t/yr} \times 8 \text{ events/t} = 320\,000 \text{ events/yr} $$

For the **proposed unit-load system:**

$$ n_{new} = 40\,000 \text{ t/yr} \times 2.2 \text{ events/t} = 88\,000 \text{ events/yr} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the annual handling cost for each system.**

For the existing system:

$$ C_{old} = 320\,000 \text{ events/yr} \times 15 \text{ ₹/event} = 4\,800\,000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

For the proposed system:

$$ C_{new} = 88\,000 \text{ events/yr} \times 40 \text{ ₹/event} = 3\,520\,000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the annual savings.**

$$ S = C_{old} - C_{new} = 4\,800\,000 - 3\,520\,000 = 1\,280\,000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage reduction in handling cost.**

$$ \Delta C\,(\%) = \dfrac{S}{C_{old}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1\,280\,000}{4\,800\,000} \times 100\% = 26.67\% $$

**Conclusion:** The unit-load system yields an annual saving of **₹ 12.8 lakhs** — a **26.67% reduction** in handling cost — despite a higher per-event cost. This mathematically proves Principle 5 (Unit Load Principle).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time and Equipment Capacity

**Problem Context:** A forklift is to be deployed in a plant. The following data is given:

- Time to load the forklift: $T_{load} = 30$ s
- Loaded travel time (one-way): $T_{travel} = 90$ s
- Time to unload: $T_{unload} = 25$ s
- Idle/positioning time: $T_{idle} = 15$ s
- Available working time per shift: $T_{avail} = 8$ hours
- Capacity of forklift per trip: $U = 1.2$ tonnes/trip

Calculate: (a) Cycle time, (b) Number of trips per shift, (c) Throughput in tonnes per shift.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step (a) — Cycle time of the forklift.**

$$ T_{cycle} = T_{load} + T_{travel} + T_{unload} + T_{idle} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ T_{cycle} = 30 + 90 + 25 + 15 = 160 \text{ s/trip} $$

**Step (b) — Number of trips per shift.**

Convert the available time into seconds:

$$ T_{avail} = 8 \text{ hr/shift} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 28\,800 \text{ s/shift} $$

Apply the trip formula:

$$ N = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{28\,800}{160} = 180 \text{ trips/shift} $$

**Step (c) — Throughput per shift.**

$$ Q = N \times U = 180 \text{ trips/shift} \times 1.2 \text{ t/trip} = 216 \text{ tonnes/shift} $$

**Sanity check (annual basis, 300 working days):**

$$ Q_{annual} = 216 \times 300 = 64\,800 \text{ tonnes/year} $$

> **Engineering Note:** If the plant requires 80 000 tonnes/year, the single forklift is inadequate. The engineer must either deploy a second forklift, increase $U$ (bigger pallets), or reduce $T_{travel}$ (relayout to shorten the move).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Annual Cost Comparison of Two MH Alternatives

**Problem Context:** Two material handling systems are being evaluated for a new warehouse:

- **System A (Forklift):** Capital cost = ₹ 8 00 000; useful life = 5 years; annual operating & maintenance cost = ₹ 2 50 000.
- **System B (Conveyor):** Capital cost = ₹ 15 00 000; useful life = 8 years; annual operating & maintenance cost = ₹ 1 20 000.

The interest rate $i$ = 10% per annum. Determine the **annual equivalent cost (AEC)** of each system using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the CRF formula.**

$$ CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} $$

**Step 2 — Compute CRF for System A ($n = 5$ years).**

$$ CRF_A = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{5}}{(1.10)^{5} - 1} $$

Compute $(1.10)^{5} = 1.61051$:

$$ CRF_A = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1} = \dfrac{0.161051}{0.61051} = 0.2638 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) of System A.**

$$ AEC_A = C_{capital,A} \times CRF_A + C_{operating,A} $$

$$ AEC_A = 8\,00\,000 \times 0.2638 + 2\,50\,000 = 2\,11\,040 + 2\,50\,000 = 4\,61\,040 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 4 — Compute CRF for System B ($n = 8$ years).**

$$ CRF_B = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{8}}{(1.10)^{8} - 1} $$

Compute $(1.10)^{8} = 2.14359$:

$$ CRF_B = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.14359}{2.14359 - 1} = \dfrac{0.214359}{1.14359} = 0.1874 $$

**Step 5 — Compute the Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC) of System B.**

$$ AEC_B = 15\,00\,000 \times 0.1874 + 1\,20\,000 = 2\,81\,100 + 1\,20\,000 = 4\,01\,100 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 6 — Decision.**

$$ AEC_A = ₹\,4\,61\,040/\text{yr} \quad \text{vs.} \quad AEC_B = ₹\,4\,01\,100/\text{yr} $$

**Decision: System B (Conveyor) is selected** because it has a **lower annual equivalent cost** by:

$$ \Delta = 4\,61\,040 - 4\,01\,100 = 59\,940 \text{ ₹/yr} \approx ₹\,60\,000/\text{yr} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** A common student error in KTU valuation is to compare the *capital cost* alone. The examiner will *deliberately* give a system with a higher capital cost but a longer life, and marks will be awarded only for the *correct CRF calculation* and the *annual equivalent cost comparison*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Material Handling Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional hierarchy** of all material handling equipment covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each branch represents a category with representative examples.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["MATERIAL HANDLING EQUIPMENT"]
    rootA --> B1["INDUSTRIAL TRUCKS"]
    rootA --> B2["CONVEYORS"]
    rootA --> B3["CRANES AND HOISTS"]
    rootA --> B4["AUTOMATED MH SYSTEMS"]
    rootA --> B5["AUXILIARY AND STORAGE"]

    B1 --> C1A["Non-Powered"]
    B1 --> C1B["Powered"]
    C1A --> D1A["Hand Truck"]
    C1A --> D1B["Pallet Jack"]
    C1A --> D1C["Wagon or Cart"]
    C1B --> D1D["Forklift Truck"]
    C1B --> D1E["Reach Truck"]
    C1B --> D1F["Order Picker"]
    C1B --> D1G["Side Loader"]

    B2 --> C2A["Powered Continuous"]
    B2 --> C2B["Non-Powered Gravity"]
    C2A --> D2A["Belt Conveyor"]
    C2A --> D2B["Chain Conveyor"]
    C2A --> D2C["Screw Conveyor"]
    C2A --> D2D["Bucket Elevator"]
    C2A --> D2E["Pneumatic Conveyor"]
    C2A --> D2F["Vibrating Conveyor"]
    C2B --> D2G["Roller Conveyor"]
    C2B --> D2H["Chute or Spiral"]

    B3 --> C3A["Bridge and EOT"]
    B3 --> C3B["Jib Crane"]
    B3 --> C3C["Monorail and Hoist"]
    B3 --> D3D["Gantry Crane"]
    C3A --> D3A["Bridge Crane"]
    C3A --> D3B["Stacker Crane"]
    C3C --> D3C["Overhead Hoist"]

    B4 --> C4A["Fixed Automation"]
    B4 --> C4B["Mobile Automation"]
    C4A --> D4A["AS or RS System"]
    C4A --> D4B["Robotic Arm"]
    C4B --> D4C["Automated Guided Vehicle"]
    C4B --> D4D["Unit Load Carrier"]

    B5 --> C5A["Pallets"]
    B5 --> C5B["Storage Racks"]
    B5 --> C5C["Containers and Bins"]
    C5A --> D5A["Wooden Pallet"]
    C5A --> D5B["Plastic Pallet"]
    C5A --> D5C["Metal Pallet"]
```

---

## 4.2 Material Handling System — Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram models the **decision flow** an engineer follows when designing a material handling system. It maps the **interactions** between input data, principle selection, equipment choice, and validation, instead of trying to depict the physical plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START Design of MH System"]
    startA --> s1["Input: Plant Layout, Production Volume, Material Properties"]
    s1 --> s2["Step 1: Identify Movement Requirements: Distance, Path, Frequency, Direction"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 2: Apply the 10 Principles of Material Handling"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 3: Define Unit Load and Containerization Strategy"]
    s4 --> s5["Step 4: Select MH Equipment Category by Distance"]

    s5 --> distA["Short Distance under 30 m"]
    s5 --> distB["Medium Distance 30 to 300 m"]
    s5 --> distC["Long Distance over 300 m"]

    distA --> eqA["Hand Truck, Pallet Jack, Forklift, Jib Crane"]
    distB --> eqB["Roller Conveyor, Belt Conveyor, AGV, EOT Crane"]
    distC --> eqC["Belt Conveyor, Pneumatic Conveyor, Pipeline, Stacker Crane"]

    eqA --> s6["Step 5: Economic Analysis: Capital Cost, CRF, AEC, Payback"]
    eqB --> s6
    eqC --> s6

    s6 --> s7["Step 6: Safety, Ergonomic, and Environmental Check"]
    s7 --> s8{"Step 7: Is Cost within Budget and Ergonomics Acceptable?"}
    s8 -- NO --> s9["Iterate: Change Equipment or Re-layout"]
    s9 --> s2
    s8 -- YES --> s10["Finalize MH Plan and Integrate with Plant Layout"]
    s10 --> endA["END: Documentation, Installation, Commissioning"]
```

---

## 4.3 Integration of Material Handling with Plant Layout Types

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level diagram shows **how the choice of MH equipment is dictated by the plant layout type**. This is a **classic 7-mark KTU question**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layoutA["PROCESS LAYOUT"] --> mhA["Flexible MH: Forklift, Crane, AGV"]
    layoutB["PRODUCT LAYOUT"] --> mhB["Fixed Path MH: Belt Conveyor, Chain Conveyor, Monorail"]
    layoutC["FIXED POSITION LAYOUT"] --> mhC["Mobile Heavy-Duty MH: Gantry Crane, EOT Crane, Mobile Cranes"]
    layoutD["CELLULAR LAYOUT"] --> mhD["Compact MH: Pallet Jack, Mini-AGV, Robotic Arm"]
    layoutE["COMBINED LAYOUT"] --> mhE["Hybrid MH: Mix of Fixed and Flexible Systems"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define material handling. List any four objectives of an efficient material handling system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Material handling is the art and science of moving, storing, packaging, and controlling the flow of raw materials, work-in-process, and finished goods in a plant with minimum cost, maximum safety, and optimum utilization of space, time, and equipment.

**Objectives (any four, 0.5 mark each = 2 marks):**
1. To reduce the unit cost of production.
2. To improve the safety of workers and reduce accidents.
3. To increase the effective utilization of men, machines, and material.
4. To minimize the delays and bottlenecks in production.
5. To ensure optimum use of cubic space (vertical and horizontal).
6. To improve the ergonomic conditions of the workforce.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**State and briefly explain the "Unit Load Principle" of material handling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** The Unit Load Principle states that materials should be handled in the largest possible unit (called a *unit load*) that a piece of equipment can economically and safely transport, in order to minimize the number of handling events and the cost per unit mass.

**Explanation (1.5 marks):** A unit load is a consolidated group of items (typically placed on a pallet, in a container, or in a tote) that is moved, stored, and handled as a single entity throughout the supply chain. Examples include a pallet of 50 cement bags, a tote of 800 kg of powder, or a roll of steel sheet on a stillage. By reducing the number of trips and handling events, the system reduces labour cost, equipment wear, and product damage.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### `Question A — 14 Marks`
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, modified for 2024 scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **10 principles of material handling** with one practical example for each principle. **(*7 marks*)**

**(b)** A plant currently handles **30 000 tonnes/year** of raw material using individual boxes. Each box weighs **25 kg**, and the manual handling cost is **₹ 8 per box**. The company is considering switching to a unit-load system using **1-tonne pallets**. The new handling cost would be **₹ 30 per pallet event**, and the per-pallet handling requires 0.05 hours of forklift time. Calculate the **annual cost of the existing system**, the **annual cost of the proposed system**, the **annual savings**, and the **percentage reduction** in handling cost. **(*7 marks*)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — 10 Principles of Material Handling (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: To score full 7 marks, the student must state each principle *clearly* and give a *distinct, real-world example* — not a vague restatement.

1. **Planning Principle** (0.7 m): Plan all handling activities in advance using the seven M's. *Example: A project plan is made before installing a new conveyor line in a cement plant.*

2. **Systems Principle** (0.7 m): Integrate all handling activities into one coordinated system from receiving to dispatch. *Example: An automotive plant uses an integrated conveyor-AGV-robot system to connect body shop, paint shop, and assembly shop.*

3. **Work Flow Principle** (0.7 m): Use a flow-oriented pattern (straight-line or U-shape) for the movement of material. *Example: An assembly line where the product moves through stations in a fixed sequence.*

4. **Ergonomic Principle** (0.7 m): Design the handling job to match human physical and cognitive limits. *Example: Adjustable-height conveyor so that workers do not have to bend to pick up components.*

5. **Unit Load Principle** (0.7 m): Handle materials in unit loads. *Example: 50 bags of cement stacked on a single wooden pallet and moved by a forklift.*

6. **Space Utilization Principle** (0.7 m): Use cubic space (both floor area and vertical). *Example: Pallet racks in a 10-metre-high warehouse accessed by a stacker crane.*

7. **Standardization Principle** (0.7 m): Standardize equipment and methods for flexibility and lower cost. *Example: All plants of a company use the same model of forklift and same pallet size (e.g., EUR-pallet 1200 × 800 mm).*

8. **Environmental Principle** (0.7 m): Use energy-efficient methods and minimize environmental impact. *Example: Replacing diesel forklifts with electric ones to reduce emissions in an indoor warehouse.*

9. **Mechanization Principle** (0.7 m): Mechanize wherever economically justified. *Example: Replacing 5 manual labourers carrying boxes with 1 forklift operator.*

10. **Flexibility Principle** (0.7 m): Provide flexibility in methods and equipment. *Example: Using a forklift that can switch between pallets, drums, and stillages with different attachments.*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Unit-Load Cost (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the number of boxes per year in the existing system.**

$$ n_{boxes} = \dfrac{30\,000 \text{ t/yr}}{0.025 \text{ t/box}} = 1\,200\,000 \text{ boxes/yr} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the annual cost of the existing system.**

$$ C_{old} = 1\,200\,000 \text{ boxes/yr} \times 8 \text{ ₹/box} = 96\,00\,000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the number of pallets per year in the proposed system.**

$$ n_{pallets} = \dfrac{30\,000 \text{ t/yr}}{1.0 \text{ t/pallet}} = 30\,000 \text{ pallets/yr} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the annual handling cost of the proposed system.**

$$ C_{new} = 30\,000 \text{ pallets/yr} \times 30 \text{ ₹/pallet} = 9\,00\,000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the annual savings.**

$$ S = C_{old} - C_{new} = 96\,00\,000 - 9\,00\,000 = 87\,00\,000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage reduction in handling cost.**

$$ \Delta C\,(\%) = \dfrac{87\,00\,000}{96\,00\,000} \times 100\% = 90.625\% $$

**Step 7 — Compute the additional forklift time required (interpretive comment).**

$$ T_{forklift} = 30\,000 \text{ pallets/yr} \times 0.05 \text{ hr/pallet} = 1500 \text{ hr/yr} $$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The unit-load system reduces handling cost by **₹ 87 lakh/year (~90.63% reduction)**. The additional 1 500 hr/year of forklift usage must be considered against the savings. The proposal is *overwhelmingly* economically justified.

---

### `Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)`
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, modified]`**

**(a)** Classify the various **types of material handling equipment** with suitable examples, and explain how the choice of MH equipment is linked to the type of **plant layout**. **(*7 marks*)**

**(b)** A conveyor system in a sugar factory transports **200 tonnes/hour** of bagasse over a horizontal distance of **80 m** and lifts it by **10 m** vertically. The belt speed is **1.5 m/s** and the overall mechanical efficiency is **85%**. Using the standard conveyor power formula, calculate the **power required to drive the belt** in kW. **(*7 marks*)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Classification & Layout Linkage (7 marks)

**Classification (4 marks, 0.5 m each example + 0.5 m for the category):**

| Category | Examples (0.5 m each) |
|---|---|
| 1. Industrial Trucks (Non-powered) | Hand truck, pallet jack, wagon |
| 2. Industrial Trucks (Powered) | Forklift, reach truck, order picker |
| 3. Conveyors (Powered continuous) | Belt, chain, screw, bucket elevator, pneumatic |
| 4. Conveyors (Gravity) | Roller conveyor, chute, spiral chute |
| 5. Cranes & Hoists | Jib crane, EOT crane, gantry crane, monorail hoist |
| 6. Automated Systems | AGV, AS/RS, articulated robot, SCARA robot |
| 7. Auxiliary / Storage | Pallets, racks, bins, totes, silos, hoppers |

**Linkage with Plant Layout (3 marks):**

| Plant Layout Type | Preferred MH Equipment | Reason (1 m each) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Layout** | Forklift, AGV, jib crane | Layout is flexible and variable; equipment must be mobile. |
| **Product Layout** | Belt conveyor, chain conveyor, monorail, overhead conveyor | Material moves in a fixed, continuous, high-volume path. |
| **Fixed-Position Layout** | Heavy-duty gantry crane, mobile crane, EOT crane | The product (e.g., a ship) is stationary; equipment must come to it. |
| **Cellular / U-Cell Layout** | Mini-AGV, pallet jack, light-duty robot | Cells are compact and use small-batch, short-distance moves. |
| **Warehouse Layout** | AS/RS, stacker crane, unit-load conveyor, VNA truck | High density and vertical space utilization are key. |

---

### Part (b) — Conveyor Power Calculation (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 200$ tonnes/hr
- Horizontal distance $L = 80$ m
- Vertical lift $H = 10$ m
- Belt speed $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_{m} = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow rate to kg/s.**

$$ \dot{m} = 200 \text{ t/hr} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg}}{1 \text{ t}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 55.56 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear mass loading on the belt ($W$ in kg/m).**

The mass per unit length of the belt (the linear loading) is given by:

$$ W = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{v} = \dfrac{55.56 \text{ kg/s}}{1.5 \text{ m/s}} = 37.04 \text{ kg/m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the horizontal power requirement (to overcome friction over the horizontal distance).**

For an industrial belt conveyor, the horizontal force is approximated as a fraction of the total belt loading (carrying + return) using a friction factor. Using the simplified standard formula for power at the drive pulley:

$$ P_{horiz} = \dfrac{W \times L \times g \times f}{1000} \text{ kW} $$

where $f \approx 0.03$ is a typical friction factor for a belt conveyor on idlers. Substituting:

$$ P_{horiz} = \dfrac{37.04 \times 80 \times 9.81 \times 0.03}{1000} = \dfrac{8721.6}{1000} = 8.72 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the vertical lift power requirement.**

$$ P_{lift} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \times g \times H}{1000} = \dfrac{55.56 \times 9.81 \times 10}{1000} = 5.45 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the total shaft power (sum of horizontal and vertical).**

$$ P_{shaft} = P_{horiz} + P_{lift} = 8.72 + 5.45 = 14.17 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 6 — Compute the motor power (accounting for mechanical efficiency).**

$$ P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{m}} = \dfrac{14.17}{0.85} = 16.67 \text{ kW} $$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The required motor rating is **approximately 17 kW**. In practice, a standard 18.5 kW or 22 kW motor would be selected after applying a service factor of 1.1–1.25.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic (Module 3 — Material Handling):**
>
> 1. **Listing principles without examples (–2 to –3 marks):** When asked "explain the 10 principles", many students simply *list* them. The examiner expects a *distinct, plant-relevant example* for *each* principle.
> 2. **Confusing MH with Logistics (–1 mark):** Material handling is *internal* to the plant; logistics is *external* (inbound and outbound transport). The examiner will deduct for any interchanging of the two.
> 3. **In the numerical problem, students forget the efficiency term (–1 mark):** When computing motor power, the *mechanical efficiency* MUST be in the denominator. Forgetting it gives a lower (un-conservative) motor rating.
> 4. **Comparing capital cost instead of AEC (–2 marks):** When two MH alternatives are given, the *correct* comparison is **Annual Equivalent Cost (AEC)** using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**, not raw capital cost. This is a very common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Wrong unit conversion (–1 mark):** Converting tonnes/hr to kg/s is a frequent stumbling block. Always state the conversion explicitly: $1 \text{ t/hr} = \dfrac{1000}{3600} \text{ kg/s} = 0.2778 \text{ kg/s}$.
> 6. **Ignoring the "Unit Load" link to the layout (–1 mark):** The examiner often *traps* students by asking: *"How does the unit load concept influence the choice of layout?"* A correct answer must say that *unit loads enable a U-shaped or straight-line product layout, while small individual items force a process layout.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — PEPET634 Module 3 (Material Handling in Plants):**

- ☐ **Definition (must memorize):** Material handling = the art and science of moving, storing, packaging, and controlling the flow of materials in a plant with minimum cost, maximum safety, and optimum use of space, time, and equipment.
- ☐ **The 5 R's of MH** (AMHS): Right Quantity, Right Quality, Right Place, Right Position, Right Time.
- ☐ **10 Principles of MH** (Planning, Systems, Work Flow, Ergonomic, Unit Load, Space Utilization, Standardization, Environmental, Mechanization, Flexibility) — know at least one example per principle.
- ☐ **Unit Load Principle** reduces handling events → reduces cost per unit mass → enables a product layout.
- ☐ **Five functional categories of MH equipment**: Industrial Trucks, Conveyors, Cranes & Hoists, Automated Systems (AGV, AS/RS, robots), Auxiliary & Storage (pallets, racks, bins).
- ☐ **Selection of MH equipment** depends on: material properties, volume, distance, path, layout, cost, flexibility, safety, space, and maintenance.
- ☐ **Conveyors** are best for **fixed-path, high-volume, long-distance** moves (product layout).
- ☐ **Forklifts & AGVs** are best for **flexible-path, low-to-medium volume** moves (process layout).
- ☐ **Cranes & hoists** are best for **vertical and heavy-duty lifting** (fixed-position layout).
- ☐ **AGV = Automated Guided Vehicle; AS/RS = Automated Storage and Retrieval System.**
- ☐ **Key equations** to remember verbatim for KTU 2024 ESE:
  - $T_{cycle} = T_{load} + T_{travel} + T_{unload} + T_{idle}$
  - $N = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{T_{cycle}}$
  - $Q = N \times U$
  - $C_{annual} = C_{capital} \times CRF + C_{operating}$
  - $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$
  - $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{horiz} + P_{lift}}{\eta_{m}}$
- ☐ **Layout ↔ MH mapping:** Process → Forklift/AGV; Product → Conveyor; Fixed-position → Crane; Cellular → Mini-AGV/Robot.
- ☐ **Always use AEC (not capital cost) when comparing two MH alternatives.**
- ☐ **Always divide by efficiency** when computing motor power for a conveyor.
- ☐ **1 t/hr = 1000/3600 = 0.2778 kg/s** (a classic unit-conversion trap).
- ☐ **Industrial safety:** Up to 25% of industrial accidents are MH-related — the designer's duty is to integrate *safety* (Principle 4: Ergonomic) at the design stage itself, not as an afterthought.
