> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Material Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Material Handling (MH)** is the art and science of **moving, packaging, storing, controlling, and protecting** materials and products throughout the processes of manufacturing, distribution, consumption, and disposal. As per the **Material Handling Institute (MHI)** — the global authority referenced in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus — it is formally defined as:

> *"Material Handling is the moving, handling, and storing of materials in a system in order to maximize productivity, minimize cost, and improve safety while ensuring quality."*

In the context of **PEPET634 – Plant Layout and Design**, Material Handling is treated as the **connecting tissue** between workstations, storage points, and shipping/receiving zones. It is not an isolated activity but a **subsystem** that integrates storage, transportation, and information flow within a plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (Dec 2023 / May 2024 pattern):** Examiners expect students to define MH with reference to the **integrative function** it plays between *men, materials, and machines* — not just as "shifting goods with forklifts."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bloodstream" Model)

Imagine a human body:
- The **organs** are the workstations (machines, assembly points, inspection zones).
- The **materials** being worked on are like the **nutrients/oxygen** they need to function.
- The **bloodstream** (arteries and veins) that carries these nutrients from one organ to another is the **Material Handling System**.

If the bloodstream is slow, blocked, or inefficient, the whole body suffocates — even if the organs themselves are perfectly healthy. Similarly, in a plant:
- **Slow MH** = high Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory
- **Blocked MH** = bottlenecks
- **Inefficient MH** = damage, delays, and cost overruns

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Material Handling is **not a value-adding activity** in itself (the product is not transformed by being moved), but it is a **value-supporting activity** — without it, no value-adding work can occur.

---

## 1.3 Why Material Handling Matters in Plant Design

| **Performance Metric** | **Without Efficient MH** | **With Efficient MH** |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing Cost | **20–50%** of total cost | Reduced to **10–20%** |
| Production Delays | Frequent | Minimized |
| Worker Injuries (Safety) | High incidence | Drastically reduced |
| Product Damage | Significant scrap | Negligible |
| Plant Throughput | Constrained | Optimized |

**Standard Industrial Benchmark:** In most discrete manufacturing plants, material handling accounts for **30% to 75% of the total operating cost**, making it the **single largest controllable cost** in plant operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – KTU 2024):** The "Principles of Material Handling" form the **decision-making framework** that determines *what* to move, *how* to move, *when* to move, and *with what equipment* to move.

---

## 1.4 Objectives of an Efficient Material Handling System

1. **Minimize handling cost** per unit produced.
2. **Maximize throughput** (units moved per unit time).
3. **Reduce material damage** and product deterioration.
4. **Improve ergonomics** and worker safety.
5. **Optimize space utilization** in the plant.
6. **Eliminate unnecessary movement** (e.g., backtracking, cross-flows).
7. **Enable flexible response** to demand and product mix changes.
8. **Integrate with storage, packaging, and information systems.**

---

## 1.5 Scope of Material Handling in Modern Plants

The scope has expanded significantly with Industry 4.0:

- **Traditional MH:** Forklifts, cranes, conveyors, hoists.
- **Modern MH:** Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS), Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs), robotic pick-and-place systems, and IoT-enabled real-time tracking (RFID, WSN).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Update:** The syllabus (Module 3) now expects students to appreciate the **shift from manual to automated MH**, so references to AS/RS and AGVs are likely in the ESE question bank.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10 Principles of Material Handling (MHI Framework)

The **Material Handling Institute (MHI)** has codified **Ten Universal Principles** that act as a logical, hierarchical decision framework. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats these as the **backbone of Module 3**.

### Principle 1 — The Planning Principle
> *"A well-planned material handling system leads to efficient operations."*

- All MH decisions must originate from a **comprehensive plan**.
- The plan must consider the **"4 Ws"**: *What* is moved, *When*, *Where*, and *Why*.
- Planning precedes equipment selection.

### Principle 2 — The Standardization Principle
> *"Standardize handling methods and equipment wherever possible."*

- Reduces variety of equipment → lower spares inventory, lower training cost.
- Standard pallets, standard container sizes, standard process flows.

### Principle 3 — The Work (Simplification) Principle
> *"Simplify handling by reducing, combining, or eliminating unnecessary movement."*

- Apply the **Principle of Economy of Movement**.
- Combine operations (e.g., combine lifting + transporting + lowering into a single crane cycle).

### Principle 4 — The Ergonomic Principle
> *"Recognize the human element in material handling."*

- Design for **operator comfort** and **safety**.
- Limit manual lifting to safe weights (standard reference: **23 kg** for an average worker under favorable conditions, per ISO 11228 and NIOSH Lifting Equation).
- Use mechanical aids to reduce repetitive strain injuries (RSI).

### Principle 5 — The Unit Load Principle
> *"Handle materials in larger units whenever feasible."*

- Consolidate small items into **unit loads** (pallets, containers, totes, drums).
- A **Unit Load** = a collection of items that are moved, handled, and stored as a single entity.
- Reduces the **number of handling cycles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Rule of Thumb:** Increasing unit load size by 2× can reduce the number of handling cycles by 50%, but only up to the equipment's rated capacity.

### Principle 6 — The Space Utilization Principle
> *"Make effective use of cubic space."*

- Don't just think 2D (floor area) — think **3D (cubic volume)**.
- Use vertical space: rack systems, mezzanines, automated stacker cranes.
- **Space Utilization Ratio** = (Volume of stored material) / (Total available cubic volume) × 100

### Principle 7 — The System Principle
> *"Integrate handling activities into a coordinated system."*

- MH is not a collection of isolated equipment; it is an **integrated system**.
- Consider the **flow**: receiving → storage → production → assembly → shipping.
- Optimize the **whole**, not the **parts** (Systems Thinking — Ackoff's Law).

### Principle 8 — The Automation Principle
> *"Automate handling operations where feasible and justified."*

- Automation is justified when:
  - Volume is **high and stable**.
  - Labor cost is **high or labor is scarce**.
  - Tasks are **repetitive, hazardous, or require high precision**.
- Examples: AS/RS, AGVs, conveyor sortation, robotic palletizers.

### Principle 9 — The Environmental Principle
> *"Minimize environmental impact and energy consumption."*

- Choose energy-efficient equipment (e.g., electric forklifts over diesel).
- Use recyclable pallets and biodegradable packaging.
- Comply with **environmental regulations** (e.g., ISO 14001).

### Principle 10 — The Life Cycle Cost (LCC) Principle
> *"Evaluate the total cost of a material handling system over its entire life."*

- **LCC = Initial Cost + Operating Cost + Maintenance Cost + Disposal Cost − Salvage Value**
- Don't optimize for **cheapest purchase price** — optimize for **lowest LCC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The 10 principles are **interdependent** and **hierarchical**. A violation of an earlier principle (e.g., Planning) cannot be fixed by a later principle (e.g., Automation). The order matters!

---

## 2.2 Classification of Material Handling Equipment

| **Category** | **Sub-category** | **Examples** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Transport Equipment** | Horizontal | Conveyors, AGVs, forklifts, hand trucks | Moving across the plant |
| | Vertical | Elevators, hoists, cranes, lifts | Moving between floors |
| **2. Positioning Equipment** | — | Robotic arms, jigs, fixtures, turntables | Orienting materials for processing |
| **3. Storage Equipment** | Static | Racks, shelving, mezzanines | Holding materials |
| | Dynamic | Carousel systems, AS/RS, vertical lift modules (VLM) | Automated storage/retrieval |
| **4. Unit Load Forming** | — | Palletizers, stretch wrappers, carton erectors | Creating unit loads |
| **5. Identification & Control** | — | RFID readers, barcode scanners, WMS | Tracking and routing |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula / Metric** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Material Handling Cost Ratio | $MHCR = \dfrac{C_{MH}}{C_{Total}} \times 100$ | %, where $C_{MH}$ = MH cost, $C_{Total}$ = total production cost |
| Unit Load Weight | $W_{UL} = n \times w_i$ | kg, where $n$ = number of items, $w_i$ = weight per item |
| Handling Cycles Reduction | $\Delta C = C_{old} - C_{new}$ | dimensionless count of cycles |
| Space Utilization Ratio | $SUR = \dfrac{V_{used}}{V_{available}} \times 100$ | %, cubic meters |
| Material Flow Distance | $D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | meters (sum of all moves) |
| Conveyor Capacity | $Q = 3600 \times v \times \rho \times A$ | tons/hour, where $v$ = belt speed (m/s), $\rho$ = bulk density (t/m³), $A$ = cross-section area (m²) |
| Forklift Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = T_{load} + T_{travel} + T_{unload} + T_{idle}$ | minutes per cycle |
| AGV Throughput | $\lambda = \dfrac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ | trips/hour |
| Life Cycle Cost | $LCC = C_{init} + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{C_{op,t} + C_{maint,t}}{(1+r)^t} - S$ | currency, discounted over life T |
| NIOSH Lifting Index | $LI = \dfrac{L}{RWL}$ | dimensionless, $RWL$ = Recommended Weight Limit |
| Handling Efficiency | $\eta_{MH} = \dfrac{T_{value-adding}}{T_{value-adding} + T_{handling}}$ | ratio $\leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always carry the **units** in the final answer. A numerical answer without units = guaranteed ½ mark deduction.

---

## 2.4 The Material Handling Cost Equation (Engineering Utility)

The single most important metric used by industrial engineers to benchmark MH performance is:

$$MHCR = \frac{C_{MH}}{C_{Total}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

**Interpretation Ranges (Industry Standards):**

- $MHCR < 10\%$ → **World-class / Lean plant** (e.g., Toyota, Tesla)
- $MHCR = 10\text{–}20\%$ → **Efficient plant**
- $MHCR = 20\text{–}40\%$ → **Typical plant** (room for improvement)
- $MHCR > 40\%$ → **Crisis-level inefficiency** (e.g., poorly laid out job-shop)

**Engineering Utility:** This ratio is used in **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)**, **layout redesign justification**, and **make-or-buy decisions** (e.g., outsource MH or do it in-house).

---

## 2.5 Integration with Plant Layout (Module 2 Recap)

| **Layout Type** | **MH Strategy** | **Dominant MH Equipment** |
|---|---|---|
| **Product (Line) Layout** | Fixed-path, high-volume flow | Powered conveyors, AGVs, overhead monorails |
| **Process (Functional) Layout** | Variable-path, low-volume | Forklifts, hand trucks, cranes |
| **Fixed-Position Layout** | Bring resources to the product | Heavy-duty cranes, gantries, SPMTs (Self-Propelled Modular Transporters) |
| **Cellular Layout** | Bounded flow within cells | Light conveyors, roller tracks, AGVs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Handling Cost Ratio (MHCR) — Full Numerical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A manufacturing plant in Kerala produces **engineered fasteners**. The annual total production cost is **₹5,00,00,000** (₹5 Crore). The annual expenditure on material handling (labor + equipment depreciation + fuel + maintenance) is **₹1,25,00,000** (₹1.25 Crore). Determine the Material Handling Cost Ratio and classify the plant's MH efficiency.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Quantities

$$
C_{Total} = 5,00,00,000 \text{ INR} = 5.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{MH} = 1,25,00,000 \text{ INR} = 1.25 \times 10^{8} \text{ INR}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The values are written in standard scientific notation to prevent digit-counting errors during manual calculation. Both values are in the same currency (INR) and same period (annual), so no unit conversion is required.

### Step 2: Apply the Material Handling Cost Ratio Formula

$$
MHCR = \frac{C_{MH}}{C_{Total}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
MHCR = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{8}}{5.0 \times 10^{8}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Fraction

The powers of 10 cancel out:

$$
MHCR = \frac{1.25}{5.0} \times 100
$$

Perform the division:

$$
\frac{1.25}{5.0} = 0.25
$$

### Step 4: Multiply by 100 to Convert to Percentage

$$
MHCR = 0.25 \times 100 = 25\,\%
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation and Classification

$$
MHCR = 25\,\% \Rightarrow \text{Typical plant (improvement possible)}
$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** The plant should target reducing $MHCR$ to **15–18%** by:
- Replacing manual forklift moves with AGVs in long-distance transport.
- Consolidating small orders into unit loads.
- Redesigning the layout to minimize backtracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $MHCR = 25\,\%$. The plant is in the "typical" category and has scope for MH optimization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conveyor Capacity for Bulk Material Handling

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A cement plant uses a belt conveyor to transport crushed limestone. The belt speed is **2 m/s**, the bulk density of limestone is **1.6 t/m³**, and the cross-sectional area of material on the belt is **0.05 m²**. Compute the conveyor capacity in **tons per hour**.

### Step 1: Recall the Standard Capacity Formula

$$
Q = 3600 \times v \times \rho \times A
$$

where:
- $Q$ = capacity (tons/hour)
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s)
- $\rho$ = bulk density (tons/m³)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of load (m²)
- **3600** = conversion factor from per-second to per-hour (seconds per hour)

### Step 2: Substitute the Given Values

$$
Q = 3600 \times 2 \times 1.6 \times 0.05
$$

### Step 3: Multiply Sequentially

First, $2 \times 1.6 = 3.2$

$$
Q = 3600 \times 3.2 \times 0.05
$$

Next, $3.2 \times 0.05 = 0.16$

$$
Q = 3600 \times 0.16
$$

### Step 4: Final Calculation

$$
Q = 576 \text{ tons/hour}
$$

### Step 5: Sanity Check

The order of magnitude is consistent — a 1-meter-wide belt at 2 m/s handling 5 cm of limestone should indeed be in the hundreds of tons/hour range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $Q = 576$ tons/hour. The conveyor can transport **5,76,000 kg of limestone per hour** = **~4.6 lakh tons per day** in continuous operation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of a Forklift Fleet

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A logistics company is evaluating two forklift options. Determine the LCC over **5 years** at a discount rate $r = 8\%$ per annum.

| **Parameter** | **Forklift A (Diesel)** | **Forklift B (Electric)** |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost ($C_{init}$) | ₹8,00,000 | ₹12,00,000 |
| Annual Operating Cost ($C_{op}$) | ₹2,00,000 | ₹1,00,000 |
| Annual Maintenance ($C_{maint}$) | ₹80,000 | ₹40,000 |
| Salvage Value ($S$) | ₹2,00,000 | ₹4,00,000 |

### Step 1: Recall the LCC Formula

$$
LCC = C_{init} + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{C_{op,t} + C_{maint,t}}{(1 + r)^t} - S
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Future costs are **discounted to present value** using the factor $\frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$, reflecting the time value of money (a rupee today is worth more than a rupee in 5 years).

### Step 2: Compute the Annual Cost for Forklift A

Annual combined cost:
$$
C_A = 2,00,000 + 80,000 = 2,80,000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

Discount factors for $r = 0.08$:

| **Year $t$** | $(1.08)^t$ | Discount Factor $\frac{1}{(1.08)^t}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0800 | 0.9259 |
| 2 | 1.1664 | 0.8573 |
| 3 | 1.2597 | 0.7938 |
| 4 | 1.3605 | 0.7350 |
| 5 | 1.4693 | 0.6806 |

Sum of discount factors:
$$
\sum = 0.9259 + 0.8573 + 0.7938 + 0.7350 + 0.6806 = 3.9926
$$

Present value of operating + maintenance:
$$
PV_A = 2,80,000 \times 3.9926 = 11,17,928 \text{ INR}
$$

Total LCC for Forklift A:
$$
LCC_A = 8,00,000 + 11,17,928 - 2,00,000 = 17,17,928 \text{ INR}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Annual Cost for Forklift B

Annual combined cost:
$$
C_B = 1,00,000 + 40,000 = 1,40,000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

Present value (using same discount factors):
$$
PV_B = 1,40,000 \times 3.9926 = 5,58,964 \text{ INR}
$$

Total LCC for Forklift B:
$$
LCC_B = 12,00,000 + 5,58,964 - 4,00,000 = 13,58,964 \text{ INR}
$$

### Step 4: Compare and Decide

$$
LCC_B = 13,58,964 < LCC_A = 17,17,928
$$

**Difference:**
$$
\Delta = 17,17,928 - 13,58,964 = 3,58,964 \text{ INR}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** **Forklift B (Electric)** is the economically superior choice over 5 years, saving approximately **₹3.59 lakh**, despite a higher initial cost. This demonstrates **Principle 10 (Life Cycle Cost Principle)** — never decide on purchase price alone.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What MH Equipment

| **Decision Variable** | **Manual MH** | **Mechanized MH** | **Automated MH** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume (units/day)** | < 100 | 100 – 5,000 | > 5,000 |
| **Distance** | < 50 m | 50 – 500 m | > 500 m (or repetitive) |
| **Variety** | High (frequent change) | Medium | Low (standardized) |
| **Unit Load Weight** | < 23 kg | 23 – 1,000 kg | > 1,000 kg or very precise |
| **Capital Available** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Labor Cost & Risk** | Low & safe | Moderate | High-risk / hazardous |
| **Technology Readiness** | Basic | Mature | Advanced (IoT, AI) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark essay questions, you may be asked to *justify* your choice of MH strategy. Use this matrix structure to organize your argument.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Framework of the 10 Principles of Material Handling

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical sequence** of the MHI principles, showing how planning at the top cascades down to operational decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Principle 1: Planning] --> B[Principle 2: Standardization]
    B --> C[Principle 3: Work Simplification]
    C --> D[Principle 4: Ergonomics]
    D --> E[Principle 5: Unit Load]
    E --> F[Principle 6: Space Utilization]
    F --> G[Principle 7: System Integration]
    G --> H[Principle 8: Automation]
    H --> I[Principle 9: Environmental]
    I --> J[Principle 10: Life Cycle Cost]

    A1[Define the 4 Ws: What When Where Why] -.-> A
    B1[Standard pallets and containers] -.-> B
    C1[Combine eliminate reduce moves] -.-> C
    D1[Limit manual lift to 23 kg] -.-> D
    E1[Consolidate items into pallets] -.-> E
    F1[Use vertical cubic space] -.-> F
    G1[Integrate flow end to end] -.-> G
    H1[AGVs AS RS robotics] -.-> H
    I1[Energy efficiency ISO 14001] -.-> I
    J1[Minimize LCC not purchase cost] -.-> J

    classDef principle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2540,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef detail fill:#d9e2f3,stroke:#5b7cb0,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J principle
    class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,G1,H1,I1,J1 detail
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** (top-down): Indicate the **logical sequence** — each principle feeds into the next.
- **Dotted arrows** (right-anchored): Show **operational examples** for each principle.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Integrated Material Handling System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT ZONE"]
        RCV[Receiving Dock]
        INSP[Incoming Inspection]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["STORAGE SUBSYSTEM"]
        RS[Racks and Shelving]
        ASRS[AS RS System]
        WMS[Warehouse Management System]
    end

    subgraph PRODUCTION["PRODUCTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        WS1[Workstation 1]
        WS2[Workstation 2]
        WS3[Workstation N]
    end

    subgraph MHTRANSPORT["MH TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        CONV[Conveyors]
        AGV[AGVs]
        FL[Forklifts]
        OHM[Overhead Monorail]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT ZONE"]
        PKG[Packaging]
        SHIP[Shipping Dock]
    end

    RCV --> INSP
    INSP --> RS
    INSP --> ASRS
    RS --> WMS
    ASRS --> WMS
    WMS --> CONV
    WMS --> AGV
    CONV --> WS1
    AGV --> WS2
    FL --> WS3
    OHM --> WS1
    OHM --> WS2
    WS1 --> OHM
    WS2 --> CONV
    WS3 --> AGV
    CONV --> PKG
    AGV --> PKG
    PKG --> SHIP

    classDef zone fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1967d2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mh fill:#fce8e6,stroke:#c5221f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ws fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#137333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    class RCV,INSP,RS,ASRS,WMS,PKG,SHIP zone
    class CONV,AGV,FL,OHM mh
    class WS1,WS2,WS3 ws
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **blue blocks** represent the receiving, storage, and shipping zones (peripheral systems).
- The **red blocks** represent the **MH Transport Layer** — the "bloodstream" of the plant.
- The **green blocks** are the value-adding workstations.
- Arrows show **bidirectional material and information flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Insight:** Notice how the WMS (Warehouse Management System) acts as the **central nervous system** — it issues routing instructions to all MH equipment. This visualization directly supports **Principle 7 (System Integration)**.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting Material Handling Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New MH Requirement] --> Q1{Volume of material per day}
    Q1 -->|Less than 100| Q2{Distance to move}
    Q1 -->|100 to 5000| Q3{Path is fixed or variable}
    Q1 -->|Greater than 5000| Q4{Budget allows automation}

    Q2 -->|Less than 50 m| M1[Manual: Hand truck or trolley]
    Q2 -->|Greater than 50 m| M2[Mechanized: Pallet jack or forklift]

    Q3 -->|Fixed path| M3[Conveyor or monorail]
    Q3 -->|Variable path| M4[AGV or forklift]

    Q4 -->|Yes| M5[AS RS with stacker crane]
    Q4 -->|No| M6[Mechanized with operator]

    M1 --> END[End: Equipment selected]
    M2 --> END
    M3 --> END
    M4 --> END
    M5 --> END
    M6 --> END

    classDef question fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef manual fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef mech fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef auto fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 question
    class M1 manual
    class M2,M3,M4,M6 mech
    class M5 auto
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Application:** In a 14-mark design question, this decision tree can serve as the **skeleton** for selecting an MH system. Walk the examiner through the decision branches logically.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define material handling. List any **four objectives** of an efficient material handling system.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Material handling is the art and science of moving, packaging, storing, controlling, and protecting materials and products throughout the processes of manufacturing, distribution, consumption, and disposal with the goal of maximizing productivity, minimizing cost, and improving safety (MHI Definition).

**Four Objectives:**
1. **Minimize handling cost** per unit produced (target MHCR < 20%).
2. **Maximize material flow efficiency** and reduce Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory.
3. **Improve worker safety and ergonomics** by reducing manual lifting.
4. **Optimize space utilization** through 3D (cubic) storage planning.

*(Any 4 valid objectives accepted — ½ mark each.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State and briefly explain the **Unit Load Principle** of material handling.

**Model Answer:**

**Unit Load Principle (Principle 5):** *"Handle materials in larger units whenever feasible."*

**Explanation:** A unit load is a collection of items assembled into a single, moveable entity (e.g., a pallet of cartons, a drum, a tote). By consolidating multiple small items into one unit load, the number of handling cycles is reduced, which in turn reduces labor cost, equipment wear, and the risk of damage. Unit loads are typically moved using forklifts, conveyors, or AGVs.

**Example:** Instead of moving 20 individual cartons one-by-one, consolidate them onto a single pallet and move them in 1 cycle — reducing 20 cycles to 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions of Module 3 carry **internal choice** between two questions (A or B), each with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The choice below mirrors this exact pattern.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Ten Principles of Material Handling** as codified by the Material Handling Institute (MHI). Emphasize why **planning** must precede all other principles.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A manufacturing plant reports an annual material handling cost of **₹80 lakhs** and a total annual production cost of **₹4 crores**. Compute the Material Handling Cost Ratio (MHCR) and recommend two engineering interventions to bring it below 15%.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

The Ten Principles of Material Handling (MHI Framework) are:

1. **Planning Principle:** A well-planned MH system leads to efficient operations. The planner must define the "4 Ws" — What, When, Where, and Why — before selecting equipment. Planning precedes all other principles because it sets the design envelope.

2. **Standardization Principle:** Use standardized equipment, methods, and unit loads (e.g., ISO-standard pallets of 1200×1000 mm) to reduce variety and simplify training.

3. **Work (Simplification) Principle:** Reduce, combine, or eliminate unnecessary moves. Apply the principle of economy of movement.

4. **Ergonomic Principle:** Recognize the human element. Limit manual lifting (standard reference: 23 kg per worker per NIOSH). Provide mechanical aids to prevent Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSI).

5. **Unit Load Principle:** Consolidate items into larger moveable units (pallets, totes, drums) to reduce handling cycles.

6. **Space Utilization Principle:** Make effective use of cubic space — use vertical storage (racks, mezzanines, AS/RS).

7. **System Principle:** Integrate handling into a coordinated end-to-end system. Optimize the whole, not the parts (Systems Thinking).

8. **Automation Principle:** Automate MH operations where justified by volume, cost, safety, and precision considerations (AGVs, AS/RS, robotics).

9. **Environmental Principle:** Minimize environmental impact. Choose energy-efficient equipment (e.g., electric over diesel), use recyclable packaging.

10. **Life Cycle Cost Principle:** Evaluate the total cost over the equipment's life — not just the purchase price.

**Why Planning Must Precede Others (1 extra mark):** Planning defines the *requirements* — volume, distance, variety, frequency. Without these specifications, no equipment selection, ergonomic design, or automation decision can be meaningfully made. Attempting Principle 8 (Automation) before Principle 1 (Planning) leads to costly retrofits.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often *list* the 10 principles but **fail to interlink them**. To score full marks, mention that the principles are **hierarchical and interdependent** — a later principle cannot compensate for the violation of an earlier one.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Given Data:**

$$
C_{MH} = 80 \text{ lakhs} = 80,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{Total} = 4 \text{ crores} = 4,00,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 1: Apply the MHCR Formula:**

$$
MHCR = \frac{C_{MH}}{C_{Total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2: Substitute:**

$$
MHCR = \frac{80,00,000}{4,00,00,000} \times 100
$$

**Step 3: Simplify:**

$$
MHCR = \frac{80}{400} \times 100 = 0.20 \times 100 = 20\,\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation:** The plant is in the "Typical" category (20–40%). It must reduce MHCR to **below 15%** to be classified as "Efficient."

**Step 5: Two Engineering Interventions:**

1. **Implement Unit Load Principle with Standardized Pallets:** Replace 200 individual small-box moves per day with 40 palletized moves using standardized ISO pallets. This reduces handling cycles by 80%, cutting labor cost and equipment fuel consumption.

2. **Deploy AGVs for Repetitive Long-Distance Transport:** For routes exceeding 200 m with high frequency, replace manual forklifts with Automated Guided Vehicles. This reduces travel time, eliminates driver fatigue, and provides 24/7 operation, reducing MHCR by approximately 5–7 percentage points.

**Target Outcome:** $MHCR$ reduces from **20% to ~13–14%** — well within the "Efficient Plant" range.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often **forget the multiplication by 100** in Step 3 and write 0.20 instead of 20%. This loses 1 mark. Always state the **unit (%)** explicitly.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Classify the various **types of material handling equipment** with at least two examples for each category. Briefly justify the use of AS/RS in modern plants.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A belt conveyor is used to transport **coal** in a thermal power plant. The belt speed is **1.5 m/s**, the bulk density of coal is **0.85 t/m³**, and the cross-sectional area of the load on the belt is **0.08 m²**. Calculate the conveyor capacity in tons per hour. If the plant needs to handle **1,000 tons/hour**, by what factor must the speed be increased (assuming cross-section and density remain constant)?

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Classification of Material Handling Equipment:**

| **Category** | **Description** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Transport Equipment (Horizontal)** | Moves material along a horizontal or inclined path | Powered conveyors, AGVs, forklifts, hand trucks, monorails |
| **2. Transport Equipment (Vertical)** | Moves material between elevations | Hoists, cranes, elevators, lifts, vertical conveyors |
| **3. Positioning Equipment** | Orients material for a process | Jigs, fixtures, robotic arms, turntables, manipulators |
| **4. Storage Equipment (Static)** | Holds material in fixed locations | Pallet racks, shelving, mezzanines, bins |
| **5. Storage Equipment (Dynamic)** | Automated storage and retrieval | AS/RS, carousels, vertical lift modules (VLM), mobile racking |
| **6. Unit Load Forming** | Creates consolidated unit loads | Palletizers, stretch wrappers, carton erectors, strapping machines |
| **7. Identification & Control** | Tracks and routes material | Barcode scanners, RFID readers, WMS software, sensors |

**Justification for AS/RS in Modern Plants (1.5 marks):**

An **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** is justified in modern plants for the following reasons:

1. **High-Density Storage:** Uses cubic space efficiently — up to **85%** space utilization vs. **30–40%** in conventional racks.
2. **Labor Cost Reduction:** Eliminates manual pickers in hazardous or repetitive zones.
3. **Accuracy:** Achieves > **99.9%** inventory accuracy through barcode/RFID integration.
4. **Speed:** Stack cranes operate at 1–4 m/s, dramatically reducing retrieval time.
5. **Integration with Industry 4.0:** Interfaces with MES and ERP for real-time inventory visibility.
6. **Safety:** Removes human entry into high-bay storage zones.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only *examples* without *describing* what each category is meant for. The **description column is mandatory** for full marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given Data:**

$$
v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}, \quad \rho = 0.85 \text{ t/m}^3, \quad A = 0.08 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 1: Recall the Conveyor Capacity Formula:**

$$
Q = 3600 \times v \times \rho \times A
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Values:**

$$
Q = 3600 \times 1.5 \times 0.85 \times 0.08
$$

**Step 3: Multiply Sequentially:**

$$
1.5 \times 0.85 = 1.275
$$

$$
1.275 \times 0.08 = 0.102
$$

$$
Q = 3600 \times 0.102 = 367.2 \text{ tons/hour}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with Required Capacity:**

$$
Q_{required} = 1{,}000 \text{ tons/hour}
$$

Since $Q = 367.2 < 1{,}000$, the current speed is insufficient.

**Step 5: Find the Required Speed $v_{new}$:**

Using the same formula and solving for $v_{new}$:

$$
v_{new} = \frac{Q_{required}}{3600 \times \rho \times A} = \frac{1{,}000}{3600 \times 0.85 \times 0.08}
$$

Compute the denominator:
$$
3600 \times 0.85 \times 0.08 = 3600 \times 0.068 = 244.8
$$

$$
v_{new} = \frac{1{,}000}{244.8} = 4.085 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Factor of Increase:**

$$
k = \frac{v_{new}}{v_{old}} = \frac{4.085}{1.5} \approx 2.72
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> - Conveyor capacity at 1.5 m/s: **$Q = 367.2$ tons/hour**
> - Required speed for 1,000 tons/hour: **$v_{new} = 4.085$ m/s**
> - Speed must be increased by a factor of **≈ 2.72×** (a 172% increase)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to **convert per-second to per-hour** by multiplying by 3600, leading to answers that are off by 3 orders of magnitude. Always double-check: $Q$ is in **tons/hour**, and the formula already includes the 3600 conversion factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

- ✅ **MH Definition (MHI):** Moving + handling + storing materials to maximize productivity, minimize cost, and improve safety.
- ✅ **Typical MH Cost Share:** **30–75%** of total operating cost in discrete manufacturing.
- ✅ **10 Principles (in order):** Planning → Standardization → Work → Ergonomics → Unit Load → Space → System → Automation → Environment → LCC.
- ✅ **Unit Load Principle:** Consolidate items to **reduce handling cycles**.
- ✅ **Ergonomic Limit:** Manual lifting should not exceed **23 kg** (NIOSH reference).
- ✅ **MHCR Formula:** $\dfrac{C_{MH}}{C_{Total}} \times 100$ — World-class if **< 10%**, typical if **20–40%**.
- ✅ **Conveyor Capacity Formula:** $Q = 3600 \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot A$ — Don't forget the **3600** factor (s → h).
- ✅ **LCC Formula:** Includes **initial + operating + maintenance − salvage**, with **discounting** for time value of money.
- ✅ **Hierarchy Rule:** Later principles **cannot fix** violations of earlier principles (e.g., Automation cannot rescue poor Planning).
- ✅ **Modern MH:** AS/RS, AGVs, AMRs, RFID tracking — Industry 4.0 integration is **expected** in 2024 scheme answers.
- ✅ **Space Utilization:** Think **3D (cubic meters)**, not 2D (square meters).
- ✅ **LCC > Purchase Price:** A cheap forklift may be **3× more expensive** over 5 years than a premium one.
- ✅ **Lifting Index (NIOSH):** $LI = L / RWL$. If $LI > 1$, redesign the task.
- ✅ **System Principle:** Optimize the **whole flow** (receiving → storage → production → shipping), not isolated equipment.
- ✅ **Environmental Principle:** Electric forklifts > diesel; recyclable pallets; ISO 14001 compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:**
> - In **Part A (3 marks)**: State the definition, list 4 points, write 2–3 lines each.
> - In **Part B (14 marks)**: Use **headings, sub-points, formulas, and at least one numerical example** for full marks. The examiner allocates marks for **structure, formulas, and numerical rigor** — not just content.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activities and Functions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activities and Functions

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of "Activity" in Plant Layout

In the context of **Plant Layout and Design**, an **Activity** is defined as a *logical, identifiable, and time-bound operation* performed by men, machines, or materials in transforming inputs into outputs within a manufacturing or service facility. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET634)** syllabus, activities form the fundamental building blocks around which a plant's physical infrastructure is conceived, designed, and arranged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Every activity requires a defined **input → process → output** cycle, a **designated space**, a **set of resources**, and a **measurable cycle time**. These four parameters dictate the layout geometry.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of "Function"

A **Function** in plant design is the *strategic role* that a department, zone, or workstation plays in the overall value chain of the organization. While an **activity** is *what is being done*, a **function** is *why it is being done* and *which business objective it serves*.

Mathematically, the relationship can be expressed as:

$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i$$

where $F$ = Function of a department, $A_i$ = individual activities, and $n$ = number of activities grouped into that function.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Designing a Home Kitchen":**
> Imagine planning the layout of a home kitchen.
> - **Activities** are micro-tasks: chopping vegetables, boiling water, washing dishes, storing groceries, plating food.
> - **Functions** are macro-zones: the *Cooking Zone* (stove + oven), the *Cleaning Zone* (sink + dishwasher), the *Storage Zone* (refrigerator + pantry).
> - Just as you would not place the dishwasher far from the sink, in a plant you would not place the *painting activity* (function: finishing) far from the *drying activity*. **Proximity equals efficiency.**
> - In both kitchen and plant, the goal is to minimize travel, avoid cross-traffic, and ensure a logical flow of materials and personnel.

## 1.4 The "5 M's" Framework of Activities

Every activity in a plant consumes a combination of the following five resources — collectively called the **5 M's**:

| # | M | Resource | Typical Example |
|---|---|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Men** | Human labour | Operator, supervisor |
| 2 | **Machine** | Equipment | CNC lathe, conveyor |
| 3 | **Material** | Raw/semi-finished goods | Steel billets, fasteners |
| 4 | **Method** | Process technology | Welding, casting, forging |
| 5 | **Money** | Capital investment | ₹/m² of floor area, ROI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** The typical activity area allowance in a heavy-engineering plant is **between 12 m² and 25 m² per workstation** including circulation space, while in light assembly it drops to **4 m² to 8 m² per workstation**.

## 1.5 Broad Classification of Activities

According to the KTU PEPET634 Module 3 framework, activities are classified into **three hierarchical tiers**:

1. **Primary (Production) Activities** — directly add value to the product (e.g., machining, assembly, painting).
2. **Secondary (Support) Activities** — enable primary activities (e.g., tool crib, quality inspection, maintenance).
3. **Tertiary (Auxiliary / Service) Activities** — provide infrastructure (e.g., canteen, parking, administrative offices, restrooms).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Activities

Activities in a plant are decomposed in a top-down manner:

- **Level 1 — Macro-Function:** e.g., *Manufacturing*.
- **Level 2 — Sub-Function:** e.g., *Fabrication*.
- **Level 3 — Activity:** e.g., *Cutting, Welding, Grinding*.
- **Level 4 — Sub-Activity (Element):** e.g., *Marking, Shearing, Edge preparation*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" Hierarchy?** A plant designer first identifies **what function** a zone will serve, then determines **what activities** are needed, and finally allocates **space and equipment**. Reversing this order leads to chaotic layouts.

## 2.2 Types of Activity Relationships

Once activities are identified, the designer must determine the **degree of closeness** required between every pair. This is captured using the **REL (Relationship) Chart** introduced by **James Muther** and refined by Richard Apple.

The standard REL symbols are:

| Code | Closeness Rating | Letter |
|------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | Absolutely Necessary | **A** |
| 2 | Especially Important | **E** |
| 3 | Important | **I** |
| 4 | Ordinary – OK | **O** |
| 5 | Unimportant | **U** |
| 6 | Not Desirable | **X** |
| — | Do not care | **—** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** An "A" rating (Absolutely Necessary) typically demands that two activities be **adjacent**, often separated by **less than 5 m** or directly sharing a wall. An "X" rating (Not Desirable) implies **physical separation by at least one department** to avoid noise, dust, or hazard.

## 2.3 Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) Logic Steps

1. **Step 1:** Enumerate all activities (typically **20–60** in a mid-size plant).
2. **Step 2:** Form an $n \times n$ matrix (where $n$ = number of activities).
3. **Step 3:** Fill diagonal with the activity name; off-diagonal cells carry the **REL code**.
4. **Step 4:** Apply **Muther's weighted score** $W_{ij}$ for each cell.
5. **Step 5:** Convert the matrix into an **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** using closeness lines.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit |
|---|-------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $A_{\text{station}} = A_{\text{equip}} + A_{\text{op}} + A_{\text{circ}}$ | Total area per workstation | m² |
| 2 | $A_{\text{plant}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i + A_{\text{aux}}$ | Total plant area | m² |
| 3 | $W_{ij} = w_1 L_{ij} + w_2 F_{ij}$ | Weighted REL score (location + flow) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $t_c = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_{\text{op}}}$ | Average cycle time per operator | seconds |
| 5 | $E_{\text{flow}} = \dfrac{\text{Forward distance}}{\text{Total distance}}$ | Material flow efficiency | ratio (0–1) |
| 6 | $N_{\text{ws}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot t_c}{T_{\text{plan}} \cdot \eta}$ | Number of workstations needed | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{\text{idle}} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_{\text{ws}} \cdot t_{\max}}$ | Line balancing idle % | % |

> [!NOTE]
> Where $Q$ = production quantity, $T_{\text{plan}}$ = planning horizon, $\eta$ = efficiency factor (typically **0.85 to 0.90**), $t_{\max}$ = bottleneck time, and $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ (commonly $w_1 = 0.6$, $w_2 = 0.4$).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive plants:** ARC drives the sequencing of the assembly line (Body Shop → Paint Shop → Assembly Shop).
- **Pharma plants:** "X"-rated activities (e.g., penicillin vs. general drugs) enforce strict **air-handling segregation** to prevent cross-contamination.
- **Process industries:** Heat-generating activities must be separated from temperature-sensitive activities, often using the REL chart as the legal compliance document.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Space Requirement for a Single Workstation

We want to compute the **total area** that one workstation will occupy in the plant.

**Step 1 — Identify sub-areas.**
Each workstation needs three sub-areas:
- Equipment footprint ($A_{\text{equip}}$)
- Operator working area ($A_{\text{op}}$)
- Circulation / material handling buffer ($A_{\text{circ}}$)

**Step 2 — Apply superposition.**

$$
A_{\text{station}} = A_{\text{equip}} + A_{\text{op}} + A_{\text{circ}}
$$

**Step 3 — Add safety margin** (factor $f_s$ between **1.15 and 1.25** for Indian statutory norms).

$$
A_{\text{station,design}} = f_s \cdot A_{\text{station}}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to $n$ workstations** of similar type, then add auxiliary space.

$$
A_{\text{plant}} = f_s \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{\text{station},i} + A_{\text{aux}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The factor $f_s$ covers aisle width (statutory minimum **900 mm** for one-way, **1500 mm** for two-way per IS 16142), emergency egress, and ergonomics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Workstations Required

We are given:
- $Q$ = annual demand (units/year)
- $T_{\text{plan}}$ = available production time (seconds/year)
- $t_c$ = average cycle time per unit (seconds)
- $\eta$ = line efficiency factor

**Step 1 — Total time needed to fulfil demand.**

$$
T_{\text{required}} = Q \cdot t_c
$$

**Step 2 — Effective time available per workstation.**

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{plan}} \cdot \eta
$$

**Step 3 — Number of workstations** (round up to next integer).

$$
N_{\text{ws}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{\text{required}}}{T_{\text{eff}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{Q \cdot t_c}{T_{\text{plan}} \cdot \eta} \right\rceil
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A small-scale CNC workshop must produce **$Q = 12{,}000$ components/year**. Each component requires an average cycle time of **$t_c = 180$ seconds**. The plant operates **250 days/year, 2 shifts/day, 8 hours/shift**. Take $\eta = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{\text{plan}}$.**

$$
T_{\text{plan}} = 250 \times 2 \times 8 \times 3600 = 14{,}400{,}000 \text{ s/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply formula.**

$$
N_{\text{ws}} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \times 180}{14{,}400{,}000 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{2{,}160{,}000}{12{,}240{,}000} = 0.1765
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.** Since $N_{\text{ws}} < 1$, **one CNC machine is sufficient**, with **82.35%** of the machine time available for secondary activities (setup, tool change, maintenance).

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** This is why activity-based design often reveals that a plant is *over-resourced* — a critical insight for the KTU Module 3 cost-of-layout discussion.

## 3.4 Derivation: Activity Relationship Weighted Score

For two activities $i$ and $j$, the closeness weight is:

$$
W_{ij} = w_1 \cdot L_{ij} + w_2 \cdot F_{ij}
$$

where $L_{ij}$ is the *location-based* REL score and $F_{ij}$ is the *flow-based* score.

**Numerical scale for $L_{ij}$:**

$$
L_{ij} \in \{64, 16, 9, 4, 1, 0\}
$$

corresponding to ratings A, E, I, O, U, X respectively.

**Worked Sub-Example:** For activity pair (i, j) rated **"A"** with flow intensity **"I"** ($F = 9$):

$$
W_{ij} = 0.6 \times 64 + 0.4 \times 9 = 38.4 + 3.6 = 42.0
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The total weighted score is then summed per activity to rank its relative importance in the layout, forming the basis of the **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Function vs. Activity Mapping

| Function | Typical Activities | Typical Share of Plant Area | Tools Used |
|----------|--------------------|------------------------------|------------|
| Production | Machining, Assembly, Welding | **40–55%** | CNC, robots, conveyors |
| Quality | Inspection, Testing, Metrology | **5–10%** | CMM, gauges |
| Material Handling | Storage, Transport, Packing | **10–20%** | Forklifts, AS/RS |
| Maintenance | Repair, Overhaul, Spare mgmt | **5–8%** | Machine shop, tool crib |
| Administration | HR, Finance, Purchase | **3–5%** | Office furniture |
| Welfare | Canteen, Restrooms, First-aid | **2–4%** | Furniture, utilities |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Plant Activities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Plant Function]:::root
    A0 --> P1[Production Function]:::lvl1
    A0 --> P2[Support Function]:::lvl1
    A0 --> P3[Auxiliary Function]:::lvl1

    P1 --> A1[Machining Activity]:::lvl2
    P1 --> A2[Assembly Activity]:::lvl2
    P1 --> A3[Painting Activity]:::lvl2

    P2 --> A4[Quality Inspection Activity]:::lvl2
    P2 --> A5[Tool Crib Activity]:::lvl2
    P2 --> A6[Maintenance Activity]:::lvl2

    P3 --> A7[Material Storage Activity]:::lvl2
    P3 --> A8[Administrative Activity]:::lvl2
    P3 --> A9[Employee Welfare Activity]:::lvl2

    A1 --> E1[CNC Lathe]:::equip
    A1 --> E2[Operator Station]:::equip
    A2 --> E3[Conveyor Line]:::equip
    A2 --> E4[Torque Tools]:::equip
    A4 --> E5[CMM Machine]:::equip
    A5 --> E6[Tool Cabinet]:::equip
    A6 --> E7[Overhead Crane]:::equip
    A7 --> E8[Racking System]:::equip
    A8 --> E9[Office Desk]:::equip
    A9 --> E10[Canteen Furniture]:::equip

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef equip fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Activity Relationship Flow (Logical Proximity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Receiving]:::step --> R2[Raw Material Store]:::step
    R2 --> R3[Machining]:::step
    R3 --> R4[Inspection A]:::step
    R4 --> R5[Assembly]:::step
    R5 --> R6[Inspection B]:::step
    R6 --> R7[Painting]:::step
    R7 --> R8[Final QC]:::step
    R8 --> R9[Dispatch]:::step

    R1 -.X.-> R9
    R2 -.U.-> R9
    R4 -.A.-> R3
    R6 -.A.-> R5
    R8 -.A.-> R7

    classDef step fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Solid arrows show the **process flow** direction. Dotted lines starting with `.-.` carry the **REL code** (A = adjacent, X = not desirable, U = unimportant). In KTU 2024 theory questions, students are often asked to draw this chart for a given set of 6–10 activities.

## 4.3 5 M's Resource-to-Activity Mapping

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((5 M's Framework))
    Men
      Skilled operators
      Supervisors
      Helpers
    Machine
      CNC machines
      Conveyors
      Cranes
    Material
      Raw stock
      WIP
      Finished goods
    Method
      Process routing
      SOPs
      Work instructions
    Money
      Capital cost
      Operating cost
      ROI target
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define "Activity" and "Function" in the context of plant layout. Differentiate between them with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Activity (1 mark):** A logical, identifiable operation performed by men/machines/materials that transforms inputs into outputs. *Example:* Drilling a hole in a flange.
- **Function (1 mark):** A strategic role or purpose served by grouping related activities into a department or zone. *Example:* The "Fabrication Function" of a plant groups cutting, welding, and grinding activities.
- **Difference (1 mark):** Activity is *what is done* (tactical/operational); Function is *why it is done* (strategic). A single function comprises multiple activities.

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**List any six REL (Relationship) codes used in the Activity Relationship Chart. State the closeness requirement for code "A".**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Six REL codes (2 marks): **A, E, I, O, U, X** (Absolutely Necessary, Especially Important, Important, Ordinary OK, Unimportant, Not Desirable).
- Closeness for "A" (1 mark): Activities with code "A" must be **adjacent** — typically sharing a common wall or separated by **less than 5 m**, with **direct material flow** between them.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of activities in a plant into **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary** categories. Give **two examples** for each category and state the typical share of total plant area they occupy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and use of an **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** as proposed by **Muther**. Mention at least **five REL codes** with their proximity meanings. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Classification of Plant Activities (4 marks):**

1. **Primary (Production) Activities** — These directly transform raw material into finished product, adding **direct value**.
   - *Examples:* (i) CNC machining of a shaft, (ii) Arc welding of a chassis.
   - They occupy the **largest share**, typically **40–55%** of total plant area.
   - Located in the **central production zone** with controlled access.

2. **Secondary (Support) Activities** — These enable, monitor, or assist primary activities without directly transforming the product.
   - *Examples:* (i) Tool crib issuing inserts, (ii) Quality inspection using a CMM.
   - Share **20–30%** of total plant area.
   - Placed **close to** the primary activities they support.

3. **Tertiary (Auxiliary / Service) Activities** — These provide infrastructure, welfare, and administration.
   - *Examples:* (i) Canteen and restroom block, (ii) Administrative office.
   - Share **15–25%** of total plant area.
   - Placed on the **periphery** of the plant for accessibility without disturbing production.

**Typical Area Distribution Summary (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Primary} = 45\% \quad \mid \quad \text{Secondary} = 25\% \quad \mid \quad \text{Tertiary} = 20\% \quad \mid \quad \text{Circulation} = 10\%
$$

(Percentages vary by industry type — process industry ≈ 50/30/15/5; assembly industry ≈ 40/25/20/15.)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Proper classification prevents the classic error of *over-crowding* the primary zone with support and welfare activities, ensuring **lean flow** and **worker comfort**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)** is an $n \times n$ matrix that records the **qualitative closeness** requirement between every pair of activities in a plant, as proposed by **James Muther** in 1961 and later formalized with **Apple's Plant Layout & Location** text.

**Step 2 — Construction Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Enumerate all activities ($n$ = typically 20 to 60).
2. Prepare an $n \times n$ matrix with activity names on the diagonal.
3. For each off-diagonal cell $(i, j)$, enter the **REL code** indicating the desired closeness between activity $i$ and activity $j$.
4. If the relationship is **asymmetric**, also note the *reason* (e.g., noise, flow, safety) in the cell.
5. Aggregate the REL codes per row to identify each activity's *most critical relationships*.

**Step 3 — REL Codes (2 marks):**

| Code | Meaning | Spatial Requirement |
|------|---------|---------------------|
| **A** | Absolutely Necessary | Adjacent, common wall |
| **E** | Especially Important | Same building, nearby |
| **I** | Important | Same plant, within 50 m |
| **O** | Ordinary – OK | Same plant zone |
| **U** | Unimportant | No specific need |
| **X** | Not Desirable | Physically separated |

**Step 4 — Sketch (ASCII representation, 1 mark):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
 & A_1 & A_2 & A_3 & A_4 \\
A_1 & A_1 & A & I & U \\
A_2 & A & A_2 & E & O \\
A_3 & I & E & A_3 & X \\
A_4 & U & O & X & A_4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Use of ARC (1 mark):** Once filled, the ARC is converted into an **Activity Relationship Diagram (ARD)** where the activities are connected by lines of varying thickness (A = thick black, E = double line, I = single line, O = dashed, U = thin, X = red zigzag with cross-hatch). The ARD then drives the **Space Relationship Diagram (SRD)** and eventually the **Block Layout**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** A manufacturing unit produces **$Q = 18{,}000$ components/year**. The plant operates **240 days/year**, **2 shifts/day**, **8 hours/shift**. The cycle time per component is **$t_c = 240$ seconds**, and the line efficiency is **$\eta = 0.85$**. Each workstation occupies an area of **12 m²** including circulation, and the safety factor is **$f_s = 1.20$**. Determine the **number of workstations** required and the **total plant area** dedicated to production. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **5 M's framework** of plant activities. Discuss how each M influences the **space requirement** of a workstation with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Given Data:**

$$
Q = 18{,}000 \text{ units/year}, \quad t_c = 240 \text{ s}, \quad \eta = 0.85
$$

$$
T_{\text{plan}} = 240 \times 2 \times 8 \times 3600 = 13{,}824{,}000 \text{ s/year}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $N_{\text{ws}}$ (3 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{ws}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{Q \cdot t_c}{T_{\text{plan}} \cdot \eta} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{18{,}000 \times 240}{13{,}824{,}000 \times 0.85} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{ws}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{4{,}320{,}000}{11{,}750{,}400} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.3676 \rceil = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** Only **1 workstation** is required.

**Step 2 — Area per workstation (2 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{station,design}} = f_s \times A_{\text{station}} = 1.20 \times 12 = 14.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Total production area (2 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{production}} = N_{\text{ws}} \times A_{\text{station,design}} = 1 \times 14.4 = 14.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Interpretation for the Examiner:** The plant has 1 CNC machine, running **36.76%** of available time. The remaining 63.24% is available for **secondary activities** like setups, tool changes, and breakdowns — a healthy buffer.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **5 M's** framework — **Men, Machine, Material, Method, Money** — was popularized by **Kaoru Ishikawa** as a cause-and-effect analysis tool, but in plant layout, the same five factors are used to **define and quantify activity requirements**.

**Discussion of each M with space impact (5 marks):**

1. **Men (1 mark):** Human operators need **0.9 m × 0.6 m** of standing space (IS 16142), plus **1.5 m** lateral reach envelope. For an assembly line with 10 operators, add **at least 1.5 m² per operator** for ergonomics, plus wider aisles for shift change.

2. **Machine (1 mark):** Each machine's *footprint* plus **maintenance access envelope** (typically 0.6 m on three sides) defines its base area. A CNC lathe (2 m × 1.5 m) may require **3 m × 2.5 m** with clearances.

3. **Material (1 mark):** WIP between stations, raw material bins, and finished goods buffers all consume space. A common rule is **0.5 m² per shift's WIP** for every station in a flow line.

4. **Method (1 mark):** The *process routing* dictates the **aisle width** and **turn radius** for material handling. A U-shaped cell needs **at least 4 m** turning radius for a forklift; a straight line needs only **2 m**.

5. **Money (1 mark):** Floor area cost in Indian industrial parks ranges from **₹ 4,000 to ₹ 12,000 per m²** (Tier-1 cities). This forces the designer to **minimize non-value-adding space** (storage, corridors).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The 5 M's framework forces a **holistic quantification** of every activity, preventing the *hidden cost* of poorly designed space — a recurring KTU Module 3 theme.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Activity" with "Function"** (–2 marks). Remember: activity = what; function = why.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor $f_s$** in area calculations (–1 to –2 marks).
> 3. **Not rounding up** $N_{\text{ws}}$ — fractional machines do not exist.
> 4. **Mismeasuring $T_{\text{plan}}$** — students often forget to multiply by 3600 to convert hours to seconds.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in the final answer — KTU examiners *actively deduct* for missing units.
> 6. **Drawing the ARC without the diagonal** — the diagonal must show the **activity name**, not be left blank.
> 7. **Writing "I" for "Important"** without explaining the spatial implication (adjacency vs. same zone). Always state the *distance interpretation*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Activity** = *what is done*; **Function** = *why it is done*. They are NOT synonyms.
- ✅ Every activity has **input, process, output, space, time, and resources**.
- ✅ Activities are classified as **Primary (40–55%)**, **Secondary (20–30%)**, **Tertiary (15–25%)**.
- ✅ The **5 M's** — Men, Machine, Material, Method, Money — define *what an activity consumes*.
- ✅ **Muther's REL chart** uses codes **A, E, I, O, U, X** to denote closeness.
- ✅ **A** = adjacent; **X** = physically separated. Codes drive the ARD and the block layout.
- ✅ **Workstation area** = equipment + operator + circulation, multiplied by **safety factor 1.15–1.25**.
- ✅ **Number of workstations** = $\lceil Q \cdot t_c / (T_{\text{plan}} \cdot \eta) \rceil$.
- ✅ **Always convert hours to seconds** (× 3600) before plugging into the formula.
- ✅ **Always round up** $N_{\text{ws}}$ to the next integer.
- ✅ **Always state units** in the final answer (m², s, units/year).
- ✅ **Weighted REL score** $W_{ij} = 0.6 L_{ij} + 0.4 F_{ij}$ is the most tested formula in KTU Module 3.
- ✅ **Activity-based design** first identifies activities, then groups them into functions, then allocates space — never the reverse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Load Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unit Load Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Load (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Unit Load** is defined as the standardized **size, weight, shape, and configuration** of a single load that is moved, stored, or handled as one indivisible entity throughout the various stages of production, material handling, storage, and shipping operations in a plant.

In the framework of **Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** developed by **Richard Muther**, the Unit Load Concept is the **second analytical step** (after the P-Q analysis) used to determine the volume, weight, and physical dimensions of the items that move through the manufacturing or assembly process. It represents the smallest unit that can be efficiently transported, stacked, stored, or handled without being broken down into smaller pieces.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Key Highlight:**
> The unit load can range from a **single component** (e.g., one piston) to a **containerized batch** (e.g., a pallet of 50 pistons), to a **complete sub-assembly** (e.g., an engine block), depending on the material handling strategy adopted.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are moving groceries from your car to your kitchen. You have two options:
- Carry **each item one-by-one** (sugar bag, then rice bag, then milk carton, then vegetables…) — this is slow and exhausting.
- Put **all the groceries into a single cardboard box** and carry the entire box in one trip — this is fast, efficient, and easy.

This **cardboard box full of groceries** is your **"unit load."** In a factory, instead of moving each small part individually, engineers group items into **unit loads** (boxes, pallets, crates, containers, or skids) so that **one material-handling movement transfers many items together**.

### 1.3 Why Unit Load Concept is Critical in Plant Layout

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?**
> - It **reduces material handling cost** (fewer trips = less labor, less equipment usage).
> - It **improves flow efficiency** and reduces bottlenecks.
> - It **standardizes storage racks, conveyor dimensions, and truck loading patterns**.
> - It enables **mechanized handling** (forklifts, cranes, AGVs).
> - It directly influences **aisle widths, dock sizes, and bay dimensions** in layout design.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Weight Metric:** **Kilogram (kg)** or **Tonnes (1000 kg)** for heavy unit loads.
- **Volume Metric:** **Cubic meters (m³)** or **Cubic feet (ft³)**.
- **Standard Pallet Size (ISO):** **1200 mm × 1000 mm** (European) or **1219 mm × 1016 mm** (North American).
- **Floor Loading Capacity of Standard Factory Floor:** **5 tonnes/m² to 10 tonnes/m²**.
- **Standard Forklift Capacity:** **1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 tonnes** variants.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components / Characteristics of a Unit Load

A unit load is described by the **"4-D" characteristics**:

| Characteristic | Description | Engineering Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimension (Size)** | Length × Width × Height of the load | Determines rack spacing, door widths, vehicle clearance |
| **Density** | Weight per unit volume | Determines handling equipment, stacking rules |
| **Durability** | Resistance to damage, fragility | Determines stacking height, packaging requirement |
| **Destination** | Where it goes (next operation, storage, shipping) | Determines handling route and equipment |

---

### 2.2 Types of Unit Loads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Standard Unit Load Types (KTU Module 3 Syllabus):**

1. **Single-Item Unit Load:** A single, individual product moved as one piece (e.g., one engine block).
2. **Container Unit Load:** Multiple items packed inside a rigid container, box, or crate.
3. **Palletized Unit Load:** Items stacked on a flat wooden/plastic pallet for forklift handling.
4. **Skid Unit Load:** Items mounted on a skid base (with or without legs), allowing forklift insertion.
5. **Bulk Unit Load:** Loose material handled in bulk (sand, grain, cement) using hoppers or conveyors.

---

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Unit Load Determination

The unit load is NOT chosen arbitrarily. The following **engineering factors** govern its selection:

- **Physical Characteristics of the Item:** size, weight, shape, fragility, surface finish.
- **Production Quantity (from P-Q Analysis):** large quantities favour large unit loads.
- **Material Handling Equipment Available:** forklift capacity, crane limit, conveyor width.
- **Storage Method:** rack dimensions, floor area, stacking rules.
- **Cost of Packaging:** pallet cost, box cost, dunnage cost vs. handling savings.
- **Safety Considerations:** toxic items, sharp items, hazardous materials.
- **Shipping / Dispatch Requirements:** truck bed size, container dimensions.
- **Distance to be Moved:** long distances favour larger unit loads to reduce trips.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU Board Exams – memorize them precisely.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Unit Loads | $N = \dfrac{Q}{q}$ | $N$ = number of unit loads, $Q$ = total production quantity (units), $q$ = quantity per unit load |
| 2 | Weight of One Unit Load | $W = q \times w_{item}$ | $W$ = weight per unit load (kg), $w_{item}$ = weight of one piece (kg) |
| 3 | Total Weight of All Loads | $W_{total} = N \times W$ | $W_{total}$ = total material to be moved (kg) |
| 4 | Number of Trips | $T = \dfrac{Q}{q \times n_{per\_trip}}$ | $T$ = total trips, $n_{per\_trip}$ = number of unit loads moved per trip |
| 5 | Trip Frequency | $f = \dfrac{Q}{q \times H}$ | $f$ = trips per hour, $H$ = available hours per shift |
| 6 | Volume Utilization | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{item}}{V_{container}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = utilization factor, $V_{item}$ = volume of items, $V_{container}$ = container volume |
| 7 | Handling Cost per Piece | $C_{piece} = \dfrac{C_{unit\_load}}{q}$ | $C_{piece}$ = cost per item, $C_{unit\_load}$ = handling cost per unit load |
| 8 | Total Handling Cost | $C_{total} = N \times C_{unit\_load}$ | $C_{total}$ = total handling cost |
| 9 | Density of Unit Load | $\rho = \dfrac{W}{V_{container}}$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) |
| 10 | Floor Space per Unit Load | $A = L \times B$ | $A$ = floor area (m²), $L$ = length (m), $B$ = breadth (m) |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where this is used in industry:**
> - **Automotive Plants:** Engine blocks moved on engine skids, body parts on JIS pallets.
> - **Warehousing:** Amazon, FedEx use standardized pallets to maximize cubic utilization.
> - **Food Industry:** Crate loads of bottles, can loads on conveyors.
> - **Pharmaceutical:** Trolley loads of medicine cartons between clean rooms.
> - **Cement / Steel Plants:** Bulk unit loads via conveyors and cranes.
> - **E-commerce Fulfillment:** Bin-to-picker unit loads optimize order picking time.

### 2.6 Methods to Determine the Optimum Unit Load

1. **Trial and Error Method** – Try different combinations and select the best.
2. **Standardization Method** – Adopt industry standard pallets/containers (ISO 6780).
3. **Economic Analysis Method** – Minimize (handling cost + packaging cost + storage cost).
4. **Time Study / Work Measurement Method** – Measure cycle times for different load sizes.
5. **Mathematical Optimization** – Use cost functions and constraints to find the optimal load size.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimum Unit Load Size from Cost Minimization

**Problem Setup:**
A factory moves $Q$ items per month. Each unit load carries $q$ items. The cost of one unit load movement includes:

- Fixed cost per trip: $C_f$ (operator wages + equipment time)
- Variable cost per piece: $C_v \times q$ (packaging, dunnage, damage)

**Total Cost Function:**

$$
C_{total} = N \times \left( C_f + C_v \cdot q \right)
$$

Since $N = \dfrac{Q}{q}$, we substitute:

$$
C_{total} = \dfrac{Q}{q} \cdot \left( C_f + C_v \cdot q \right)
$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$
C_{total} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_f}{q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot C_v \cdot q}{q}
$$

The $q$ terms cancel in the second fraction:

$$
C_{total} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_f}{q} + Q \cdot C_v
$$

**Cost per Piece:**

$$
C_{piece} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{Q} = \dfrac{C_f}{q} + C_v
$$

**Finding the Optimum $q$:**
For minimum cost, differentiate $C_{piece}$ with respect to $q$ and set to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d C_{piece}}{dq} = -\dfrac{C_f}{q^2} = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the derivative is **never zero** for finite positive $q$, the function is **monotonically decreasing**. Therefore, the **largest feasible unit load** (subject to equipment capacity and safety limits) gives the **minimum cost per piece**.

**Engineering Conclusion:** Use the **maximum allowable unit load** within equipment and safety constraints.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Trips and Trip Frequency Calculation

**Given Data (Sample Problem):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Total monthly production | $Q$ | **10,000 units** |
| Items per unit load (pallet) | $q$ | **50 units** |
| Working days per month | $D$ | **25 days** |
| Shifts per day | $S$ | **2 shifts** |
| Hours per shift | $H$ | **8 hours** |

**Step 1: Number of Unit Loads**

$$
N = \dfrac{Q}{q} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{50} = 200 \text{ unit loads}
$$

**Step 2: Daily Quantity**

$$
Q_{daily} = \dfrac{Q}{D} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{25} = 400 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Trips per Day** (assuming 1 unit load moved per trip)

$$
T_{daily} = \dfrac{Q_{daily}}{q} = \dfrac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ trips/day}
$$

**Step 4: Trip Frequency per Hour**

$$
f = \dfrac{T_{daily}}{S \times H} = \dfrac{8}{2 \times 8} = 0.5 \text{ trips/hour}
$$

**Step 5: Time Between Trips (Cycle Time)**

$$
t_{cycle} = \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{f} = \dfrac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ min/trip}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Volume Utilization and Density Check

**Given:** Container dimensions = $1.2 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m}$ (length × width × height).  
Each box inside the container holds 50 items, each item dimensions = $0.2 \text{ m} \times 0.2 \text{ m} \times 0.1 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1: Container Volume**

$$
V_{container} = 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Volume Occupied by Items (50 items)**

$$
V_{item} = 50 \times (0.2 \times 0.2 \times 0.1) = 50 \times 0.004 = 0.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Volume Utilization Factor**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{V_{item}}{V_{container}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.2}{1.2} \times 100\% = 16.67\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Low utilization (< 30%)** indicates either:
> - The container is oversized (use a smaller box).
> - The item shape is irregular (use custom dunnage/fillers).
> - The quantity per load is too small.

**Ideal industrial target:** $\eta \ge 60\%$ to $80\%$ for cost-efficient unit loads.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Unit Load Type

| Item Weight | Quantity/Month | Distance | Best Unit Load Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $< 5$ kg | High ($>10{,}000$) | Long | **Palletized** with cartons | Maximum handling efficiency, lowest cost/piece |
| $< 5$ kg | Low ($<1{,}000$) | Short | **Carton / Tote box** | Low capital investment |
| $5$ kg to $50$ kg | High | Long | **Palletized + Crate** | Mechanized handling, stackable |
| $50$ kg to $500$ kg | High | Long | **Skid with Crate** | Forklift-friendly, rigid structure |
| $> 500$ kg | Any | Any | **Engine Skid / Heavy Pallet** | Crane or heavy-duty forklift required |
| Loose bulk | Continuous | Long | **Conveyor / Hopper** | Bulk unit load via pneumatic or belt conveyors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Unit Load Determination Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: P-Q Analysis Output] --> B[Identify Item Characteristics]
    B --> B1[Size & Shape]
    B --> B2[Weight & Fragility]
    B --> B3[Quantity Q]
    B1 --> C[Select Candidate Unit Load Types]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Single Item]
    C --> C2[Container Box]
    C --> C3[Pallet]
    C --> C4[Skid]
    C --> C5[Bulk Hopper]
    C1 --> D[Check Constraints]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Equipment Capacity]
    D --> D2[Safety Limit]
    D --> D3[Storage Space]
    D --> D4[Cost Budget]
    D1 --> E[Compute Number of Loads N]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Handling Cost]
    F --> G{Is Cost Minimum?}
    G -- No --> H[Adjust q and Re-evaluate]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[Finalize Unit Load Specification]
    I --> J[End]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Unit Load Architecture in a Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Store] -- Unit Load A --> B[Cutting / Machining]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each arrow represents a **specific unit load type** optimized for that transfer. For example, raw materials may use **bulk hoppers (Unit Load A)**, while precision sub-assemblies use **plastic totes on wheeled carts (Unit Load C)**.

### 4.3 Decision Matrix for Unit Load Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Is item single heavy piece > 500 kg?}

    R4 --> END
    R5 --> END
```
