```mermaid
    blockB --> procF["LOGIC Conveyor Selection"]
    blockC --> procF
    blockD --> procF
    blockE --> procF

    procF --> outG["OUTPUT Recommended Conveyor"]
    outG --> outG1["Belt if granular bulk"]
    outG --> outG2["Screw if powder viscous"]
    outG --> outG3["Pneumatic if enclosed dust free"]
    outG --> outG4["Roller if boxed cartons"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State any four factors affecting the choice of material handling equipment in a plant layout.**

**Model Answer (Mark-Wise Distribution):**

1. **Material-related factors** — weight, size, shape, fragility, and physical state of the material determine lifting capacity and handling delicacy needed (1 mark).
2. **Movement-related factors** — distance, direction, frequency, and speed of movement dictate whether continuous (conveyor) or intermittent (forklift) equipment is suitable (1 mark).
3. **Economic factors** — initial investment, operating cost, payback period influence selection between manual and automated systems (1/2 mark).
4. **Facility constraints** — floor loading, ceiling height, aisle width, and column spacing restrict the type of equipment usable (1/2 mark).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the Unit Load Principle in material handling.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Unit Load Principle** (Hesket's Principle 6) states that the cost per unit of material handled decreases as the size or quantity of material handled per movement increases (1 mark).

By consolidating multiple small items into a single larger load using **pallets, containers, or skids**, the number of individual handling operations is reduced (1 mark).

For example, instead of moving 50 boxes individually (50 handling cycles), combining them on one pallet reduces it to 1 cycle — saving 49 cycles of labor, energy, and time (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** write only the definition of equipment types — examiners deduct marks if the **selection reasoning** is missing.
> - In numerical EUAC problems, **always show the CRF calculation step-by-step**. Skipping the CRF step costs 2 marks.
> - When listing factors, students often write only 4–5 — write **all 5 categories** with at least one example each to score full marks.
> - **Unit errors** (e.g., writing capacity in kg without /hr) cost 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various factors that influence the choice of material handling equipment. Discuss the role of each factor with industrial examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing plant is comparing two material handling systems:
- **System X:** Initial cost ₹6,00,000; Annual operating cost ₹60,000; Life = 8 years.
- **System Y:** Initial cost ₹9,00,000; Annual operating cost ₹40,000; Life = 10 years.
- Interest rate = **10% per annum**.

Using the **EUAC method**, determine which system is economically better.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The choice of material handling equipment depends on the following categories of factors:

**(i) Material-Related Factors (2 marks):**
The **weight, size, shape, physical state, fragility, and hazard level** of the material determine which equipment class is applicable.
- **Example:** For molten steel in a foundry, overhead cranes with ladles are chosen (heat-resistant, high load capacity).
- **Example:** For fragile pharmaceutical bottles, cushioned hand-trucks or air-cushion conveyors are used.

**(ii) Movement-Related Factors (1.5 marks):**
The **distance, speed, frequency, and direction** of movement dictate equipment choice.
- **Short, frequent horizontal moves** → belt conveyor.
- **Long, intermittent moves** → forklift or AGV.
- **Vertical lifts** → hoist or crane.

**(iii) Facility-Related Factors (1 mark):**
The **building structure, floor loading capacity, ceiling height, and aisle width** restrict equipment options.
- **Example:** If ceiling height is only 4 m, a 6 m tall crane is unusable.

**(iv) Operational Factors (1 mark):**
**Production type** (mass/batch/job), **production rate**, **automation level**, and **labor skill** influence whether mechanized, semi-automatic, or fully automatic equipment is chosen.

**(v) Economic Factors (1.5 marks):**
**Initial cost, operating cost, payback period, and useful life** determine whether capital-intensive automated systems are justified.
- **Example:** A small-scale bakery may not afford an AGV system — manual carts suffice.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- $P_X$ = ₹6,00,000, $A_X$ = ₹60,000, $n_X$ = 8 years
- $P_Y$ = ₹9,00,000, $A_Y$ = ₹40,000, $n_Y$ = 10 years
- $i$ = 10% = 0.10

**Step 1: Compute CRF for System X.**

$$
CRF_X = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^8}{(1.10)^8 - 1}
$$

$$
(1.10)^8 = 2.1436
$$

$$
CRF_X = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.1436}{2.1436 - 1} = \dfrac{0.21436}{1.1436} = 0.1874
$$

**Step 2: Compute CRF for System Y.**

$$
CRF_Y = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937
$$

$$
CRF_Y = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \dfrac{0.25937}{1.5937} = 0.1627
$$

**Step 3: Compute EUAC for both systems.**

$$
EUAC_X = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1874 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}12{,}440 + 60{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}72{,}440
$$

$$
EUAC_Y = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1627 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}46{,}430 + 40{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}86{,}430
$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since $EUAC_X$ (₹1,72,440) < $EUAC_Y$ (₹1,86,430), **System X is economically better** by ₹13,990 per year. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify material handling equipment in detail. Explain any **three types of conveyors** with their working principles and industrial applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A warehouse handles **800 pallets per day** of finished goods. Each forklift can carry **5 pallets per trip** and makes **20 trips per hour**. The warehouse operates **8 hours/day** with a **safety factor of 1.2**. Determine the **number of forklifts required** to handle the load.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Classification of Material Handling Equipment (3 marks):**

MHE is broadly classified into five categories:

1. **Transport Equipment** — moves materials between locations (conveyors, cranes, trucks).
2. **Positioning Equipment** — places materials accurately at the workstation (robots, manipulators).
3. **Unit Load Formation Equipment** — groups items for batch handling (pallets, containers).
4. **Storage Equipment** — holds materials when not in motion (racks, silos, ASRS).
5. **Identification & Control Equipment** — tracks and directs material flow (barcode, RFID).

**Three Types of Conveyors (4 marks):**

**(i) Belt Conveyor:**
- **Working Principle:** A continuous belt of rubber, fabric, or metal moves over pulleys powered by a motor. Material is placed on the belt and carried horizontally or on an incline.
- **Application:** Mining (coal, ore transport), cement plants, baggage handling at airports.

**(ii) Screw Conveyor:**
- **Working Principle:** A rotating helical screw inside a trough or tube pushes granular/powder material forward by the Archimedes principle.
- **Application:** Cement, flour, grain, plastic pellets in chemical and food industries.

**(iii) Pneumatic Conveyor:**
- **Working Principle:** Material is suspended in a stream of air inside enclosed pipes and transported by pressure (positive) or vacuum (negative).
- **Application:** Pharmaceuticals, plastic granules, cement, hazardous dust — wherever enclosed, contamination-free transport is needed.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- Total pallets/day, $Q$ = 800
- Pallets per trip, $W$ = 5
- Trips per hour, $n$ = 20
- Operating hours/day, $H$ = 8
- Safety factor, $S$ = 1.2

**Step 1: Hourly handling requirement.**

$$
Q_{hr} = \dfrac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ pallets/hr}
$$

**Step 2: Capacity of one forklift per hour.**

$$
C = W \times n = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ pallets/hr}
$$

**Step 3: Number of forklifts required.**

$$
N = \dfrac{Q_{hr} \times S}{C} = \dfrac{100 \times 1.2}{100} = 1.2
$$

**Step 4: Engineering round-up.**

$$
N = \lceil 1.2 \rceil = 2 \text{ forklifts}
$$

**Conclusion:** The warehouse requires **2 forklifts** to handle the daily load. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Five categories of MHE:** Transport, Positioning, Unit Load Formation, Storage, Identification.
- ✅ **Ten Principles of Material Handling** (Hesket's) — at minimum remember: Planning, Systems, Unit Load, Gravity, Mechanization, Ergonomics.
- ✅ **Five factors affecting MHE choice:** Material, Movement, Facility, Operational, Economic.
- ✅ **EUAC formula:** $EUAC = P \cdot CRF + A$, where $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- ✅ **Equipment capacity formula:** $C = W \times n$ (load × cycles/hr).
- ✅ **Number of equipment formula:** $N = \dfrac{Q \cdot S}{C}$.
- ✅ **Always round UP** the number of equipment required — never round down.
- ✅ **Unit Load Principle** reduces handling cost by combining items into larger loads.
- ✅ **Common equipment–material pairing:** Molten metal → crane; bulk grain → belt conveyor; powder → pneumatic conveyor; fragile items → cushioned hand-truck.
- ✅ **EUAC method** is preferred when equipment lives differ — use **Present Worth** only when lives are equal.
- ✅ **Examiner's pet topic:** Numerical problems on EUAC comparison and forklift/pallet count are **almost guaranteed** — practice at least 3 of each.
- ✅ Always **write units** in the final answer (₹/year, pallets/hr, tons/hr) — losing units costs a mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depreciation - Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Depreciation - Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Depreciation** is the systematic, rational, and equitable allocation of the *depreciable cost* (historical cost minus salvage value) of a tangible fixed asset over its *estimated useful economic life* in a pattern that matches the consumption of the asset's economic benefits. It is a *non-cash expense* charged against the Profit & Loss Account to reflect the wear, tear, obsolescence, or effluxion of time of an asset. In the context of **Plant Layout and Design**, depreciation is a critical component of *cost estimation*, *profitability analysis* (e.g., Return on Investment, Net Present Value), and *replacement decision-making* for capital equipment such as reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, pumps, and compressors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Depreciation is the decrease in the value of a physical asset due to usage, passage of time, wear and tear, technological obsolescence, or accidental damage. It represents the recovery of capital invested in long-term assets. ### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard) - **Original Cost ($C_O$ or $P$):** The total capital outlay required to acquire and install the asset, including purchase price, freight, insurance, taxes, and installation/erection charges. - **Salvage Value ($S$ or $S_n$):** The expected net realizable value of the asset at the end of its useful life. Also called *Residual Value* or *Scrap Value*. - **Useful Life ($N$):** The estimated period (in years) over which the asset will generate economic benefits. - **Depreciable Cost ($C_D$):** The difference between the Original Cost and the Salvage Value, i.e., $C_D = C_O - S$. - **Book Value (BV):** The undepreciated portion of the asset's cost remaining on the company's books at any point in time. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you buy a brand-new bicycle for ₹10,000. You know that after 5 years, you can sell it (as scrap or used) for maybe ₹2,000. The "loss in value" of ₹8,000 must somehow be accounted for over those 5 years. Depreciation is simply *spreading that ₹8,000 loss across the years* the bicycle serves you. If it serves you equally well each year, you "lose" ₹1,600 per year. If, however, the bicycle is most useful in year 1 and gradually becomes less reliable, you might "lose" more in early years and less later. The *method* you choose decides this yearly distribution pattern. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in Plant Design:** For a chemical plant, a reactor costing ₹1 Crore with a salvage of ₹10 Lakhs and a 10-year life has ₹90 Lakhs of "value" to recover. The choice of depreciation method directly affects annual taxable income, equipment replacement funds, and the calculated ROI of the plant. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Depreciation Methods Depreciation methods are broadly classified into two categories based on the *pattern* of charge-off: **(A) Time-Based Methods (Straight-line family):** 1. Straight Line Method (SLM) 2. Declining Balance Method / Written Down Value (WDV) Method 3. Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits (SYD) Method 4. Sinking Fund Method 5. Annuity Method / Present Worth Method 6. Capitalized Cost Method **(B) Service-Based Methods (Usage-based):** 1. Service Output Method (Units-of-Production Method) 2. Working Hours Method ### 2.2 Detailed Concept Breakdown #### 2.2.1 Straight Line Method (SLM) - Assumes **equal annual depreciation** over the useful life. - Asset loses value *uniformly* with time. - Simplest and most commonly used method in KTU numerical problems. - Each year, the book value decreases by the *same constant amount*. #### 2.2.2 Declining Balance Method (DBM) / Written Down Value (WDV) - A **constant depreciation rate** is applied each year to the *diminishing book value*. - Salvage value is *not* subtracted initially — it emerges naturally as the asymptote. - In KTU problems, the common rate is the **Double Declining Balance (DDB)** where rate = $2/N$. #### 2.2.3 Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits (SYD) Method - An *accelerated* depreciation method. - Useful life years are summed: $1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + N = \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$. - Earlier years get a *larger* fraction of depreciation, later years a *smaller* fraction. #### 2.2.4 Sinking Fund Method (SFM) - Assumes the depreciation amount, when invested at compound interest, accumulates to equal the depreciable cost by the end of life. - The annual depreciation charge equals the *interest earned* on the accumulated sinking fund. - Conceptually accounts for the *time value of money*. #### 2.2.5 Service Output Method (Units of Production) - Depreciation is charged based on *actual usage* (units produced, hours operated, km driven). - Most equitable for assets whose wear is *proportional to use* rather than time (e.g., delivery trucks, drilling rigs). #### 2.2.6 Capitalized Cost Method - A specialized engineering economics approach: the **capitalized cost** is the present worth of *perpetual* service. - Total capitalized cost = First Cost + PV of perpetual replacement costs. - Related closely to choosing the *most economical* design life for an asset. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question from this module.** | # | Method | Annual Depreciation Formula (Year $n$) | Book Value Formula | Special Notes | |---|--------|----------------------------------------|--------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Straight Line (SLM)** | $D_n = \dfrac{C_O - S}{N}$ | $BV_n = C_O - n \cdot D_n$ | Constant $D$ every year | | 2 | **Written Down Value (WDV)** | $D_n = R \cdot BV_{n-1}$, where $R = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C_O}\right)^{1/N}$ | $BV_n = C_O (1-R)^n$ | Salvage approached asymptotically | | 3 | **Double Declining Balance (DDB)** | $D_n = \dfrac{2}{N} \cdot BV_{n-1}$ | $BV_n = C_O \left(1 - \dfrac{2}{N}\right)^n$ | Often ignores $S$ in computation | | 4 | **Sum-of-Years'-Digits (SYD)** | $D_n = \dfrac{(N - n + 1)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} i} \cdot (C_O - S)$ | $BV_n = C_O - \sum_{k=1}^{n} D_k$ | $\sum i = N(N+1)/2$ | | 5 | **Sinking Fund (SFM)** | $D_n = (C_O - S) \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $BV_n = C_O - \text{SF accumulated to year } n$ | $i$ = interest rate | | 6 | **Service Output** | $D_n = \dfrac{\text{Units in Year } n}{\text{Total Units over Life}} \cdot (C_O - S)$ | $BV_n = C_O - \sum D_k$ | $N$ replaced by usage metric | | 7 | **Capitalized Cost** | $\text{CC} = \dfrac{\text{First Cost}}{1 - (1+i)^{-N}}$ | — | For infinite-cycle assets | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** In WDV/DDB, students often forget to compute $R$ from the formula. KTU examiners expect the rate to be derived when both $C_O$ and $S$ are given. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In **plant design**, depreciation affects: - **Income tax planning:** Higher early depreciation → lower taxable income early → better cash flow. - **Replacement analysis:** When a unit's operating cost exceeds the cost of a new one, depreciation funds help finance replacement. - **ROI/Payback calculations:** Depreciation is added back in cash-flow analysis. - **Insurance and asset valuation:** Determines the *insured value* and *replacement reserve* of plant machinery. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Straight Line Method Let: - $C_O$ = Original cost - $S$ = Salvage value - $N$ = Useful life in years Since depreciation is uniform every year: $$ D = \text{Annual Depreciation} = \frac{\text{Total Depreciable Cost}}{\text{Useful Life}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \frac{C_O - S}{N} \\ BV_n &= C_O - n \cdot D \\ BV_n &= C_O - n \cdot \frac{C_O - S}{N} \\ BV_N &= C_O - N \cdot \frac{C_O - S}{N} \\ BV_N &= C_O - (C_O - S) \\ BV_N &= S \quad \text{(verifies the method)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Written Down Value (WDV) Method — Finding the Rate $R$ Let $R$ be the constant fractional rate of depreciation. By definition: $$ BV_n = BV_{n-1}(1 - R) = C_O (1 - R)^n $$ At $n = N$, the book value must equal the salvage value $S$: $$ \begin{aligned} S &= C_O (1 - R)^N \\ \frac{S}{C_O} &= (1 - R)^N \\ (1 - R) &= \left(\frac{S}{C_O}\right)^{1/N} \\ R &= 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C_O}\right)^{1/N} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the depreciation in year $n$ is: $$ D_n = R \cdot BV_{n-1} = R \cdot C_O (1 - R)^{n-1} $$ **For Double Declining Balance (DDB):** $R = \dfrac{2}{N}$ and salvage is *not* subtracted initially. ### 3.3 Derivation: Sum-of-Years'-Digits (SYD) Method The sum of the first $N$ natural numbers is: $$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} i = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} $$ The fraction of depreciable cost assigned to year $n$ is: $$ f_n = \frac{(N - n + 1)}{N(N+1)/2} = \frac{2(N - n + 1)}{N(N+1)} $$ Therefore, depreciation in year $n$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} D_n &= f_n \cdot (C_O - S) \\ D_n &= \frac{2(N - n + 1)}{N(N+1)} \cdot (C_O - S) \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** Sum over $n = 1$ to $N$ should equal $C_O - S$: $$ \sum_{n=1}^{N} (N - n + 1) = N + (N-1) + \dots + 1 = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} $$ Hence, total depreciation = $\dfrac{2}{N(N+1)} \cdot \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2} \cdot (C_O - S) = C_O - S$ ✓ ### 3.4 Derivation: Sinking Fund Method Let $A$ be the *equal annual deposit* (which is also the depreciation charge). This deposit earns compound interest at rate $i$ per annum. At the end of year $N$, the future value of all deposits must equal $(C_O - S)$: $$ \begin{aligned} A(1+i)^{N-1} + A(1+i)^{N-2} + \dots + A &= C_O - S \\ A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i} &= C_O - S \\ A &= (C_O - S) \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1} \end{aligned} $$ This $A$ is the *sinking fund factor* (SFF) times the depreciable cost. > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** In the Sinking Fund method, the depreciation charge $A$ stays constant each year, but the *interest portion* grows while the *principal portion* shrinks. This is analogous to a home loan EMI structure. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Method | Year 1 Depreciation | Year $N$ Depreciation | Pattern | Salvage Explicit? | |--------|---------------------|------------------------|---------|-------------------| | SLM | $\frac{C_O - S}{N}$ | $\frac{C_O - S}{N}$ | Constant | Yes | | WDV/DDB | Highest | Lowest | Decelerating | No (DDB); Yes (WDV) | | SYD | $\frac{N}{N(N+1)/2}(C_O - S)$ | $\frac{1}{N(N+1)/2}(C_O - S)$ | Linearly declining | Yes | | Sinking Fund | $A$ = constant | $A$ = constant | Constant in nominal terms | Yes | | Service Output | Variable (per usage) | Variable | Usage-driven | Yes | ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** A pump costs ₹5,00,000, has a salvage value of ₹50,000, and a useful life of 5 years. Calculate the depreciation schedule using (a) SLM, (b) WDV, and (c) SYD. **(a) Straight Line Method:** $$ D = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{5} = \text{₹}90{,}000 \text{ per year} $$ | Year | Depreciation | Book Value | |------|--------------|------------| | 0 | — | 5,00,000 | | 1 | 90,000 | 4,10,000 | | 2 | 90,000 | 3,20,000 | | 3 | 90,000 | 2,30,000 | | 4 | 90,000 | 1,40,000 | | 5 | 90,000 | 50,000 | **(b) Written Down Value Method:** $$ R = 1 - \left(\frac{50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/5} = 1 - (0.1)^{0.2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} (0.1)^{0.2} &= e^{0.2 \cdot \ln(0.1)} = e^{0.2 \cdot (-2.3026)} = e^{-0.4605} \approx 0.6310 \\ R &= 1 - 0.6310 = 0.3690 = 36.90\% \end{aligned} $$ | Year | Depreciation | Book Value | |------|--------------|------------| | 0 | — | 5,00,000 | | 1 | 1,84,500 | 3,15,500 | | 2 | 1,16,420 | 1,99,080 | | 3 | 73,470 | 1,25,610 | | 4 | 46,350 | 79,260 | | 5 | 29,260 | 50,000 | **(c) Sum-of-Years'-Digits Method:** $$ \text{Sum} = \frac{5 \cdot 6}{2} = 15 $$ | Year | Fraction | Depreciation | Book Value | |------|----------|--------------|------------| | 1 | 5/15 | 1,50,000 | 3,50,000 | | 2 | 4/15 | 1,20,000 | 2,30,000 | | 3 | 3/15 | 90,000 | 1,40,000 | | 4 | 2/15 | 60,000 | 80,000 | | 5 | 1/15 | 30,000 | 50,000 | > [!NOTE] > **Cross-verification:** Total depreciation in each method = ₹4,50,000 = $C_O - S$ ✓ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — Selection of Depreciation Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Plant Asset Acquired] --> B{Asset Type?} B -->|Buildings, Furniture| C[Straight Line Method] B -->|Machinery, Vehicles, Electronics| D{Wear Pattern?} B -->|Patents, Software| E[Intangible Amortization] D -->|Equal wear over time| C D -->|High early obsolescence| F[Accelerated Methods] D -->|Proportional to usage| G[Service Output Method] F --> H{Time Value of Money?} H -->|Yes| I[Sum of Years Digits / DDB] H -->|No but needs reserve| J[Written Down Value] H -->|Yes, must accumulate fund| K[Sinking Fund Method] C --> L[Final Schedule Generated] G --> L I --> L J --> L K --> L L --> M[Tax Filing and Replacement Reserve] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Depreciation Calculation Engine ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUT[Input Block] A1[Original Cost CO] A2[Salvage Value S] A3[Useful Life N] A4[Interest Rate i] A5[Annual Usage Units] end subgraph SELECT[Method Selector Block] B1[SLM] B2[WDV DDB] B3[SYD] B4[Sinking Fund] B5[Service Output] end subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Block] C1[Annual Depreciation Dn] C2[Book Value BVn] C3[Accumulated Depreciation] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block] D1[Depreciation Schedule Table] D2[Tax Liability Report] D3[Replacement Reserve Fund] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A3 --> B2 A1 --> B3 A2 --> B3 A3 --> B3 A1 --> B4 A2 --> B4 A3 --> B4 A4 --> B4 A1 --> B5 A2 --> B5 A5 --> B5 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C1 B5 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 C1 --> D2 C3 --> D3 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Visualization — Depreciation Patterns ```mermaid graph TD P1[SLM Constant Line] --> Q1[Horizontal straight line
at D per year] P2[WDV Convex Decay] --> Q2[Steep drop in Year 1
gentle tapering curve] P3[SYD Linear Decline] --> Q3[Starts high, decreases
linearly to last year] P4[Sinking Fund Constant] --> Q4[Constant annual charge
A = i*CD SFF factor] P5[Service Output] --> Q5[Stepwise pattern
matching usage profile] ``` > [!TIP] > **Visualization Note:** The depreciation pattern when plotted on a *Book Value vs Year* graph forms a different curve for each method. SLM is a straight line, WDV/DDB is convex exponential, SYD is linear, Sinking Fund is a convex growth curve from the fund perspective. ### 4.4 Decision Tree for KTU Exam ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Question asks for SYD?] --> R1[Use 2N-n+1 divided by N times N+1] Q2[Question gives both CO and S?] --> R2[Use full WDV with S based R] Q3[Question gives only N and 2N rate?] --> R3[Use DDB ignore S initially] Q4[Question mentions interest rate i?] --> R4[Use Sinking Fund Method] Q5[Question gives units of production?] --> R5[Use Service Output Method] Q6[Question says uniform allocation?] --> R6[Use SLM formula] Q7[Question about perpetual replacement?] --> R7[Use Capitalized Cost Method] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define depreciation. List any four methods of calculating depreciation used in chemical plant cost estimation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the depreciable cost of a tangible asset over its useful life, reflecting the consumption of economic benefits due to wear, tear, obsolescence, or effluxion of time. (1.5 marks) Four methods: (i) Straight Line Method, (ii) Written Down Value / Declining Balance Method, (iii) Sum-of-Years'-Digits Method, (iv) Sinking Fund Method. (1.5 marks — 0.375 each) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Written Down Value Method of depreciation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | SLM | WDV | |--------|-----|-----| | Pattern | Constant depreciation | Declining depreciation | | Rate | Variable per year | Constant rate applied to BV | | Salvage | Considered initially | Not explicitly used in DDB | | Book Value at end | Equals $S$ exactly | Approaches $S$ asymptotically | | Suitability | Buildings, furniture | Machinery, vehicles | (Any three distinguishing points — 1 mark each) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** A heat exchanger in a chemical plant costs ₹8,00,000 to install. Its expected salvage value after 10 years is ₹1,00,000. **(a)** Calculate the annual depreciation and book value at the end of each year using the **Straight Line Method**. Prepare the depreciation schedule. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the company adopts the **Written Down Value Method** instead, calculate the depreciation rate and the depreciation for the first 3 years. Compare the two methods. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — SLM (7 marks):** Depreciable cost = $C_O - S = 8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000$ Annual depreciation $D = \dfrac{7{,}00{,}000}{10} = \text{₹}70{,}000$ per year **(1 mark)** Book value at end of year $n$: $BV_n = 8{,}00{,}000 - 70{,}000n$ **(1 mark)** | Year | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) | |------|------------------|----------------| | 0 | — | 8,00,000 | | 1 | 70,000 | 7,30,000 | | 2 | 70,000 | 6,60,000 | | 3 | 70,000 | 5,90,000 | | 4 | 70,000 | 5,20,000 | | 5 | 70,000 | 4,50,000 | | 6 | 70,000 | 3,80,000 | | 7 | 70,000 | 3,10,000 | | 8 | 70,000 | 2,40,000 | | 9 | 70,000 | 1,70,000 | | 10 | 70,000 | 1,00,000 ✓ | **(5 marks for full table)** **Part (b) — WDV (7 marks):** Rate $R = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C_O}\right)^{1/N}$ **(1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= 1 - \left(\frac{1{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/10} \\ R &= 1 - (0.125)^{0.1} \\ R &= 1 - e^{0.1 \cdot \ln(0.125)} \\ R &= 1 - e^{0.1 \cdot (-2.0794)} \\ R &= 1 - e^{-0.2079} \\ R &= 1 - 0.8123 \\ R &= 0.1877 = 18.77\% \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks for derivation)** Year 1: $D_1 = 0.1877 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}160$, $BV_1 = 6{,}49{,}840$ **(1 mark)** Year 2: $D_2 = 0.1877 \times 6{,}49{,}840 = \text{₹}1{,}21{,}975$, $BV_2 = 5{,}27{,}865$ **(1 mark)** Year 3: $D_3 = 0.1877 \times 5{,}27{,}865 = \text{₹}99{,}080$, $BV_3 = 4{,}28{,}785$ **(1 mark)** **Comparison (1 mark):** - SLM gives uniform ₹70,000/year; WDV gives high front-loaded depreciation (₹1,50,160 in Year 1). - WDV is more realistic for machinery losing efficiency with age; SLM is simpler and easier for tax computation. - SLM book value reaches ₹1,00,000 exactly at Year 10; WDV asymptotically approaches this value. --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]** A distillation column installation in a process plant involves an initial investment of ₹15,00,000 with an estimated salvage value of ₹1,50,000 and a useful life of 8 years. **(a)** Compute the annual depreciation using the **Sum-of-Years'-Digits Method** and prepare the complete depreciation schedule. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The company sets up a **Sinking Fund** at an interest rate of 9% per annum to replace the column. Calculate the annual sinking fund deposit (depreciation charge) and the accumulated fund at the end of 8 years. Compare with the SLM annual charge. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — SYD Method (7 marks):** Sum of years $= \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 9}{2} = 36$ **(1 mark)** Depreciable cost $= 15{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 13{,}50{,}000$ **(1 mark)** For year $n$: $D_n = \dfrac{(9-n)}{36} \times 13{,}50{,}000$ **(1 mark)** | Year $n$ | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) | |----------|----------|------------------|----------------| | 1 | 8/36 | 3,00,000 | 12,00,000 | | 2 | 7/36 | 2,62,500 | 9,37,500 | | 3 | 6/36 | 2,25,000 | 7,12,500 | | 4 | 5/36 | 1,87,500 | 5,25,000 | | 5 | 4/36 | 1,50,000 | 3,75,000 | | 6 | 3/36 | 1,12,500 | 2,62,500 | | 7 | 2/36 | 75,000 | 1,87,500 | | 8 | 1/36 | 37,500 | 1,50,000 ✓ | **(4 marks for complete table)** **Part (b) — Sinking Fund Method (7 marks):** Annual deposit $A$ satisfies: $A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i} = C_O - S$ **(1 mark)** With $i = 0.09$ and $N = 8$: $$ (1.09)^8 = 1.9926 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A &= (15{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000) \cdot \frac{0.09}{1.9926 - 1} \\ A &= 13{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.09}{0.9926} \\ A &= 13{,}50{,}000 \cdot 0.09067 \\ A &= \text{₹}1{,}22{,}400 \text{ per year (approx.)} \end{aligned} $$ **(3 marks for full derivation)** Accumulated fund at end of Year 8 = $C_O - S = \text{₹}13{,}50{,}000$ ✓ **(1 mark)** SLM annual charge: $D_{SLM} = \dfrac{13{,}50{,}000}{8} = \text{₹}1{,}68{,}750$ **(1 mark)** **Comparison (1 mark):** - Sinking Fund charge (₹1,22,400) < SLM charge (₹1,68,750) because the SF method leverages compound interest to accumulate the replacement amount. - SF method is preferred when the company wants to build an actual cash reserve for replacement; SLM is preferred for simplicity in bookkeeping. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > 1. **WDV rate computation:** Many students assume $R = 1/N$ instead of deriving $R = 1 - (S/C_O)^{1/N}$. KTU examiners specifically test the *rate derivation*. **Lose 2 marks** if skipped. > 2. **DDB vs WDV confusion:** In DDB, salvage value is *not* deducted from cost initially. Using the SLM-style depreciation base is a frequent error. > 3. **SYD fraction reversal:** The fraction must be $\dfrac{N - n + 1}{N(N+1)/2}$, not $\dfrac{n}{N(N+1)/2}$. Reversing gives an *increasing* schedule, which is wrong. > 4. **Sinking Fund formula:** The denominator is $(1+i)^N - 1$, not $(1+i)^{N-1} - 1$. Using the latter gives a slightly different (wrong) answer. > 5. **Unit consistency:** Cost figures in lakhs and crores are often mixed. Maintain consistent units throughout. > 6. **Salvage value verification:** Always verify that the final book value matches the given salvage value — if not, your calculation has an error. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Depreciation Methods** - **Definition:** Systematic allocation of depreciable cost over useful life; non-cash expense. ✓ - **Depreciable Cost:** $C_O - S$. The total amount to be written off. ✓ - **SLM:** Constant annual $D = (C_O - S)/N$. Book value decreases linearly. ✓ - **WDV rate:** $R = 1 - (S/C_O)^{1/N}$ when both $C_O$ and $S$ are given. ✓ - **DDB rate:** $R = 2/N$, applied to diminishing book value. ✓ - **SYD sum:** $\sum i = N(N+1)/2$. Year-$n$ fraction is $(N-n+1)/\text{sum}$. ✓ - **Sinking Fund:** $A = (C_O - S) \cdot i / [(1+i)^N - 1]$. Assumes reinvestment at rate $i$. ✓ - **Service Output:** Based on actual units produced / hours run. Useful for wear-and-tear assets. ✓ - **Capitalized Cost:** Used for infinite-life assets; $CC = \text{First Cost}/[1-(1+i)^{-N}]$. ✓ - **Pattern of choice:** SLM for buildings; accelerated methods (WDV/DDB/SYD) for machinery. ✓ - **Tax planning implication:** Accelerated depreciation → higher tax shield in early years → better cash flow. ✓ - **Verification step:** Sum of all yearly depreciations must equal $C_O - S$ in every method. ✓ - **Units:** Always maintain ₹/lakhs/crores consistently; KTU numericals usually have nice round values. ✓ - **Examiner's eye:** KTU values step-by-step derivations and final book-value tables — never skip them. ✓ > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy:** For any depreciation problem, always: (1) Identify the method from the question keywords, (2) State the formula, (3) Substitute values with units, (4) Tabulate the schedule, (5) Verify that final BV = Salvage. Following this 5-step ritual guarantees full marks. ---

topics.title: Simple Problems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple Problems

# 🎯 Simple Problems in Plant Layout and Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

**Simple Problems** in the context of Plant Layout and Design (KTU Module 4) refer to standard, numerically solvable engineering exercises involving **single-facility location**, **center of gravity computation**, **inter-departmental distance estimation**, and **material handling cost minimization**. These problems form the quantitative backbone of layout planning where students compute optimal plant location, evaluate layout alternatives based on distance × load × cost metrics, and rank departmental closeness based on relationship scores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must demonstrate competency in solving simple numerical problems related to **(a) Plant Location using Center of Gravity (Weber) Method**, **(b) Inter-Departmental Distance Computation**, and **(c) Material Handling Cost Calculation**. These carry direct weightage in Part B (14-mark) examination questions.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are setting up a small **bakery distribution point** that needs to serve **three housing colonies** (A, B, C). Colony A has 100 families needing bread, Colony B has 200 families, and Colony C has 50 families. You want to place your bakery truck parking spot (the facility) such that the **weighted average travel distance** to all colonies is minimized. Intuitively, you would place the bakery closer to Colony B because it has the highest demand (load).

This is exactly what the **Center of Gravity Method** does in plant location. Instead of "families needing bread," we use **load (tonnage/material flow)** as the weight, and instead of distance, we use **straight-line map distances**. The facility is placed at the **weighted centroid** of the load locations.

### 📐 Standard Metrics Used

- **Load / Quantity (Q):** Measured in **tonnes** or **tonnes/month** or **kg/day**.
- **Distance (d):** Measured in **kilometers (km)** or **meters (m)**.
- **Material Handling Cost (MHC):** Measured in **₹/tonne-km** or **$/unit-distance**.
- **Total Cost (TC):** Summation of all material flow cost lines, in **₹/month** or **$/year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to remember:** The earth curvature and altitude are **neglected** in simple problems — all locations are assumed on a **flat 2D Cartesian plane (X-Y coordinates)**.

---

### 🎯 Categories of "Simple Problems" in KTU Module 4

For KTU 2024 Scheme assessment, three standard categories dominate:

| # | Problem Type | Core Formula | Typical Marks |
|---|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | Center of Gravity (Weber Problem) | $X^* = \frac{\sum Q_i X_i}{\sum Q_i}$ | 7 or 14 |
| 2 | Distance Computation between Departments | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(X_j - X_i)^2 + (Y_j - Y_i)^2}$ | 7 |
| 3 | Material Handling Cost (MHC) | $MHC = \sum (Q_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times C)$ | 14 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 Operational Breakdown — How Each Problem Type Works

### 🔵 Problem Type 1: Center of Gravity (Weber) Method

This is a **single-facility location problem** where we want to find the **optimal (X, Y) coordinate** for placing a new plant such that the total weighted distance to existing sources/destinations is minimized.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Identify all existing facilities** (sources of raw material, markets, warehouses) that the new plant will serve.
2. **Assign Cartesian coordinates (Xᵢ, Yᵢ)** to each existing facility from a reference grid.
3. **Determine the load/weight (Qᵢ)** — this could be tonnes shipped per month, units demanded, etc.
4. **Compute weighted averages** — separately for X-coordinates and Y-coordinates.
5. **The resulting (X*, Y*) is the optimal plant location.**

**The "Why":** This is mathematically equivalent to finding the **center of mass** of a system of point masses, where each mass is the load $Q_i$. Just as gravity pulls equally toward the center of mass, transportation cost "pulls" the facility toward high-load points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption:** The cost per unit distance per unit load is **constant (uniform)** for all routes. If costs differ, the *Weber Problem with Variable Weights* (using modified coordinates) must be used, which is **out of scope** for simple problems.

---

### 🟢 Problem Type 2: Inter-Departmental Distance Computation

Given the (X, Y) coordinates of two departments $i$ and $j$, the **rectilinear (Manhattan) distance** or **Euclidean (straight-line) distance** is computed.

**Decision Rule for KTU:**

| Distance Type | Formula | When Used |
|---------------|---------|-----------|
| Euclidean (straight-line) | $\sqrt{(X_j-X_i)^2 + (Y_j-Y_i)^2}$ | When aisles are free-form (rare in industry) |
| Rectilinear (Manhattan) | $\vert X_j - X_i \vert + \vert Y_j - Y_i \vert$ | Most common in factory layouts with orthogonal aisles |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Always check whether the question specifies **"rectangular grid"** or **"Manhattan distance"** → use Rectilinear. Otherwise, default to **Euclidean**.

---

### 🟡 Problem Type 3: Material Handling Cost Calculation

Once departments are placed and distances computed, the **Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC)** between all pairs of interacting departments is:

$$TMHC = \sum_{i<j} Q_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot C$$

Where $Q_{ij}$ is the load between departments $i$ and $j$, $d_{ij}$ is the distance, and $C$ is the cost per unit load per unit distance.

**Logic behind it:**
- If two departments exchange **100 tonnes/day**, are **20 m apart**, and handling costs **₹5/tonne-m**, then daily cost = $100 \times 20 \times 5 = ₹10,000$.
- Summing across **all** such pairs gives the **total handling cost** of the layout.
- Lower TMHC = **better layout**.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optimal X-coordinate (Weber) | $X^* = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i X_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | km or m | Weighted average of X-coordinates |
| 2 | Optimal Y-coordinate (Weber) | $Y^* = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i Y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | km or m | Weighted average of Y-coordinates |
| 3 | Euclidean Distance | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(X_j - X_i)^2 + (Y_j - Y_i)^2}$ | km or m | Straight-line distance |
| 4 | Rectilinear Distance | $d_{ij} = \vert X_j - X_i \vert + \vert Y_j - Y_i \vert$ | km or m | Manhattan / grid distance |
| 5 | Pair-wise Material Handling Cost | $MHC_{ij} = Q_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times C$ | ₹/month | Cost between departments $i$ and $j$ |
| 6 | Total Material Handling Cost | $TMHC = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_{ij} d_{ij} C$ | ₹/month | Sum over all interacting pairs |
| 7 | Closeness Rating (REL Chart) | Weighted score of letters A, E, I, O, U, X | Unitless | A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=1 |
| 8 | Activity Relationship Score | $Score = \sum w_r \times n_r$ | Numeric | Sum of (rating weight × count) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** Center of Gravity method is used in **warehousing, dispatch centers, dairy plants, and cement distribution hubs**. Material handling cost calculations drive decisions in **automotive assembly plants, FMCG factories, and e-commerce fulfillment centers** — typically 20–50% of operating cost in manufacturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 Derivation 3.1 — Center of Gravity (Weber) Formula

**Starting Premise:** We wish to minimize the total weighted transportation cost $TC$ for a facility at $(X^*, Y^*)$ serving $n$ locations with loads $Q_i$ at $(X_i, Y_i)$.

$$TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot d_i \cdot C$$

Where $d_i$ is the Euclidean distance from facility to location $i$:

$$d_i = \sqrt{(X^* - X_i)^2 + (Y^* - Y_i)^2}$$

Assuming $C$ is constant, minimizing $TC$ is equivalent to minimizing $T = \sum Q_i d_i$.

**Differentiating $T$ with respect to $X^*$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial T}{\partial X^*} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial X^*}\left[\sqrt{(X^* - X_i)^2 + (Y^* - Y_i)^2}\right] \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \frac{(X^* - X_i)}{\sqrt{(X^* - X_i)^2 + (Y^* - Y_i)^2}} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \frac{(X^* - X_i)}{d_i} \\
&= 0 \quad \text{(for optimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Similarly for $Y^*$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \frac{(Y^* - Y_i)}{d_i} = 0$$

**In the simple (uniform weight) case**, $d_i$ cancels out from all terms (a simplifying assumption valid when $Q_i \cdot \frac{1}{d_i}$ contribution is uniform), giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i (X^* - X_i) &= 0 \\
X^* \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i X_i \\
\therefore X^* &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i X_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y^* &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i Y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **This is the exact form used in KTU simple problems.**

---

## 📐 Worked-Out Example 3.1 — Center of Gravity Problem

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern - 7 Mark Type]**

A company wants to locate a new central warehouse to serve four retail outlets. The coordinates (in km) and monthly load demand (in tonnes) are given below. Find the **optimal location using the Center of Gravity method**.

| Outlet | X (km) | Y (km) | Load Q (tonnes/month) |
|--------|--------|--------|------------------------|
| A | 10 | 5 | 200 |
| B | 30 | 15 | 500 |
| C | 25 | 35 | 300 |
| D | 5 | 25 | 400 |

### ✅ Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute $Q_i X_i$ for each outlet.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_A X_A &= 200 \times 10 = 2000 \\
Q_B X_B &= 500 \times 30 = 15000 \\
Q_C X_C &= 300 \times 25 = 7500 \\
Q_D X_D &= 400 \times 5 = 2000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the products:

$$\sum Q_i X_i = 2000 + 15000 + 7500 + 2000 = 26{,}500 \text{ tonne-km}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\sum Q_i$:

$$\sum Q_i = 200 + 500 + 300 + 400 = 1400 \text{ tonnes/month}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $X^*$:

$$X^* = \frac{26{,}500}{1400} = 18.93 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5:** Similarly compute $Q_i Y_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_A Y_A &= 200 \times 5 = 1000 \\
Q_B Y_B &= 500 \times 15 = 7500 \\
Q_C Y_C &= 300 \times 35 = 10500 \\
Q_D Y_D &= 400 \times 25 = 10000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Sum:

$$\sum Q_i Y_i = 1000 + 7500 + 10500 + 10000 = 29{,}000 \text{ tonne-km}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $Y^*$:

$$Y^* = \frac{29{,}000}{1400} = 20.71 \text{ km}$$

### ✅ Final Answer:

$$\boxed{(X^*, Y^*) = (18.93 \text{ km}, \ 20.71 \text{ km})}$$

---

## 📐 Worked-Out Example 3.2 — Material Handling Cost Problem

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern - 14 Mark Type]**

A factory layout has 5 departments with the following coordinates and material flows:

| From → To | Load Q (tonnes/day) |
|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 → 2 | 50 |
| 1 → 3 | 30 |
| 2 → 4 | 20 |
| 3 → 4 | 40 |
| 3 → 5 | 60 |
| 4 → 5 | 25 |

**Coordinates:** Dept 1: (0, 0); Dept 2: (10, 0); Dept 3: (10, 10); Dept 4: (20, 10); Dept 5: (20, 0). Material handling cost = **₹2 per tonne per meter**.

Using **Rectilinear (Manhattan) distance**, compute the **Total Daily Material Handling Cost**.

### ✅ Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute rectilinear distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12} &= \vert 10 - 0 \vert + \vert 0 - 0 \vert = 10 \text{ m} \\
d_{13} &= \vert 10 - 0 \vert + \vert 10 - 0 \vert = 20 \text{ m} \\
d_{24} &= \vert 20 - 10 \vert + \vert 10 - 0 \vert = 20 \text{ m} \\
d_{34} &= \vert 20 - 10 \vert + \vert 10 - 10 \vert = 10 \text{ m} \\
d_{35} &= \vert 20 - 10 \vert + \vert 0 - 10 \vert = 20 \text{ m} \\
d_{45} &= \vert 20 - 20 \vert + \vert 0 - 10 \vert = 10 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute individual MHC values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MHC_{12} &= 50 \times 10 \times 2 = ₹1000 \\
MHC_{13} &= 30 \times 20 \times 2 = ₹1200 \\
MHC_{24} &= 20 \times 20 \times 2 = ₹800 \\
MHC_{34} &= 40 \times 10 \times 2 = ₹800 \\
MHC_{35} &= 60 \times 20 \times 2 = ₹2400 \\
MHC_{45} &= 25 \times 10 \times 2 = ₹500 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum all MHC values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TMHC &= 1000 + 1200 + 800 + 800 + 2400 + 500 \\
&= ₹6700 \text{ per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### ✅ Final Answer:

$$\boxed{Total Daily Material Handling Cost = ₹6700}$$

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Layouts (when asked)

If KTU gives **two alternative layouts (A and B)** with the same departments and same loads but different coordinates, the student must:

1. Compute TMHC for Layout A.
2. Compute TMHC for Layout B.
3. **Recommend** the layout with lower TMHC, stating: *"Layout [X] is preferred since it results in a total material handling cost saving of ₹[Y] per day/month."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 4.1 — Center of Gravity Concept (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Plant Location Problem] --> Collect[Collect Data: Coordinates Xi Yi and Loads Qi]
    Collect --> ComputeX[Compute Sum Qi times Xi]
    Collect --> ComputeY[Compute Sum Qi times Yi]
    Collect --> ComputeQ[Compute Sum Qi]
    ComputeX --> FormulaX[Apply Formula X star equals Sum QiXi divided by Sum Qi]
    ComputeY --> FormulaY[Apply Formula Y star equals Sum QiYi divided by Sum Qi]
    ComputeQ --> FormulaX
    ComputeQ --> FormulaY
    FormulaX --> Combine[Combine to get Optimal Location X star Y star]
    FormulaY --> Combine
    Combine --> Plot[Plot on Map]
    Plot --> Decision[Verify Site Feasibility: Soil, Water, Power, Labor]
    Decision --> Output[Final Plant Location Selected]
```

## 📊 Diagram 4.2 — Material Handling Cost Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Layout Coordinates of All Departments] --> B[Compute Pairwise Distances dij]
    B --> C[Identify Material Flow Loads Qij]
    C --> D[Use Cost Rate C per tonne per meter]
    D --> E[Compute Pairwise MHC equals Qij times dij times C]
    E --> F[Sum all MHCij values]
    F --> G[Obtain Total Material Handling Cost]
    G --> H{Layout Acceptable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Finalize Layout]
    H -- No --> J[Modify Department Positions and Recompute]
    J --> A
```

## 📊 Diagram 4.3 — Problem-Solving Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Identify Type of Simple Problem] --> Q2{What is being Asked?}
    Q2 -->|Optimal location of single facility| R1[Center of Gravity Method]
    Q2 -->|Distance between two departments| R2[Use Rectilinear or Euclidean]
    Q2 -->|Total cost of layout| R3[MHC equals Load times Distance times Rate]
    R1 --> S1[Sum QiXi, Sum QiYi, Sum Qi, Divide]
    R2 --> S2[Apply Distance Formula Based on Grid Type]
    R3 --> S3[Compute Each Pair, Then Sum All Pairs]
    S1 --> T1[Report X star Y star]
    S2 --> T2[Report Distance in m or km]
    S3 --> T3[Report Total MHC in Rupees per time]
```

## 📊 Diagram 4.4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture (For Plant Layout Optimization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Inputs: Coordinates, Loads, Cost Rate] --> Process1[Distance Computation Module]
    Input --> Process2[Load Aggregation Module]
    Process1 --> Engine[MHC Computation Engine]
    Process2 --> Engine
    Input --> Process3[Cost Rate Module]
    Process3 --> Engine
    Engine --> Output1[Pair-wise MHC Matrix]
    Engine --> Output2[Total Layout Cost]
    Engine --> Output3[Optimal Location Coordinates]
    Output1 --> Compare[Layout Comparison Module]
    Output2 --> Compare
    Output3 --> Report[Final Report Generation]
    Compare --> Report
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define the **Center of Gravity method** for plant location. Mention any two assumptions.

> **Model Answer:**
> The Center of Gravity method (also called the Weber Problem) is a quantitative technique used to find the optimal location of a single new facility that minimizes the total weighted transportation cost to a set of existing facilities. The optimal coordinates $(X^*, Y^*)$ are computed as the load-weighted averages of the X and Y coordinates of existing facilities:
> $$X^* = \frac{\sum Q_i X_i}{\sum Q_i}, \quad Y^* = \frac{\sum Q_i Y_i}{\sum Q_i}$$
>
> **Two Assumptions:**
> 1. Transportation cost per unit distance per unit load is **constant** for all routes.
> 2. All locations lie on a **flat 2D plane** (curvature of earth neglected).
>
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

### **Question 2:** Distinguish between **Euclidean distance** and **Rectilinear (Manhattan) distance** with formulas.

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Euclidean | Rectilinear |
> |--------|-----------|-------------|
> | Formula | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(X_j-X_i)^2 + (Y_j-Y_i)^2}$ | $d_{ij} = \vert X_j - X_i \vert + \vert Y_j - Y_i \vert$ |
> | Path Type | Straight-line (as the crow flies) | Grid-aligned (axis-parallel) |
> | Industrial Use | Warehousing, open layouts | Factory floors with orthogonal aisles |
>
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ❓ Choice Question A (14 Marks) — **Center of Gravity + Distance Hybrid**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted Model Question]**

A steel plant must be located to serve **four distribution centers** with the following data:

| Center | X (km) | Y (km) | Load (tonnes/month) |
|--------|--------|--------|---------------------|
| P | 20 | 30 | 800 |
| Q | 50 | 10 | 600 |
| R | 40 | 60 | 400 |
| S | 10 | 50 | 200 |

**(a)** Compute the optimal plant location using the Center of Gravity method. (7 marks)

**(b)** After locating the plant at the optimal point, compute the **total monthly transportation cost** if the cost rate is **₹3 per tonne per km** and Euclidean distances are used. (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Center of Gravity (7 marks)

**Compute $Q_i X_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_P X_P &= 800 \times 20 = 16000 \\
Q_Q X_Q &= 600 \times 50 = 30000 \\
Q_R X_R &= 400 \times 40 = 16000 \\
Q_S X_S &= 200 \times 10 = 2000 \\
\sum Q_i X_i &= 64000 \text{ tonne-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $Q_i Y_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_P Y_P &= 800 \times 30 = 24000 \\
Q_Q Y_Q &= 600 \times 10 = 6000 \\
Q_R Y_R &= 400 \times 60 = 24000 \\
Q_S Y_S &= 200 \times 50 = 10000 \\
\sum Q_i Y_i &= 64000 \text{ tonne-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $\sum Q_i$:**

$$\sum Q_i = 800 + 600 + 400 + 200 = 2000 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Optimal Coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X^* &= \frac{64000}{2000} = 32 \text{ km} \\
Y^* &= \frac{64000}{2000} = 32 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{(X^*, Y^*) = (32, 32) \text{ km}}$$

#### Part (b) — Total Transportation Cost (7 marks)

**Euclidean distances from (32, 32) to each center:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_P &= \sqrt{(32-20)^2 + (32-30)^2} = \sqrt{144 + 4} = \sqrt{148} = 12.17 \text{ km} \\
d_Q &= \sqrt{(32-50)^2 + (32-10)^2} = \sqrt{324 + 484} = \sqrt{808} = 28.43 \text{ km} \\
d_R &= \sqrt{(32-40)^2 + (32-60)^2} = \sqrt{64 + 784} = \sqrt{848} = 29.12 \text{ km} \\
d_S &= \sqrt{(32-10)^2 + (32-50)^2} = \sqrt{484 + 324} = \sqrt{808} = 28.43 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Transportation Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= C \sum Q_i d_i = 3 \times (800 \times 12.17 + 600 \times 28.43 + 400 \times 29.12 + 200 \times 28.43) \\
&= 3 \times (9736 + 17058 + 11648 + 5686) \\
&= 3 \times 44128 \\
&= ₹1{,}32{,}384 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Total Monthly Cost = ₹1{,}32{,}384}$$

---

### ❓ Choice Question B (14 Marks) — **Material Handling Cost Comparison**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted Model Question]**

A factory has **5 departments**. Two alternative layouts are proposed. Material handling cost rate = **₹5/tonne-meter**. Compute the **Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC)** for each layout and recommend the better one.

**Material Flow Data:**

| From → To | Load (tonnes/day) |
|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 → 2 | 100 |
| 1 → 3 | 50 |
| 2 → 4 | 80 |
| 3 → 5 | 120 |
| 4 → 5 | 60 |

**Layout 1 (coordinates in meters):**
| Dept | X | Y |
|------|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 20 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 20 |
| 4 | 20 | 20 |
| 5 | 40 | 10 |

**Layout 2 (coordinates in meters):**
| Dept | X | Y |
|------|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 10 |
| 2 | 10 | 0 |
| 3 | 30 | 10 |
| 4 | 30 | 30 |
| 5 | 50 | 10 |

Use **Rectilinear distance**.

### ✅ Model Solution:

#### Layout 1 — Rectilinear Distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12} &= \vert 20-0 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 20 \\
d_{13} &= \vert 0-0 \vert + \vert 20-0 \vert = 20 \\
d_{24} &= \vert 20-20 \vert + \vert 20-0 \vert = 20 \\
d_{35} &= \vert 40-0 \vert + \vert 10-20 \vert = 50 \\
d_{45} &= \vert 40-20 \vert + \vert 10-20 \vert = 30 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Layout 1 — MHC Calculations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MHC_{12} &= 100 \times 20 \times 5 = ₹10{,}000 \\
MHC_{13} &= 50 \times 20 \times 5 = ₹5{,}000 \\
MHC_{24} &= 80 \times 20 \times 5 = ₹8{,}000 \\
MHC_{35} &= 120 \times 50 \times 5 = ₹30{,}000 \\
MHC_{45} &= 60 \times 30 \times 5 = ₹9{,}000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Layout 1 — Total:

$$\boxed{TMHC_{Layout 1} = ₹62{,}000 \text{ per day}}$$

---

#### Layout 2 — Rectilinear Distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12} &= \vert 10-10 \vert + \vert 0-10 \vert = 10 \\
d_{13} &= \vert 30-10 \vert + \vert 10-10 \vert = 20 \\
d_{24} &= \vert 30-10 \vert + \vert 30-0 \vert = 50 \\
d_{35} &= \vert 50-30 \vert + \vert 10-10 \vert = 20 \\
d_{45} &= \vert 50-30 \vert + \vert 10-30 \vert = 40 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Layout 2 — MHC Calculations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MHC_{12} &= 100 \times 10 \times 5 = ₹5{,}000 \\
MHC_{13} &= 50 \times 20 \times 5 = ₹5{,}000 \\
MHC_{24} &= 80 \times 50 \times 5 = ₹20{,}000 \\
MHC_{35} &= 120 \times 20 \times 5 = ₹12{,}000 \\
MHC_{45} &= 60 \times 40 \times 5 = ₹12{,}000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Layout 2 — Total:

$$\boxed{TMHC_{Layout 2} = ₹54{,}000 \text{ per day}}$$

### ✅ Recommendation:

$$\text{Savings} = 62000 - 54000 = ₹8{,}000 \text{ per day} = ₹24{,}000 \text{ per month (30 days)}$$

> **Layout 2 is preferred** because it reduces material handling cost by **₹8,000 per day** (≈ 12.9% savings) compared to Layout 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "× Cost Rate" factor:** Many students compute only $Q \times d$ and forget to multiply by $C$. This loses **3 marks instantly** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Using Euclidean when Rectilinear is asked:** Read the question carefully. Most KTU questions on factory layouts specify "Manhattan" or "Rectangular grid" — defaulting to Euclidean loses **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate steps:** Even though the formula is one line, KTU expects the **$\sum Q_i X_i$ table expanded** — show every product explicitly.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing km and m in the same problem leads to **wrong TMHC by orders of magnitude**. Always normalize units first.
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Carry 4 decimals until the final answer to avoid cumulative rounding error.
> 6. **Forgetting to sum $\sum Q_i$ separately:** Students often forget the denominator, especially when $Q$ is given in different units (some in tonnes/month, some in kg/day).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist

- ✅ The **Center of Gravity Method** solves **single-facility location** problems using load-weighted average of coordinates.
- ✅ Formula: $X^* = \dfrac{\sum Q_i X_i}{\sum Q_i}$, $Y^* = \dfrac{\sum Q_i Y_i}{\sum Q_i}$.
- ✅ **Euclidean distance:** $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(X_j-X_i)^2 + (Y_j-Y_i)^2}$.
- ✅ **Rectilinear distance:** $d_{ij} = \vert X_j - X_i \vert + \vert Y_j - Y_i \vert$.
- ✅ **Material Handling Cost:** $MHC = Q \times d \times C$.
- ✅ **Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC):** Sum of all pair-wise MHCs — lower is better.
- ✅ **Always normalize units** (all distances in m or all in km; all loads in tonnes/day or tonnes/month).
- ✅ **KTU expects tabular work** — present $\sum Q_i X_i$, $\sum Q_i Y_i$, $\sum Q_i$ as separate computations.
- ✅ For comparison problems, always **state the saving** and recommend with justification.
- ✅ Use **Rectilinear distance** unless the problem explicitly says "straight-line" or "Euclidean".
- ✅ Assumptions to state: (1) uniform cost rate, (2) flat 2D plane, (3) loads are deterministic.
- ✅ The Center of Gravity solution is **not necessarily the global optimum** — only a heuristic. State this if asked about limitations.
- ✅ **Common error trap:** Don't include self-loops ($i = j$) in TMHC summation — only **$i \neq j$** pairs.
- ✅ **Notation:** $Q_{ij}$ is symmetric in plant layout, so compute only once per pair.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"W-D-C-S"** → **W**eighted average (location), **D**istance (rectilinear default), **C**ost rate, **S**um all pairs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Replacement: Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET634 - PLANT LAYOUT AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equipment Replacement: Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equipment Replacement – Repair** is a strategic industrial engineering function that evaluates whether an existing piece of physical asset (machine, equipment, or plant component) should continue to be maintained through **repair (rehabilitation)** or be **replaced (substituted)** with a new or alternative asset, based on minimizing the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** or the **Total Life Cycle Cost (TLCC)** over its economic service life.

In the context of **Plant Layout and Design**, the *Repair* decision branch of Equipment Replacement Analysis (ERA) deals specifically with the engineering-economic problem of determining **how long an ageing asset should be kept in service by periodic or corrective repair**, and at what point the cumulative cost of repeated repairs outweighs the cost of procuring a modern replacement.

The decision framework is governed by the **Minimum Cost Life** principle and the **Capitalized Cost** model, both of which are derived under the assumption of **constant discount rate (i)** and **time-value-of-money**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Old Family Car**
> Imagine owning a 15-year-old car. Every year it breaks down — new brakes, clutch plate, suspension, AC repair. Each repair makes the car "work again," but the **annual repair bills keep rising**. One day, a mechanic tells you: *"Buying a new car for ₹8 lakh will save you ₹1.2 lakh per year in combined running + repair costs."* The decision — *"Should I keep repairing the old car or replace it?"* — is **exactly** the Equipment Replacement: Repair problem in industrial engineering.

- **Repair** = patching the old car every year.
- **Replacement** = buying a new car with lower running cost.
- **Decision Point** = the year where Annual Cost of Old (Repairs + Maintenance) **exceeds** Annual Cost of New.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Defender** | The existing equipment being repaired and evaluated. |
| **Challenger** | The proposed replacement equipment. |
| **Minimum Cost Life (N\*)** | Service life at which Equivalent Annual Cost is minimum. |
| **Economic Service Life** | Optimal age of replacement. |
| **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** | Factor to compute future value of periodic deposits. |
| **Repair Limit** | Maximum justifiable repair expenditure per cycle. |
| **Capitalized Cost (CC)** | Present worth of an infinite series of uniform annual costs. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical/Engineering Constants Used:**
> - Discount rate (interest rate): typically **i = 8% to 15%** in KTU problems.
> - Economic life assumption: typically **5 to 15 years** for industrial machinery.
> - Salvage value: typically **0 to 20%** of original cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Repair Decisions

The Equipment Replacement: Repair analysis rests on **three pillars**:

**(a) Time Value of Money**
A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow. All repair costs, salvage values, and operating costs are brought to a **common reference point** (present worth, annual worth, or capitalized cost) using the interest rate **i**.

**(b) Cost Gradient Behaviour**
As an equipment ages:
- **Operating & Maintenance (O&M) cost rises** approximately linearly with age.
- **Repair cost rises** exponentially or sharply after a threshold year.
- **Salvage value declines** with age.
- **Annual capital recovery cost declines** with age (because capital is spread over more years).

The **intersection of rising repair cost and falling capital recovery cost** defines the **Minimum Cost Life** — the year where Total Equivalent Annual Cost is at its minimum.

**(c) Defender vs Challenger Comparison**
When the Minimum Cost Life of the *Defender* (old equipment) exceeds the minimum EAC of a *Challenger* (new equipment), replacement is justified.

### 2.2 The Mathematical Model

Let:
- **P** = Original purchase cost of equipment.
- **Sn** = Salvage value after *n* years.
- **Mn** = Annual maintenance + repair cost in year *n*.
- **i** = Effective annual interest rate (discount rate).
- **n** = Service life in years.

**Step 1 — Present Worth of one life cycle:**

$$PW_n = P - S_n \cdot (P/F, i, n) + \sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k \cdot (P/F, i, k)$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC):**

$$EAC_n = PW_n \cdot (A/P, i, n)$$

**Step 3 — Find Minimum Cost Life (N\*):**

$$N^* = n \;\; \text{where} \;\; EAC_n \;\; \text{is minimum}$$

**Step 4 — Capitalized Cost (for infinite replacement):**

$$CC = \frac{EAC_{N^*}}{i}$$

### 2.3 Simplified Linear Repair-Cost Model (Most Used in KTU Exams)

For KTU 2024 Scheme problems, the **linear gradient** model is standard:

$$M_n = M_1 + (n-1) \cdot g$$

where:
- **M₁** = Maintenance cost in year 1.
- **g** = Annual gradient increase in maintenance cost.
- Salvage value declines linearly: **Sn = P − n·d** (where d is depreciation per year).

Then:

$$EAC_n = (P - S_n) \cdot (A/P, i, n) + i \cdot S_n + \left[M_1 + \frac{(n-1)g}{2}\right]$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **complete, exam-ready formula sheet** for Equipment Replacement: Repair. Every KTU 2024 problem on this topic can be solved using these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Compound Factor Form | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Single Payment Present Worth | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | $(P/F, i, n)$ | ₹ |
| 2 | Single Payment Future Worth | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | $(F/P, i, n)$ | ₹ |
| 3 | Capital Recovery (A from P) | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $(A/P, i, n)$ | ₹/yr |
| 4 | Sinking Fund Factor | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $(A/F, i, n)$ | ₹/yr |
| 5 | Present Worth of Gradient | $P_G = G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$ | $(P/G, i, n)$ | ₹ |
| 6 | Capitalized Cost (Infinite) | $CC = A / i$ | — | ₹ |
| 7 | Minimum EAC | $EAC_n = (P - S_n)(A/P,i,n) + iS_n + M_n$ | — | ₹/yr |
| 8 | Optimal Life Condition | $EAC_{n^*} \le EAC_{n^*-1}$ and $EAC_{n^*} \le EAC_{n^*+1}$ | — | year |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The factor $(A/P, i, n)$ is **NOT** simply $1/n$. It includes interest on the unpaid balance. Confusing it with straight-line depreciation is the **#1 error** in valuation.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of This Analysis

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Decides whether to overhaul an old CNC machine or buy a new one.
- **Process Industries:** Determines repair-vs-replace for pumps, compressors, heat exchangers.
- **Power Sector:** Economical life of turbines, generators, transformers.
- **Transport:** Fleet renewal of trucks, ships, locomotives.
- **Facility Management:** HVAC systems, boilers, elevators.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)

**Statement:** *Derive the Equivalent Annual Cost of an equipment whose purchase price is P, salvage value after n years is Sn, and annual maintenance cost follows the linear pattern Mn = M₁ + (n−1)g.*

**Step 1 — Identify the three cost components.**

A piece of equipment in service for *n* years incurs three distinct cash-flow types:
1. **Initial Capital Outlay (P)** at year 0.
2. **Annual Operating + Repair Cost (Mn)** every year from year 1 to year n.
3. **Salvage Recovery (Sn)** at the end of year *n* (an inflow).

**Step 2 — Compute Present Worth of the salvage value.**

Salvage is a *future* cash flow at year *n*. To bring it to year 0:

$$PW_{salvage} = S_n \cdot (P/F, i, n) = \frac{S_n}{(1+i)^n}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Present Worth of annual maintenance costs (Arithmetic Gradient Series).**

Annual cost in year *k* is:

$$M_k = M_1 + (k-1) \cdot g$$

This is the classic *arithmetic gradient* series. Its present worth has two parts:
- Uniform series of amount M₁ over *n* years.
- Gradient portion: $0, g, 2g, 3g, ..., (n-1)g$.

Present worth of uniform part:

$$PW_{uniform} = M_1 \cdot (P/A, i, n) = M_1 \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

Present worth of gradient part:

$$PW_{gradient} = g \cdot (P/G, i, n) = g \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$$

Therefore, total present worth of maintenance:

$$PW_{M} = M_1 \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} + g \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble total Present Worth of one life cycle.**

$$PW_n = P - \frac{S_n}{(1+i)^n} + M_1 \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} + g \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Present Worth to Equivalent Annual Cost.**

Multiply by the Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$:

$$EAC_n = PW_n \cdot (A/P, i, n)$$

Substituting:

$$EAC_n = \left[P - \frac{S_n}{(1+i)^n} + \frac{M_1[(1+i)^n - 1]}{i(1+i)^n} + \frac{g[(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1]}{i^2 (1+i)^n}\right] \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify using the relation $S_n(A/P,i,n) = \frac{i \cdot S_n}{1-(1+i)^{-n}}$.**

After algebraic simplification (dropping the long expansion), the textbook compact form is:

$$\boxed{EAC_n = (P - S_n)(A/P, i, n) + i \cdot S_n + M_1 + \frac{(n-1)g}{2}}$$

**Interpretation of each term:**
- $(P - S_n)(A/P, i, n)$ → annualized recovery of *lost* capital (depreciation + interest on declining balance).
- $i \cdot S_n$ → annual interest that *could have been earned* on the salvage value (opportunity cost).
- $M_1$ → year-1 maintenance baseline.
- $(n-1)g/2$ → average gradient cost over *n* years.

### 3.2 Worked Example (Standard KTU-Style Problem)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> A machine costs **₹50,000** with installation. Its maintenance cost in year 1 is **₹4,000**, increasing by **₹1,500 per year** thereafter. Salvage value is **zero**. The interest rate is **10%**. Find the **Minimum Cost Life**.

**Step 1 — List the data.**

$$P = 50{,}000, \quad M_1 = 4{,}000, \quad g = 1{,}500, \quad S_n = 0, \quad i = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — Compute EAC for n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8.**

When $S_n = 0$, the formula simplifies to:

$$EAC_n = P \cdot (A/P, 10\%, n) + M_1 + \frac{(n-1)g}{2}$$

**Year 1:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 1) = 1.1000$
- $EAC_1 = 50{,}000 \times 1.10 + 4{,}000 + 0 = 55{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 59{,}000$ ₹

**Year 2:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 2) = 0.5762$
- $EAC_2 = 50{,}000 \times 0.5762 + 4{,}000 + 750 = 28{,}810 + 4{,}750 = 33{,}560$ ₹

**Year 3:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 3) = 0.4021$
- $EAC_3 = 50{,}000 \times 0.4021 + 4{,}000 + 1{,}500 = 20{,}105 + 5{,}500 = 25{,}605$ ₹

**Year 4:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 4) = 0.3155$
- $EAC_4 = 50{,}000 \times 0.3155 + 4{,}000 + 2{,}250 = 15{,}775 + 6{,}250 = 22{,}025$ ₹

**Year 5:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 5) = 0.2638$
- $EAC_5 = 50{,}000 \times 0.2638 + 4{,}000 + 3{,}000 = 13{,}190 + 7{,}000 = 20{,}190$ ₹

**Year 6:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 6) = 0.2296$
- $EAC_6 = 50{,}000 \times 0.2296 + 4{,}000 + 3{,}750 = 11{,}480 + 7{,}750 = 19{,}230$ ₹

**Year 7:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 7) = 0.2054$
- $EAC_7 = 50{,}000 \times 0.2054 + 4{,}000 + 4{,}500 = 10{,}270 + 8{,}500 = 18{,}770$ ₹

**Year 8:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 8) = 0.1874$
- $EAC_8 = 50{,}000 \times 0.1874 + 4{,}000 + 5{,}250 = 9{,}370 + 9{,}250 = 18{,}620$ ₹

**Year 9:**
- $(A/P, 10\%, 9) = 0.1736$
- $EAC_9 = 50{,}000 \times 0.1736 + 4{,}000 + 6{,}000 = 8{,}680 + 10{,}000 = 18{,}680$ ₹

**Step 3 — Identify Minimum.**

$$\boxed{N^* = 8 \text{ years}, \quad EAC_{min} = 18{,}620 \text{ ₹/yr}}$$

The minimum lies at n = 8 because $EAC_8 < EAC_7$ and $EAC_8 < EAC_9$.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Table

| n | (A/P, 10%, n) | Capital Recovery | Maintenance | **EAC (₹)** |
|---|---------------|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | 1.1000 | 55,000 | 4,000 | 59,000 |
| 2 | 0.5762 | 28,810 | 4,750 | 33,560 |
| 3 | 0.4021 | 20,105 | 5,500 | 25,605 |
| 4 | 0.3155 | 15,775 | 6,250 | 22,025 |
| 5 | 0.2638 | 13,190 | 7,000 | 20,190 |
| 6 | 0.2296 | 11,480 | 7,750 | 19,230 |
| 7 | 0.2054 | 10,270 | 8,500 | 18,770 |
| **8** | **0.1874** | **9,370** | **9,250** | **18,620 ← MIN** |
| 9 | 0.1736 | 8,680 | 10,000 | 18,680 |

### 3.4 Repair-Limit Formula (Capitalized Cost Approach)

When a single repair is contemplated (e.g., should we spend ₹X now to extend life by Y years?), the **repair limit** is:

$$\text{Repair Limit} = EAC_{new} \cdot (P/A, i, Y) - \text{avoided future costs}$$

If actual repair cost ≤ Repair Limit → **Repair is justified.**
If actual repair cost > Repair Limit → **Replace immediately.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Equipment Replacement Decision Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Equipment In Service] --> B{Age n of Equipment?}
    B -- n = 0 --> C[Acquire New Asset]
    B -- n > 0 --> D[Collect Annual Data]
    D --> D1[Maintenance Cost Mn]
    D --> D2[Salvage Value Sn]
    D --> D3[Operating Cost On]
    D1 --> E[Compute EAC for each n]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Minimum EAC found at N*?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Identify N star]
    G --> H{Compare with Challenger}
    H -- Defender EAC lower --> I[REPAIR and Continue]
    H -- Challenger EAC lower --> J[REPLACE Now]
    I --> K[Continue Monitoring till EAC Defender equals EAC Challenger]
    J --> L[Procure Challenger Asset]
    K --> M[End of Economic Life - Final Replacement]
    L --> M
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Minimum Cost Life" of an equipment with respect to equipment replacement analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Minimum Cost Life (N\*)** of an equipment is defined as the service life (in years) at which the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** of owning and operating the equipment is at its **minimum value**. It represents the economically optimal age at which the equipment should ideally be replaced. Mathematically, N\* is the value of *n* that satisfies:
> $$\frac{d(EAC_n)}{dn} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad EAC_{n^*} < EAC_{n^* \pm 1}$$
> Beyond this life, the rising maintenance and repair costs outweigh the benefit of spreading the capital cost over more years.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the "Defender-Challenger" concept in equipment replacement? Explain in 3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Defender-Challenger** concept is a comparative economic framework used in equipment replacement analysis.
> - **Defender** = the existing equipment that is being evaluated for continued use (with possible repair).
> - **Challenger** = the proposed alternative (new or used equipment) that could replace the defender.
> The decision is made by comparing the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** of both: the option with the **lower EAC** is selected.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** of an equipment whose annual maintenance cost increases in **arithmetic gradient** of ₹g per year, starting from ₹M₁ in year 1. Assume purchase price **P**, salvage value **Sn** after **n** years, and interest rate **i**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine is purchased for **₹1,00,000**. The maintenance cost in year 1 is **₹6,000**, rising by **₹2,000 per year** (gradient). The salvage value after n years is given by $S_n = 10{,}000 - 1{,}000n$ for n = 1, 2, ... The interest rate is **12% per annum**. Determine the **economical life (Minimum Cost Life)** of the machine. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of EAC (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the three cash-flow streams over *n* years:
1. Initial cost **P** at year 0.
2. Maintenance cost in year k: $M_k = M_1 + (k-1)g$.
3. Salvage value **Sn** received at year n.

**Step 2:** Present Worth of one cycle:

$$PW_n = P - S_n(1+i)^{-n} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} M_k (1+i)^{-k}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute gradient series:

$$PW_n = P - S_n(1+i)^{-n} + M_1 \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} + g \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - in - 1}{i^2(1+i)^n}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to Equivalent Annual Cost by multiplying with $(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$:

$$EAC_n = (P - S_n)(A/P, i, n) + iS_n + M_1 + \frac{(n-1)g}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Term-by-term interpretation:**
- **Term 1:** Annualized recovery of depreciated capital.
- **Term 2:** Interest foregone on the salvage value.
- **Term 3:** Baseline maintenance of year 1.
- **Term 4:** Average annual gradient cost.

**Marks distribution (7):** Formulation (2) + PW computation (2) + EAC conversion (2) + Interpretation (1).

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$P = 1{,}00{,}000$, $M_1 = 6{,}000$, $g = 2{,}000$, $i = 0.12$, $S_n = 10{,}000 - 1{,}000n$.

**Compute EAC for n = 1 to 8:**

Formula used:

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **three primary methods** of equipment replacement analysis: (i) Minimum Cost Life method, (ii) Capitalized Cost method, and (iii) Defender-Challenger method. Compare their applicability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An existing machine has an **EAC of ₹45,000/year** at its minimum cost life of 7 years. A new machine (challenger) costs **₹3,00,000** with annual operating cost **₹18,000** and salvage **₹50,000** after **10 years**. The interest rate is **15%**. Determine whether the existing machine should be **repaired or replaced**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Three Primary Methods (7 marks):**

**1. Minimum Cost Life Method:**
- Finds the service life *N\** where EAC is minimum.
- Useful when only **one equipment** is considered.
- Doesn't directly compare alternatives.

**2. Capitalized Cost Method:**
- Computes **present worth over infinite life**.
- Used for **perpetual assets** (e.g., building, infrastructure).
- Formula: $CC = EAC_{N^*}/i$.
- Best for **long-horizon comparison**.

**3. Defender-Challenger Method:**
- **Direct pairwise comparison** of two equipment alternatives over their respective economic lives.
- Decision rule: choose the option with **lower EAC** (or lower CC).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Min Cost Life | Capitalized Cost | Defender-Challenger |
|--------|---------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Scope | Single asset | Infinite horizon | Two alternatives |
| Output | N\* and EAC | CC value | Replace or Repair |
| Use case | Initial planning | Long-life assets | Real-time decision |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Defender EAC = ₹45,000/year (given directly).
- Challenger: P = 3,00,000, O&M = 18,000/year, S₁₀ = 50,000, i = 0.15, n = 10.

**Step 1: Compute EAC of Challenger (assuming minimum cost life is 10 years):**

$$EAC_{C} = (P - S_n)(A/P, 15\%, 10) + i \cdot S_n + O\&M$$

Compute $(A/P, 15\%, 10)$:

$$(A/P, 15\%, 10) = \frac{0.15(1.15)^{10}}{(1.15)^{10} - 1} = \frac{0.15 \times 4.0456}{4.0456 - 1} = \frac{0.6068}{3.0456} = 0.1993$$

**Step 2: Substitute:**

$$EAC_{C} = (3{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000)(0.1993) + 0.15(50{,}000) + 18{,}000$$

$$EAC_{C} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.1993 + 7{,}500 + 18{,}000$$

$$EAC_{C} = 49{,}825 + 7{,}500 + 18{,}000 = 75{,}325 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 3: Compare with Defender.**

$$EAC_{Defender} = 45{,}000 < EAC_{Challenger} = 75{,}325$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{Since Defender's EAC (₹45,000) < Challenger's EAC (₹75,325), the existing machine should be REPAIRED and retained.}}$$

**Sensitivity check:** Even if challenger is cheaper to operate, its high capital cost makes it uneconomical at i = 15%. If interest rate were lower (say 8%), the decision might flip.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mistake:** Treating $(A/P, i, n)$ as $1/n$ (straight-line). → **Loss: 1 to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Mistake:** Forgetting to subtract salvage before multiplying by $(A/P, i, n)$. → **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Mistake:** Confusing *salvage interest term* $i \cdot S_n$ with *salvage recovery*. → **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Mistake:** Not checking that $EAC_{n-1} > EAC_n > EAC_{n+1}$ at the minimum. → **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Mistake:** In Defender-Challenger problems, comparing **present worths of unequal lives** without converting to EAC. → **Loss: 2-3 marks.**
> 6. **Mistake:** Failing to consider **negative salvage** (demolition cost) — if $S_n$ becomes negative, the problem ends.
> 7. **Mistake:** Wrong interpolation or stopping the calculation 1 year before the actual minimum.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
